title: A Minimal Brain–Computer Interface for Efficient Mission-Level Control in Industrial Human–Robot Interaction
authors: Mojtaba Porghoveh, Raffaele Carli, Mariagrazia Dotoli
abstract: As robots increasingly operate in industrial environments, ergonomic and hands-free human–robot interaction becomes essential. This paper proposes a brain–computer interface (BCI) for mission-level icon selection (e.g., scan, mill, pick, return), emphasizing operator comfort and low computational cost. Electroencephalography data are preprocessed by low-pass filtering, and wavelet coefficients are extracted as descriptive features. To reduce hardware complexity, two electrode selection methods are tested: (i) Forward Stepwise Feature Selection and (ii) Independent Component Analysis based ranking. For classification, a compact neural network with Fuzzy C-means clustering is employed. Using a public P300 speller dataset, mission icons are mapped to the stimulus matrix to emulate drone mission selection in warehouse scenarios. The reduced-channel approach achieves accuracy comparable to full-channel models while requiring only six electrodes, a training set of 360 samples, and fewer than 10–16 training epochs. The results show that compute-efficient BCIs with minimal electrodes can effectively support practical mission-level robot control in industrial settings.

title: Privacy-Preserving and Coercion-Resistant E-Voting via Unlinkable Biometric Credentials on a Hybrid Permissioned Blockchain
authors: Kumar Shubhranshu, Harsh Kumar Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The research presents a secure voting system which enables verification of all electoral processes through its complete verification mechanisms at the ballot box using fingerprint authentication and permissioned blockchain technology to issue anonymous credentials. The proposed system enables local eligibility authentication through its biometric data protection which does not require biometric template storage in its on-chain system. Voters use the blind signature system to obtain credentials which allow them to send encrypted votes with zero-knowledge Aproofs that verify their ballot’s authenticity while keeping their voting choice hidden. The system records votes onto a Byzantine fault-tolerant ledger which uses threshold decryption to calculate final results. The system establishes auditability through its Merkle root system which periodically registers ballot Merkle roots on the public chain. The system maintains voter privacy through its revoting feature which enables voters to cast new votes while the system handles large election events. The experimental tests performed on a Hyperledger Besu system with ten nodes showed that the system maintained a throughput rate above 1600 TPS while it achieved confirmation times below one second during the controlled testing input conditions.

title: Ensemble learning methods as a tool for more robust prediction of mobile payment usage
authors: Mariia Godz, Francisco Liébana-Cabanillas, Juan Lara-Rubio, David A. Pelta
abstract: The rapid digitalization of financial services has transformed mobile peer-to-peer (P2P) payments, exemplified by Bizum in Spain, into a critical area for understanding and shaping consumer behavior. This paper examines the application of machine learning (ML) techniques for predicting individual adoption of P2P mobile payments, with a particular focus on the practical utility of predictions for FinTech companies and financial institutions, rather than solely on forecasting performance. It evaluates a range of individual classifiers alongside two ensemble learning methods - Hard Voting and a Weighted Average method - those aggregates predictions according to the strengths of each individual model. The results show that the ensemble learning methods not only improve forecast reliability but also bring practical operational value to FinTech stakeholders.

title: Relay Protection Functioning Under Integration of Distributed Generation: Challenges for Ukraine
authors: Mykola P. Bolotnyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The integration of small-scale distributed generation into power system requires an impact assessment on stability and reliability of adjacent electric network. The characteristics research of electric power systems modes with small-scale distributed generation was carried out. The features of electrical modes that have the greatest influence on relay protection functioning was revealed in paper. Approaches to management of small-scale distributed generation under emergency are proposed. The relay protection requirements for electric power systems with smallscale distributed generation have been formed for management decentralization in power system of Ukraine.

title: Adaptive Knowledge Retrieval in Multimodal GraphRAG via Cross-Modal Relationship Learning
authors: Dipanjan Sengupta, Samriddha Chatterjee, Prayati Dutta, Avinandan Bandyopadhyay
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enriching large language models (LLM) with domain-specific knowledge. While GraphRAG extends this approach through Knowledge Graphs (KG), existing methods remain largely confined to text-based entities. This paper proposes a multimodal framework that integrates textual and illustration content into a unified KG, further aggregated into community-level knowledge for deeper contextualization, while preserving modality specific indices for efficient data storage. The framework supports client-driven customization at two stages: (1) during graph construction, where relationship-building strategies can be tailored to control how associations are derived, and (2) at query time, where users can select retrieval scope-text-only, illustration-only, or multimodal. Retrieval follows a two-stage process: direct matches are first identified within modality-specific indices, then expanded through the KG to capture semantically related entities from the other modality. For multimodal queries, a reranking step is applied after LLM inference to optimize relevance and coherence across modalities. This architecture enables faster, adaptive and context-rich augmentation of LLM, overcoming the limitations of existing GraphRAG approaches. By supporting multimodal reasoning, customizable retrieval pathways and inference-aware reranking, the framework provides a scalable foundation for enterprises to enhance decision-making, compliance and knowledge management across diverse domains.

title: Synthesize, Adapt, Steal: A Few-Shot Domain Adaptive Model Stealing Attack for Tabular Data
authors: Maxime Schwarzer, Gustavo Sánchez, Thies Möhlenhof, Laurin Holz, Johannes F. Loevenich, Roberto Rigolin F. Lopes, Veit Hagenmeyer
abstract: The growing popularity of Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) is creating new attack vectors. Model theft poses a particular threat because it enables Intellectual Property (IP) to be stolen and can serve as a starting point for further attacks. While attacks on computer vision (CV) are well-studied, tabular data crucial for industrial applications remains less explored. We present a few-shot, domain-adaptive, model-stealing attack optimized specifically for tabular data. Our approach combines three components: (1) generating a synthetic yet plausible data pool using a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CTGAN); (2) a hybrid query strategy combining active learning with adversarial augmentation to efficiently exploit the query budget; and (3) a novel Relational Attention Corrector (RAC) that implicitly learns and enforces semantic validity. Experiments on an energy critical infrastructure intrusion detection dataset (specifically, the ERENO IEC 61850 dataset) and standard benchmarks (Adult Income and Diabetes) demonstrate that our attack significantly outperforms previous methods. It improves the macro F1-score by over 24 percentage points with only 7,000 queries, closely approaching the performance of an attacker with full data knowledge.

title: Efficient Disruption of Criminal Networks through Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithms
authors: Yehezkiel Darmadi, Thanh Thi Nguyen, Campbell Wilson
abstract: Criminal networks, such as the Sicilian Mafia, pose substantial threats to public safety, national security, and economic stability. Outdated disruption methods with a focus on removing influential individuals or key players have proven ineffective due to the covertness of the network. Thus, researchers have been trying to apply Social Network Analysis (SNA) techniques, such as centrality-based measures, to identify key players. However, removing individuals with high centrality often proves to be inefficient, as it does not mimic the real-world scenarios that Law Enforcement Agencies (LEAs) face. For instance, the operational costs limit the LEAs from exploiting the results of the centrality-based methods. This study proposes a multi-objective optimisation framework like the Weighted Sum Genetic Algorithm (WS-GA) and the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to identify disruption strategies that balance two conflicting goals, maximising fragmentation and minimising operational cost which is captured by the spatial distance between nodes and the nearest LEA headquarters. The study utilises the "Montagna Operation" dataset for the experiments. The results demonstrate that although centrality-based approaches can fragment network effectively, they tend to incur higher operational costs. In contrast, the proposed algorithms achieve comparable disruption outcomes with significantly lower operational costs. The contribution of this work lies in incorporating operational costs in a form of spatial distance constraints into disruption strategy, which has been largely overlooked in prior studies. This research offers a scalable multi-objective capability that improves practical application of SNA in guiding LEAs in disrupting criminal networks more efficiently and strategically.

title: Design and Simulation of a Battery Electric Vehicle Model for Underground Mining Applications with Synthetic Data Generation for Machine Learning Model
authors: Tamsa, Arpit Garg, Wen L. Soong, S. Ali Pourmousavi
abstract: The shift to electrifying underground mining operations poses considerable challenges in accurately predicting energy consumption due to the intricate nature of operations and the scarcity of real-world performance data for mining electric trucks. This paper introduces a comprehensive dataset synthesis framework as well as the application of four machine learning techniques aimed at forecasting energy usage in electric mining trucks used underground. This addresses the essential need for data-driven optimisation tools in sustainable mining practices. Four machine learning models, including decision tree, random forest, extreme gradient boosting, and deep neural network, were thoroughly assessed using the synthetic dataset comprising 50,000 operational scenarios created through physics-based modelling involving 11 operational parameters. Extensive hyperparameter tuning was conducted using 5-fold cross-validation with a focus on minimising mean absolute error. The Deep Neural Network exhibited outstanding predictive performance (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9998, RMSE = 1.02 kWh, MAE = 0.60 kWh), showcasing strong generalisation across various operational scenarios. Model validation against physics-based calculations for a typical haul profile confirmed prediction accuracy. This framework provides mining operators with reliable tools to manage energy-efficient fleets, optimise routes, and plan operations.

title: LLM-Augmented Feature Engineering for Machine Learning Pipelines
authors: A. Rancea, I. Anghel, T. Cioara
abstract: Feature engineering represents a fundamental challenge in machine learning (ML) pipelines for extracting meaningful representations that capture underlying patterns, starting from raw data. Usually the process requires manual, time consuming, deep domain expertise, and is difficult to be scaled across multiple domains. Automatic methods attempt to address these challenges by generating predefined statistical features or selecting optimal subsets from a feature set, rather than creating new ones. In this paper, we propose a Large Language Model (LLM)-based automated feature engineering pipeline using Mistral-7B-Instruct with Retrieval-Augmented Generation and a multi-criteria validation process. It starts with prompt text and additionally integrates domain and data knowledge, to create and validate new features. We evaluate the solution in the context of an ML-based heart rate prediction process and show that the generated features achieve 9-48% MSE improvements across three different time series architectures (LSTM, Transformer, and Informer). The results demonstrate that LLM-based feature generation can significantly optimize and improve ML prediction tasks.

title: Convivial Robotics and Silicopathy: Toward a Cross-Cultural Ethics of Human-Robot Coexistence
authors: Minoru Asada, Michio Okada
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Recent discussions on the Ethical, Legal, and Social Issues (ELSI) of social robots highlight the importance of understanding the ethical nature of human-robot relationships beyond issues of safety and regulation. Drawing on the philosophy of technology-from Ivan Illich's concept of convivial tools to contemporary relational approaches-this paper proposes a conceptual framework for human-robot coexistence based on two complementary paradigms: convivial robotics and silicopathy. Convivial robotics, inspired by Illich's notion of conviviality and exemplified by the “weak robot” approach, emphasizes robots that enhance human capabilities through imperfect yet mutually supportive interaction. Silicopathy, by contrast, refers to the emergence of artificial empathy grounded in representations of pain and affective states, through which ethical behavior is generated from internally constructed value dynamics, enabling morally meaningful relationships between humans and robots. Together these paradigms form a dual ethical framework that integrates human capability enhancement with empathic moral relations. The framework is further discussed from a cross-cultural perspective, contrasting Western autonomy-centered ethics with Eastern relational philosophies emphasizing harmony and compassion, thereby offering a conceptual basis for future human-robot coexistence, including its governance across diverse cultural and societal contexts.

title: From Co-Pilots to Co-Workers: A Formal Typology of Human–Agent Collaboration in Organizations
authors: Francisco-Javier Rodrigo-Ginés, Jorge Chamorro-Padial, Jorge Pretel-Villanueva, Javier Aguilera-Aguilera, Valentino Lugli
abstract: Organizations are increasingly deploying agentic AI to function as semi-autonomous collaborators within end-to-end business processes. Yet, existing human–autonomy teaming research seldom addresses organizational roles, handoffs, and accountability in enterprise contexts. This paper introduces a design-oriented framework for human–agent collaboration that defines a typology of roles characterized by four measurable dimensions (autonomy, reversibility, criticality, and accountability) and a governance-oriented taxonomy of human control levels. The framework includes an indicative mapping to major AI governance standards (IEEE 7001 transparency levels, NIST AI RMF, and ISO/IEC 23894) and proposes process-level metrics such as task success, end-to-end latency, override rate, incident or near-miss frequency, and explainability coverage. Three enterprise scenarios (IT Operations, Customer Service, and Legal Operations) illustrate how to calibrate autonomy and oversight without eroding accountability, offering a practical guide for designing trustworthy hybrid organizations.

title: Interpretable Topic Modeling with Online Dirichlet Compound Negative Multinomial Mixture Model and Feature Saliency
authors: Ornela Bregu, Nizar Bouguila
abstract: The Dirichlet Compound Negative Multinomial (DCNM) distribution is a flexible probabilistic model used to model overdispersion and burstiness in multivariate count data. In this paper, we adopt a rising-factorial parametrization of the DCNM and develop a Minorization–Maximization (MM) algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation in a DCNM mixture model with feature saliency (DCNM-FS). The novel MM scheme yields closed-form updates for both mixture parameters and feature-saliency weights, improves numerical stability, reduces dependence on initialization, and avoids repeated evaluation of expensive derivatives and Hessians. To simultaneously select the number of components and the salient features, we use a convex combination of the Minimum Message Length (MML) and the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). The weighted criterion yields a more reliable model-selection signal for DCNM-FS mixtures by exploiting MML’s information-theoretic foundation while grounding it with BIC’s stronger sparsity-inducing tendency, resulting in more stable estimates of both the number of components and the salient feature set. Moreover, we introduce an online extension of DCNM-FS in which parameters and feature-saliency weights are updated incrementally from mini-batches, enabling streaming topic discovery without retraining from scratch. We embed the offline and online DCNM-FS mixtures in a modular topic-modeling pipeline, where the learned component parameters, in particular the feature saliencies, directly define interpretable topic keywords, reducing reliance on large language models for document representation. The proposed approach is validated on standard topic-detection benchmarks, where it demonstrates improved robustness, interpretability, and efficiency compared to existing clustering and topic-modeling baselines.

title: Fuzzy Logic Optimization of Electrode Geometry in a Microfluidic Device for Malaria Cell Separation
authors: Jenil Patel, Rushvi Patel, Mahesh Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Current work demonstrates numerical investigation of a microfluidic cell sorting device for separation of malaria infected cells from healthy red blood cells using dielectrophoresis (DEP) phenomenon. In present study, a microfluidic device microchannel was designed using finite element method-based simulations to examine the impact of electrode geometry concerning its height and width on cell separation trajectories. The cell separation performance was evaluated based on four performance parameters which include red blood cell purity, malaria cell purity, red blood cell yield, and malaria cell yield and used to model a fuzzy inference system model implemented in MATLAB to determine suitable geometrical dimension of electrode geometry for desired performance parameters. The results have shown that there is a direct impact of electrode dimension variation on electric field distribution and further on dielectrophoretic force acting on the cells. The optimized geometry can provide high purity and yield of malaria –infected cells. Results have shown that ~100% blood cell purity and ~100% malaria cell yield has been achieved at 3V and 100 kHz with effectively focused cells and aligned cell trajectories before entering in DEP cell separation effects.

title: HeuRiskUNet: UAV Navigation in Risk Maps through Improved Path Planning Heuristics
authors: E. Aldao, F. Veiga-López, C. P. C. Chanel, Y. Watanabe, H. González-Jorge
abstract: In recent years, UAVs have been increasingly deployed across various applications, including environmental monitoring, infrastructure inspection, and logistics. While many of these operations are still conducted by human remote pilots, the current trend is towards the development of fully autonomous solutions, which require efficient and robust control and path-planning systems. Traditionally, the most widely used path-planning algorithm is A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> due to its deterministic behaviour and computational efficiency. This approach discretises the navigation environment into a grid with adjacent-node connectivity, thus constructing a graph, and minimises the path length through a graph search guided by heuristic functions. Nevertheless, the applicability of this approach is often limited to shortest-path problems where admissible heuristic functions are available. In other optimisation scenarios, such as when accounting for factors like operational risk, identifying heuristics that are both admissible and capable of efficiently guiding the search can be highly challenging. Another drawback of A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> approaches lies in their discretisation, which restricts UAV movement to adjacent nodes. This limitation reduces vehicle manoeuvrability and compromises both path smoothness and optimality. To address these challenges, this paper presents a system based on machine-learning based heuristics for navigation within complex risk maps. The system is integrated into a customised A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> framework that employs a k-Non-Aligned Nearest Neighbours (k-NANN) approach to compute smooth and optimal paths while maintaining low computational cost, deterministic behaviour, and interpretability.

title: SKYWALKER: Dual-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Crawling in a Planar Microgravity Analog : Sim-to-Real Evaluation at ESA’s Orbital Robotics Lab
authors: Dadi H. Davidsson, Bruno M. De Salas, Glenn G. Svendsen, Rasmus D. Kristiansen, Gustav Hansen, Artem Melnyk, Alaa E. Kawara, Thor Nielsen, Jon Argjaboda, Simon Bøgh, Anton B. Mortensen
abstract: SKYWALKER is a dual-agent reinforcement learning framework that enables a free-floating robotic platform to "crawl" hand-over-hand between fixed anchor points in a planar microgravity analog. The task is divided between two cooperating RL agents, one for arm-based anchoring and one for base repositioning. Both agents are trained in a frictionless simulation environment.In hardware trials conducted at the European Space Agency’s Orbital Robotics Lab (ORL) as part of the ESA Academy Experiments Programme, the learned arm policy (Agent A) successfully performed autonomous docking, while the base-motion policy (Agent B) encountered sim-to-real transfer issues and was temporarily replaced with a scripted fallback. Subsequently, Agent B was refined and validated on a physical ball-bearing testbed at Aalborg University, enabling fully learned anchor-to-anchor crawling.These results demonstrate the feasibility of learning-based control for on-orbit assembly tasks and highlight the critical role of high-fidelity simulation and robust safety measures in deploying reinforcement learning for space robotics.

title: Intelligent Spectrum Sensing and Access Using Deep Learning for Cognitive Communication Systems
authors: Mridul Gupta, Nookala Venu, Karthick Cherladine, Tailor Nikunjkumar Champakbhai, Deepak Upadhyay, Aakash Singh Ranswal
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Wireless communications are expanding rapidly; as a result, at times, the radio frequency spectrum will be overcrowded (some bands used heavily) while other bands remain underutilized. An investigation into an intelligent spectrum sensing and access framework utilizing deep learning for cognitive communication systems has been conducted. The proposed system utilizes a convolutional neural network to identify whether a given spectrum band is occupied or vacant under various levels of interference. The CNN was trained and tested on synthetic wireless signal data generated within a simulated environment. Results from this research demonstrated the use of the proposed CNN resulted in higher detection probabilities and lower false alarm rates than those produced by energy detection methods. When utilized in conjunction with intelligent decision making processes related to access, the CNN also improved spectrum usage efficiency. Although the signals used to generate the training data were simulated, results show that deep learning can enhance cognitive communication systems efficiency.

title: Energy Efficient Control of Electric Heaters in Biogas Reactors
authors: Mykhailo Spodoba, Oleksandr Spodoba
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article examines the influence of the positional and linear laws of change of the power applied at the beginning of operation to the electric heating device on the consumption of electrical energy for heating the substrate in a biogas reactor. The paper presents the results of numerical studies using differential equations, which were carried out in the Wolfram Mathematica software package. The paper shows graphical dependences of changes in the temperature of the substrate in the biogas reactor using two laws of control of the heating device placed directly in the volume of the substrate. The change in temperature of the substrate in the first 100 seconds of operation of the electric heating device is shown graphically. The result of the numerical study is the determination of the influence of the control laws on the consumption of energy for heating and the establishment that, using the positional and linear laws of power change, the time of heating the substrate in a metal biogas reactor with a volume of 40 liters is the same, while the amount of energy spent on heating is different. Using the linear law of power change, the energy consumption for one substrate heating cycle in the biogas reactor is 4.3 kJ less. The significance of the conducted research is that the use of the linear law of change of the applied power at the beginning of work to the heating device (cable) leads to a positive effect, which consists in reducing the energy costs for one cycle of heating the substance in the biogas reactor, while increasing its profitability.

title: Resilient Distributed Optimal Power Flow Algorithm Against False Data Injection Attacks
authors: Mohannad Alkhraijah
abstract: Distributed algorithms allow multiple agents to collaboratively solve large-scale optimization problems by solving a sequence of smaller subproblems. Solving optimization problems using distributed algorithms requires repeated data communication. The communication dependency increases the vulnerability of distributed algorithms to communication noise and cyberattacks. This paper presents a machine learning-based method to enhance the resiliency of distributed optimization algorithms solving optimal power flow (OPF) problems to false data injection attacks. We propose a variation to the Auxiliary Problem Principle (APP) algorithm that is secure against false data injection attacks. The proposed variation uses a classification algorithm to validate the received data from the neighboring agents. We train the model on data generated from running multiple simulations of the APP algorithm. We benchmark the results with a naive model that eliminates large deviations from previous iterations. The results show that the proposed algorithm in this paper enhances the resiliency of the APP algorithm to error injection cyberattacks and outperforms the naive model in terms of convergence speed and accuracy.

title: Towards Quantum Software for Quantum Simulation
authors: Maja Franz, Lukas Schmidbauer, Joshua Ammermann, Ina Schaefer, Wolfgang Mauerer
publish: Proceedings of the 7th IEEE/ACM International Workshop on Quantum Software Engineering
abstract: None

title:
Dynamic Event-Triggered MPC for Nonlinear Systems: Application to Vehicle Active Suspensions
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Wenting He, Daiwen Yao, Michael Shi, Xin Yu, Ning Zhao, Huiyan Zhang, Levente Kovács
abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) faces the challenge of heavy online computational burden in vehicle active suspension systems. To alleviate this issue, this paper proposes a Dynamic Event-Triggered MPC (DET-MPC) method considering nonlinear actuator constraints. By introducing an internal dynamic variable to adaptively adjust the triggering threshold, this method updates the control law only when significant changes occur in the system states, thereby substantially reducing the number of optimization computations. Theoretical analysis demonstrates the uniform asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system and excludes Zeno behavior. Simulation results show that, compared with time-triggered and static event-triggered strategies, the proposed method reduces the controller update frequency by up to 82.9% and significantly lowers the CPU execution time, while maintaining comparable performance in body acceleration and suspension travel control, significantly alleviating the computational and communication burden.

title: The Impact of Quality Control, Curated Gene Sets, and Statistical Frameworks on Alzheimer’s Disease Gene Expression Analysis in Single-Cell Data
authors: Natasha Petrovska, Maria Larrondo-Petrie, Mirjana Pavlovic
abstract: Single-cell transcriptomics enables detailed investigation of Alzheimer’s disease (AD)–associated gene expression, yet findings are highly sensitive to preprocessing, gene selection, and statistical methods. Motivated by the need for reproducible detection of sex- and age-specific transcriptional patterns, we systematically evaluated the impact of quality control (QC), curated gene sets, and non-parametric analyses on human and mouse datasets. Our objective was to determine how these methodological choices influence gene-level variance, differential expression, and cross-species reproducibility. QC improved data integrity by removing low-quality cells and outliers, enhancing variance and interpretability. Comparison of three curated gene sets—GeneCards, Seligman, and Driessens—revealed that the GeneCards subset captured a balanced spectrum of AD-related variability. Sex-stratified Wilcoxon analyses identified female-biased upregulation of UCHL1, while LITAF and OPCML displayed opposite sex-biased modulation in mice versus humans. Age-dependent Kruskal–Wallis tests revealed multiple conserved genes (SQSTM1, APOE, ITM2B, MAPT, UCHL1, MT-ND1, PRNP, FUS, VCP, and BLMH) with significant changes across age groups. This study demonstrates robust sex-and age-specific signatures and highlights how QC, gene-set selection, and statistical frameworks jointly influence reproducible single-cell AD analyses.

title: A Containerized Multimodal Architecture for On-Device Driver-State Feedback with ROS 2 and Edge AI Services
authors: Raul Fernandez-Matellan, David Martin Gomez, Arturo de la Escalera Hueso
abstract: Modern automated-driving platforms increasingly rely on multimodal driver monitoring to support supervision and adaptive interaction. However, real-vehicle deployment is often hindered by dependency coupling across robotics middle-ware, sensor drivers, and heterogeneous artificial-intelligence runtimes. We validated an on-device, containerized driver-state feedback architecture that combines Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) acquisition with ROS-free inference containers via a shared-memory (SHM) Bridge, distilling multi-camera video and wearable physiology into compact semantic messages. Vision pipelines were evaluated across heterogeneous runtimes (OpenVINO for head pose, YOLOv8 via ONNX Runtime for person/proximity, and Ultralytics YOLOv8 for driver–object interaction), together with a physiological awareness module. The loop was closed with a local large language model (LLM), served via Ollama (Llama 3.2), to generate structured on-screen and audio feedback. In a one-hour sustained benchmark, the SHM-Bridge overhead averaged 2.45 ms (median 0.21 ms), remaining small relative to inference latency (5.23 ms physiology, 39.26 ms OpenVINO, 189.40 ms ONNX, 313.74 ms Ultralytics, and 2093.05 ms LLM). These results support SHM-Bridge decoupling as a practical strategy for modular multimodal driver monitoring in real vehicles by isolating software dependencies and mitigating runtime conflicts.

title: Perception and Acceptance of Quadruped Robots Based on Indirect Exposure
authors: Veronika Egger, Layali Mashkaf, Günter Josef Hofer, Dea Hasanaj, Mathias Brandstötter
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Creating social robots is a multidisciplinary challenge that requires understanding human perception, behavior interpretation, and social–ethical expectations in human–robot interaction. Animal-like robots are a key category in this domain, yet quantitative evidence on user acceptance and perceived safety, especially for quadruped robots, remains limited. We therefore conducted a multilingual survey on the perception and acceptance of quadruped robots with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{4 5 4}$</tex> participants. Importantly, participants formed their judgments from indirect exposure to standardized visual stimuli (images and short videos) rather than from direct physical interaction. The results suggest that reported comfort under indirect exposure is associated with robot appearance and the coherence of design cues across animal-like and machine-like constructions, with mismatched zoomorphic cues tending to be viewed less favorably. Concerns about job loss and accidents are negatively associated with perceived safety, while concerns about unfair decisions and privacy show no significant influence. Prior experience with robotics is associated with higher acceptance and reduced anxiety. Appearance shows up to support initial sense-making, but did not correspond to uniformly high reported trust; responsibility attribution in the event of malfunction also remained contested, with responses clustering into technology-centered, human-centered, and shared-responsibility positions. Overall, the findings provide exploratory quantitative evidence on mediated first impressions of quadruped robots and motivate follow-up studies using direct human–robot interaction to test, validate, and extend these observations.

title: Modern Trends in the Use of Hydrogen as a Motor Fuel: Analysis, Recent Advancementsand Prospects
authors: Sergii Boichenko, Iryna Shkilniuk
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The global shift towards sustainable and environmentally friendly energy sources has necessitated the exploration of alternative fuels and energy carriers. Hydrogen, with its inexhaustible reserves and minimal environmental impact, stands out as a promising contender in the transportation sector. This article provides an informational and analytical review of the current state of hydrogen technologies use as motor fuel.

title: Disaster Response Gross Motor, Fine Motor and Tactile Workload Prediction Using Multi-layer Perceptrons
authors: Mark-Robin Giolando, Julie A. Adams
abstract: Human-robot teams executing disaster response missions require human workload predictions to enable future robots to preemptively adapt to adverse workload conditions. Workload estimation algorithms have demonstrated the ability to estimate the current human workload based on physiological response metrics; however, existing prediction methods for future workload values have only made mid-mission workload predictions up to 30 seconds, and only for cognitive workload. Three physical workload component (i.e., gross motor, fine motor and tactile) prediction models are presented in this work, making predictions up to 420 seconds. Pretrained workload estimation models converted physiological response signals into historical and current workload inputs to act as inputs for the forecasting models. Multi-layer perceptron models were trained to forecast gross motor, fine motor and tactile workload, with accurate predictions made for three minutes for the gross motor, and seven minutes for the fine motor and tactile. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-minute workload predictions for human-robot teams executing physical tasks.

title: Impact of Renewable Integration on Day-Ahead and Real-Time Market Prices under One- and Two-Price Imbalance Settlements
authors: Gergo Varhegyi, Mutasim Nour
abstract: This paper investigates the influence of renewable energy integration on the pricing dynamics of day-ahead and real-time markets under one- and two-price imbalance settlement methods. As the penetration of renewable energy sources increases, the accurate allocation of revenue and payments becomes critical to ensure market efficiency and participant equity. One-price settlements ensure that all imbalances are settled at a single price, typically the system marginal price, leading to revenue and payment neutrality. In contrast, two-price settlements differentiate prices based on whether the imbalance decreases or increases the overall system balance, often resulting in different financial outcomes for participants, as well as profit for the market operator. This study, powered by PLEXOS, provides a comprehensive methodology to accurately model one- and two-price imbalance settlements. The methodology combines in-software adjustments with Python-based automation to enhance data handling and reporting accuracy, enabling precise reflection of the financial impacts of renewable energy on market prices and settlements. Through a detailed test network model, the study analyses the discrepancies in market prices and allocations caused by the variability of renewable energy. The findings highlight the significant impact of renewable energy on both the day-ahead and real-time market prices, providing actionable insights for market operators and participants to optimise financial outcomes and maintain market stability. This work contributes to the broader goal of integrating renewable energy sources into electricity markets, offering practical solutions to manage price volatility and ensuring transparent and equitable market operations.

title: Mathematical Modeling of Negative Environmental Consequences Due to Thermal Radiation
authors: Yurii Skob, Oksana Pichugina, Mykola Kalinichenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The relevance of this work stems from the imperative to address the contemporary scientific a nd practical challenge of minimizing the risks associated with potential disruptions to the safe operation of man-made objects. This entails a comprehensive analysis of gas mixture movement in the emission zone of gas impurities, predicting air conditions in man-made environments under the influence of temperature disturbances, and the exploration of effective designs for protective equipment to mitigate or eliminate the negative consequences of such impacts. This research aims to systematically enhance and improve mathematical models, methodologies, and software for the computer implementation of a decision support system model. The model is intended to analyze the movement of a multicomponent gas mixture within the vicinity of manmade objects under the influence of t hermal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r$</tex> adiation. The theoretical foundation of the work is rooted in mathematical modeling methods, computational techniques in mathematical physics, probit analysis, risk assessment theories, and decisionmaking principles. The software tool developed for the computer implementation of the system model adheres to the principles of the theory of computer modeling systems. Based on the modeling results, distributions of the conditional probability of a fatal injury to a person due to exposure to thermal radiation from a high-temperature gas jet at specific control points a re derived for two design scenarios: one without protection for the personnel workplace and another with a continuous protective barrier impervious to radiation. The observed distribution patterns emphasize the crucial consideration of barriers impervious to heat flows when assessing safety at man-made facilities releasing high-temperature gases into the atmosphere.

title: SAFE-GUARD: Audio Emotion Recognition System for Real-Time Public Place Abuse Detection
authors: Kanan, Rashmi Chawla, Giancarlo Fortino
abstract: Ensuring safety in privacy-sensitive environments requires unobtrusive monitoring methods that respect user dignity while remaining effective. Audio-based approaches provide a promising alternative to video surveillance but face challenges of robustness, generalization, and interpretability. To address these, we propose a multimodal Voice Distress Detection System for real-time safety monitoring. The system combines acoustic descriptors extracted with TorchAudio, speech transcripts generated by Whisper, and schema-constrained inference through a fine-tuned DialoGPT-medium model. Outputs are structured to include distress level, confidence, reasoning, and recommended action, ensuring interpretability in safety-critical contexts. Evaluation on four benchmark datasets (RAVDESS, CREMA-D, TESS, IEMOCAP) demonstrates strong performance, achieving 90.65% accuracy and 98.72% recall on distress cases. Compared to a logistic regression baseline trained on acoustic features alone, our multimodal fusion approach significantly improves sensitivity. These results highlight the potential of our framework as a privacy-preserving solution for real-time abuse detection in sensitive spaces.

title: Generative AI-driven Group Emotion Recognition: A Comparative Analysis of Holistic and Compositional Strategies
authors: Pasquale Molinaro, Mattia Presta, Giancarlo Fortino
abstract: Group Emotion Recognition (GER) represents a significant challenge in computer vision, as traditional Convolutional Neural Networks often fail to capture the semantic context required to interpret collective affective states. This paper investigates the potential of Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) applied to GER in a zero-shot setting, eliminating the need for task-specific training. We propose a comparative study between two distinct methodological pipelines: a Compositional approach, which aggregates individual facial expressions detected in the scene, and a Holistic approach, which leverages the VLM’s capability to interpret global scene context and interactions. Six state-of-the-art open-source VLMs are evaluated (LLaVA-1.6, GLM-4.1V, MiniCPM-V-4.5, InternVL3, Moondream2 and PaliGemma2) on a curated dataset derived from MELD. To overcome the limitations of standard binary classification, performance is assessed using a proposed semantic metric based on Russell’s Circumplex Model, measuring the coherence of predictions, assessing a more nuanced, sophisticated and precise evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate the distinct superiority of the Holistic approach (Subset Accuracy 86% vs. 62%). Moreover the study leverages a new methodology for GER in real-time, with a latency time near to the zero (Paligemma model inference time in ≈ 0.16 seconds)

title: Automated Detection of Hip Replacements and Fiducial Markers in Pelvic CT Scans: A Comparative Study of Rule-Based vs Deep Learning Approaches
authors: Sambit Bhattacharya, Aniruddh Karunaker, Brian Anderson, Daxell Wells, Yuan Gao, Lawrence Marks, Shiva Das, Arcot Rajasekar, Lukasz Mazur
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive comparative study of automated detection of hip replacements and fiducial markers in pelvic computed tomography (CT) scans, evaluating rule-based anatomically-constrained methods against modern deep learning techniques. We develop and independently evaluate two distinct approaches: (1) a novel rule-based system utilizing metallic implant detection, fiducial marker identification, and anatomical constraint analysis specifically designed for hip replacement detection, and (2) a convolutional neural network (CNN) enhanced with Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for hip prosthesis and fiducial detection. Our evaluation on a comprehensive dataset of 444 pelvic CT cases demonstrates that the rule-based approach achieves superior performance (sensitivity: 90.4%, specificity: 88.2%, accuracy: 89.0%) while providing interpretability and clinical reasoning. The CNN achieves 70.8% ± 5.4% accuracy with GradCAM visualizations enabling spatial understanding of predictions for hip prosthesis and fiducial detection. This comparative analysis reveals that specialized rule-based methods can significantly outperform deep learning approaches while offering the interpretability essential for clinical adoption in medical imaging applications focused on data curation and quality control.

title: Lightweight LLM Adaptation for Intrusion Detection via Token-Efficient Flow Representation
authors: Akram Laamari, Hajar Moudoud, Zakaria Abou El Houda
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are emerging as a promising approach for intrusion detection using structured network flow data. However, their practical deployment is constrained by context window limitations and the excessive token overhead introduced by conventional tabular serialization formats such as JSON. Verbose data representations inflate sequence lengths, often exceeding model input limits and causing feature truncation. Additionally, it remains unclear which LLM architecture is more suitable for structured intrusion detection tasks under limited training resources. To tackle this issue, we propose a novel representation-aware intrusion detection framework based on Token-Oriented Object Notation (TOON), a compact serialization format that maximizes token efficiency while preserving schema structure. Also, we integrate a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) scheme to enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Finally, we evaluate the proposed framework as an encoder–decoder model (T5-Small) with a decoder-only model (Qwen2.5) on three benchmark datasets, including NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, and CIC-IDS2018 for binary and multi-class classification scenarios. The results show that our tokenizer-aligned, representation-aware preprocessing combined with lightweight encoder–decoder adaptation provides a practical and resource-efficient foundation for LLM-based intrusion detection.

title: Explainability of Large Language Models in Healthcare Information Systems: A Review
authors: Robin Sullivan, Nelly Elsayed
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have produced unprecedented results in areas such as language understanding, machine translation, and question and answering. However, a major challenge with adopting these and other forms of deep learning models into information systems is their opaque nature. Answering the question of how the model drew its conclusions is imperative for adoption in many disciplines, particularly in healthcare and medicine, finance, and legal, where transparency, trust, and regulatory compliance are critical. This study investigates approaches being used to explain transformer-based LLMs independent of any particular model that supports different organizations in improving governance, trust, and accountability while fostering these models into decision-making systems.

title: CausalRAG-AD: Multimodal MRI Classification and Guideline-Compliant MRI Captioning for Alzheimer’s Diagnosis
authors: Ramisa Farha, Md Mahmudur Rahman, Fahmi Khalifa
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a degenerative disease, and current AI-based diagnostic procedures often lack calibrated probabilities, clear causal explanations, and comprehensive reporting. We propose CausalRAG-AD, an end-to-end framework that (i) trains calibrated transformer classifiers for AD staging from structural MRI and (ii) generates NIA-AA guideline–compliant, evidence-grounded MRI captions. The proposed architecture augments Video Vision Transformer (ViViT) with ROI-gated attention (CausalRAG-ViViT) from FreeSurfer hippocampus/ventricle/temporal z-scores and a light clinical gate; per-fold temperature scaling yields calibrated probabilities. It is evaluated against ViT+BiLSTM and ViViT-lite baselines using Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) cohort. For binary AD vs. Cognitively Normal (CN), CausalRAG-ViViT achieves the strongest calibration (ECE 0.078). In triclass CN / mild cognitive impairment (MCI) / AD, CausalRAG-ViViT reaches the best Acc (0.519) with competitive F1 (0.452). The proposed system produced 4,427 structured per-visit MRI reports. Every report contained all required sections, and most explicitly documented MRI provenance (scanner/vendor and field strength; 93.6%). All pipeline-generated numbers—MRI z-scores, MMSE, and CDR-SB—were replicated exactly from source tables (100% fidelity), and Aβ42 values matched the source (93.9%). When the necessary biomarkers were available, the AT(N) labels in the report consistently matched with our rule-based determination (100%).

title: Feature Extraction Methods in Semiconductor Manufacturing Systems
authors: Zhihui Ji, Huijie Mao, Ximeng Wang, Kai Zhang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: In semiconductor manufacturing process, analyzing vast amounts of stored data is essential for process control and management. Reliable feature extraction on massive process data is crucial for equipment health monitoring and Fault Diagnosis (FD), thereby informing proper maintenance decisions. The key challenge lies in overcoming data non-stationarity and distribution drift, driven by process variations, to extract robust key features. This paper presents a categorized review centered on the application of feature extraction methods in semiconductor manufacturing systems, and provides an analysis and summary of the results.

title: HeteroSTA3D: A CPU-GPU Heterogeneous Static Timing Analysis Engine for 3D ICs
authors: Yuhao Ji, Zizheng Guo, Zuodong Zhang, Yibo Lin, Bei Yu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Three-dimensional integrated circuits (3D ICs) based on hybrid bonding have emerged as a key technology for sustaining performance scaling beyond the limits of conventional transistor shrinkage. However, the timing analysis of 3D ICs introduces challenges absent in two-dimensional (2D) designs, including combinatorial explosion in multi-corner analysis across heterogeneous technology nodes, inter-die parasitic modeling for hybrid bonding interconnects, and cross-die critical path extraction through a unified timing graph. No existing static timing analysis (STA) engine addresses these challenges natively. In this paper, we present HeteroSTA3D, the first CPU-GPU heterogeneous STA engine for 3D ICs. Targeting hybrid-bonding-based face-to-face two-die integration, HeteroSTA3D supports dual-die Liberty library management with independent process nodes, configurable bonding terminal parasitic modeling, multi-corner analysis across per-die PVT combinations, and end-to-end GPU-accelerated cross-die critical path search. HeteroSTA3D is released as an embeddable shared library with both industry-standard file formats and a zero-overhead flattened heterogeneous API for seamless integration into GPU-accelerated EDA flows. Experimental results show that GPU acceleration reduces runtime compared to CPU-only execution. A case study integrating HeteroSTA3D into a timing-driven 3D placement flow further demonstrates its effectiveness, achieving improved timing with affordable runtime overhead. HeteroSTA3D is freely available for academic use at https://heterosta3d.pkueda.org.cn/.

title: Feature-Based Machine Learning for Real-Time Detection of Malicious QR and NFC Content
authors: Eisa Mohammed Al-Mannai, Nabil Litayem, Abdelhak Belhi, Mustafa al Samara
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Quick Response (QR) codes and Near Field Communication (NFC) tags are widely used in payments, transportation, retail, authentication, and public services. However, their convenience introduces a critical security challenge: users often interact with embedded links or actions without visibility into the underlying destination or content. This paper presents a practical web-based security scanning platform that performs pre-interaction analysis of QR and NFC content and classifies associated URLs as safe, suspicious, or malicious. The system integrates a browser-based frontend, a backend analysis service, feature-based URL processing, and Machine Learning (ML) classification, while external threat-intelligence services (e.g., VirusTotal) are used as a secondary verification layer. A dataset of 20,000 URLs is transformed into structured feature vectors using a standardized tri-value scoring scheme and used to evaluate nine ML models under a stratified 80/20 train-test split. Results show that XGBoost achieves the best performance with 90.15% accuracy, outperforming Random Forest (89.98%) and Multi-Layer Perceptron (89.53%), while also achieving the highest precision, recall, and F1-score values. The prototype is further validated in realistic QR/NFC usage scenarios using Android-based browser testing, with iOS/device-policy and hardware compatibility limitations documented. The results demonstrate that proactive, browser-accessible screening can effectively enhance security in everyday QR/NFC interactions.

title: Evolutionary Osmotic Migration for Real-Time Urban Traffic Control: Balancing Delays and Power in Multi-Tier Networks
authors: Rishit Kamboj, Ayush Goyal, Ritesh Ranjan, Benazir Neha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Many smart city systems now depend on a lot of resource-limited devices to monitor, evaluate, and improve services of a city, like traffic control, etc. These devices require real-time data acquisition and control, but due to high latency, too many computations and network congestions, the devices run into problems. This study proposes a solution which uses an osmotic offloading method to reduce these problems by dynamically controlling the movement of microservices and their operations across different edge-fog-cloud layers. This study uses genetic algorithm which optimizes this distribution of tasks at cloud-fog-edge. It ensures that the devices which need computing power and require low latency are handled at the best layer accordingly. The solution reduces latency, balances the energy usage and eases network congestion by breaking large applications into smaller parts. It then distributes these smaller parts in an apt way across edge-fog-cloud layers. The simulations of the algorithm shows that the osmotic offloading based on genetic algorithm greatly improves average latency and energy efficiency.

title: Gait Analysis for Disease Detection using CNN and LSTM
authors: Anil Kumar Meher, Rajesh Kumar Ojha, Gopi Krishna Panda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Gait analysis focuses on in which way people are walking by analyzing different factors such that distance of each step, average distance between each step, the speed or velocity of walking style, the position or angle of everyone’s feet as well as hips, the movement of pelvis and the bend structure of knee position at the time of walking. It is also focusing on the force generated by the feet while walking with different styles. From these factors we can find out the health issues with muscles, nervous systems or bones and use to diagnose them and also keeping records of all the factors affected by walking. Gait analysis is here mostly dealing with the nervous system or the musculoskeletal system for identifying the major or minor health issues. Generally traditional gait analysis is more costly and requires special kind of sensors which are very difficult for the persons to use. In this paper, we introduce an AI-based method that uses deep learning as well as computer vision and to learn walking styles and predict possible health issues. Our approach uses OpenPose to detect key body points from walking videos and combines a hybrid CNN-LSTM model to understand both the spatial as well as time-based gait features. The method is tested by us using the GAVD dataset, which we made more realistic by adding simulated abnormal walking patterns. Our system was able to correctly classify normal, limping, and shuffling walks with 92% accuracy. We also developed a real-time system using a Flask API and a React.js interface for showing results. For both clinics and remote healthcare environments these are used as a low-cost, non-invasive gait analysis tools.

title: Results of Incorporating the World Skills Professional Standard and Assessment Standard into the Curriculum
authors: Munkhjargal Nergui, Ganbat Danaa
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This study explores the integration of the WorldSkills Mechanical Engineering-CAD professional standard into the CAD-II course curriculum within the Technical Vocational Education Program for Industrial Equipment Maintenance and Operation. The training process was redesigned to align with this standard, and its effectiveness was evaluated through the results of the Mongol Skills-2025 school-level competition and the CAD-II course assessments. Findings from these evaluations were used to inform and plan future research initiatives aimed at further enhancing the curriculum and student outcomes.

title: ARIA: An Agentic Release Intelligence Architecture for Autonomous End-to-End Mobile DevOps - From Build Prediction to Incident Resolution
authors: Satyanarayana Gudimetla, Suresh Gangula, R Adinarayana, Chandrakanth Challa, Sudhakar Vunnam, Sudhamadhuri Buhyavarapu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Enterprise mobile delivery pipelines remain fragmented: build, CI, release, deployment, and incident response operate as disconnected stages, with post-deployment learning rarely propagating upstream. We present ARIA (Agentic Release Intelligence Architecture), a multi-agent architecture whose central contribution is the ARIA Feedback Protocol (AFP)—a confidence-gated, time-decayed mechanism providing two complementary benefit pathways: (i) per-incident observability acceleration that enriches diagnosis context for every incident, and (ii) multi-incident feedback accumulation that improves upstream test selection over time. ARIA coordinates five specialized agents through a shared provenance substrate and a graduated autonomy model whose governance components are mapped to NIST AI Risk Management Framework practices. A controlled simulation study parameterized from the public CI/CD histories of Signal Android, Firefox for Android (Fenix), and Wikipedia Android reports baseline, ablation, and sensitivity analyses over 30 stochastic replications. The per-incident mechanism yields a statistically significant MTTR reduction from 51.0 to 44.6 minutes (−12.6%, p<0.001, d=0.86) and governance improvement from 8.3 to 9.1 (+10.1%, p<0.001, d=1.30). The multi-incident mechanism (DDR +1.0%, RIR −5.8%) shows favorable trends; a post-hoc power analysis determines that ≈670 observations (≈22 applications) are needed for 80% statistical power, providing a concrete sample-size target for future production studies. An offline replay against 30 held-out CI failure events yields 83.3% prediction accuracy (vs. 60.0% baseline), supporting simulation fidelity.

title: Trimodal Disentangled Representation Learning for Motion Understanding
authors: Myungin Kim, Ri Yu
abstract: Trimodal motion understanding systems that integrate motion, video, and text face a fundamental challenge: existing approaches typically excel at either generation or retrieval, but rarely both. This trade-off arises from conflicting optimization objectives—generation requires modality-independent representations to preserve diversity, whereas retrieval benefits from tightly aligned cross-modal embeddings for accurate matching. To address this challenge, we introduce a TRImodAl representation Disentanglement framework (TRIAD) that decomposes trimodal embeddings into task-specific components: a generative subspace for generation and a discriminative subspace for retrieval. A shallow dual-router Mixture-of-Experts architecture enables independent optimization pathways for stable task-specific disentanglement. Our results demonstrate that explicit task-specific decomposition allows a single unified model to perform both generation and retrieval. TRIAD provides empirical analysis on jointly supporting generation and retrieval within a single trimodal framework.

title: The Hidden Complexity of Mental Health: Multi-Entropy Analysis of Response Patterns in Depression Severity Assessment
authors: Ayesha Siddika, Ahnaf Atif Rafi, Maria Jahan Noon, Md Junayed Hossain, Ashraful Islam, Sanzar Adnan Alam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) is generally used to determine the level of depression, and clinical decisions are usually made based on the overall score. Nevertheless, there can be significant variation in the response pattern of individuals who have the same score, indicating that there are variations in the organization of symptoms. To overcome this drawback, this paper presents an entropy-based computation system that describes PHQ-9 responses in a way that is not limited by a score measure. Responses to individual items are fused with information-theoretic quantities, such as Shannon entropy, Sample entropy, Permutation entropy, and Multiscale entropy, to form a more detailed feature space to classify depression severity. The proposed framework significantly increased the accuracy of the sum-score baseline in fivefold stratified cross-validation of the existing data, with the Random Forest adding 18.0 percentage points to the accuracy of 81.2 percent, reaching 99.2 percent. Statistical analysis also shows that the relationship between response entropy and the severity of depression is not linear, where entropy is at its highest when depression is moderate and reduced with severe depression. A potentially useful screening signal in this data set was observed to be an entropy threshold (0.439). These results suggest the complexity of the response patterns as a concomitant measure of digital depression.

title: Generalized Bayesian Linearized AVO Inversion using Box–Cox Transform
authors: Dario Grana, Leandro Passos De Figueiredo, Fernando Luis Bordignon, Rodrigo Exterkoetter, Mauro Roisenberg, Bruno Barbosa Rodrigues
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: A generalized Bayesian linearized seismic inversion framework is presented to extend the classical Bayesian linearized inversion by assuming non-Gaussian prior distributions for elastic properties through a parametric normalizing transformation applied directly to the elastic variables. The approach applies a monotonic invertible power function, namely the Box–Cox transformation, to map non-Gaussian elastic variables into a Gaussian space, where computationally efficient Bayesian linearized inversion is performed using linearized amplitude-versus-offset (AVO) models. A low-frequency background trend is incorporated to mitigate the band-limited nature of seismic data. Posterior mean and covariance in the Gaussian space are obtained analytically and subsequently mapped back to the physical domain, yielding posterior distributions that capture realistic uncertainty and prior statistical behavior. The Box–Cox transformation implicitly defines a flexible parametric family for the elastic properties, referred to as the Box–Cox normal distribution, whose shape is controlled by a single parameter λ estimated from measured data by minimizing the skewness of the transformed variable. This family encompasses the lognormal distribution and both right-and left-skewed distributions. The methodology is demonstrated on synthetic and field seismic datasets to estimate the posterior distributions of P-wave velocity, S-wave velocity, and density conditioned on the seismic observations, and to infer the most likely elastic model of the reservoir.

title: Low-complexity Grouped Sparse Superposition Coding Based on Binary Golay Sequences
authors: Linjie Yang
abstract: Sparse superposition coding (SSC) is one of the key enabling techniques for ultra-reliable and low-latency communications. To further improve the block error rate (BLER) performance of SSC, a grouped sparse superposition coding scheme is proposed in this paper. At the transmitter, the information bits are divided into two parts. The first part is conveyed through sparse vector coding, while the second part is modulated as quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) symbols. Specifically, the sparse-mapped bits are evenly partitioned into N segments, where each segment is uniquely mapped to a spreading sequence, resulting in a structured sparse vector representation. At the receiver, a modified multi-path matching pursuit (MMP) algorithm is developed to explicitly exploit the inherent group-wise sparsity structure for efficient data recovery. Moreover, binary Golay sequences are employed to construct the spreading sequences. Benefiting from their favorable algebraic properties, the proposed scheme eliminates the need for storing both the spreading sequence set and the index table required in conventional SSC, thereby significantly reducing the computational complexity and memory overhead.

title: A Phase-Expanded Propulsion Method to Suppress Attractive Force Difference in a Levitation-Propulsion-Integrated Magnetic Levitation Transporter System
authors: Akito Konishi, Shota Mitsui, Ken-ichi Kondo, Shunsuke Ohashi
abstract: The objective of this study is to develop a magnetic levitation transporter system with a simple system structure. In the proposed system, both levitation and propulsion are achieved using a unified mechanism. A linear stepper motor is employed to simultaneously generate attractive and propulsive forces. Propulsion is realized using a DC power supply, without the need for position sensors in the propulsion direction. In previous research, levitation was achieved, and the maximum allowable attractive force imbalance between the front and rear of the carrier which is necessary for stable levitation was identified. However, propulsion had not yet been realized. This paper proposes a novel excitation switching method to suppress attractive force imbalance and stabilize levitation during propulsion. In this new method, a four-phase excitation state was inserted between successive three-phase excitation states, thereby halving the propulsion step length. Experiments were conducted, and the results demonstrate that the transporter system achieves stable propulsion while maintaining levitation.

title: A Novel Transient Disturbance Waveform Compression Method in Distribution Networks Based on Dictionary Learning
authors: Diantao Sun, Hao Liu, Tianshu Bi, Kenneth Martin
abstract: The distribution network with a large-scale integration of distributed renewable energy sources exhibits more complex dynamic behavior. The widespread deployment of high-resolution waveform monitoring in distribution networks is hindered by the massive data transmission and storage requirements. To mitigate this communication bottleneck, this paper proposes a novel transient disturbance waveform compression method in distribution networks based on dictionary learning. A new framework for disturbance waveform compression on terminal side and reconstruction on master station side has been proposed to outline the offline learning and online decomposition steps. Then a determination method of the start and end times of transient disturbance waveforms based on the relative energy acceleration operator (REAO) has been proposed for disturbance waveform segmentation, which clarifies the disturbance processes that need to be monitored. To improve the decomposition and reconstruction efficiency, an overcomplete dictionary design method that considers feature redundancy within the dictionary based on clustering pruning optimized K SVD (CPK-SVD) has been proposed. The redundant atoms in the dictionary are pruned based on Gaussian mixture model (GMM) clustering method after feature extraction. Simulation and field recorded waveform data tests show that the proposed method can achieve a compression ratio of over 10 while ensuring measurement accuracy.

title: Time–Power Optimization for Ultraviolet Communication-Positioning with Energy Harvesting System in NLOS Channels
authors: Xiaohuan Zhang, Huimin Lu, Danyang Chen, Xuecheng Wei, Zihuan Liang, Junyan Zhou, Jianli Jin, Jianping Wang
abstract: In this work, an integrated ultraviolet (UV) communication– positioning with energy harvesting (UV-CPEH) system in non-line-of-sight (NLOS) channels is considered. Under constraints on positioning error, total transmit power, and harvested energy, a joint optimization model based on the time-switching and power-splitting (TS–PS) mechanism is formulated to enhance the achievable system rate. To address the inherent non-convexity, an efficient iterative algorithm leveraging successive convex approximation (SCA) is proposed to obtain a convergent solution. Numerical results show that the proposed time–power optimization strategy effectively balances the trade-off among communication, positioning, and energy harvesting.

title: LLT: Lossless Transmission using Local Recirculation for WANs
authors: Wenyi Wang, Junchang Wang, Yu Hong, Lei Han, Xin He, Weibei Fan, Zixuan Guan, Xiaolong Zheng, Fu Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: As distributed applications increasingly span geographically distributed data centers, the demand for high-performance, long-distance transmission has been continuously growing. While intra-data-center networks have employed techniques like remote direct memory access (RDMA) to meet these design goals, extending these techniques toWANs presents unique challenges. WANs notably suffer from inherent packet losses due to buffer overflows in routers and switches, leading to decreased throughput and making distributed applications barely usable. This paper proposes Lossless Transmission (LLT), a novel buffer management scheme for enabling lossless WAN transport. LLT intelligently integrates on-chip switch buffers with an off-chip caching system to absorb traffic bursts that would otherwise cause packet loss. Its data plane logic uses a multi-level threshold system to selectively offload only critical flows during congestion. A closed-loop control protocol, managed by a stateful flow table, ensures these offloaded packets are later re-injected with guaranteed lossless and in-order delivery, effectively protecting latency-sensitive applications from retransmission overhead. We evaluate LLT using both ns-3 simulations and P4-programmable devices. The experimental results show that in typical use cases (RTT > 30ms), LLT improves link bandwidth utilization by 1.9% to 29.5% and reduces the P99 percentile tail latency by 17% to 66% in WANs compared to the state-of-the-art solutions. Overall, LLT provides a scalable, efficient, and reliable framework for long-distance data transmission, addressing critical challenges in WANs. Additionally, LLT eliminates the need for expensive WAN infrastructure modifications.

title: A GAN-Based Method for Mountain Shadow Restoration in Optical Remote Sensing Image
authors: Zirui Song, Jun Lu, Yuemin Yue, Xiaowei Tong, Kelin Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Topographic shadows in high-resolution optical re mote sensing cause coupled degradation of texture, contrast, and spectral fidelity, conventional illumination normalization or histogram transfer methods offer limited effectiveness on deep shadow regions, as the missing information cannot be recovered through brightness correction alone. This study proposes a boundary-guided generative adversarial network (GAN) for restoring vegetation shadows in high-resolution multispectral imagery over karst terrain: boundary cues explicitly control shadow transitions, and a selective fusion rule preserves non shadow pixels by construction, training employs a realistic shadow synthesis pipeline that jointly simulates illumination attenuation, chromatic shift, contrast suppression, and texture degradation, combined with complementary losses enforcing texture fidelity, edge consistency, and brightness recovery. Experiments on Gaofen-2 (GF-2) multispectral data show that the proposed method achieves a peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of 35.78 dB and an structural similarity index (SSIM) of 0.96 at 200 epochs, outperforming the U-Net baseline by 13.8% and 4.3% and the ResNet baseline by 28.5% and 12.9%, respectively, notably, it reaches 20.34 dB PSNR at epoch 1 (versus 8.42 dB for U-Net and 9.81 dB for ResNet), demonstrating significantly faster convergence, while both baselines plateau or degrade after epoch 100. These results indicate that generative shadow restoration can effectively extend the usable coverage of high-resolution optical imagery in topographically complex regions.

title: A Novel Quadratic Ultra-High Step-Up DC-DC Converter Based on Coupled Inductor
authors: Saeed Azmoon, Ebrahim Babaei, Darioush Alizadeh, Mehran Sabahi
abstract: This paper proposes a novel high-step-up DC-DC converter featuring continuous input current and a quadratic voltage gain. The converter uses a three winding coupled inductor (TWCI), and a passive clamp circuit to reduce voltage stress across the power switches. Furthermore, the voltage gain is extended by integrating the TWCI's tertiary winding into the output stage alongside a voltage multiplier cell (VMC). By leveraging the leakage inductance of the TWCI, the converter operates in a quasi-resonant operation (QRO), resulting in resonant current waveforms for the switches and VMC diodes. This characteristic facilitates true and pseudo Zero Current Switching (ZCS) for these diodes, which eliminates reverse recovery issues and minimizes switching losses. Consequently, the reduced losses and low component stress lead to high overall efficiency. This paper presents the converter's steady-state analysis in both Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM), dynamic modeling, and design considerations. A comprehensive comparison with recently reported high step-up converters demonstrates that the proposed topology provides a superior trade-off among voltage gain, semiconductor voltage stress, switching device power (SDP), power density, and cost. Finally, the analysis is validated by experimental results from 400 V, 226 W prototype, achieving peak efficiency of 96.2% at 200 W.

title: DynaGNN-3D: Dynamic Graph Neural Networks with Velocity-Aware Edge Reconfiguration for Real-Time LiDAR-Based 3D Object Detection in Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Vijayakumar Venganti, Seetaram Rao Rayarao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for LiDAR-based 3D object detection have achieved strong performance on autonomous vehicle benchmarks, yet existing approaches construct static k-nearest-neighbor (k-NN) graphs that ignore object motion dynamics between frames. This static edge assignment limits both detection accuracy for fast-moving objects and inference efficiency, since background point clusters receive the same computational budget as foreground objects. We present DynaGNN-3D, a novel architecture featuring velocity-aware dynamic edge reconfiguration: graph edges are re-drawn per inference frame using per-point velocity estimates, concentrating connectivity within moving object clusters while sparsifying background regions. DynaGNN-3D incorporates a dedicated velocity prediction head whose output feeds back into edge construction, enabling temporally coherent 3D bounding box estimation. We also propose an anchor-free center-based detection head compatible with dynamic graph topology. Experiments on nuScenes and Waymo Open Dataset benchmarks demonstrate that DynaGNN-3D achieves 58.5% mAP and NDS of 63.2 on nuScenes validation, surpassing CenterPoint by +2.5 mAP and +3.6 NDS, while reducing inference latency by 26% (38 ms → 28 ms) on an NVIDIA A4000 GPU. INT8 TensorRT deployment achieves 90 FPS with only −0.8% mAP degradation, demonstrating practical edge deployability. Ablation studies confirm that dynamic edge reconfiguration and the velocity head each contribute independently to detection quality.

title: Photonics Breakthroughs 2025: Three-Dimensional Holographic Incoherent Imaging Through Scattering Media
authors: YoonSeok Baek, Hilton B. de Aguiar, Sylvain Gigan
abstract: High-resolution three-dimensional (3D) imaging through optically complex media remains a highly desirable yet persistent challenge for numerous applications. This Highlight focuses on our recent method for noninvasive 3D reconstruction from scattered incoherent light, while placing it within the broader context of recent progress in imaging through highly scattering environments, particularly for spatially incoherent modalities such as fluorescence microscopy. In contrast to earlier approaches that were largely limited to two-dimensional imaging, our framework enables volumetric reconstruction by computationally retrieving the underlying optical field together with the associated scattering information. Using emission signals alone, the method achieves diffraction-limited 3D reconstruction and provides a route toward robust imaging through complex media in a fully noninvasive manner. Finally, we outline remaining technical limitations and discuss computational strategies for improving robustness and extending this approach, as well as related methods, to demanding microscopy applications.

title: A Comparison of Deep Learning and Machine Learning Models for Toddler Autism Spectrum Disorder Screening
authors: Dipak Mishra, Rakesh Ranjan Kumar, Subhadra Mishra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder(ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that affects communication, social interaction, and behavior of a toddler[3]. For prompt intervention and better results, early detection is essential[2].
