title: Reconstruction of Graph Signals on Complex Manifolds with Kernel Methods
authors: Yu Zhang, Linyu Peng, Bing-Zhao Li
abstract: Graph signals are widely used to describe vertex attributes or features in graph-structured data, with applications spanning the internet, social media, transportation, sensor networks, and biomedicine. Graph signal processing (GSP) has emerged to facilitate the analysis, processing, and sampling of such signals. While kernel methods have been extensively studied for estimating graph signals from samples provided on a subset of vertices, their application to complex-valued graph signals remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces a novel framework for reconstructing graph signals using kernel methods on complex manifolds. By embedding graph vertices into a higher-dimensional complex ambient space that approximates a lower-dimensional manifold, the framework extends the reproducing kernel Hilbert space to complex manifolds. It leverages Hermitian metrics and geometric measures to characterize kernels and graph signals. Additionally, several metric-induced and graph fractional spectral kernels are proposed for reconstructing complex graph signals. Finally, experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of this framework in accurately reconstructing complex graph signals, outperforming conventional kernel-based approaches. This work lays a foundational basis for integrating complex geometry and kernel methods in GSP.

title: AdRo-FL: Secure and Informed Client Selection for Federated Learning under Adversarial Aggregator_supp1-3696080.pdf
authors: Walid Aljoby

title: Mechanistic Insights into Voltage Maintaining Performance of Dielectric Films: Material Dependence and Field Effects_supp1-3698069.docx
authors: Zhi-Min Dang
abstract: Metallized film capacitors in pulsed power technology are capable of releasing large amounts of energy within a short time. After charging is completed and the power supply is disconnected, the capacitor enters a break stage during which inevitable voltage decay occurs. Understanding the voltage maintaining performance (VMP) of capacitors and the dielectric films is essential for improving the efficiency of pulsed systems and enabling spatiotemporal control of stored energy. In this work, the field effects and material dependence of VMP were investigated using representative dielectrics—BOPP, PEI, and PVDF. The field-accelerated decay of BOPP and PEI follows the Poole-Frenkel-like hopping model, whereas PVDF exhibits an exponential relationship consistent with the Landau–Khalatnikov (L–K) ferroelectric dynamics. Voltage decay mainly comes from leakage and depolarization, with depolarization being the dominant factor. The electric field enhances both contributions synergistically. During depolarization, ferroelectric PVDF exhibits a higher proportion of fast polarization channels, while BOPP and PEI show a more balanced contribution of fast and slow components. This study aims to provide new insights into VMP and offers guidelines for material selection and optimization in different application scenarios.

title: Comprehensive comparison of acceleration voltage of selected dielectric liquids as a critical non-standard parameter describing dielectric properties of insulating liquids
authors: Maciej Lalik, Filip Stuchala, Pawel Rozga, Bartlomiej Pasternak
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This paper presents the results of comparative studies on acceleration voltage determination of various dielectric liquids of different origin. The results obtained for classical non-uniform field represented by point-sphere gap of 25 mm are discussed in terms of both voltage polarities of standard lightning impulse voltage. The data on experimental approach are discussed as well to show that comparison is only possible when same testing conditions are applied to each dielectric liquid considered. The results obtained show that acceleration voltage parameter brings important information on behavior of the liquid under lightning impulse stress, which is critical issue when considering the alternative liquid for transformer applications. The differences noted indicate that acceleration voltage may be a new indicator, which can be considered as standard parameter used in assessing the dielectric properties of liquids. When comes to the numbers, both in case of negative and positive lightning impulse voltage hydrocarbon based liquids (mineral oil, GTL, bio-based hydrocarbon) are characterized by much higher acceleration voltage than alternative liquids such as esters.

title: DSFF: Dynamic–Static Feature Fusion Framework for Robust Cross-Dataset Deepfake Detection
authors: Hui-Ying Huang, Ming-Yi Huang, Hao Teng, Chai-Yu Lin
abstract: Deepfake, a technology driven by generative AI, has become an urgent threat due to its capability to generate fake images and synthesize realistic videos. Ensuring the authenticity of digital content requires robust deepfake detection; however, existing detectors struggle with cross-manipulation and cross-dataset generalization as new forgery techniques emerge and dataset distributions vary. To address these challenges, we propose a Dynamic and Static Feature Fusion (DSFF) Framework. DSFF jointly leverages static appearance cues from RGB images and dynamic motion features derived from dense optical flow. It further incorporates cross-attention for modality alignment and channel attention for feature recalibration, enabling the model to capture subtle spatial–temporal inconsistencies introduced by diverse manipulation methods. Extensive experiments on FF++, SBI, and cross-quality settings demonstrate that DSFF consistently outperforms blending-based baselines. Notably, it surpasses the baseline method by approximately 3% AUROC under unseen manipulation types and cross-dataset evaluation. Moreover, on the low-resolution cross-manipulation data, DSFF improves performance by an average of 2% improvement over the baseline method, demonstrating greater stability and stronger robustness to resolution degradation across all manipulation types. Overall, the proposed DSFF framework offers a robust and generalizable approach to deepfake detection, particularly in unseen manipulation scenarios and cross-dataset conditions.

title: An Agentic Framework for Efficient and Scalable Web Content Classification
authors: Dan-Gabriel Tudor, Costin Bădică
abstract: This paper introduces an automated framework designed for classifying web page content into the IAB Content Taxonomy in a resource-efficient and cost-effective way, addressing the needs of companies and researchers in the space of Digital Advertising and Search Engine Optimization. Motivated by the high cost of Large Language Models (LLMs) for high-volume tasks, we investigate the efficacy for this task of BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), a small transformer model, and further compare it with smaller Machine Learning models. The agent's architecture encompasses a robust pipeline for web-content processing, including content extraction, multilingual translation, classification, and results logging. Due to the hierarchical nature of the taxonomy and the large number of categories (345 categories), a great amount of work was dedicated to the construction of a broad knowledge base, created by aggregating real web data alongside synthetically generated data. We present a comparative evaluation of five models—BERT, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Naïve Bayes, and Random Forest—as the agent's classification engine. Experimental results demonstrate that a fine-tuned BERT model achieves the highest accuracy (0.8923 Tier 1 Accuracy), while a Calibrated LinearSVC offers a superior balance of performance (0.731 Tier 1 Accuracy) and computational efficiency. The research is concluded by building a functional, user-ready prototype, validating the agentic framework as a useful solution to real-world SEO and Ad-tech applications.

title: Granular Convolutional Neural Networks: Interpretable Classification with Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Inas Shadoul, Majdi Mansouri, Mostefa Mesbah, Rami Al-Hmouz
abstract: This paper presents an adapted framework of Granular Convolutional Neural Networks (GCNNs) designed for classification tasks. Built upon conventional convolutional architectures, the framework introduces interval-valued (granular) convolutional connections, where each interval forms an information granule around the pixel value. This representation provides a quantifiable measure of uncertainty and interpretability in convolutional feature extraction and decision making. By optimally distributing information granularity across the input space, the proposed GCNN model seeks to maximize classification confidence while preserving specificity. To achieve this objective, a single objective particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm is employed, guided by classification coverage and specificity criteria. Using this approach, the regions that contribute the most to the classes can be identified by the formation of the intervals.

title: Perception of Gendered Voice and Expressed Apology in Warehouse Robots
authors: Ava Megyeri, Maria Kyrarini, Natasha Kholgade Banerjee, Sean Banerjee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: When industrial collaborative robotic assistants make mistakes, trust between the human and robot is likely to be enhanced if the robot issues an apology when it makes a mistake in its assigned task. To evaluate user perceptions toward an industrial robot, we design a virtual reality (VR) digital twin of a warehouse where a worker and the twin of a Universal Robots UR10 manipulator arm collaborate to lift boxes from a conveyor belt and sort them based on the adhesive tape color. Using VR enables us to collect data at scale due to the challenges of replicating the warehouse in a typical lab setting. We evaluate four versions of the robot responding to the event that the human misses a box—an idle robot that never lifts a box, a robot that attempts to lift human-missed boxes but does not apologize for a missed box, and versions of the robot embodied with female and male voices to issue apologies for a missed box. We conduct a human subject study with 32 participants, including 7 identifying as female, 1 Nonbinary, 1 Man, and 23 male to understand how perceptions change based on the gendered voice and expressed apology from the robot. We show significant differences in likeability, animacy, and perceived intelligence between idle robots and robots that issue apologies. We show that self-identified gender and prior experience in warehousing play roles in how participants perceive gendered voice and expressed apology in robots.

title: Discrete quantum-classical walks for link prediction
authors: A. Marín, M. Soto-Gomez, G. Valentini, E. Casiraghi, C. Cano, D. Manzano
abstract: Link prediction is a fundamental task in network science that involves estimating the likelihood of missing or future connections between nodes based on the structural patterns of a graph. While Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become a dominant approach, Random Walk (RW)-based methods remain highly relevant due to their lower computational demands in large-scale networks. Although recent hybrid quantum-classical walks have shown promise in exploring graph structures, existing continuous-time models are computationally expensive as they require solving high-dimensional systems of differential equations. In this paper, we propose a discrete version of a recent hybrid quantum-classical random walk algorithm that significantly improves computational efficiency by utilizing state vector representations instead of density matrices. Our method, inspired by the Quantum Jumps approach, alternates between coherent evolution and measurement-driven state collapse to generate trajectories for node embeddings. We integrate this algorithm into a link prediction pipeline and evaluate its performance across various synthetic topologies, including Grid, Power-Law, Erdös-Rényi, Barabási-Albert, and Community graphs. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed discrete hybrid walks consistently outperform classical random walk approaches in Community and Grid topologies, showing that quantum-inspired dynamics can capture complex structural information more effectively than classical dynamics for specific topologies.

title: Analysis of Routing Problems in Payment Channels and Optimization of the Payment System in Trading Platforms of Microgrid Networks
authors: Volodymyr Khilenko, Bence Ladoczki, Halyna Bielokha, Oleksii Stepanov, Serhii Denysiuk, Vasyl Khylenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article is devoted to the development of smart management systems of trading platforms for energy systems and consideration of the problems of building a system of optimal calculations in Microgrid networks. It is believed that the trading platform of the participants of the energy network makes payments using the blockchain, which makes it urgent to solve the problems of routing and dynamic selection of the amount of the commission for transactions. Optimizing the operation of the network imposes requirements on the management system regarding the selection of the shortest route between the participants of the trade operation. The paper addresses the problem of unbalanced channel states in payment channel networks (PCN) and investigates the robustness of fee adjustment strategies by conducting simulations on different PCN graphs to show that dynamic methods can lead to better performance.

title: Comparative Analysis of Autonomous Robotic and Manual Techniques for Ultrasonic Sacral Osteotomy: A Preliminary Study
authors: Daniyal Maroufi, Yash Kulkarni, Justin E. Bird, Jeffrey H. Siewerdsen, Farshid Alambeigi
abstract: In this paper, we introduce an autonomous Ultrasonic Sacral Osteotomy (USO) robotic system that integrates an ultrasonic osteotome with a seven-degree-of-freedom (DoF) robotic manipulator guided by an optical tracking system. To assess multi-directional control along both the surface trajectory and cutting depth of this system, we conducted quantitative comparisons between manual USO (MUSO) and robotic USO (RUSO) in Sawbones phantoms under identical osteotomy conditions. The RUSO system achieved sub-millimeter trajectory accuracy (0.11 mm RMSE), an order of magnitude improvement over MUSO (1.10 mm RMSE). Moreover, MUSO trials showed substantial over-penetration (16.0 mm achieved vs. 8.0 mm target), whereas the RUSO system maintained precise depth control (8.1 mm). These results demonstrate that robotic procedures can effectively overcome the critical limitations of manual osteotomy, establishing a foundation for safer and more precise sacral resections.

title: Evaluating Histogram Matching for Robust Deep Learning–Based Grapevine Disease Detection
authors: Ruben Pascual, Inés Hernández, Salvador Gutiérrez, Javier Tardaguila, Pedro Melo-Pinto, Daniel Paternain, Mikel Galar
abstract: Variability in illumination is a primary factor limiting deep learning robustness for field-based plant disease detection. This study evaluates Histogram Matching (HM), a technique that transforms the pixel intensity distribution of an image to match a reference profile, to mitigate this in grapevine classification, distinguishing among healthy leaves, downy mildew, and spider mite damage. We propose a dual-stage integration of HM: (i) as a preprocessing step for normalization, and (ii) as a data augmentation technique to introduce controlled training variability. Experiments using 1,469 RGB images (comprising homogeneous leaf-focused and heterogeneous canopy samples) to train ResNet-18 models demonstrate that this combination significantly enhances robustness on real-world canopy images. While leaf-focused samples showed marginal gains, the canopy subset improved markedly, indicating that balancing normalization with histogram-based diversification effectively bridges the domain gap caused by uncontrolled lighting.

title: Optimal Techno-Economic Energy Storage System Sizing for Islanded Microgrids
authors: Abdullah Alharbi, Abdullah Alamri, Azeddine Draou
abstract: This work employed HOMER Pro software to simulate an islanded microgrid (MG) and determine energy storage systems' (ESSs) technical and economic impact. Data related to weather, like global horizontal irradiance (GHI) and temperature, were collected. The energy-generating units used were photovoltaics (PVs) panels and diesel generators (DGs). The other MG components were the converter and ESSs. The work involved designing a base case, that has only DGs, and two case studies to analyze and determine the optimal solution. The first case study comprised DGs, PVs, and ESSs (lead-acid batteries). The second case study comprised generators, DGs, PVs, and ESSs (lithium-ion batteries). The analysis showed that the optimal solution would be a case that consists of lithium-ion batteries with PVs and DGs. Such a case would be technically and economically feasible, and its levelized energy cost would be $0.154.

title: Probabilistic Causal Modelling of Factors Contributing to Stress, Anxiety, and Depression for Nurses and Social Workers
authors: Olha Shaposhnyk, Daria Zahorska, Amy Beck, Katherine Bright, Linda Duffett-Leger, Svetlana Yanushkevich
abstract: This study aims to explore the causal relationships between psychological and demographic factors using data from a survey of nurses and social workers, applying data-driven causal modelling techniques. The primary goal was to identify potential causal relationships between the factors such as stress, anxiety, depression, age, education, and professional experience.We employed a multi-stage analysis integrating reliability and correlation analysis, synthetic data generation, and causal structure learning. Statistical techniques, such as Copulas, were used to reproduce the joint distribution of observed variables, allowing for the reliable estimation of dependencies under limited data conditions. Several causal discovery algorithms, both data-driven and score-based, were evaluated. The resulting causal graphs revealed strong associations between stress, anxiety, and depression scores, and education level and years of experience in practice. Time-related features of stress events (i.e. minimum and modal frequency) were linked to higher stress and anxiety scores, suggesting their potential as behavioural indicators of daily stress exposure. A combination of data-driven and structure-based approaches provided the most stable and interpretable network structure.

title: LLM-Seeded Genetic Improvement: Hierarchical AST Evolution with Multi-Level Donor-Code Transplants
authors: Michael Schladt, John C. Gallagher
abstract: Automated software transformation remains a key challenge in search-based software engineering, particularly when targeting high-level behavioral changes. We present a novel genetic improvement (GI) framework that evolves program source code using a novel hierarchical abstract syntax tree (AST) representation and a suite of specialized genetic operators. Our approach supports multilevel structural edits, including insertion, deletion, and replacement of AST subtrees, and enables code transplants from a pool of LLM-generated donor programs. Donor code is generated automatically by prompting a large language model (LLM) to solve the target task in diverse ways, then extracting and preserving every syntactically valid AST node from its outputs. Candidate variants are evaluated using a multi-stage fitness function that prioritizes compatibility, functional correctness, and behavioral alignment with a specified target. As a proof-of-concept, we evolve a breadth-first search implementation into a depth-first search solely through automated AST-based edits. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach efficiently discovers minimal and correct transformations, underscoring the importance of both representation and operator design in automated software evolution. Our results broaden GI’s capabilities toward domain-agnostic, behavior-changing code evolution. This work lays a foundation for more scalable automated software adaptation and repair techniques.

title: Sustainability-Aware Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for UAV Response and Monitoring with Joint Communication and Sensing
authors: Islam Guven, Mehmet Parlak
abstract: Multi-UAV networks are increasingly deployed for large-scale inspection and monitoring missions, where operational performance depends on the coordination of sensing reliability, communication quality, and energy constraints. In particular, the rapid increase in overflowing waste bins and illegal dumping sites has created a need for efficient detection of waste hotspots. In this work, we introduce JCAS-MARL, a resource-aware multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for joint communication and sensing (JCAS)-enabled UAV networks. Within this framework, multiple UAVs operate in a shared environment where each agent jointly controls its trajectory and the resource allocation of an OFDM waveform used simultaneously for sensing and communication. Battery consumption, charging behavior, and associated CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions are incorporated into the system state to model realistic operational constraints. Information sharing occurs over a dynamic communication graph determined by UAV positions and wireless channel conditions. Waste hotspot detection requires consensus among multiple UAVs to improve reliability. Using this environment, we investigate how MARL policies exploit the sensing-communication-energy trade-off in JCA-Senabled UAV networks. Simulation results demonstrate that adaptive pilot-density control learned by the agents can out-perform static configurations, particularly in scenarios where sensing accuracy and communication connectivity vary across the environment.

title: Prompt-Oriented Documentation: A Definition and Framework for Interactive User Manual and Developer Guides
authors: Daniel Ciochiu, Claudiu Trăistaru, Marius Brezovan, Mircea Bădoi, Gabriel Nedianu
abstract: This study, situated in the field of user and developer documentation, introduces two novel concepts: Prompt-Oriented Application and Prompt-Oriented Documentation. Focusing primarily on the latter, we introduce an LLM-assisted framework designed to interactively guide users through live examples that demonstrate how to use or integrate a software product. Furthermore, a series of experiments were conducted using language models ranging from 1.1B to 120B parameters, with performance evaluated according to semantic accuracy, action appropriateness, structural compliance, and hallucination rate. The evaluation revealed that models exceeding 20B parameters achieve performance levels comparable to a 120B model, while smaller models consistently underperformed, often entering infinite response loops. Code-instructed models did not significantly improve structured response quality, indicating that model size plays a more critical role.

title: PCR-GP: Prior-Coupled Residual Gaussian Processes for Few-Shot Lipid Nanoparticle Formulation
authors: Adrian Kucia, Jacob Sanderson, Wai Lok Woo, Gary Montague, Yvonne Perrie, Thomas McLean, David Vaughan Palmer, Bruce Richard Williams
abstract: We study few-shot surface transfer in formulation science: given a dense response surface at pH 4.5 over inputs (DDAB, TFR) to Z-average (DLS), infer the full pH 6 surface while measuring as few pH 6 points as possible. We propose a pH-anchored Prior–Coupled Residual Gaussian Process (PCR–GP) that treats the target surface as a global affine transform of a source GP plus a learnable discrepancy: f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf>(x) = ρg(x) + β + r(x). The source GP g is trained once on the dense pH 4.5 data and provides a strong spatial prior. The coupling (ρ, β) is estimated from the few pH 6 labels using a stability-aware freeze→ridge→clip procedure, after which a residual GP r is fit on the target labels to capture cross-pH differences. Active selection evaluates target uncertainty on a feasible candidate set and snaps the next experiment to the highest-uncertainty unused location. The framework reports fixed-scale uncertainty maps and log-scaled error curves for comparability across rounds. On real formulation data, PCR–GP reconstructs the pH 6 surface with very few additional assays while maintaining high accuracy, outperforming naive direct learning and prior-informed baselines. The approach is simple, sample-efficient, and broadly applicable whenever one condition is cheap/dense and another is expensive/sparse.

title: Federated Recommendations with Built-in Privacy and Explainability: A Conceptual Model
authors: Alexandra Vultureanu-Albişi, Costin Bădică
abstract: This paper proposes a job recommender system that unifies four key principles: federated learning, explainability, privacy protection, and personalized explanations. The model trains collaboratively without centralizing user data, preserving privacy while leveraging graph attention networks to learn from user-job-skill knowledge graphs. To enhance transparency, it integrates interpretable AI and contrastive reasoning, generating user-adapted explanations.

title: Bridging the Infrastructure Gap: An AI-Driven Framework for Automated Grant Matching in Environmentally Threatened Rural Communities
authors: Alpana Desai
abstract: Identifying relevant federal funding opportunities for rural infrastructure development remains a significant challenge, particularly for underserved communities such as those in Alaska. Existing platforms such as Grants.gov rely primarily on keyword-based search, limiting their ability to capture the contextual needs expressed in community planning documents. This paper presents a hybrid retrieval framework that integrates keyword-based grant discovery with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to semantically interpret local planning documents. The proposed system ingests publicly available community plans and hazard mitigation reports, embeds them using transformer-based models, and aligns extracted infrastructure needs with funding opportunities retrieved via the Grants.gov API. By combining structured federal data with unstructured local knowledge, the framework enhances the relevance and contextual accuracy of grant recommendations. This work contributes a practical architecture for bridging semantic gaps in public-sector funding discovery and provides a foundation for future evaluation and deployment.

title: The analysis of customized tokenizer for the development of isiNdebele language model
authors: Promise Tshepiso Malatji, Thipe Isaiah Modipa
abstract: The quality of tokenization depends on the dataset quality used to develop the tokenizer. SentencePiece model requires enormous data to train a tokenizer. IsiNdebele is a low-resourced language with little dataset to train a tokenizer or text generation model. The use of customized tokenizers (monolingual) has proved to be more effective for the development of low-resourced language models. In this study, we develop two custom tokenizers, the isiNdebele monolingual, and the Nguni language family, using existing datasets from National Centre for Human Language Technology. To evaluate the tokenizers, we then developed isiNdebele language models using the multilingual text-to-text transfer transformer. After evaluating the two tokenizers we then optimized the tokenizer that showed potential to properly handle the isiNdebele language. Model type and vocabulary size experiments were conducted to improve the ROUGE F1 and the BERTScore of the tokenizer. The optimized tokenizer was then used in the development of our final isiNdebele language model. The BLEU and perplexity scores of 0.8837 and 1.0258 respectively, achieved by the developed language model indicate that the custom tokenizer has a promising ability to deal with a morphologically rich low-resourced language.

title: Effective-rotor model in frequency-domain stray losses estimation of the stator end-zone in a 240 MW turbogenerator
authors: Janusz Kołodziej, Mariusz Jagieła, Piotr Mynarek, Paweł Berger, Tomasz Dusza, Rafał Maniara
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: The analysis of stray losses in the turbogenerator's stator end-zone often requires a computationally intensive 3D time-stepping analysis. In this work, a much less demanding frequency-domain model is considered. In such a case, one must address the altered rotor geometry, excitation, and saturation mechanisms. All these differ from those in a real system. This study introduces an improvement to the conventional approach, based on linear rotor material, by incorporating the rotor saturation factor, thereby broadening its applicability across a wider range of operating conditions. The study is conducted for a 240 MW turbogenerator operating in an overexcitation area, where a noticeable change in the predicted ohmic losses is observed in response to the reduction in rotor magnetomotive force due to magnetic saturation.

title: Comparative Performance of Modern ML Architectures for Operational Seismic Forecasting
authors: Shreyash Singh Kushawah, Paras Rawal, Amit Gupta
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Different groups of researchers have thought within the field of computational studies, including the understanding of the short-term behavior of earthquakes in the Southern California region, which further gave rise to this study. For the period of time between the years 2012 and 2024, we managed to gather 17,755 recorded events and created a dataset in which we generated a total of 20 features in the hopes of capturing the various aspects with regard to the earthquakes in these given areas. As opposed to working with one particular model, we decided to use several models that consisted of ensembles with the LSTMs applied in some sort of hybrid approach, with the aim of understanding how well each one of them could deal with the tasks of estimating magnitudes and creating a general risk class, as well as modelling the temporal shifts of the events. Each of the gradient boosting models, e.g., XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, performed uniformly and systematically in that each of them generated very low errors w.r.t. magnitude prediction, around 0.02, something that is greatly acceptable in the area of ML. As far as the magnitude estimation is concerned, however, the LSTM framework estimated the magnitude of the event at a mean error of 0.14, which is relatively good. Within the scope of risk classification, all of the ensemble models, as a group, did really well, obtaining an AUC-ROC on the scale of 1.0 with very good precision, recall, and F1 score, which all combine to make a strong indication that we have a good classification. Therefore, with the four of them working together, we achieved more and, as a consequence, brought some level of understanding in the area of active seismicity.

title: Online Weighted Majority Rule with Provably Optimal Weights for Multi-Agent Robotics
authors: Michael M. Mann
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: This paper presents a theoretically grounded approach to multi-agent decision aggregation in robotics based on online learning of the classical Weighted Majority Rule (WMR). Unlike prior methods that require known agent reliability or use heuristic weight updates, the proposed approach learns agent competence probabilities online and applies the provably optimal WMR aggregation rule with log-odds weights. This enables agents with persistent errors to naturally acquire negative influence on group decisions while maintaining convergence guarantees. Through comprehensive Monte Carlo simulations with 100 independent trials across 20 agents and 100 decision rounds, results demonstrate a mean accuracy of 99.3% compared to 95.0% for simple majority voting. Critically, the method achieves 500 out of 2000 negative weights (25%), all correctly assigned to poor agents with 100% precision. The approach operates online without prior knowledge of agent reliability and provides a principled framework for multi-robot coordination and sensor fusion.

title: A Low Noise Amplifier Design with High Image Rejection and Selectivity Using Integrated BPF
authors: Sadhana Kumari, Arun Kumar G, Rakesh Kumar, Naman Jain, Mohammad Masir, Naveen Kumar Maurya
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: A low-noise amplifier (LNA) with integrated image-suppression and bandpass selectivity is designed in this paper. A cheap FR4 substrate is used to create the circuit. The simulated performance demonstrates that the amplifier forms transmission zeros at 1.99 GHz and 2.98 GHz, while maintaining a gain of 14.49 dB and a noise figure (NF) of 1.27 dB at the operating frequency of 2.4 GHz. For intermediate frequencies between 400 MHz and 600 MHzThe simulation outcomes demonstrate image suppression levels of 35 dB and 48 dB at IFs of 400 MHz and 600 MHz, respectively.

title: The Equivalent Parameters of Losses in Inactive Parts of Transformers Under Nonsinusoidal Loads
authors: Volodumyr Zinovkin, Iurii Krysan, Vira Savchenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The conditions and criteria for applying the relevant equivalence parameters of surface losses in non-active components of power transformer structures are provided, taking into account the nonlinear dependence on the magnetic field intensity scattered on their surface.
