publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)

title: Cyberlogic: A Foundational Framework for Cross-Space Logic in the Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking Hyperspace
authors: Huansheng Ning, Lei Zhou, Jifar Wakuma Ayana, Shan Cui, Mahmoud Daneshmand, Jianguo Ding
abstract: The convergence of physical, social, and cognitive processes in cyberspace has created the Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking (CPST) hyperspace, a multi-domain environment where entities and interactions span fundamentally distinct logical foundations. Existing space-specific logics, including causal logic in physical space, normative logic in social space, and cognitive logic in thinking space, were not designed for cross-space operation and exhibit critical structural limitations: representational misalignment, non-equivalent mapping, and reasoning inconsistency. Generative AI compounds these challenges by introducing endogenous cyberlogic, which can lead to opaque reasoning and semantic drift in safety-critical contexts. This paper presents Cyberlogic, a foundational framework for coordinating logic across the CPST hyperspace. Rather than replacing existing logics, Cyberlogic enables their principled coexistence through a four-layer Entity-Mapping-Reasoning-Governance architecture. It introduces the cyberentity as a unified carrier for cross-space representation, characterizes non-equivalent mappings, and distinguishes between cyberized logic (migrated from other spaces) and endogenous cyberlogic (originating within cyberspace). A formal symbolic system supports rigorous reasoning and extensibility. An information-theoretic perspective, based on mapping entropy and reasoning entropy, complements integrity evaluation by providing a quantitative measure of uncertainty propagation across spaces. Operational principles and the integrity evaluation perspective together guide deployment. The framework is illustrated through a smart city emergency response case study and further substantiated by a prototype implementation with information-theoretic analysis, demonstrating its practical coordination across all layers. Cyberlogic provides a theoretical foundation for organizing cross-space reasoning and governance in the transition from IoT to IoX, mitigating semantic drift, temporal misalignment, and logical inconsistency as generative AI assumes a greater role in cross-domain decision-making.

title: Peak Factor Method for Predicting Maximum Response and Control Force in Across-Wind Direction for Active Base-Isolation
authors: Yinli Chen, Ryuki Kamano, Daiki Sato, Kou Miyamoto
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter proposes a simple method for estimating the maximum responses and control forces of base-isolated structures with active structural control (active base-isolation) without numerical simulations. An equivalent passive system of an active base-isolation is constructed to obtain the first mode's input and modal transfer function. Based on these parameters, a simple method for estimating the standard deviations of the responses and control forces in active base-isolation is developed.

title: Communication-Augmented Wide Neural Network Control for Accelerated SoC Equalization in Battery Energy Storage Systems
authors: S T Saranya, Pritam Bhowmik
abstract: Fast and effective state of charge (SoC) equalization in battery energy storage systems (BESS) is important for the robustness, longevity, and stability of DC microgrids. Conventional decentralized and heuristic-based control methods often exhibit slow convergence and limited adaptability to dynamic load conditions and battery aging. To address these drawbacks, this paper proposes a wide neural network (WNN)-based communication control architecture that integrates local and global system information to accelerate SoC equalization. Unlike deep learning methods, the proposed approach maps voltage, current, SoC, and multiple battery states to the optimal reference power and converter duty cycle using a two-stage regression process. Input features are selected using MRMR and ReliefF techniques to obtain a computationally efficient and highly reliable control algorithm suitable for real-time applications. Under dynamic conditions, the WNN-based system with global information reduces SoC equalization time by 56.8%, with duty cycle prediction RMSE of 4.63 × 10⁻⁴, thus offering energy management for microgrids.

title: MSMGNet: Multi-Scale Modeling of Inter-Series Dependency for Time Series Anomaly Detection
authors: Silin Wang, Zhou Yong, Yuncong Wei, Piao Yong
abstract: Multivariate time-series anomaly detection is of great importance in condition monitoring and malfunction identification for multi-sensor systems. Among existing multivariate time-series anomaly detection methods, reconstruction-based models detect anomalies by reconstructing test sequences using knowledge learned exclusively from normal data and identifying anomalies through reconstruction errors. However, in practical scenarios, these models frequently suffer from overgeneralization, resulting in unexpectedly accurate reconstructions even for subtle anomalous sequences—this issue further leads to high false negative rates in practical applications. Moreover, different inter-series relationships exist across various scales, and anomalies typically cause changes in these relationships—there remains a significant research gap in leveraging inter-series correlations across multiple temporal scales to reliably detect anomalous sequences. To address these challenges, this paper proposes the MSMGNet model: this model mitigates the issue of anomaly overfitting in sequence reconstruction through a contrastive learning objective, while autonomously discovering and modeling critical inter-series correlations across multiple temporal resolutions from training data. During the training phase, the model performs multi-scale decomposition on sequences and uses a memory graph structure to record the prototypes of filtered and unfiltered sequences for each scale. During the inference phase, the model leverages these learned relationships to identify anomalous sequences marked by abrupt changes in inter-series dependencies. Extensive experiments conducted on five multisensor system datasets from the aerospace, server, and water treatment domains demonstrate that the proposed model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in anomaly detection accuracy.

title: Intermittent Event-Triggered Fixed-Time Containment Control for Disturbed Second-Order Multi-Agent Systems Under DoS Attacks
authors: Xinyi Lin, Zhuoning Zhang, Lu Ren, Mengwei Sun, Jian Liu, Changyin Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: This paper proposes an intermittent dynamic event triggered control (ETC) algorithm for disturbed second-order multi-agent systems (MASs). The proposed control algorithm aims to guarantee practical fixed-time containment under Denial of-Service (DoS) attacks. First, a novel detection algorithm is designed to identify whether the MAS is under connectivity-broken DoS attacks. Subsequently, based on the detection algorithm, an intermittent dynamic event-triggered control algorithm is devised to attain the fixed-time containment for disturbed second-order MASs by considering the connectivity-broken DoS attack periods as rest intervals. By introducing suitable parameters, the control algorithm obtains an upper bound for the convergence time, which is independent of the initial conditions. Then, based on the dynamic ETC algorithm, a self-triggered control (STC) algorithm is proposed to avoid continuous monitoring of neighboring followers and leaders. Furthermore, Zeno phenomenon is avoided in both the dynamic ETC and STC algorithms. Ultimately, the effectiveness of the proposed control algorithms is systematically evaluated through numerical simulations.

title: A Tri-Sensor dToF Architecture for Contactless Neonatal Respiratory Monitoring with Edge-Based Multi-View Fusion
authors: Giuseppe Coviello, Giuseppe Brunetti, Marco Ingravallo, Caterina Ciminelli
abstract: Early detection of respiratory instability in neonates requires reliable, contactless monitoring solutions capable of operating in crib-scale environments without physical attachment to the patient. This work presents a tri-sensor direct Time-of-Flight (dToF) architecture for contactless neonatal respiratory monitoring, based on three 8×8 multizone infrared depth sensors arranged to provide multi-view geometric coverage. The proposed system integrates centroid-based displacement tracking with amplitude-driven multi-view fusion executed on an STM32F401 microcontroller, enabling fully embedded edge processing. Respiratory motion is extracted from localized depth variations within the 100–350 mm range using spatial filtering and band-limited temporal analysis. The weighted fusion strategy dynamically prioritizes the sensor exhibiting the highest oscillatory signal quality, enhancing robustness under viewpoint changes and partial occlusions. Experimental validation was conducted using a programmable mechanical neonatal model reproducing thoracic displacements between 5 and 20 mm, including simulated supine, lateral, and apnea-like conditions. In addition, preliminary measurements on healthy adult subjects were performed to verify the capability of the proposed sensing approach to detect respiratory motion on real human surfaces under realistic conditions, including both bare and clothed configurations. Results demonstrate stable respiratory rate estimation across tested configurations while maintaining low computational complexity compatible with real-time embedded operation. Although clinical validation on neonates remains future work, the proposed architecture establishes a system-level framework for robust contactless respiratory monitoring based on geometric redundancy and lightweight edge-based fusion.

title: Unleash Your Potential
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing

title: LEABS : A Latency- and Resource-Aware Bi-Directional Service Scheduling Framework for Relay Chains in the Internet of Vehicles
authors: Yawen Zhao, Nan Ding, Chengyi Shuai, Li Xu
abstract: Multiple stakeholders such as automakers, service providers, and regulators tend to maintain their own blockchain systems in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). This fragmentation leads to data silos and hinders service interoperability across domains. Relay Chains are emerging as a cross-chain service-governance layer, enabling coordination among heterogeneous blockchains. However, scheduling multi-input multi-output (MIMO) transactions introduces new challenges, including complex transaction dependencies, heterogeneous node capabilities, and strict latency constraints. To address these issues, this paper proposes LEABS, a bi-directional service-aware scheduling framework that simul taneously considers user-side service quality and relay-chain-side resource governance. LEABS formulates cross-chain transaction scheduling as a service-delivery optimization problem, modeled by a Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP) formulation and stabilized through Lyapunov-based queue governance. A Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) method is further in troduced to learn adaptive policies under dynamic workloads and heterogeneous network conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LEABS achieves a superior balance among confirmation latency and load fairness, highlighting its effec tiveness as a Quality-of-Service(QoS)-driven cross-chain service governance mechanism for future IoV ecosystems.

title: A New Quantum Efficient Fault Tolerant Parity Preserving Clifford + T ALU
authors: Shekoofeh Moghimi, Mohammad Reza Reshadinezhad, Antonio Rubio
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Reversible logic is characterized by its feature of being a prominent application in low-power conventional computers and the most compatible logic in quantum computers. Quantum circuits executes quantum algorithms and are important in a new generation of advanced computing. However, they are highly error-prone and also subject to the use of limited and sensitive physical resources like qubits and quantum gates. This paper introduces a comprehensive fault tolerant quantum-based reversible circuit which can calculate a high variety number of arithmetic and logic operations. It uses both: parity preserving method for detecting one or an odd number of bit errors, and Clifford + T implementation for protecting circuits against decoherence faults. First, we propose a new fault-tolerant (parity preserving and Clifford + T) adder/subtractor and derive a quantum reversible Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU). These modules reach the most optimized extent of circuit line, ancilla inputs, and garbage outputs according to the logical analysis done in this work. This article focuses on reducing T-cost metrics in conjunction with enhancing the number of operations and quantum costs in proposed circuits compared to other counterparts. We propose 4-bit fault tolerant ALU derived from our proposed single-bit module to show the proposed circuit can be extended to any larger size. The proposed circuit is implemented in the Qiskit simulation tool, and output results confirm the accuracy of our design functionality. The proposed full adder/ subtractor achieves an average of 64% and 43%, and the proposed ALU circuit achieves an average of 38% and 27% improvement in T-count and T-depth, respectively compared to their counterparts. The proposed reversible quantum-optimized fault tolerant adder/subtractor and ALU units can be applied as the basic part of DSP systems and quantum processor units in quantum computing devices.

title: From TF-IDF to Transformers: A Comparative and Ensemble Approach to Sentiment Classification
authors: Dip Biswas Shanto, Mitali Yadav, Prajwal Panth, Suresh Chandra Satapathy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Sentiment analysis, also referred to as opinion mining, primarily tries to extract opinion from any text-based data. In the context of movie reviews and critics, sentimental analysis can be a helpful tool to predict whether a movie review is generally positive or negative. It can be difficult for the ML models to understand the context or metaphysical sentiment accurately, as ML models rely largely on statistical word representations. The objective of this paper is to examine and categorize movie reviews into positive and negative sentiments. Diverse machine learning models are considered in doing so, and Natural Language Processing (NLP) methodologies are employed for data preprocessing and model assessment. IMDb dataset is used. Specifically, Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVM), LightGBM, LSTM, and transformer-based models such as RoBERTa and DistilBERT were evaluated. After a lot of testing with accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC, RoBERTa performed better than all the other models, with an accuracy of 93.02%. A soft voting ensemble that combined all the models also improved classification performance, showing that model ensembling works well for sentiment analysis.

title: Education Methodologies(Pedagogy) and Child Development Programs for Individuals with Down Syndrome
authors: Sushil Kumar, Selvakumar K, Mamata Das
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Down syndrome is one of the most common genetic conditions affecting cognitive development and learning patterns. Effective education methodologies and early child development programs can significantly enhance adaptive skills, communication, and social integration for individuals with Down syndrome. This paper presents a pedagogical framework tailored to the learning styles and developmental needs of children with Down syndrome. The proposed approach emphasizes multi-sensory learning, structured teaching strategies, language enhancement, motor skill training, and inclusive education. The framework integrates clinical understanding, psychological support, and individualized educational planning to create holistic learning environments that foster independence and social participation.

title: Research on the degradation mechanism of Stacked Aluminum Polymer Capacitors in Humid Environments
authors: Ming Sha, Lei Shi, Wenjie Liu, Zhe Lv, Huanan Cui, Yuhuan Zhang, Qingyu Zeng, Xuxin Chen, Jianqun Yang, Weiqi Li, Yuhang Jing, Xingji Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: Stacked Aluminum Polymer Capacitors (SAPCs) exhibit high operating voltage, large capacitance, ultra-low equivalent series resistance (ESR), and stable performance across a wide temperature range. These characteristics position SAPCs as a strong candidate for high-reliability electronic systems. Despite their advantages, the absence of systematic studies on their failure mechanisms under humid stress limits further reliability improvements. This study integrates experimental analysis with first-principles simulations to clarify the degradation mechanisms of SAPCs in moist environments. Increased leakage current is primarily governed by defect-assisted electronic leakage in the γ-Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> dielectric, while under humid conditions hydration-related phase transformation further accelerates this same process through local phase transformation and weak-point formation. The increase in ESR is associated with moisture-induced swelling of the cathode, compositional alterations, and interfacial mismatches. Based on these results, a cyclic voltammetry based rapid screening and preconditioning approach is proposed for SAPCs exposed to humid environments.The research provides a theoretical explanation for understanding the impact of moisture ingress on SAPCs’ performance and offers practical recommendations for managing reliability throughout production, storage, transportation, and testing.

title: Lightweight Vision-Based Docking for UAVs on Resource-Constrained Edge Devices
authors: Krishnapriya A, Keerthana Sri V, Sahaya Anselin Nisha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Autonomous charging docking for UAV platforms requires centimeter-level positional precision under strict computational constraints. This paper presents a lightweight hybrid navigation architecture optimized for resource-constrained edge devices such as the Raspberry Pi Zero 2W. The system integrates regression-based ArUco localization, hardware-aware fiducial acceleration, pointer-based terminal alignment, wheel encoder odometry, and Federated Kalman Filter fusion. Experimental validation demonstrates docking errors below 6 cm and sub-degree angular deviation while sustaining 60 Hz visual processing under low-power operation. The proposed architecture enables practical autonomous docking without high-end LiDAR or GPU hardware.

title: Parallel Secure Pattern Matching with Differential Privacy and Consistency Checking
authors: Zhicheng Li, Jian Xu, Teng Lu, Jianting Ning, Qiang Wang, Fucai Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Secure Pattern Matching (SPM) aims to identify all occurrences of a target pattern within a text while preserving data confidentiality and has vital applications in bioinformatics, digital forensics, and cloud-based healthcare. However, existing SPM schemes often suffer from limited scalability on large scale datasets and provide insufficient correctness assurances under outsourced cloud settings. To address these limitations, we propose PSPM, a parallel SPM framework built upon secure multi-party computation (MPC), supporting both single- and multi-pattern queries with comprehensive wildcard functionality. The proposed scheme integrates differentially private Read and Write primitives to obfuscate memory access patterns and enable secure, oblivious data operations. To enhance efficiency, the input text is divided into overlapping sliding windows, each processed in parallel under SIMD-style execution. Each window performs bidirectional scanning to fully leverage parallelism and maximize throughput. For single-pattern queries, local matching is achieved through a border-array–based algorithm, while multi-pattern matching employs an MPC-adapted Aho–Corasick automaton. We design a lightweight cross-consistency checking mechanism that validates outputs via wildcard-augmented variants, thereby enabling detection of inconsistency-inducing single-path computation faults under the standard non-colluding semi-honest setting. Formal security proofs and extensive experimental evaluations on large genomic datasets demonstrate that our framework outperforms prior SPM protocols by up to 1.73× in single-pattern tasks and 10.56× in multi-pattern tasks.

title: A Caterpillar-Type Miniature Robot for Adaptive Locomotion and Exploration of Tiny Rigid/Soft Pipes_supp1-3697140.pdf
authors: Jinyang Gao
abstract: Caterpillar-type robots are widely used for medium- and large-sized pipe inspections. However, existing prototypes smaller than 80 mm lack both an active variable diameter capability and a contact force sensing function, which are crucial for safe and automatic exploration of unknown rigid/soft pipes (e.g., the colon). This study develops a variable diameter caterpillar-type miniature robot (VCMR) featuring a small size of Φ34.6 mm × 41 mm, a large variable-diameter range of 34.6-89.6 mm, and an integrated contact force sensing function. The VCMR actively adapts to pipe diameter changes using contact force feedback, demonstrates high load capacity in both vertical and horizontal rigid/soft pipes, and traverses a 150-cm colon phantom with sharp bends at an average velocity of 3.07 ± 0.48 cm/s. It holds promise for exploring tiny variable-diameter rigid/soft pipes and delivering cargoes through such pipes.

title: An End-to-End GPS-Based Traffic Speed Prediction Framework Without Explicit Map-Matching
authors: Beiyu Song, Yu Yang, Tingting Fan, Hongbo Ye, Edward Chung, Jiannong Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Traffic speed prediction is crucial in traffic management and vehicle routing by forecasting future speeds on urban roads using historical and real-time traffic data. Vehicle trajectories provide valuable spatial-temporal insights into city-wide traffic patterns, and thus are important in modeling speed dynamics. However, existing trajectory-based traffic prediction methods rely heavily on map-matching, which requires extensive GPS data preprocessing and introduces potential errors and uncertainties, particularly in dense road networks with long gaps between recorded locations. In this study, we propose GT-Net, an end-to-end GPS-based traffic prediction network that eliminates the need for map-matching. GT-Net initially reorganizes traffic speed data at the segment level to capture spatial-temporal intercorrelations. It then employs a GPS-to-Road attention network to estimate the likelihood of road matches while preserving the sequential orders of trajectory points. Additionally, a time-aware attention-based trajectory network is designed to approximate the driver’s likely path, effectively modeling the impact of varying driving patterns on traffic speed dynamics. GT-Net outperforms state-of-the-art approaches and also demonstrates strong robustness based on extensive experiments conducted on a large city-scale real-world dataset with significant GPS drift.

title: AL-KalmanNet: Neural Network-Aided Kalman Filtering Based on Active Learning_supp1-3698385.pdf
authors: Wen-An Zhang
abstract: Neural network-aided Kalman filters (KFs) can learn from data to reliably estimate states in dynamic systems with complex and partially known dynamics. A key characteristic of current neural network-aided KF designs is their reliance on large amounts of labeled data. However, acquiring suitable datasets with a sufficient number of labeled data points consumes significant manpower, making it impractical for real-world applications. Thus, we propose Active Learning-KalmanNet (AL-KalmanNet), a neural network-aided KF that integrates recent advances in deep learning-based filtering into the active learning framework in a practical manner. In AL-KalmanNet, an uncertainty-based acquisition function is developed to select the most informative unlabeled data points for querying, enabling neural network-aided KFs to learn from small amounts of labeled data and achieve performance comparable to that of neural network-aided KFs trained with sufficient data. Numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Multifrequency Hysteresis Compensation and Fuzzy-Adaptive Fast Terminal Sliding Mode for Piezoelectric Nanorobots_supp1-3692843.docx
authors: zhan yang
abstract: Rate-dependent hysteresis and external disturbances critically constrain the tracking performance and operational stability of piezoelectric nanorobots. This study proposes a hybrid control strategy combining multifrequency feedforward compensation with fuzzy-adaptive feedback control to overcome these limitations. The feedforward component is built upon an enhanced Bouc–Wen model, with parameters accurately identified over the 0.1–20 Hz range via a modified particle swarm optimization, which effectively linearizes the actuator by canceling rate-dependent hysteresis, reducing the maximum tracking error to 0.64% with an RMSE of 0.29%. Building upon this linearized base, a fuzzy-adaptive fast terminal sliding mode controller (Fuzzy-FTSMC) suppresses residual disturbances and model uncertainties. It dynamically adjusts switching gains in real-time via a context-aware fuzzy inference system, thereby balancing finite-time convergence against chattering. Experimental validation confirms the method's efficacy, showing a single-axis repetitive positioning accuracy with a maximum error of 3.5 nm and RMSE below 2.8 nm across amplitudes from 1 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m down to 50 nm, and multiaxis contour tracking with a maximum error of 4.9 nm and RMSE of 3.9 nm for circular paths. The proposed framework provides an integrated solution that effectively combines multifrequency hysteresis compensation with adaptive feedback control, demonstrating high-precision performance in piezoelectric nanomanipulation tasks.

title: Attention-Based Geo–Textual Fusion Network for Disaster Risk Prediction
authors: Mohammad Shafat Ahsan, Mst Sanjida Alam, Syed Sajjad Ahmed, MD Tanzimul Islam, Hashibul Ahsan Shoaib, M. F. Mridha, Md. Jakir Hossen
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Natural disasters pose recurring threats to human life and infrastructure, demanding intelligent systems that can process heterogeneous data streams and provide actionable insights in real time. Existing approaches often treat textual signals from social media and emergency communications separately from spatial hazard attributes, limiting their effectiveness in capturing the full complexity of evolving crises. This paper proposes an AI-driven geo–textual intelligence framework that integrates disaster-related text with GIS-based hazard features for real-time risk prediction and evacuation planning. The framework employs contextual text encoders and a neural GIS encoder, fused through an attention mechanism that dynamically weights cross-modal signals. Experiments on three datasets; social media disaster tweets, multi-label disaster response messages, and flood hazard GIS attributes. It shows that the proposed model achieves an accuracy of 0.94, macro-F1 of 0.89, IoU of 0.81, and kappa of 0.78, surpassing all classical and deep baselines. Evacuation simulations further demonstrate that the model produces safer and faster routes, reducing travel time from 42.1 to 41.5 minutes and increasing the safety index from 0.61 to 0.81. These results highlight the novelty of aligning linguistic cues with spatial context and underscore the potential of deep geo–textual fusion for enhancing situational awareness and supporting critical decision-making in disaster response.

title: Simulated-Annealing-Assisted Artificial Hummingbird Algorithm for Efficient Parcel Delivery in Smart City_supp1-3659807.pdf
authors: MengChu Zhou - SENIOR EDITOR
abstract: With the rapid development of modern information technology, e-commerce has become an integral part of our daily life, which demands intelligent and highly efficient logistics systems. Currently, fixed partitioning methods are widely adopted. To optimize delivery efficiency, this work introduces a changing distribution partitioning network. The core of this network is the concept of reachable distance, whose use enables us to well balance delivery workloads of couriers. The optimization problem addressed in this work consists of two classic NP-hard problems: Distribution Partitioning and Vehicle Routing ones. To solve it, we propose a Simulated-Annealing-Assisted Artificial Hummingbird Algorithm (SAHA), such that its total transportation cost is minimized. Through experimental validation, we determine that setting the reachable distance to 40% of the distance to the nearest distribution center yields optimal or near-optimal performance. Compared to the traditional community logistics algorithm, SAHA reduces the total transportation distance by approximately 8% and shortens the convergence time by 59%. Additionally, we confirm the robustness of SAHA across both small-scale suburban and high-density urban environments. These results underscore the strong potential of SAHA to enhance operational efficiency and adaptability in smart logistics systems.

title: SynCAFG-Net: Synergistic Cross-Attention Feature Generation for High-Accuracy Lightweight Image Classification
authors: Peng Li, Roman Senkerik, Zuzana Kominkova Oplatkova
abstract: Lightweight convolutional neural networks frequently trade representational power for efficiency, leading to limited spatial adaptability and slow convergence on complex datasets. In this paper, we propose Synergistic Cross-Attention Feature Generation Network (SynCAFG-Net), a novel and compact CNN that addresses these limitations through a unified Cross-Attention Feature Generation (CAFG) block. The CAFG block synergistically integrates global context descriptors, deformable spatial sampling, and context-conditioned hyperkernels within a learnable dual-gating framework. By dynamically modulating both sampling offsets and convolution weights based on image-wide content, SynCAFG-Net effectively balances local feature preservation with global refinement. Extensive experiments across five challenging benchmarks—CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, FashionMNIST, and TinyImageNet—demonstrate that SynCAFG-Net consistently outperforms established lightweight and traditional architectures, including ResNet-18, MobileNetV3, and ResNeXt-50, by margins of up to 58.7% in top-1 accuracy. Furthermore, our model exhibits markedly faster convergence and smoother optimization dynamics compared to static lightweight baselines. While incurring a moderate training-time overhead of 15–35%, the substantial gains in accuracy and stability establish SynCAFG-Net as a new Pareto-efficient solution for high-accuracy visual recognition in resource-constrained environments.

title: Optimization of the Daily Load Schedule of the Microgrid According to the Criterion of the Electricity Purchase Cost
authors: Ihor Blinov, Yevhen Parus, Serhii Tancevych, Oleksii Kalaida, Alan Kennedy, Shahid Mughal
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article relates to the optimization model of the Microgrid functions to solve the problem of reducing the cost of electricity purchase at the enterprise. The conceptual foundations for the simulation model are determined. The objectives of optimization problem are formalized. The target function specifies the minimization of the electricity purchase cost and is supplemented by penalty functions designed to cut off technically inadmissible modes of the Microgrid. A mathematical model of the energy storage system (ESS) is provided, in which the functions of calculating the technological costs of electricity are formalized. A system of constraints for the problem of minimization of the electricity purchase cost is provided. An example of daily optimization of the Microgrid electricity balances for the enterprise with its own solar power plant (SPP) and ESS is presented.

title: Decentralization of Management in Ukrainian Power System: Understanding and Challenges
authors: Mykola P. Bolotnyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The clarification and development of a complex power system concept led to posing of methodological, mathematical and technological problems. The research features of power systems functioning are shown, taking into account the development of smart grid, power quality management system, the need to process a large amount of operational information, the integration of distributed generation, renewable energy sources, electric storage systems. The paper conducted research to determine concepts describing the complexity of power supply systems and challenges for decentralization of Ukraine's power industry.

title: Evaluation of a Transfer-Learning Strategy for a Prototype Wearable sEMG Armband Trained for Upper Limb Grasp Classification
authors: Escobar-Saltarén D., Salazar Sánchez M. B., Henao Sofia. C.
abstract: This work investigates a transfer-learning approach for grasp classification using forearm surface electromyography (sEMG). Data from the Ninapro DB4 database and a prototype low-cost EMG armband were processed using seven envelope extraction methods combined with low-pass filtering to obtain stable energy signals. Feature extraction, correlation analysis, feature-importance ranking, and clustering were applied to both datasets, resulting in a common subset of six discriminative features. Two baseline models (Decision Trees and XGBoost), a multilayer perceptron (MLP), and two convolutional architectures (1D CNN + MLP and a 2D CNN using channel-by-feature matrices) were trained and evaluated. Classification accuracy on individual datasets ranged from 60% to 79%, with improvements observed after 200–300 training epochs. Models pretrained on Ninapro and fine-tuned on Armband data achieved up to 84% accuracy, with further gains after an additional 130–160 epochs. Key limitations and directions for future work are discussed.

title: Poisoning Object Detection Models for Surface Defect Inspection in Steel Manufacturing
authors: Jaan Johansson, Leila Belmerhnia, Georgios Spathoulas, Samuel Marchal
abstract: As Machine Learning (ML) becomes integral to automated quality assurance, the security of ML models emerges as a critical concern for manufacturing processes. Among the threats posed by adversarial machine learning attacks, data poisoning — the corruption of training data to introduce malicious behavior in ML models — represents the most concerning ML-related security risk to the industry [1].This paper investigates the vulnerability of ML-based quality assurance systems to data poisoning attacks in manufacturing, with steel surface defect inspection as a use-case. Using a popular object detection model trained on an industrial steel manufacturing image dataset, we evaluate two data poisoning approaches: 1) image poisoning and 2) label poisoning, targeting three adversarial objectives: a) misclassification of defect criticality, b) erroneous size estimation, and c) missed defect detection. Our experiments show that label poisoning is a serious threat to the accuracy of steel defect inspection, potentially leading to significant misevaluation in defect size and defect criticality even when less than 12% of the training data is compromised. On the other hand, we show that image poisoning has little impact on the accuracy of steel defect inspection even when more than half of the samples in a class are poisoned.

title: A GA-Optimised Wavelet-Based Ensemble Learning Model for Stock Index Trend Prediction
authors: Bashar Alhnaity
abstract: Predicting stock market trends remains a longstanding challenge due to the nonlinear, non-stationary, and highly volatile nature of financial time series. Traditional statistical models often fail to capture such dynamics, while single machine learning models tend to overfit or overlook key temporal dependencies. To address these limitations, this paper introduces a novel hybrid model Wavelet Transform Genetic Algorithm Weighted Average (WT–GA–WA) that integrates time–frequency decomposition, heterogeneous learning, and bio-inspired optimisation for robust stock index trend prediction. The proposed framework first applies the Haar Wavelet Transform to denoise and decompose the raw data into multi-resolution sub-series. These sub-series are then modelled using Support Vector Machine (SVM), Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), and Naïve Bayes classifiers, and their outputs are fused via a GA-optimised weighted ensemble. Empirical analysis on three major stock indices FTSE 100, S&P 500, and Nikkei 225 demonstrates that the proposed WT–GA–WA model consistently outperforms individual models and benchmark approaches, achieving the lowest MSE and RMSE across all datasets. These results highlight the potential of bio-inspired hybrid architectures for explainable, adaptive, and data-driven financial forecasting.

title: Themis: An explainable AI-enabled framework for Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback
authors: Andreas Chouliaras, Luke Connolly, Dimitris Chatzopoulos
abstract: Training safe Reinforcement Learning (RL) systems is inherently challenging, with no guarantee of avoiding unwanted behaviors. The most effective defenses against this are (i) transparency through explainability and (ii) alignment via human feedback. While both show promising results, no publicly available framework currently combines them. To address this, we introduce Themis, an XAI-enabled testing and evaluation framework for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Themis supports over 200 widely used environments and is easily configurable for experiments in RL, transparency, and alignment. Our results show that Themis can train reward models that match or outperform the environment’s true reward signal using human preferences. We also provide a cloud-based platform for collecting human feedback and managing experiments. It is user-friendly, auto-scalable, and supports large participant groups across multiple experiments without extra development overhead. Tests show Themis can support one thousand users in back-to-back experiments on a modest commercial machine.

title: Multimodal Fusion for Time Series Forecasting: Learning from Temporal and Visual Data
authors: José Manuel Oliveira, Patrícia Ramos
abstract: Accurate time series forecasting is crucial across various domains, yet traditional models that rely solely on numerical data often struggle to capture complex patterns in dynamic environments. This work proposes a novel multimodal forecasting framework that integrates visual and numerical data to enhance predictive performance. The framework leverages a FT-Transformer for temporal data processing and a TIMM-based convolutional network for visual data extraction. A hybrid fusion strategy combines these modalities, enabling the model to capture complementary information that improves forecasting accuracy. Empirical evaluations on the M4 dataset demonstrate that the multimodal model consistently outperforms unimodal approaches, achieving up to a 7.0% reduction in Normalized Root Mean Squared Error across multiple forecast horizons. The proposed framework also incorporates an automated training pipeline powered by Optuna, ensuring efficient hyperparameter tuning and scalability across diverse datasets. These results highlight the effectiveness of multimodal integration in advancing time series forecasting performance.

title: Longitudinal Digital Phenotyping for Early Cognitive-Motor Screening
authors: Diego Jimenez-Oviedo, Ruben Vera-Rodriguez, Ruben Tolosana, Juan Carlos Ruiz-Garcia, Jaime Herreros-Rodriguez
abstract: Early detection of atypical cognitive-motor development is critical for timely intervention, yet traditional assessments rely heavily on subjective, static evaluations. The integration of digital devices offers an opportunity for continuous, objective monitoring through digital biomarkers.In this work, we propose an AI-driven longitudinal framework to model developmental trajectories in children aged 18 months to 8 years. Using a dataset of tablet-based interactions collected over multiple academic years, we analyzed six cognitive-motor tasks (e.g., fine motor control, reaction time). We applied dimensionality reduction (t-SNE) and unsupervised clustering (K-Means++) to identify distinct developmental phenotypes and tracked individual transitions between these profiles over time.Our analysis reveals three distinct profiles: low, medium, and high performance. Crucially, longitudinal tracking highlights a high stability in the low-performance cluster (>90% retention in early years), suggesting that early deficits tend to persist without intervention. Conversely, higher-performance clusters show greater variability, potentially reflecting engagement factors.This study validates the use of unsupervised learning on touchscreen data to uncover heterogeneous developmental paths. The identified profiles serve as scalable, data-driven proxies for cognitive growth, offering a foundation for early screening tools and personalized pediatric interventions.

title: Partial Audio Deepfake Detection: Are We Really Detecting Synthetic Media or Just Dataset Content Biases?
authors: Arthur Pimentel, Yi Zhu, Tiago H. Falk
abstract: In recent years, the generation of highly realistic audio deepfakes has raised significant concerns regarding privacy and information integrity. While most research has focused on fully bonafide or spoofed speech, partial deep-fakes, where only segments of an utterance are manipulated, remain less explored. Given the nature of the task, existing datasets are relying on large language models to manipulate bonafide speech signals into partial deepfakes by altering, deleting, or replacing segments with synthetic content. These manipulations may alter the semantics and sentiment of the generated content, creating biases that can be captured by deepfake detection models, leading to overly optimistic performance and poor generalizability. In this work, we investigate the presence of such biases in two popular partial deepfake datasets, namely AV-Deepfake1M and PartialEdit. We explore four self-supervised learning models, two relying only on text transcriptions (RoBERTa and RoBERTa-sentiment) and two relying on universal speech representations (WavLM Large and Wav2vec2-XLSR). Our experiments show that, while speech models achieve higher in-domain performance, they do not generalize to out-of-domain conditions. In turn, models trained on only transcribed speech can effectively distinguish manipulated content, achieving up to 97.8% AUC in-domain and nearly 70% AUC out-of-domain. These results suggest that linguistic patterns and dataset confounds may indeed be biasing partial deepfake detection models, leading to poor generalizability.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Time-Critical UAV Medical Supply Delivery with Dynamic Task Allocation
authors: Islam Guven, Mehmet Parlak
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used to support time-critical medical supply delivery, providing rapid and flexible logistics during emergencies and resource shortages. However, effective deployment of UAV fleets requires coordination mechanisms capable of prioritizing medical requests, allocating limited aerial resources, and adapting delivery schedules under uncertain operational conditions. This paper presents a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for coordinating UAV fleets in stochastic medical delivery scenarios where requests vary in urgency, location, and delivery deadlines. The problem is formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) in which UAV agents maintain awareness of medical delivery demands while having limited visibility of other agents due to communication and localization constraints. The proposed framework employs Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) as the primary learning algorithm and evaluates several variants, including asynchronous extensions, classical actor-critic methods, and architectural modifications to analyze scalability and performance trade-offs. The model is evaluated using real-world geographic data from selected clinics and hospitals extracted from the OpenStreetMap dataset. The framework provides a decision-support layer that prioritizes medical tasks, reallocates UAV resources in real time, and assists healthcare personnel in managing urgent logistics. Experimental results show that classical PPO achieves superior coordination performance compared to asynchronous and sequential learning strategies, highlighting the potential of reinforcement learning for adaptive and scalable UAV-assisted healthcare logistics.

title: Combating Disinformation: A Deep Learning Approach for Supply Chain Resilience
authors: Md Rafiul Hassan, Mohammad Anwar Rahman
abstract: The rapid increase of misinformation on digital platforms created significant risks for companies connected to global supply chains. Misinformation and fake news result in severe disruptions to customer perspectives, cause order delays, erode stakeholder trust, and increase costs. The traditional fact-checking methods often do not keep pace with the volume and velocity of online content. In the mix of such a complex disinformation environment, an automated detection system is in demand to address this challenge. We propose a multimodal framework using two deep learning techniques: a Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) for textual semantic analysis and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) method for visual feature extraction. A key innovation in our model is the integration of a two-sigma preprocessing step to remove outliers to improve input data quality. We evaluated a portion of the Fakeddit dataset and applied the proposed BERT-CNN model. The proposed model achieved an average test accuracy of 61.36%, outperforming a benchmark model by 4.21 percentage points. The test results demonstrate that the integrated framework we propose improved general misinformation detection across varying test datasets. Our model can be applied across many industries and provides a reliable, resilient solution for those who engage in global supply chain operations. Mitigating the risks of misinformation can continuously influence our ability to do business in the digital environment. This research provides a valuable tool to minimize supply chain disruptions and improve operational efficiency.

title: Automated detection of Enterobius vermicularis eggs in microscopic images using YOLOv8
authors: Srikrishna Kataru, Atul Dubey
abstract: Enterobius vermicularis infections, commonly known as pinworms, affect approximately 40 million individuals in the United States, primarily children. The current diagnostic gold standard, the microscopic tape test, is hampered by its reliance on manual observation, which often leads to subjective interpretations, diagnostic delays, and high rates of human error. This study evaluates the efficacy of the YOLOv8 architecture for automated pinworm egg detection using a dataset of 1,882 microscopic images from IEEEDataPort. To determine the optimal training configuration, a grid search was conducted across five learning rates (10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−5</sup>–10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup>) and epoch values of 5, 10, 15, and 20. Experimental results indicate that the model achieved optimal validation performance at 15 epochs with a learning rate of 0.001, yielding a test mAP50 of 98.1%. The optimized model was deployed via a web application. This transfer-learning-based YOLOv8 approach provides a high-accuracy, low-latency, and cost-effective diagnostic tool suitable for global pathology workflows.

title: Face Density as a Proxy for Data Complexity: Quantifying the Hardness of Instance Count
authors: Abolfazl Mohammadi-Seif, Ricardo Baeza-Yates
abstract: Machine learning progress has historically prioritized model-centric innovations, yet achievable performance is frequently capped by the intrinsic complexity of the data itself. In this work, we isolate and quantify the impact of instance density (measured by face count) as a primary driver of data complexity. Rather than simply observing that "crowded scenes are harder," we rigorously control for class imbalance to measure the precise degradation caused by density alone.Controlled experiments on the WIDER FACE and Open Images datasets, restricted to exactly 1 to 18 faces per image with perfectly balanced sampling, reveal that model performance degrades monotonically with increasing face count. This trend holds across classification, regression, and detection paradigms, even when models are fully exposed to the entire density range. Furthermore, we demonstrate that models trained on low-density regimes fail to generalize to higher densities, exhibiting a systematic under-counting bias, with error rates increasing by up to 4.6×, which suggests density acts as a domain shift.These findings establish instance density as an intrinsic, quantifiable dimension of data hardness and motivate specific interventions in curriculum learning and density-stratified evaluation.

title: Exploring Deep Learning and Ultra-Widefield Imaging for Diabetic Retinopathy and Macular Edema
authors: Pablo Jimenez-Lizcano, Sergio Romero-Tapiador, Ruben Tolosana, Aythami Morales, Guillermo González de Rivera, Ruben Vera-Rodriguez, Julian Fierrez
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) and diabetic macular edema (DME) are the leading causes of preventable blindness among working-age adults. Traditional approaches in the literature focus on standard color fundus photography (CFP) for the detection of these conditions. Nevertheless, recent ultra-widefield imaging (UWF) offers a significantly wider field of view compared to CFP. Motivated by this, the present study explores state-of-the-art deep learning (DL) methods and UWF imaging on three clinically relevant tasks: i) image quality assessment for UWF, ii) identification of referable diabetic retinopathy (RDR), and iii) identification of DME. Using the publicly available UWF4DR Challenge dataset, released as part of the MICCAI 2024 conference, we benchmark DL models in the spatial (RGB) and frequency domains, including popular convolutional neural networks (CNNs) as well as recent vision transformers (ViTs) and foundation models. In addition, we explore a final feature-level fusion to increase robustness. Finally, we also analyze the decisions of the DL models using Grad-CAM, increasing the explainability. Our proposal achieves consistently strong performance across all architectures, underscoring the competitiveness of emerging ViTs and foundation models and the promise of feature-level fusion and frequency-domain representations for UWF analysis.

title: UMixKAN: A Kolmogorov-Arnold Network Enhanced U-Shaped Decoder for ViT-Based Semantic Segmentation
authors: Wenhao Lin, Yancong Deng
publish: Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Control and Computer Vision
abstract: None

title: Research on the Immersive Development Path of “Red Heritage Integration” Resources in Jilin Province Driven by Digital Twin Technology
authors: Jiasi Feng
publish: Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Digital Management and Information Technology

authors: Peng Wu, Wei Peng, Weiwei Liu, Xiaohu Zheng, Tingsong Jiang, Xiaoya Zhang, Jiahui Li

title: Robust Continuous Hand Gesture Spotting: An Empirical Study with Deep Sequential Encoding and Probabilistic Time-Series Modeling
authors: Hyeonkyu Lee, Moonju Park, Young-Eun Lee, Jaeheung Park
abstract: Real-time continuous hand gesture spotting is challenging due to ambiguous boundaries and non-gesture transitions. We propose a hybrid framework integrating MediaPipe Hands for 3D landmarks, an LSTM Autoencoder for 256-dimensional spatiotemporal encoding, and Gaussian HMMs for probabilistic modeling. To suppress spurious detections, an ergodic threshold mechanism filters low-likelihood segments based on background modeling. Evaluated on ten command gestures, our system achieves 96.56% accuracy, 97.89% segmental F1-score, and 6.55% WER on continuous streams while maintaining 28-32 FPS on a CPU-only device. Our results demonstrate that unifying deep representation learning with probabilistic dynamics enables reliable boundary detection without heavy overhead. The framework is data-efficient and extensible, bridging the gap between research and real-world VR/AR applications.

title: Delopment of Method for Assessment of Power Supply Efficiency of Shop Electricity Network
authors: Vladimir Popov, Denys Fedosha, Anatolii Zabolotnyi, Valeriia Prykhno
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Estimating the efferent cost calculation for a feasibility comparison is a laborious task, since there is great variability in the choice of the circuit solution, the choice of cross sections of the conductors of the supply and distribution shop network, as well as the design features of the shop network Therefore, the pressing issue is the formalization of the process of calculating the consolidated costs for the workshop network, which is discussed in the article. The research method is to simulate the process of development of shop electric power supply and to determine the technical and economic indicators of the shop network. The efficiency of the power supply network networks largely determines the efficiency of the industrial enterprise's power supply system as a whole and is determined by minimizing the consolidated costs, which have two components, caused by capital costs and the cost of power losses in the plant networks. On the basis of the conducted researches the technique of selection of shop electric supply on the criterion of minimum consolidated costs is developed. The proposed method of calculating the consolidated costs for the construction of the shop network allows to formalize the calculation process taking into account the design features of the shop network. The practical importance of the work is to increase the accuracy of the calculation taking into account the mode of electricity consumption of electric receivers, and also allows to determine how the consolidated costs will change when the ratio of the specific costs for the construction of shop networks and the tariff for electricity is changed.

title: Cross-Modal Proxy Prompt Alignment for Fine-Grained Image Classification
authors: Jin Han, Junlin Hu
abstract: Fine-grained image classification remains challenging due to subtle inter-class differences, large intra-class variations, and lack of explicit semantic guidance in purely visual representations. Existing methods often struggle to construct stable and discriminative class prototypes, especially when textual annotations are unavailable. In this paper, we propose a cross-modal proxy prompt alignment (CPPA) method that introduces proxy prompts and obtains learnable class prototypes that function as auxiliary textual information to encode and inject fine-grained semantic cues into the model. The proxy prompts are directly optimized via multiple image-text alignment objectives, enabling them to autonomously acquire class-specific semantic structure without requiring additional annotations. Building on the aligned proxy prompts, we introduce a fusion mechanism that enables deep bidirectional interaction between visual features and textual proxies, effectively integrating local visual details with class-level semantic cues. Through a two-stage training procedure, our CPPA learns an enriched and more discriminative feature space while maintaining training stability. Experiments on CUB-200-2011, Stanford Dogs, and NABirds datasets show that our CPPA consistently enhances fine-grained image classification performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of proxy prompt alignment and cross-modal fusion for fine-grained image classification.

title: Quantum Kernels for SVM Entanglement Detection
authors: Ernesto Acosta, Carlos Cano, Daniel Manzano, Guillermo Botella
abstract: This work presents a quantum machine learning approach for bipartite entanglement detection in 3×3 quantum systems using a support vector machine with quantum kernels. The classifier is trained to distinguish separable from entangled states, which includes both PPT-detectable and PPT-non-detectable instances, based on classical simulations of parameterized quantum feature maps combined with dimensionality reduction via PCA. Our results show that Pauli-based feature map kernels achieve higher classification accuracy than amplitude embedding at the same PCA dimension, reaching up to 78% accuracy for PCA =8, while amplitude embedding requires PCA =32 to attain comparable performance. We also show that increasing the number of Pauli feature map repetitions does not lead to consistent accuracy improvements, despite significantly increasing circuit depth and computational overhead.

title: Two-timer-KAN: Dual-Exclusive Fourier KANs with Gaussian Fusion for Few-Shot Multimodal Remote Sensing Imagery Classification
authors: Jiaojiao Li, Hailong Wu, Haitao Xu, Rui Song, Yunsong Li, Qian Du
abstract: Multimodal remote sensing imagery classification (MRSIC) aims to synergistically leverage complementary information from heterogeneous data sources, enabling precise land-cover classification. Existing MRSIC approaches predominantly rely on abundant annotated samples, facing critical performance degradation under data-scarce scenarios that are particularly exacerbated by the inherent complexity of heterogeneous multi-modal data. Furthermore, effectively extracting spatial-spectral information of multimodal data and fusing the cross-modal heterogeneous features persists as a significant challenge. To address these obstacles, we propose a pioneering few-shot MRSIC network, Two-timer-KAN, which integrates modality-specific feature extraction for spectral- and spatial-dominant data. Specifically, leveraging the nonlinear power of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), we develop the Dual-Exclusive Fourier KAN (DEF-KAN) encoder, which captures modality-specific global features in the frequency domain, bridging spectral and spatial gaps across various datasets. Following this, a Multivariate-Gaussian-based Cross-KAN (MG-Cross-KAN) is dedicated to enhancing the robustness of cross-modality fusion by capturing modality-shared features in a distribution-based manner. Finally, to further tackle classification ambiguity under limited annotated samples, we present a visual-textual bidirectional alignment strategy, which leverages textual descriptions as supplementary semantical knowledge to clarify class feature centers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed Two-timer-KAN achieves superior performance, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness.

title: Novel Pattern Reconfigurable Antenna Designs Using Third Resonant Mode Dipoles: Standalone and Wearable Implementations
authors: Muhammad Zaka Ali, Mourad Nedil, Ismail Ben Mabrouk
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper introduces a novel paradigm for pattern reconfigurability in planar antennas based on the controlled excitation of two orthogonally arranged third resonant mode (TRM) electric dipoles. Unlike conventional Huygens-based techniques that require complementary electric–magnetic radiators with precise amplitude and phase balancing, the proposed design uses only two electric dipoles. It achieves four distinct high-gain endfire beams while significantly reducing structural complexity. The standalone antenna achieves peak simulated gains exceeding 8.0 dBi, together with wide impedance bandwidth. For wearable applications, a compact artificial magnetic conductor (AMC) based on interdigital capacitor (IDC)-loaded unit cells is developed, enabling a 38.7% reduction in unit-cell size compared to conventional square-patch designs. The AMC enhances forward radiation, suppresses body coupling, and ensures compliance with specific absorption rate (SAR) limits. The wearable configuration also produces four directional beams with gains exceeding 11.0 dBi, outperforming existing reconfigurable wearable antennas. These results reveal, for the first time, that higher order mode (HOM) excitation can achieve compact, high-gain, and low-complexity pattern reconfigurability without relying on magnetic counterparts, offering an efficient and practical solution for wearable body-centric communications, wireless body area networks (WBANs), and future adaptive wireless systems.

title: DQ-VeDT: A Decentralized Quorum-based Authentication for Quantum-Safe Vehicular Digital Twins
authors: Peer Azmat Shah, Mohsin Iftikhar, Hassan Osman Ali, Mueen Uddin, Abdulhalim Dandoush
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Vehicular Digital Twins (VDTs) are emerging as a key driver for secure, adaptive, and data-driven Internet of Vehicles (IoV) systems. However, existing VDT-based authentication approaches often rely on centralized coordination or suffer from high communication and consensus overhead in case of distributed mechanism. This limits their suitability for highly dynamic vehicular environments. This paper proposes DQ-VeDT, a Decentralized, Quantum-safe Vehicular Digital Twin architecture that enables low-latency, resilient authentication without continuous reliance on infrastructure. The proposed design treats digital twins as first-class entities and employs localized quorum verification with overlap-based continuity to support mobility-aware authentication and Byzantine resilience. A lightweight trust and cryptographic state model is integrated to ensure robustness against insider and external adversaries without compromising the scalability. The simulation results show that DQ-VeDT achieves low authentication latency, stable performance under high vehicle density, and graceful degradation under adversarial conditions. The results indicate that DQ-VeDT provides a good trade-off between security, accuracy, and communication efficiency. Hence, it is well-suited for next-generation vehicular digital twin deployments.

title: Diffusion Bridge-Based Gradual Domain Adaptation for Fault Diagnosis: Concept, Algorithms and Applications
authors: Chuang Wang, Zidong Wang, Hongli Dong
abstract: Gradual domain adaptation (GDA) has been recognized as an effective strategy for alleviating domain shift by decomposing large distribution discrepancies into a sequence of progressive adaptation steps, thereby enabling smoother knowledge transfer from source to target domains. However, existing GDA methods still face two major challenges: the construction of intermediate domains is often heuristic and poorly constrained, which may result in unstable or unreliable adaptation trajectories; meanwhile, semantic information can be progressively distorted during gradual adaptation, leading to distribution misalignment and performance degradation. To address these issues, a diffusion bridge-based data adaptation (DBDA) framework is proposed in this article. Specifically, a forward-reverse diffusion bridge is constructed to progressively map target-domain features toward the source domain in a scalable and flexible manner. In addition, a knowledge retention mechanism is incorporated to preserve semantic consistency throughout the adaptation trajectory. Furthermore, a history-aware boundary extension loss is developed to enhance decision robustness under out-of-distribution conditions. Extensive experiments on cross-domain pipeline fault diagnosis datasets, as well as the public sleep european data format (Sleep-EDF) dataset, demonstrate that the proposed DBDA framework consistently outperforms existing GDA approaches in terms of accuracy, generalization capability, and stability.
