title: Deep Learning-Driven Decision Fusion: Spatio-Spectrogram Features for Inner Speech Recognition From Electroencephalogram Signals
authors: Hussna E. M. Abdalla, Hamidon Basri, Ishak Aris, Abdul Hanif Yusof, Muhammad S. Adha, Hisham Neyaz, Amna Saga, Sadiq H. Abdulhussain, Basheera M. Mahmmod, Nurbek Saparkhojayev, Syed Abdul Rahman Al-Haddad
abstract: Inner speech recognition using electroencephalogram (EEG) signals shows strong potential for developing assistive communication technologies. Existing methods often process spatial and temporal features separately, lack interpretability, and are usually tested on a single dataset, limiting their generalization. This study proposes a dual-branch deep learning framework that combines spatial features extracted through common spatial patterns (CSPs) with spectral-temporal features derived from multitaper spectrograms, using convolutional and long short-term memory networks. The model was evaluated on two public datasets, achieving classification accuracies of 89.99% and 92.47% in subject-dependent experiments. Subject-independent evaluation using leave-one-subject-out cross-validation yielded reduced accuracies of 26.20% and 20.47%, reflecting intersubject variability. Interpretability analyses using saliency maps, gradient-weighted class activation mapping, and feature contribution ratios highlighted physiologically meaningful patterns related to model decisions. The proposed method demonstrates strong performance and interpretability for subject-dependent inner speech recognition; while future work will focus on increasing data diversity and improving subject-independent generalization. This study contributes to the development of reliable and explainable EEG-based inner speech decoding for communication applications.

title: MSFANet: Infrared Small Target Detection Based on Multiscale Frequency- and Spatial-Domain Attention Network
authors: Yuezhen Jing, Yongsong Li, Zhengzhou Li, Abubakar Siddique
abstract: Infrared small target detection (ISTD) is crucial for civilian and military applications, but accurately identifying weak targets amid complex backgrounds remains challenging. To address this, we propose MSFANet, a multi-scale frequency-and spatial-domain attention network. MSFANet employs a multi-scale mixed attention module for effective feature extraction and fusion, and introduces a dynamic frequency-spatial module (DFSM)， which specifically enhances high-frequency signals by Fourier transform of high-level features, preserves details by combining spatial convolution of low-level features, and dynamically fuses them to suppress background noise. Deep supervision and a combined loss strategy further improve segmentation accuracy and robustness. Experiments show MSFANet outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on challenging infrared datasets.

title: The NOAA-21 CrIS Sensor: A NOAA Operational Asset Providing Continuity for Weather Forecast and Environmental Monitoring
authors: Flavio Iturbide-Sanchez, David Tobin, Larrabee Strow, David G. Johnson, Kun Zhang, Denis Tremblay, Peter J. Beierle, Joseph Taylor, Joseph P. Predina, Yana Williams, Rebecca Malloy, Sara Glass, Lin Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: This manuscript describes the major prelaunch and postlaunch calibration and validation activities, along with the methodologies developed and implemented to successfully demonstrate the operational capability of the NOAA-21 Cross-track Infrared Sounder (CrIS) sensor in space. These activities were essential for generating calibrated NOAA-21 CrIS observations suitable for numerical weather prediction (NWP) models and environmental monitoring applications. The NOAA-21 CrIS sensor was launched in November 2022, becoming the third operational CrIS sensor deployed in space within the same orbital plane, thereby supporting continuity across the Joint Polar Satellite System (JPSS). Following the reception of initial scientific data on February 10, 2023, intensive calibration and validation efforts were conducted to ensure that NOAA-21 CrIS observations meet the JPSS Level-1 requirements with ample margin by September 2023. The NOAA-21 CrIS observations were assimilated operationally at the NOAA National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) in December 2023 and at the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) in January 2024. As demonstrated in this manuscript, the NOAA-21 CrIS calibrated observations exhibit quality comparable to that of corresponding observations from the Suomi National Polar-orbiting Partnership (SNPP) and NOAA-20 CrIS sensors. NOAA-21 CrIS radiometric biases relative to SNPP and NOAA-20 CrIS are within ± 0.2 K, while spectral errors are within 2 ppm. The NOAA-21 CrIS has been declared the primary JPSS infrared sounder observatory since March 2024, serving as a critical NOAA asset that ensures continuity and redundancy within the global Earth-observing system through high-quality infrared observations characterized by high spectral resolution and global coverage.

title: Improving Coastal Sea Surface Salinity Retrieval During Rain Using Machine Learning
authors: Lanjie Zhang, Haoming Song, Wen Fan, Xiaobin Yin, Yibo Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Coastal sea surface salinity (SSS) is essential to understanding the land-ocean water cycle and coastal dynamics. However, accurate SSS retrieval in coastal and estuarine regions remains challenging, particularly under rainy conditions. In this study, we propose a unified SSS retrieval and correction framework applicable to both rainy and non-rainy conditions in low and middle latitude regions based on Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) observations. The framework is built upon a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM) model, with model parameters optimized using 10-fold cross-validation and grid search. To further address systematic biases that persist in low-salinity and estuarine regions, a novel correction method is introduced, in which the bias between satellite-derived SSS and in-situ observations is predicted and added as a compensation term. Validation against HYCOM and in-situ SSS demonstrates that the proposed framework significantly improves SSS accuracy in coastal regions. Compared with in-situ SSS, the corrected LGBM SSS reduces the root mean square error (RMSE) by 75% relative to the corrected SMAP CAP SSS, and by 86% and 75% relative to the corrected SMAP RSS SSS at 40 km and 70 km resolutions, respectively. Under rainy conditions, the LGBM SSS effectively captures rainfall-induced freshening, reducing the RMSE by up to 84% compared with SMAP CAP SSS. Particularly in estuarine regions, the corrected LGBM SSS exhibits superior performance to HYCOM SSS, effectively capturing rapid salinity variations that traditional physical methods often fail to resolve. These results indicate that the proposed unified SSS retrieval and correction framework provides a robust and practical solution for improving coastal SSS under complex environmental conditions, with significant implications for coastal hydrological monitoring, river runoff estimation, and ecosystem management in climate change.

title: Joint Trajectory–Energy Design for Integrated Satellite–Terrestrial Networks with Hybrid Light/RF Energy Harvesting
authors: Dengke Wang, Tiantian Chu, Zhihong Zeng, Chen Chen, Min Liu, Harald Haas
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Integrated satellite–terrestrial networks (ISTNs) are envisioned as a key component of 6G to support global connectivity for massive Internet of Things (IoT) devices. However, while terminals operate under strict energy and computational constraints and face additional challenges from satellite dynamics, current designs rarely integrate orbital information with device energy states in a unified system design, leaving significant room for improvement. Motivated by this, we propose a joint trajectory- and energy-aware framework for ISTNs operating in dynamic environments, supported by a low-complexity algorithm tailored for energy-constrained IoT devices. Specifically, we first develop a system model centered on a day–night separated hybrid energy-harvesting (DNEH) mechanism, where each device harvests light energy during the daytime and radio frequency (RF) energy from the gateway (GW) at night. Meanwhile, the GW broadcasts simplified orbital information to assist device operations, thereby forming an integrated communication–energy transfer framework that enhances system sustainability. Analytical characterizations are subsequently derived for the joint coverage probability, and simulations capture orbital dynamics such as the real-time distance variations. Building on the semi-analytical model, the proposed drift-regularized per-frame (DRPF) algorithm determines transmission opportunities that optimize the average Age of Information (AoI) by capturing the coupling between trajectory dynamics and energy availability. Numerical evaluations confirm the theoretical advantages of the proposed framework, showing substantial performance gains over the Immediate and Minimum Distance transmission baselines. Moreover, the optimized TD-SWIPT configuration and transmission scheduling translate these findings into actionable design guidelines for energy-constrained ISTNs.

title: Iterative Cross-LLM Agent Framework for Personalized Resume Optimization
authors: B. U. Anu Barathi, R. Bhuvanesh, K. S. Dakshinaa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Monotony bias is a challenge that arises when a LLM agent is architected to correct itself. The LLMs are trained on specific training dataset, thereby hindering it to produce output beyond its input capabilities. This paper introduces heterogenous LLMs architecture to break the bias, the framework is applied to the application of resume optimization, by utilizing two LLM agents tasked with specific roles; GPT-4o acts technical director and Claude 3 Opus acts as the critic. Optimizing a resume is a multi-objective task because it requires to satisfy the robotic Applicant Tracking System (ATS) using keywords, while still being readable for a human HR manager. This architecture involves looping between the two LLM agents, calculating the TF-IDF Cosine Similarity score to evaluate every draft, and strictly reject any iteration that scores lower than previous iteration. This framework achieved 53.66% keyword relevance score compared to the 43.76% baseline. Additionally, 1.36% versus 0.70% convergence velocity uplift per iteration. The difference was statistically significant (p<0.05), validated using Wilcoxon Signed-Rank test, thereby proving the decomposition of labor works in generative AI and emphasizes the requirement of diverse agent architecture to break the local minima.

title: Patch-Based EfficientNet–BiLSTM Framework for Ternary Pneumonia Classification from Paediatric Chest X-Rays
authors: Atharva Tembhurnikar, Sharvari Deshmukh, Preeti S. Voditel, Vedansh Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Pneumonia is a major cause of illness and death in children, making timely and accurate diagnosis extremely important. Bacterial and viral pneumonia are treated differently, but majority of automated systems to analyze the chest X-ray (CXR) is restricted to binary categorization which is of little use in the actual clinical practice. Ternary classification is more difficult, because of similar radiographic appearances, unbalanced classes and because global image characteristics may conceal small yet significant local anomalies. In order to overcome these issues, this paper suggests a hybrid deep learning model that integrates patch-based EfficientNet-B0 image feature extractions and a BiLSTM(Bidirectional Long-shor term memory) model to detect normal, bacterial, and viral pneumonia paediatric CXRs. A grid of patches is formed at 4x4 to extract local lung characters in each image. The BiLSTM processes features derived out of these patches in a sequential manner to comprehend the relationship between observations in one area of the lungs to other adjacent regions, just as radiologists observe in chest X-rays. The model is trained with a public paediatric CXR dataset<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">[1]</sup> where manually assigned labels are used and a weighted loss minimization is applied to manage the imbalance of the classes. The experiment findings indicate a total accuracy of 83.49%, with specific high recall value in the case of bacterial pneumonia identification. This work shows that patch-wise sequential modelling can be effectively used to enhance the pneumonia subtype classification, at no extra computational cost, which makes it a good option in clinical environments with limited resources.

title: A Framework Enabling Object-based Queries Within Images on Digital Documents
authors: Yilmaz Dikilitas, Ahmet Sayar
abstract: Digital documents frequently contain visual information such as photographs, illustrations, charts, and graphical elements that cannot be accessed through conventional text-based search mechanisms. This study proposes an object-based visual querying framework for digital documents. The proposed framework first applies document layout analysis to detect image regions within PDF pages, then performs object and chart detection on the extracted images. The detected classes, confidence scores, page numbers, image identifiers, and bounding-box coordinates are stored in a hierarchical JSON-based metadata structure. A logical query mechanism is then used to retrieve visual content according to object-based expressions containing AND, OR, and NOT operators. Finally, the matching objects are highlighted on the original document pages and a reconstructed result document is generated. Experimental results show that the proposed framework enables object-level search over visual document content and provides a scalable basis for visual indexing in digital archives. The system is particularly useful for large document collections in which manual inspection of images is impractical.

title: Semantic-Aware Multipath TCP: Enhancing Industrial IoT Flexible Manufacturing Networks With GAN-Based Packet Recovery
authors: Deajin Han, Nhu-Ngoc Dao, Sungrae Cho, Woongsoo Na
abstract: Flexible manufacturing in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) environments leverages these capabilities to adapt dynamically to changing demands. However, IIoT applications face significant challenges in ensuring reliable data transmission due to high packet loss, resource constraints, and strict latency requirements. Multipath transmission control protocol (MPTCP) has emerged as a critical protocol for IIoT flexible manufacturing, leveraging multiple network paths to enhance throughput and reliability. Despite its advantages, MPTCP suffers from Head-of-Line (HoL) blocking. This article addresses the challenge of reliable data transmission in IIoT flexible manufacturing networks, focusing on the latency and throughput degradation caused by HoL blocking in MPTCP. We propose semantic-aware MPTCP (SA-MPTCP), integrating semantic communication with a generative adversarial network-based packet recovery mechanism that mitigates reordering delays and minimize retransmission overhead. SA-MPTCP prioritizes semantically significant data recovery, minimizes retransmission delays, and stabilizes congestion control. Evaluations show SA-MPTCP achieves 96% recovery accuracy in low-loss and over 85% in high-loss conditions, reducing delays by up to 90% compared to retransmission. SA-MPTCP enhances throughput and reliability, particularly for IIoT streaming and real-time control applications.

title: Radar Mounting Angle Estimation in Operational Driving Conditions
authors: Simin Zhu, Satish Ravindran, Lihui Chen, Alexander Yarovoy, Francesco Fioranelli
abstract: The problem of estimating the mounting angle of millimeter-wave automotive radars installed on moving vehicles is investigated. We address this angle estimation problem during normal driving, without relying on controlled environments, dedicated radar targets, or specially designed driving routes. To achieve this, we propose a signal processing pipeline that combines radar and inertial measurement unit (IMU) data to enable accurate and reliable estimation under realistic driving conditions. Unlike previous studies, the method employs neural networks to process sparse and noisy radar measurements, reject detections from moving objects, and estimate radar motion. In addition, a measurement model is introduced to correct IMU bias and scale factor errors. Using vehicle kinematics, the radar mounting angle is then computed from the estimated radar motion and the vehicle’s yaw rate. To benchmark performance, the proposed approach is comprehensively compared with two alternative problem formulations and four estimation techniques reported in the literature. Validation is carried out on the challenging RadarScenes dataset, covering over 79 km of real-world driving with different velocities and trajectories. Results show that stable and accurate mounting angle estimates are obtained within approximately 25 seconds of driving. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate that automotive radar mounting angles can be estimated during complex, real traffic conditions using only onboard sensor data.

title: Multiobjective Energy Management for Hybrid Electric Vehicles via Reinforcement Learning With Joint Cross-Domain Data Feature Learning
authors: Haoyuan Lan, Haozhong Huang, Juhuan Qin, Hualin Lu, Yi Wang, Xiaoyu Guo
abstract: The energy management strategy critically determines hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) overall performance. To fully exploit cooptimization potential between energy allocation and thermal management, this article proposes a reinforcement learning (RL) model based on joint cross-domain data feature learning. The core model is further enhanced by integrating a bidirectional long short-term memory network. This enhancement enables the RL agent to jointly perceive and adapt to multi-condition driving patterns for globally optimized control decisions. For evaluation, a coupled HEV powertrain-thermal management model is developed and validated using multiple standard driving cycles. Experimental results show that, compared to existing mainstream RL methods, the proposed approach demonstrates superior performance. It more effectively maintains battery state-of-charge stability, keeps battery and motor temperatures within safe limits, and significantly reduces equivalent fuel consumption per 100 km. Overall, the method exhibits strong robustness and application potential, offering an effective solution for HEV energy management under complex driving conditions.

title: Koopman Identification of Nonlinear Systems via Reservoir Liftings
authors: Weibin Gu, Chen Yang, Lu Shi
abstract: Learning tractable linear representations of nonlinear dynamical systems via Koopman operator theory is often hindered by dictionary selection, temporal memory encoding, and numerical ill-conditioning. Inspired by Reservoir Computing (RC) paradigm, this paper introduces the RC–Koopman framework, which interprets reservoir as a stateful, finite-dimensional Koopman dictionary whose temporal depth is explicitly controlled by its spectral radius. We show that the Echo State Property (ESP) guarantees well-posedness and favorable numerical conditioning of the lifted Koopman approximation. A correlation-based spectral radius selection algorithm aligns reservoir memory with dominant system timescales. Analysis reveals how the finite memory of the reservoir determines which Koopman eigenfunctions remain observable from the lifted features. Evaluation on synthetic benchmarks demonstrates that RC–Koopman achieves a favorable balance between reconstruction accuracy of the underlying nonlinear dynamics, numerical conditioning, and dynamical stability, compared to Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) and Hankel-based lifting approaches. Code: https://github.com/ NEAR-the-future/RC-Koopman.git.

title: Bridging Cross-View Domain Gaps in Semantic Segmentation With Language-Guided Representations
authors: Wankang Zeng, Zhiyang Lu, Zhu Wang, Ming Cheng, Siqi Shen, Changwang Zhang, Chenglu Wen
abstract: Current unsupervised domain adaptive semantic segmentation (UDASS) approaches have achieved considerable success in mitigating the domain gap between synthetic and real-world data, thereby facilitating effective knowledge transfer from source to target domains. However, these methods exhibit diminished performance under the significant viewpoint discrepancies between car-mounted and drone-mounted camera setups. While some of these approaches implicitly leverage the discrepancies of text embeddings from different viewpoints to account for these variations, their language descriptions often fail to capture the distinctiveness of various categories from diverse perspectives. To address this challenge, we propose LDCView—a novel Language Descriptions guided Cross-View Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation framework. Specifically, our method leverages carefully designed prompts to guide a multimodal large language model in generating view-specific language descriptions for each semantic class. These descriptions are subsequently used to derive view-specific class prototypes. Furthermore, we introduce a text-image cross-attention decoder that facilitates the learning of view-specific semantic representations across both source and target domains. We also propose Cross-Encoder Attention Fusion (CEAF), a novel module that injects view-invariant and domain-agnostic semantic priors from a pre-trained CLIP vision encoder into the segmentation network via a cross-attention mechanism. Quantitative and qualitative experimental results on three challenging cross-view UDASS benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of LDCView over state-of-the-art methods.

title: Transformer-Based Anomaly Behavior Analysis for CPS in Water Infrastructure
authors: Zhanglong Yang, Qinxuan Shi, Sicong Shao, Prakash Ranganathan
abstract: As Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) such as water treatment and distribution systems grow in scale and complexity, their operational technology becomes computationally heterogeneous, spanning from resource-abundant cloud platforms to resource-constrained edge devices. This fundamental diversity challenges the deployment of a single, effective anomaly detection solution, creating a significant architectural dilemma for critical infrastructure operations. Deep learning methods achieve strong anomaly detection capability, but they are often too heavy for resource-constrained edge devices. Although lightweight models can reduce the demand for computing resources, their detection capability also decreases. To address these issues, this paper proposes Transformer-based Anomaly Behavior Analysis for Cyber-Physical Systems (TABA-CPS), a holistic architecture designed to address this conflict through its flexible dual-mode deployment strategy. At its core is a novel Expectation-Maximization Anomaly Transformer (EM-AT) model, an extension of the Anomaly Transformer (AT) that automatically sets the detection threshold, eliminating manual inspection. TABA-CPS offers a flexible, dual-mode deployment strategy: a Performance mode for environments with enough computational resources, which maximizes anomaly detection accuracy and enables Large Language Model (LLM)-based anomaly behavior investigation; and a Computational Resource-constrained Edge (CRCE) mode for resource-constrained devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi), which utilizes model quantization and the ExecuTorch runtime to deliver efficient, low-latency inference. Additionally, the architecture introduces the Green-Water System Anomaly Detection Evaluation (Green-WSADE) framework, inspired by the Green AI idea, to evaluate overall performance on different devices. Experiment results show that Performance mode achieves F1 scores of 96.32% and 92.47% on Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) and Water Distribution(WADI) datasets, respectively, surpassing leading methods. CRCE mode significantly lowers computational resource consumption, including a 41% reduction in average inference power with only a marginal F1 score drop (about 0.14% on SWaT, 0.11% on WADI). Moreover, the CRCE mode consistently achieves a computational resource efficiency grade of 1 to 3 across heterogeneous edge devices under the Green-WSADE framework, achieving superior resource efficiency compared to Performance mode on identical resource-constrained hardware. The results demonstrate that TABA-CPS delivers accurate and rapid on-site anomaly detection at the network edge while lowering resource usage, supporting long-term, reliable operation in water treatment and distribution systems.

title: A Gradient Stabilization Approach for Reducing Noise in Image Gradient Computation Using Gaussian Pre-Smoothing for Robust Feature Detection
authors: Ganesan P, T. Jerry Alexander, M. Easwaran, V. Elamaran, A.Aranganathan, Merlin Sheeba G, G. Sajiv
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Image gradient estimation is the utmost step in most image processing applications such as edge detection, feature extraction and other higher-level vision tasks, but derivative based operators also tend to increase impulse noise, resulting in unsteady gradient fields, and spurious edge responses. The proposed work presents a gradient stabilization model which utilizes the Gaussian pre-smoothing that follows gradient computation to eliminate noise extremity whilst maintaining structural data. The method is assessed with a salt and pepper noise which is controlled with a multi-scale Gaussian strategy (σ = 1.0, 1.5, 2.0) to determine the trade-off between denoising performance and feature preservation. Experimental results clearly reveal that both the quality of perceptions and gradient stability are improved substantially. Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) rises to 29.07 dB at the higher levels of smoothing, compared to 18.03 dB of the noisy image and Structural Similarity (SSIM) rises to 0.8305 from 0.3073. The gradient variance (GV) declines to 0.0192 from 0.0445, which means that edge structures are better stabilized. The proposed method attained its highest possible Edge Preservation Index (EPI) of 0.7997 and the Gradient Signal-to-Noise Ratio (GSNR) of 2.38 among scales. The best trade-off between noise suppression and structural fidelity is obtained with σ = 1.5. The investigational outcomes undoubtedly illustrates that the Gaussian pre-smoothing process remarkably improves the robustness of gradientbased feature detection performance in impulse noise degraded imaging environs.

title: Explainable Real-Time Multimodel Framework For Skin Disease Prediction Using EfficientNet-B0
authors: K. Malarvzhi, Mohammed Suhail Mk, Jasir R, Rithik Santhosh, Al Ameen Pa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Early and accurate diagnosis of dermatological conditions continues to be a challenge due to the small number of available specialists and the visual similarities of many skin diseases. Selecting many of these systems that generate high classification accuracy, they do not offer real-time systems, interpretability, and a clinical validation. The proposed solution attempts to realize diagnosis of the symptoms in real time coupled by the efficient multimodal decisions using image analysis and user inputs through the EfficientNet-B0. This is Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) which creates visual heat maps for interpreting model predictions in a better way. The system was trained on DermNet database which consists of 16000 images with 8 categories, achieving an accuracy of 97.41% in the test set, with high values for precision, recall and F1 score. It offers automated and real-time communication via email, containing expected outcomes, confidence rates and heat maps and care instructions. A human-in-the-loop (HITL) expert validation workflow could then help medical experts to validate state predictions to guarantee the clinical reliability. These aspects are unique to the proposed system and absent altogether in conventional standalone classification models thus improving the feasibility of the practical usage of the system for dermatological decision.

title: AI-Driven Framework for Automated Cyberbullying Detection Using Severity-Aware Behavioral Modeling, Temporal Consistency, and Social Network Analysis
authors: Venkateswaran Radhakrishnan, Sarath Sriram A, Akash S, Kirubavathi G
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Existing cyberbullying detection systems rely on binary aggression labels, failing to identify relational roles, ignoring severity gradients, and missing temporal escalation patterns. We propose a five-component framework — four-factor Peerness, three-tier Severity Annotation, Temporal Consistency via normalised Shannon entropy, Social Network Analysis with role classification, and the Cyberbullying Risk Index (CRI) — providing an interpretable, moderation-ready risk model. Five classifiers are evaluated under leakage-safe StratifiedGroupKFold cross-validation on 8,360 directed pairs from 100 users. Gradient Boosting achieves AUC 0.9091; Logistic Regression achieves F1 0.7234. Bootstrap resampling yields F1 = 0.9915 [0.9902, 0.9931]. Ablation-derived CRI weights raise Spearman correlation by 20.23 percentage points, with Intent-to-Harm carrying weight 0.617. SHAP TreeExplainer provides transparent role-level risk attribution for auditable moderation across social media, gaming, and educational platforms, with Severe interactions reaching precision 0.89.

title: Optimization of Lotka-Volterra Equations using Ant Colony Optimization
authors: Smriti Kaul, Nakul D. M., Rishabh R., T. V. Smitha, Jyotsna Chandrasekharan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: The optimization of dynamic systems has been a complex research problem in various fields of study, especially in ecology and communications. In the field of ecology, the Lotka-Volterra model explains the interaction between predator and prey populations and provides mathematical equations of their dynamics. Traditional mathematical models are usually unable to accommodate the dynamic involvement, which calls for more flexible ideas. Ant Colony optimization, which is a metaheuristic weight-based algorithm, uses multiple iterations to solve tough optimization problems efficiently, where classical methods have failed. To assess the effectiveness of adaptive optimization in ecological systems, the proposed framework is tested on both synthetic predator–prey datasets and the classical Hudson Bay lynx–hare population data. Compared to traditional baselines, it showed improved trajectory fitting and stability characteristics. This paper demonstrates the potential of Dynamic Impact Ant Colony optimization to be able to stabilize and optimize Lotka-Volterra systems under dynamic conditions.

title: Dc-EEMF: Decision-level constrained multi-focus image fusion for multi-modality photoacoustic microscopy_supp1-3697753.pdf
authors: Xueli Chen

title: Large Scale Tunnel Air-Ground Collaboration With FLISP: Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner_supp1-3697669.mp4
authors: RUNJIE SHEN
abstract: Hydropower tunnel inspection is critical for infrastructure integrity yet remains inefficient and hazardous using manual methods. We propose FLISP (Fast LiDAR-IMU Synchronized Path Planner), a mapless planning framework for cooperative UGV-UAV inspection. Unlike traditional map-based paradigms, FLISP features three core contributions: (1) a unified architecture where a single UGV-mounted LiDAR-IMU suite drives synchronized path generation for both platforms; (2) platform-specific solvers utilizing an enhanced Firefly Algorithm for UGV obstacle avoidance and a dynamic iterative optimizer for UAV flight; and (3) a hierarchical refinement strategy ensuring kinematic feasibility without state estimation drift. Benchmarks in a 1.2 km operational tunnel demonstrate that FLISP circumvents structural bottlenecks of map-based methods, eliminating map rasterization overhead (Fast-LIO2 + A*) and sampling instability (LIO-SAM + RRT*). FLISP achieves a 100% success rate with ∼7 ms latency, representing a 7-fold speedup over grid-based and three-order-of-magnitude improvement over sampling-based baselines. Validated in operational hydropower tunnels, this approach offers a scalable solution for robotic inspection in feature-degraded linear infrastructure. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/Y ezs1PfLJ4, and the code at https://github.com/ArchibaldGuo/FLISP.git.

title:
Cryogenic Forming/Breakdown of HfO
                    <sub>x</sub>
                    for Reduced C2C Variability in Filamentary RRAM
authors: Siddharth Kurup, Madhav Vadlamani, Fanuel Mammo, Abraham L. Weinstein, Michael A. Filler, Shimeng Yu, Eric M. Vogel
abstract: Repeatable switching with low variability is a highly desirable feature in resistive random access memory (RRAM) devices. Variability in RRAM is often associated with the structure of the conductive filament (CF), as well as the migration of oxygen ions and vacancies in and out of the CF. This work investigates the impact of forming the CF at reduced temperatures on the cycle-to-cycle (C2C) variability of HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> RRAM devices operated at room temperature (RT). We find that the C2C variability of resistances, as well as the set and reset voltages is reduced by approximately 50% for devices formed at 77K compared to those formed at 300K. This reduced variability is attributed to structural changes in the CF arising from electroforming at low temperatures. Furthermore, NeuroSim simulations suggest that devices formed at lower temperatures could potentially achieve higher classification accuracy as a result of the reduced C2C variability.

title: Application of Triple Order Generalised Integrator Based Control for Power Quality Improvement of SEIG Using SAPF
authors: Jotirmoy Samanta, Ralli Sangno, Rajen Pudur
abstract: Self-Excited Induction Generators (SEIGs) are low-cost and ruggedly constructed machines used in renewable energy sources and stand-alone systems. This paper examines the mitigation of multiple power quality issues (PQIs) in the SEIG edge-of-grid system under various loading conditions using a shunt active power filter (SAPF). A double-enhanced triple-order generalised integrator (DETOGI) based control scheme is implemented to generate the in-phase and quadrature signals from the sensed voltage and current signals. To generate a reference current for the SAPF dual fundamental component extraction (DFCE) method is used. The control scheme proved to be prominent under unbalanced loaded conditions and during dynamic load changes. PQIs, such as SEIG's terminal voltage, are regulated, including correction of power factor (PF) to unity, and total harmonic distortion (THD) is reduced. To prove the efficacy of the DETOGI-based control scheme, it is compared with ETOGI and modified double second-order generalised integrator (MDSOGI) based control schemes. The OPAL-RT OP4510 is used to validate the real-time simulation of the model.

title: A D-Band Phase Detector With 5.55mV/° Responsivity for Built-in Self-Test and Self-Calibration in 130-nm SiGe BiCMOS Technology
authors: Volkan Erturk, Seyyid Dilek, Alper Karakuzulu, Batuhan Sutbas, Corrado Carta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a D-band phase detector (PD) circuit along with a differential quadrature frequency multiplication chain, allowing the phase difference to be externally varied for characterization. The PD employs exclusive-<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">or</small> (<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">xor</small>) and exclusive-<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nor</small> (<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">xnor</small>) logic gates, followed by a low-pass filter (LPF), to convert phase differences between two D-band signals into a voltage difference. The advanced 130-nm SG13G2 technology of IHP with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$f_{T}/f_{\max }$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 350/450GHz enables operation across the entire D-band (110 to 170GHz). The PD achieves a responsivity of 5.55mV/° and a minimum detectable phase of 0.123°, with measurements confirming its performance as the first reported implementation above 100GHz for built-in self-test (BIST) and built-in self-calibration (BISC) applications. The PD consumes 25.5mA from a 3.3V supply voltage while the frequency multiplication chain used for characterization purposes has 288.2 mW power consumption. The measured results show that the PD is suitable for BIST and BISC functionalities in resilient D-band transceiver front-ends.

title: Stacked Intelligent Metasurface Assisted Multiuser Systems With Transceiver Hardware Impairments
authors: Junjie Fang, Chao Zhang, Jiancheng An, Mérouane Debbah, Chau Yuen
abstract: While stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) have demonstrated significant technical and cost advantages in multiuser scenarios, existing literature universally assumes ideal transceiver hardware. Addressing this gap, this paper investigates the design and optimization of a SIM-assisted multiuser downlink multiple-input single-output (MISO) system under practical transceiver hardware impairments (HWIs). To accurately capture distortion effects at both the base station (BS) and user equipment, we adopt an aggregate HWI model based on improper Gaussian statistics. The considered impairments include finite-resolution digital-to-analog converters (DACs), power amplifier (PA) nonlinearities, in-phase/quadrature (I/Q) imbalance, and other radio-frequency (RF) front-end non-idealities. We formulate a sum-rate (SR) maximization problem that jointly optimizes digital beamforming at the BS and multi-layer analog beamforming at the SIM. To tackle this highly non-convex optimization challenge, we propose a closed-form-based iterative algorithm that alternately updates BS and SIM beamforming with guaranteed convergence. Extensive simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, quantify the impact of different HWI sources, and demonstrate that SIM deployment significantly improves system robustness, mitigates HWI-induced performance degradation, and reduces DAC resolution requirements without substantial performance loss.

title: Real-Time Holography Guided 3D Printing for Photocurable Hydrogel Microstructures With Tailored Morphology and Stiffness_supp1-3694211.pdf
authors: Huaping Wang
abstract: Digital light processing (DLP) enables rapid fabrication of photocurable hydrogel microstructures, which serve as critical functional components in micro-optical systems and microrobotics, and whose performance depends on micron-scale morphological features and local stiffness. Traditional visual feedback methods provide horizontal data but struggle to quantify axial topography and local stiffness in real-time. Although digital holographic microscopy (DHM) offers advantages for sample characterization, its real-time capabilities are limited by the challenge of dynamically correcting for optical distortions. To address this, we present a real-time feedback control algorithm featuring a novel partial matrix Zernike fitting (PMZF) method. PMZF is analytically derived to estimate background phase distortion from partially occluded fields of view, enabling accurate phase reconstruction at 5 fps. With PMZF-based feedback, the system achieves 4.88 μm axial precision and 4.29 kPa stiffness precision, improving accuracy by 71.9% over open-loop DLP. Moreover, the spatially resolved control of stiffness within single microgels leads to region-specific fluorescent release, demonstrating a functional behavior not attainable with conventional printing. This work provides an effective closed-loop strategy for controlling both geometry and stiffness, paving the way for advanced functional devices in tissue engineering, MEMS, and beyond.

title: Identifying Severities in Osteoarthritis
authors: Caitlyn Zuppinger, Vincent Stadelmann, Susanne Suter
publish: 2026 IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS)
abstract: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) severity is commonly assessed using the Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grading system, but KL-scores are discrete, subjective, and variable in early disease, making them a noisy target for clinical stratification and machine learning. In addition, datasets used for OA classification are often strongly class-imbalanced, with a predominance of healthy to mid-range cases. We propose a KL-independent OA severity representation with unsupervised clustering of patient-reported outcomes and demographic features. Using a dataset of knee radiographs and clinical data, we first derive a radiographic anomaly score (XA-score) using a Siamese X-ray anomaly detection model. Questionnaire features are then clustered using UMAP and HDBSCAN, and cluster structure is evaluated against the XA-score. A multi-modal variant incorporating the XA-score during clustering is also explored. Radiograph-derived representations separate advanced OA well, questionnaire features yield clearer cluster structure, and the multi-modal configuration provides the most consistent clustering, producing coherent symptom profiles and strong separation of severe OA, though early disease remains difficult to distinguish under KL-based benchmarks. Expert inspection further supports the visual coherence of questionnaire-derived clusters. Overall, these findings support multi-modal, unsupervised stratification as a promising direction toward clinically meaningful OA phenotyping beyond traditional KL-based severity grading.

title: Robust Transmission Design for Secure RSMA-Aided ISAC Systems with Uncertain and Unknown Malicious Target Location
authors: Ziwei Wang, Xianfu Lei, Mingjiang Wu, Xingwang Li, Xiaohu Tang
abstract: This paper explores the security issues of a rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA)-aided integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system. A dual-function base station communicates with multiple users via rate-splitting technology and simultaneously senses multiple targets, which also act as colluding malicious eavesdroppers. For the case where coarse target locations are known but subject to estimation errors, we construct an equivalent wiretap channel model incorporating channel uncertainty under the collusion scenario. Based on this model, the transmit covariance matrix is optimized for the sensing-only task, representing an ideal sensing-oriented transmitter design. However, in ISAC systems, the practical transmit covariance also needs to account for communication performance. In order to improve the sensing performance while ensuring communication security, we formulate an optimization problem that jointly optimizes transmit beamforming and rate-splitting to minimize covariance mismatch, subject to secrecy rate and transmit power constraints. This non-convex problem is transformed using convex relaxation techniques and solved via a robust block coordinate descent algorithm. We further consider the case where the locations of malicious sensing targets are unknown, and then formulate an optimization problem to minimize communication power while enhancing sensing capability via an omnidirectional beam, subject to rate constraints. We then develop an efficient block-wise algorithm based on convex reformulation. Simulation results confirm the efficacy of the proposed RSMA scheme in addressing system uncertainties, as well as reveal influences of various key parameters.

title: Identification of Diseases in Mulberry Leaves Using a CNN-Based Sequential Model
authors: Hithaishi M A, Lata B T, Manjunath D G
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Mulberry leaves are the only source of nutrition for silkworms, and their condition directly influences silk yield and quality in sericulture. Early identification of leaf diseases is therefore important to prevent production losses. In practice, disease monitoring is often carried out through manual observation, which can be time-consuming and dependent on individual expertise. To improve reliability and efficiency, an automated disease detection approach based on a Sequential Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was developed. The system is structured as a step-by-step pipeline that includes image collection, preprocessing, feature learning, model training, and final classification. The network categorizes leaf images into three classes: healthy, leaf rust, and leaf spot. Experimental evaluation indicates that the trained model delivers consistent and accurate predictions. Due to its streamlined architecture and moderate computational requirements, the framework can be deployed in large-scale cultivation environments and adapted for mobile or edge-based agricultural monitoring systems.

title: An ICP Process Prediction Method Based on the AE-Transformer Neural Network
authors: Tingyang Qin, Zerui Jin, Tiantian Shi, Wenbin Zhou, Yiming Liao, Feng Yan, Xiaoli Ji
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Etching is one of the core processes in integrated circuit manufacturing. Nanometer-scale process nodes pose new requirements for etching profile prediction, including high precision, three-dimensionality, defect sensitivity, and real-time response. This work proposes a Cl<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>/Ar plasma etching profile prediction method based on the combination of technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulation data and an AE-Transformer neural network model. In this work, TCAD process simulation data are used to form the dataset for constructing the AE-Transformer neural network. This neural network can achieve accurate and rapid etching profile prediction, with a structural similarity index (SSIM) of 0.98, a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.004, and a prediction time of only 0.08s per instance. Moreover, this method is also applicable to other etching processes.

title: Performance Evaluation in a SMART Artificial Intelligence Inclusive Classroom using Modified-Item Response Theory (M-IRT)
authors: Shadeeb Hossain
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This model proposes a Modified Item Response Theory (M-IRT) framework, extending the traditional Two-Parameter Logistic (2 PL) model by redefining the discrimination parameter as a function of multiple contextual variables. While conventional Item Response Theory (IRT) models primarily account for item difficulty and learner ability, M-IRT incorporates additional passive factors that influence response behavior and learning outcomes. Specifically, the model integrates: (i) response time, (ii) encoded learning style, (iii) lesson duration, (iv) instructor reputation, and (v) participation score- each contributing to a dynamic discrimination slope. The paper presents the mathematical formulation of the model, supported by a computational algorithm designed to estimate these parameters effectively. The proposed framework offers a more nuanced understanding of learner performance and holds promises to enhance adaptive assessment and personalized instruction in the educational environment.

title: Effect of Quantum Well Thickness on the Internal Quantum Efficiency in DUV Leds
authors: Zijing Zhang, Ye Li, Guangyang Xu, Shiwei Zhuang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: AlGaN is a crucial material for deep ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (DUV LEDs), yet these devices still exhibit low external quantum efficiency. Experimental optimization is often limited by growth challenges and high costs, thus making numerical simulation a valuable tool. This study establishes a simulation model to explore how the active region structure affects carrier distribution and optical performance. Results show that the proposed design increases carrier concentration and enhances the recombination, thereby improving internal quantum efficiency. These findings offer both theoretical insights and practical guidance for developing high-efficiency AlGaN-based optoelectronic devices.

title: Balancing Latent Capacity and Heat Generation in PCM-Cooled Li-Ion 5S6P Modules
authors: Bansilal Bairwa, Vaishnavi KM, Sachin P, Sapthami K, Reigo Melwin S, Vinay Kumar Jadoun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: This work quantifies how phase-change storage must balance cell heat in a compact 5S6P lithium-ion module. A MATLAB/Simulink model couples Bernardi heat generation to a lumped thermal network with an apparent-heat-capacity PCM and calibrated interfaces. A design of experiments spanning PCM mass, melting range, effective conductivity, and convection maps peak temperature, gradient, and melt utilization. With T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</inf> 38<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">°</sup>C and E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L,PCM</inf>135 kJ, the PCM saturates in 9.3 min while total heat reaches 658.85 kJ; peak cell temperature rises to 80°C and f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">melt</inf> 1.0, violating T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> ≤ 45°C and ∆T ≤ 5 K. An energy-balance sizing rule links duty, ambient, and PCM mass, and results show that conductivity enhancement and modest forced convection are required to keep T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> < 45°C and avoid full melt under sustained load. The method yields sizing charts and trigger thresholds for hybrid PCM-assisted BTMS in 19 V, 12 Ah, 228 Wh packs.

title: Frequency-Tuned RF Power Effects on Plasma Stability in Reactive Sputtering: Validation by Unsupervised Analysis of OES and Impedance Mapping Data
authors: Cheng-Yuan Kao, Hsuan-Fan Chen, Hsiao-Han Lo, Chih-Hao Tsui, Ting-Yueh Yang, Yiin-Kuen Fuh, Tomi T. Li
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Radio-frequency (RF) power delivery plays a crucial role in plasma-based thin-film processes, where interactions between the RF generator and plasma impedance can lead to significant instabilities under specific operating conditions. In this study, an AlN thin film sputtering process was investigated using a frequency-tuned RF generator operating at 13.56 ± 5 % MHz under fixed power conditions with Ar as the sputtering gas. Plasma instability was observed at combinations of frequency and power, manifested as forward power oscillations. During the deposition process, both optical emission spectra (OES) and impedance trajectories on the Smith chart were continuously monitored. These two datasets were subsequently analyzed using unsupervised learning algorithms to cluster the data into stable and unstable plasma regimes. The clustering results effectively delineated the operational boundaries of plasma stability and correlated distinct OES spectral features with impedance variations. This approach demonstrates a data-driven framework for diagnosing plasma instability in frequency-tuned RF sputtering systems, providing valuable insight for real-time process monitoring and stability optimization in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: BSI Bonding Loop Edge Issue Improvement by CMP
authors: Yan Gao, Kun Zhang, Oliver Huang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: BSI (Back-side Illumination) CIS is widely used in the visual field, it is the core components of camera modules. BSI manufacturing process is more complex than FSI, the yield of the early BSI process is very low, because it needs high global planarity for bonding and strict defects performance. Bonding loop process plays critical roles on CIS photoelectric performance, and NG performance of one process may carry the defect risk to downstream processes. This paper introduces a new CMP CIP process around bonding to cover high far-edge range after Trimming process to avoid edge defect issue while bonding.

title: Data-Driven Root Cause Analysis for Semiconductor Device Unrepeatable Issue
authors: Jingqi Wang, Guangyao Zhang, Ziyang Liu, Qiaoling Tian, Kai Zhang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This paper addresses the unrepeatability issue in semiconductor device. A data-driven root cause analysis (RCA) framework is proposed to identify key process variables causing wafer-to-wafer variation. By employing an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), we analyze high-dimensional process data to rank variable importance. Further, a case study of the heating system demonstrates the framework's effectiveness in pinpointing critical factors like power and argon flow, providing actionable insights for restoring process stability.

title: Prompt-as-Heuristic: A Hyperheuristic Framework for Meta-Reasoning in Large Language Models
authors: Kassem Danach, Samir Haddad, Jinane Sayah, Khouloud Eledlebi, Joseph Merhej, Kallab Chadi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Prompt engineering has emerged as a dominant paradigm for controlling the reasoning behavior of large language models (LLMs). However, existing approaches rely on static, manually designed prompts that lack adaptability, theoretical grounding, and robustness under task distribution shift. In this paper, we introduce the Prompt-as-Heuristic paradigm, in which prompts are formalized as reasoning heuristics and controlled by a higher-level hyperheuristic mechanism. The proposed framework learns to select, adapt, and evolve prompt heuristics online based on task context, solution quality, and reasoning cost. We formulate prompt selection as a meta-reasoning optimization problem and propose a general learning architecture compatible with bandit-based, evolutionary, and reinforcement learning hyperheuristics. Extensive experiments across mathematical reasoning, program synthesis, abstraction tasks, and combinatorial optimization benchmarks demonstrate that adaptive prompt hyperheuristics consistently outperform static prompting strategies while achieving superior reasoning efficiency and generalization.

title: Jamming a Smart Multiuser MISO System: A Shared Equilibrium to Three Jamming Transmit Beamforming Games
authors: Bai Shi, Jingran Lin, Lihui Yu
abstract: This paper will discuss a series of game-based adversarial jamming beamforming problems in the electronic countermeasures domain, and aims to develop a unified method to solve these problems as well as finding their shared solution. In that scenario, a smart multi-antenna jammer attempts to minimize the maximum signal-to-interference-plus-jamming-plus-noise ratio (SIJNR) of multiple single-antenna users, while a smart multi-antenna base station tries to maximize the minimum SIJNR. According to the transmission order of jammer and communication system, the jamming problem can be modeled by the Nash game, the jammer-leader Stackelberg game, or the jammer-follower Stackelberg game. However, the jammer may find it enormously difficult to detect the transmission order, so it can not decide which game to choose. To address this issue, this paper will show that the three games share an equilibrium by introducing three surrogate problems and exploring their relations, so that the information of transmission order is redundant. Subsequently, to find the shared equilibrium, the surrogate problems are transformed into the problems of the mathematical program with equilibrium constraint, and a convex-concave-procedure-based penalty method is developed to solve them. The numerical results verify the existence of the shared equilibrium and demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method.

title: Enhancements of High Throughput TSV Etching Repeatability in Mass Production
authors: Zheng Ryan Ruan, Zhe Li, Yunpeng Yin
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Through Silicon Via (TSV) etch process is in high demand in advanced packaging, by employing repeated cycles of etch and passivation, also referred to as Rapidly Alternating Process (RAP). SF6 is used as an etchant while C4F8 serves as the polymer-rich passivation gas to maintain vertical and smooth sidewall profile. Lam Syndion<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">®</sup> series tool provides high throughput and mask-selective TSV etch processes with high power (up to 4kw) and enhanced RAP (with a minimum phase time of 100ms). After the completion of TSV etch process development, the main challenges encountered in mass production are overheating of the window due to high power and chamber condition shifting caused by the accumulation of rich CxFy polymers or by-products, which consequently results in the shift of TSV depth or depth uniformity. In this paper, we have studied the hardware and process solutions to maintain the repeatability of TSV etch. We have introduced enhanced condense air amplifier, upgraded turbo pump and foreline, coil current tuning and highly efficient waferless auto clean, and established TSV mass production benchmark with Lam Syndion Series.

title: Cross-Modality Deep Learning for Automated PET Segmentation
authors: Hajer Brahim, Hajer Khachnaoui, Rostom Mabrouk, Nawres Khlifa
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Accurate delineation of regions of interest (ROIs) in positron emission tomography (PET) remains a persistent challenge in the quantitative assessment of Parkinson’s disease (PD), primarily due to the modality’s intrinsically low spatial resolution and limited anatomical contrast. This study introduces a cross-modality deep learning framework that leverages structural priors from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to guide automated PET segmentation, thereby enhancing functional boundary definition through anatomical knowledge transfer. The proposed pipeline integrates MRI-based atlas labeling, multimodal co-registration, and quantification-aware learning to achieve anatomically consistent segmentation and physiologically reliable biomarker estimation. Four state-of-the-art architectures—U-Net, Attention U-Net, Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), and DeepLabV3+—were systematically trained and evaluated on paired PET–MRI data from the H828 cohort. Experimental results demonstrated that MRI-guided supervision substantially improved both segmentation accuracy and biomarker reproducibility. Among the evaluated models, FPN achieved the best overall performance, with a mean Dice coefficient of 0.907, Intersection-over-Union (IoU) of 0.56, and standardized uptake value ratio (SUVr) of 1.15, outperforming baseline models by over 10% in Dice score and ensuring consistent quantification across all dopaminergic regions. Overall, the proposed MRI-informed PET segmentation framework achieves over 12% improvement in biomarker stability, representing a significant advancement in multimodal neuroimaging pipelines for PD diagnosis, disease progression monitoring, and precision clinical decision support.

title: Detection-Guided Deep Learning for Segmentation Free Skin Lesion Diagnosis
authors: Hagar Selim, Hadeer Elkady, Nahla A. Belal, Mohamed Khedr
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The paradigm of automated skin lesion analysis has traditionally been dominated by multi-stage pipelines that prioritize lesion segmentation as a prerequisite for classification. However, pixel-level segmentation is computationally intensive and highly sensitive to varying image conditions. This paper investigates a critical research question: Is segmentation truly necessary for high-accuracy diagnosis? We propose a segmentation-free, detection-guided classification framework that leverages YOLOv8 for precise lesion localization and ResNet18 for multi-class categorization. By treating localization as an object detection task rather than a pixel-wise masking task, we streamline the diagnostic pipeline. Our framework was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 dataset, targeting three clinically significant categories: Melanoma (MEL), Melanocytic Nevus (NV), and Benign Keratosis (BKL). The results demonstrate that the proposed detection model achieves a mAP@0.5 of 0.986, while the classification module maintains robust performance across all classes. Our findings suggest that detection-guided localization provides sufficient spatial context for reliable diagnosis, offering a more efficient and scalable alternative to traditional segmentation-based Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems.

title: Promoting Generalization for Exact Combinatorial Solvers via Adversarial Instance Augmentation
authors: Haoyang Liu, Jie Wang, Yufei Kuang, Xijun Li, Yongdong Zhang, Feng Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Machine learning has been successfully applied to accelerate Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solvers. However, the learning-based solvers often suffer from severe performance degradation on unseen MILP instances due to the limited number of training instances and diversity of training distributions. To tackle this problem, we propose a novel data augmentation approach, called Adversarial Instance Augmentation, to promote the data diversity for learning-based branching modules in branch-and-bound (B&B) Solvers (AdaSolver). To generate diverse augmented instances, AdaSolver uses an augmentation policy to augment the structures of the bipartite graphs representing MILP instances. The major technical contribution is that AdaSolver formulates the non-differentiable learning problem of the augmentation policy as a contextual bandit problem, enabling efficient gradient-based adversarial training for the learning-based solver and augmentation policy. To the best of our knowledge, AdaSolver is the first general and effective framework for understanding and improving the generalization of both imitation-learning-based and reinforcement-learning-based B&B solvers. Experiments demonstrate that AdaSolver leads to a remarkable roughly 35% improvement in solving time over the B&B solver across various distributions. Moreover, AdaSolver significantly improves the sample efficiency by roughly 40% reduction in solving time compared to the competitive GNN branching baseline with only 1% of the training instances.

title: Domain-Specific Legal AI Assistant with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
authors: Khurram Shahzad, Ekereuke Udoh, Philiph Pryce, Anwar Ul Haq
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Large Language Models demonstrate impressive text generation capabilities but suffer from hallucinations and lack jurisdiction-specific grounding when applied to legal domains. This research implements a Retrieval-Augmented Generation system tailored specifically for UK law, combining dense vector retrieval with citation-backed response generation. The system processes legal documents from BAILII and UK legislation, creating a searchable knowledge base through sentence embeddings and FAISS indexing. Evaluation across multiple test queries demonstrates significant improvements over baseline LLM outputs, with hallucination rates reduced from 34.8% to 5.2% and citation accuracy increased to 94.1%. A web-based interface enables users to query the system and upload additional legal documents dynamically. The results validate RAG as an effective approach for building accountable AI systems in high stakes professional domains where accuracy and traceability are essential.

title: Ensemble and Neural Methods for Multi-Class Forest Cover Classification Using Cartographic Data
authors: Saleh Alghannam, Razan Fahmi Abuowaimer, Kerem Demirboga, Nidal Nasser, Asmaa Ali
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper presents the development and evaluation of several classification models to predict forest cover types using cartographic features from the well-known Forest CoverType dataset. Our approach compares Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Neural Networks on multiple performance metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC curves. We focus on a multi-class scenario with seven cover types, employing various preprocessing steps such as scaling, feature engineering, and handling class imbalance with SMOTE. The results demonstrate that ensemble methods and well-optimized models outperform simpler models, offering promising accuracy and robust generalization. Future work will include more advanced ensemble techniques, deeper neural architectures, and domain-specific enhancements to improve practical utility in forestry management and ecological decision-making.

title: Stacked Ensemble Learning and Explainable AI for Dust Storm Prediction in Saudi Arabia
authors: Hager Saleh, Mohammad Wajeeh, Sarah Osama, Michael McCann, John G. Breslin, Priyanka Verma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Dust storms are a recurring environmental hazard in the Middle East, with serious effects on public health, transportation, and infrastructure. This paper introduces a dataset for daily dust-storm detection in Saudi Arabia. The dataset integrates multiple data sources, including dust-related atmospheric variables from NASA’s MERRA-2 reanalysis, meteorological variables from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) ERA5-Land reanalysis, and aerosol measurements derived from the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS). Different pre-processing steps are applied: handling missing values, deriving variables, and data integration to enhance the quality of the dataset. In addition, Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) feature importance is used to select the best features. Also, Grid Search (GS) with three-fold cross-validation is used as an optimization model technique to optimize the model and enhance the results. We propose a stacking ensemble model that integrates Random Forest (RF), XGBoost, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers as base models, with RF as the meta-learner to improve performance and enable generalization. Also, the results showed that the stacking ensemble achieves the best performance with full features, with 86.10% precision and 85.70% recall, compared to individual ML models and to AdaBoost and XGBoost ensembles with selected features. Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) provides interpretable insights into model predictions by quantifying each feature’s contribution to the final decision, thereby enhancing transparency and trust in machine learning systems.

title: Towards Lightweight Voice Cloning: Quantization of LLaMA-based TTS Transformers
authors: Ali Zain
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Recent advancements in Text-to-Speech (TTS) have been propelled by Large Language Models (LLMs), leading to systems like Llasa that achieve remarkable naturalness and speaker similarity in voice cloning. However, these models, with parameter counts in the billions, present significant challenges for deployment on resource-constrained devices due to their substantial memory footprint and computational demands. This paper investigates the application of post-training quantization (PTQ) to the Llasa family of TTS models (1B, 3B, and 8B parameters). Our goal is to drastically reduce their size while maintaining high-fidelity voice cloning performance. We apply an 8-bit (INT8) quantization scheme and evaluate the trade-offs between model compression and synthesis quality using a suite of objective metrics. Our experiments reveal that INT8 quantization provides a substantial reduction in model size, making large models more accessible for resource-constrained environments. This efficiency gain comes at the cost of a modest, predictable decrease in synthesis quality and, in our software-based setup, an increase in inference latency. Furthermore, we note issues of output stability, suggesting that quantization is a mitigant, not a panacea. These findings highlight a critical trade-off between model footprint and performance, providing a practical guide for deploying large-scale TTS models.

title: Band Gap Engineering for Enhancing Performance in a Novel Dual-Bit SONOS Flash Memory
authors: Zikuan Yang, Yongyu Wu, Xuan Li, Dawei Gao, Dianyu Qi
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This work presents a detailed study on the band gap engineering of a novel Silicon-Oxide-Nitride-Oxide-Silicon (SONOS) flash memory device to enhance the performance. The primary innovation lies in the precise modulation of the Silicon-to-Nitrogen (Si/N) ratio within the charge trap layer during its deposition. We investigated the impact of varying Si/N ratios on the charge trapping characteristics. Electrical measurements reveal that the Si-rich device achieves a wider memory window and faster Program/Erase (P/E) speeds, whereas the N-rich device exhibits superior cycle endurance. This work provides a critical insight into the band-gap-level design of the charge trap layer and establishes a viable material engineering strategy for developing high-performance embedded non-volatile memory solutions.

title: SAINet: Scale-aware Adaptive Interactive Semantic Segmentation Network for Remote Sensing Scenarios
authors: Shuaiqi Liu, Zhonghao Deng, Jiahui Cao, Bing Li, Yudong Zhang, Hui Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In the semantic segmentation of remote sensing images (RSIs), heterogeneous modalities often exhibit distribution discrepancies and noise interference. How to effectively mitigate modality gaps and reduce noise while fully exploiting the complementary information across multimodal data remains a challenging problem. To tackle this issue, a Scale-aware Adaptive Interactive Network (SAINet) was proposed for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing scenes. First, SAINet employs an Adaptive Feature Interaction (AFI) module to align features from optical RSIs and Digital Surface Models (DSM) at both spatial and channel levels, thereby alleviating cross-modal inconsistency and enhancing the complementary utilization of multimodal information. Second, by introducing hierarchical contextual semantic representations, SAINet constructs a Multi-Scale Context Aggregation (MSCA) module, which strengthens the model’s capability to perceive objects at different scales as well as complex land-cover distributions. Furthermore, a Symmetric Dilated Spatial Mixer (SDSM) is designed to effectively balance local and global dependency modeling, while maintaining favorable computational efficiency and stability when processing high-resolution inputs. Finally, comprehensive experiments are performed on two widely used segmentation datasets, namely Vaihingen and Potsdam, and the obtained results indicate that SAINet delivers superior segmentation performance.

title: MigrationMapper: Fine-Grained Detection of Third-Party Java Library Migration
authors: Hussein AlRubaye, Mohamed Wiem Mkaouer, Ali Ouni
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Third-party software library reuse is becoming a common practice in software engineering. With the exponentially growing number of available and competing libraries in software ecosystems, migrating from one library to another is widely acknowledged to be a complex, time-consuming, and error-prone activity. In this paper, we introduce MigrationMapper, an automated tool that detects code migrations and recommends method mapping that is performed between Java third-party libraries. Given a list of open-source projects, the tool detects potential library migration code changes and collects the specific code fragments in which the developer replaces methods from the retired library with methods from the new library. To support the migration process, MigrationMapper detects method-level mapping between added/removed libraries using state-of-the-art Substitution Algorithm. We evaluate our approach on a benchmark of manually validated library migrations. Results show that MigrationMapper achieves high accuracy in detecting migration code and detecting method mapping. A demo video of MigrationMapper is available at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D-01g2GjuTg. The project repository is available at https://github.com/hussien89aa/MigrationMapper.

title: A Robust Adversarial GANs Approach to Pharmaceutical Data Generation
authors: Samiul Islam Niloy, Md Rahat Kader Khan, Nourhan Mostafa, Eman A. Ashour, Kasem Khalil
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Self-emulsifying drug delivery systems (SEDDS) formulation faces significant challenges due to limited experimental datasets and the complexity of predicting dual-target outcomes for nanoparticle size and drug release profiles. This inspires the development of a comprehensive framework integrating Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) with adversarial robustness testing for synthetic SEDDS dataset generation. The methodology employed a constraint-driven GAN architecture to generate realistic formulations of Capryol 90, Tween 20, and Transcutol HP while maintaining pharmaceutical feasibility constraints, with Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) implemented to evaluate model robustness. The framework is evaluated using multiple matrices including Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics, Jensen-Shannon divergence, multivariate coverage analysis, and manifold intrusion scoring to quantify synthetic data fidelity. The GAN-generated synthetic dataset demonstrated exceptional performance with multivariate coverage of 77.46%, manifold intrusion score of 0%, enhanced feature interpretability showing Capryol 90 importance scores of 0.755 for Release and 0.902 for Particle Size, predictive consistency R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> scores of 0.35 for synthetic versus 0.47 for real data, and strong distributional alignment with Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics ranging from 0.137 - 0.279 and Jensen-Shannon divergence values of 0.331 - 0.392. This study establishes GAN-based synthetic data generation as a transformative methodology for pharmaceutical formulation research, successfully addressing data scarcity and providing comprehensive validation frameworks that position this approach as a powerful tool for accelerating drug delivery system development enabling rational formulation design in resource-constrained environments.
