abstract: Compartmented secret sharing schemes form an important category within secret sharing, characterized by a compartmented access structure where the participant set is divided into parts, and all participants within the same part assume equivalent roles. In this paper, we introduce explicit constructions of ideal linear secret sharing schemes that realize compartmented access structures using partial MDS (PMDS) codes. We first present the construction methodologies for two new families of PMDS codes. Utilizing the specialized structure of these codes, we then offer efficient explicit constructions of ideal linear secret sharing schemes that accommodate compartmented access structures, complete with both upper and lower bounds. Notably, our approach is distinguished by the use of PMDS array codes, unlike existing literature that typically employs scalar codes for such constructions.

title: Optimization on Riemannian Manifolds: A Filter SQP Framework with Applications to IoT-related Multi-Robot Systems and Network Partitioning
authors: Mingyu Shen, Zhongchao Liang
abstract: This paper presents a filter-based Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) framework for constrained optimization on Riemannian manifolds. Unlike traditional penalty-function methods, which rely on predefined penalty parameters and often involve nondifferentiable terms, the proposed approach employs a filter mechanism to balance objective reduction with constraint satisfaction, thereby enhancing global convergence and robustness. In addition to integrating the filter strategy into Riemannian SQP, the framework extends several numerical techniques from Euclidean optimization—such as second-order correction—to the manifold setting. The effectiveness of the framework is validated through two representative IoT-related applications: (1) multi-robot local formation planning, and (2) network partitioning via balanced cut, modeling clustering and resource distribution in IoT networks. Detailed modeling, algorithmic design, and simulations demonstrate that the proposed approach attains efficient and feasible convergence, demonstrating its potential for a broad class of optimization problems.

title: Aerial-Ground Video-based Person Re-Identification with Neural ODE-Enhanced Fusion_supp1-3697779.pdf
authors: Huy Nguyen
abstract: Person re-identification (Re-ID) across visible and infrared modalities is crucial for 24-hour surveillance systems, but existing datasets primarily focus on ground-level perspectives. While ground-based IR systems offer nighttime capabilities, they suffer from occlusions, limited coverage, and vulnerability to obstructions—problems that aerial perspectives uniquely solve. To address these limitations, we introduce AG-VPReID.VIR, the first aerial-ground cross-modality video-based person Re-ID dataset. This dataset captures 1,837 identities across 4,861 tracklets (124,855 frames) using both UAV-mounted and fixed CCTV cameras in RGB and infrared modalities. AG-VPReID.VIR presents unique challenges including cross-viewpoint variations, modality discrepancies, and temporal dynamics. We propose TCC-VPReID, a novel three-stream architecture designed to address the joint challenges of cross-platform and cross-modality person Re-ID. We enhance this with ODE-Fusion, a neural ODE framework for fusing paired RGB-IR captures from the same platform, facilitating effective cross-modal feature fusion through continuous state evolution. Our approach bridges the domain gaps through style-robust feature learning, memory-based cross-view adaptation, intermediary-guided temporal modeling, and ODE-based fusion. Experiments show that AG-VPReID.VIR presents distinctive challenges compared to existing datasets, with our enhanced TCC-VPReID framework achieving significant performance gains across multiple evaluation protocols. Dataset and code will be made available.

title: Towards Scalable Multi-View Clustering without Predefined Clusters via Inter-Anchor Graph Learning
authors: Suyuan Liu, Lijun Zhang, Siwei Wang, Miaomiao Li, Xueling Zhu, Xinwang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Multi-view clustering aims to leverage complementary information from multiple data sources to improve clustering quality. Traditional graph-based Non-k multi-view clustering methods enable automatic cluster number determination but suffer from severe scalability issues due to their reliance on constructing large sample-level affinity graphs with quadratic complexity. To address this limitation, we propose a novel scalable Non-k Multi-View Clustering framework via Inter-Anchor Graph learning (MVC-IAG). Our method first extracts a small set of representative anchors via k-means on concatenated multi-view features, then learns a unified inter-anchor graph by integrating multi-view structural information and feature similarity priors. Our framework performs Non-k cluster discovery directly on this compact, learned inter-anchor graph, thereby enabling automatic cluster number determination, and subsequently propagates the results to all samples. Extensive experiments on multiple large scale datasets demonstrate that MVC-IAG significantly reduces computational cost while achieving competitive or superior clustering performance compared to state-of-the-art Non-k multi view clustering approaches.

title: A Dielectric-Modulated Dual-Material Gate MOS-HEMT for Ultra-High Sensitivity Label-Free Biosensor
authors: Hitesh Kumar, Kaushal Kumar Nigam, Chitrakant Sahu, Bandi Venkata Chandan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: This manuscript presents a comprehensive performance evaluation of a dielectric-modulated dual-material gate metal–oxide–semiconductor high electron mobility transistor (DMG MOS-HEMT) for various neutral and charged biomolecules incorporating source-side cavity to enable label-free detection. In this structure, dual-material gate electrodes with distinct work functions are employed to achieve a more uniform electric field distribution and to enhance electron velocity in the channel, thereby improving the device sensitivity. The neutral biomolecules are modeled by introducing a dielectric constant within the cavity, whereas charged biomolecules are simulated by varying the interface charge density at the oxide/AlGaN interface. The device structure is implemented and analyzed using the Silvaco ATLAS TCAD simulator. The performance of the device is evaluated by examining variations in drain current (I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</sub>), threshold voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</sub>), and channel conductance (g<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</sub>). It has been observed that the proposed DMG MOS-HEMT exhibits superior sensitivity with respect to single-material gate (SMG) MOS-HEMT, with maximum changes of 256 mA/mm in I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</sub> and 2.0 V in V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</sub> for neutral biomolecules, and 76 mA/mm and 0.37 V, respectively, for charged biomolecules. Hence, these results substantiate the promise of DMG MOS-HEMT architecture for next-generation biosensing applications demanding ultra-high sensitivity.

title: In-Memory Computing Enabled Deep MIMO Detection to Support Ultra-Low-Latency Communications
authors: Tingyu Ding, Qunsong Zeng, Kaibin Huang
abstract: The development of sixth-generation (6G) mobile networks imposes unprecedented latency and reliability demands on multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communication systems, a key enabler of high-speed radio access. Recently, deep unfolding-based detectors, which map iterative algorithms onto neural network architectures, have emerged as a promising approach, combining the strengths of model-driven and data-driven methods to achieve high detection accuracy with relatively low complexity. However, algorithmic innovation alone is insufficient; software-hardware co-design is essential to meet the extreme latency requirements of 6G (i.e., 0.1 milliseconds). This motivates us to propose leveraging in-memory computing, which is an analog computing technology that integrates memory and computation within memristor circuits, to perform the intensive matrix-vector multiplication (MVM) operations inherent in deep MIMO detection at the nanosecond scale. Specifically, we introduce a novel architecture, called the deep in-memory MIMO (IM-MIMO) detector, characterized by two key features. First, each of its cascaded computational blocks is decomposed into channel-dependent and channel-independent neural network modules. Such a design minimizes the latency of memristor reprogramming in response to channel variations, which significantly exceeds computation time. Second, we develop a customized detector-training method that exploits prior knowledge of memristor-value statistics to enhance robustness against programming noise. Furthermore, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the IM-MIMO detector's performance, evaluating detection accuracy, processing latency, and hardware complexity. Our study quantifies detection error as a function of various factors, including channel noise, memristor programming noise, and neural network size. The insights gained from these tradeoffs provide valuable guidance for practical system implementation.

title: DFT-Based Spectral Precoding for Scalable Unique-Word OFDM
authors: Salil Sharma, Syed Waqas Haider Shah, Jesus O. Lacruz, Joerg Widmer
abstract: Unique-word orthogonal frequency division multi-plexing (UW-OFDM) addresses the spectral inefficiency of cyclic prefix (CP) OFDM by embedding the guard interval within the symbol period. However, its practical adoption is hindered by a prohibitive computational bottleneck. Conventional methods for embedding unique word within the DFT window rely on complex matrix inversions at the transmitter (Tx). While alternative zero-padded (ZP) schemes avoid this complexity, they place guard interval outside DFT window, breaking natural circularity and necessitating noise-enhancing Overlap-Add receiver (Rx). This letter proposes a scalable, closed-form isometric Spectral Precoding architecture that resolves this trade-off. By exploiting frequency-domain interpolation, we implicitly construct the required near-zero-energy tail within the DFT window using only deterministic linear transformations. We prove this construction reduces Tx complexity to log-linear order while preserving standard single-tap equalization without Rx noise enhancement. Validated with 5G-NR numerologies, the scheme matches CP-OFDM error performance while eliminating CP overhead.

title: Detection of Unknown Attack in Cyber-Physical Power Systems via Self-Supervised Cross-Layer and Cross-Temporal Consistency
authors: Luyu Wang, Kaifeng Zhang, Biplab Sikdar, Ying Wang, Wenjun Bi, Jia Ning
abstract: Modern power grids are increasingly exposed to diverse cyber-physical attacks that originate in the cyber layer and disrupt physical operation. A critical defense challenge is to rapidly determine whether an attack has occurred and identify its type. To address this challenge, this paper aims to develop a generalized detection scheme capable of identifying multiple types of attacks, particularly unknown ones. It is first observed that, under normal operation, cyber-layer data and physical-layer states are consistent across layers and evolve smoothly over time, while attacks disrupt these consistencies. Building on this insight, a self-supervised Cross-Layer Cross-Temporal Contrastive Learning (CL-CTCL) framework is proposed, which employs a Graph Attention Network-Gated Recurrent Unit (GAT-GRU) encoder to capture spatial dependencies and temporal dynamics. Two complementary contrastive objectives are introduced: Variance-Invariance-Covariance Regularization (VICReg) for cross-layer alignment and Information Noise-Contrastive Estimation with Momentum Contrast (InfoNCE-MoCo) for temporal consistency. Residual deviations from these consistencies provide reliable attack fingerprints, enabling detection and classification of both known and unknown attacks. Experiments on an IEEE 118-bus cyber-physical testbed show that the proposed CL-CTCL achieves high accuracy on known attacks and generalizes well to unknown ones, outperforming baseline methods across standard metrics.

title: Land Surface Temperature Downscaling via Deep Cross-Fusion of Spatiotemporal Features
authors: Huifang Li, Chenglin Shao, Lei Zhou, Huanfeng Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Land surface temperature (LST) serves as a critical parameter for understanding surface energy exchange processes in urban areas. Acquiring LST data with high spatial resolution is vital for analyzing urban thermal environments. However, most existing downscaling methods rely on pixel-level statistical or machine-learning models, and even recent deep learning-based methods still have difficulty in preserving thermal consistency and recovering fine spatial details over highly heterogeneous urban surfaces. To overcome this challenge, this paper proposes a deep spatiotemporal cross-fusion framework for robust LST downscaling from 1 km to 100 m. First, a diverse set of spatiotemporal feature factors is extracted from multi-source data. Then, a spatiotemporal cross-fusion network (SCF-Net) with multiple attention modules is designed to model the nonlinear relationships between LST and heterogeneous spatiotemporal features. Guided by a global-local hybrid loss, SCF-Net can accurately reconstruct fine-scale LST in complex urban areas. Extensive experiments across representative urban regions demonstrate that SCF-Net outperforms several state-of-the-art traditional and deep learning-based methods. Quantitatively, SCF-Net achieves superior performance with R² = 0.85, CC = 0.92, and RMSE = 0.82 K. Compared with the second-best method, it further reduces RMSE by 0.20 K and improves R² and CC by 0.09 and 0.05, respectively. Moreover, SCF-Net exhibits exceptional robustness in recovering spatial textures and maintaining stable thermal patterns across different seasons and heterogeneous land covers, highlighting its effectiveness for high-resolution urban thermal environment monitoring.

title: Dual Feature Selection-Based Hybrid GRU-Attention-XGBoost Model for Stock Index Price Prediction
authors: Rajesh Kumar Ghosh, Bhupendra Kumar Gupta, Ajit Kumar Nayak
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Predicting stock prices is challenging since financial markets are volatile and cannot be predicted in a linear pattern. This study addresses the issue by presenting a hybrid forecasting system that prioritizes efficient feature selection. The suggested two-step feature selection procedure starts by using correlation-based filtering and Random Forest (RF) ranking to find fifteen significant features. In the second stage, Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) are applied to determine the fifteen most influential predictors, and only the features that appear in both stages are retained for model development. Using the selected features, a hybrid Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)-Attention Mechanism (AM)-Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) model is developed. The model combines the sequential learning capacity of GRU, the ability of the AM to explain things, and the predictive strength of XGBoost. There are three benchmark indexes used to measure performance: the Nifty 50, the S&P 500, and the Nikkei 225. The proposed method was compared with RF, XGBoost, long short-term memory (LSTM), GRU, and GRU-XGBoost. It gave lower root mean square error (RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE) and a higher coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>). The Nifty50 dataset showed the best results with an RMSE of 0.048, MAE of 0.031, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.991. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining advanced feature selection with hybrid modelling for accurate and generalizable stock price forecasting.

title: IoT–Cloud Integrated LSTM Model for Early Detection and Predictive Diagnosis of Breast Cancer
authors: S.Muthulakshmi, Lakshminarayanan R, Swati Kedar Nadgaundi, Abdyrakman Eshiev, Somasundaram K, Muruganantham Ponnusamy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Improving patient outcomes and reducing mortality rates in breast cancer requires early identification and predictive diagnosis. The growing number of health problems has put more strain on healthcare providers, underscoring the importance of cutting-edge technical solutions that enable prompt and precise diagnosis. An AI-powered data-driven strategy for early breast cancer detection is introduced in this work. As part of the process, the data is thoroughly prepared by cleaning, standardizing, extracting features, and integrating it. The most important features were identified using the Recursive Feature Elimination method, which improved classification accuracy. A combination of Long Short-Term Memory networks was created to circumvent the shortcomings of traditional neural networks when it comes to representing nonlinear systems. The novel algorithm that trained this hierarchical recurrent structure combines backpropagation with backpropagation across time. The experimental findings show that the suggested model outperformed the conventional methods with an astounding accuracy of 98.42%. Finally, the hybrid LSTM model provides a robust foundation for breast cancer early detection and predictive diagnosis, allowing for more accurate, quicker, and more efficient clinical decision-making and less diagnostic burden on healthcare providers.

title: TriPrune-HMA: A Large-Scale Heterogeneous Multi-Core LSTM Accelerator Based on Two-Stage Triangular Pruning
authors: Quanyou Li, Le Yu, Wenxin Li, Jin Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is widely used in tasks such as stock prediction and sentiment analysis. By introducing additional parameters into Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), LSTM improves accuracy but also increases computational complexity and inference latency in large and deep models. To address these challenges in LSTM-based time series prediction, this paper proposes TriPrune-HMA, a Heterogeneous Multi-Core LSTM Accelerator based on a structured two-stage Triangular Pruning method. The approach adopts algorithm–hardware co-optimization to compress the model while enabling efficient hardware execution. Algorithmically, a two-stage triangular pruning method is introduced, performing block-wise triangular region selection and nested boundary refinement under hardware-aware constraints to generate compact sparsity patterns that reduce network parameters while maintaining prediction accuracy. Hardware-wise, TriPrune-HMA employs a CPU-FPGA heterogeneous architecture with a dedicated data transmission protocol and multiple parallel processing units (PUs) implemented in the FPGA executor. Combined with optimizations such as Fixed-16 bit-width representation, block-wise storage, and piecewise linear activation functions, it improves resource utilization and energy efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that, under latency-critical streaming inference settings, TriPrune-HMA reduces latency by over 3.3× compared with high-end CPUs and GPUs in latency-critical batch-1 inference scenarios, improves energy efficiency by more than 38.9×, and achieves a throughput of 988.7 GOP/s, thereby providing an efficient and scalable solution for temporal inference in edge computing scenarios.

title: Towards Autonomous UGV-Based Radio-Frequency Measurement Campaigns: Tutorial and Application
authors: Martina Callea, Roberto Masocco, Alexandru Cretu, Giorgio Manca, Alessandro Tenaglia, Simone Mattogno, Luca Chiaraviglio, Daniele Franci, Settimio Pavoncello, Giuseppe Marsico, Daniele Carnevale
abstract: In-situ RF measurements are essential for regulatory Electromagnetic Field (EMF) exposure assessment and radio coverage characterization. These tasks have historically relied on labor-intensive surveys based on discrete spot measurements, which provide limited spatial resolution and therefore often require support from simulation tools. These limitations call for a shift toward automated and scalable RF measurement campaigns. This paper formalizes a set of design guidelines for autonomous RF measurement campaigns using mobile robotic platforms and illustrates their implementation within a modular system architecture, showing howto integrate RF sensing and autonomous robotic navigation to enable consistent, geo-referenced data collection and adaptive acquisition strategies tailored to specific measurement objectives and driven by observed radio conditions. The proposed framework is validated through an outdoor measurement campaign on a private 5G Standalone network using a quadruped robot equipped with professional RF instrumentation. Experimental results show that the system supports the autonomous execution of measurement missions for both coverage characterization and EMF exposure assessment, demonstrating the potential of the proposed approach as a scalable and effective alternative to traditional RF measurement practices.

title: Optical Wireless Communications: Illuminating the Path for Cooper’s Law
authors: Mahmoud Wafik Eltokhey, Muhammad Salman Bashir, Othman Isam Younus, Zabih Ghassemlooy, Harald Haas, George K. Karagiannidis, Shivani Rajendra Teli, Stanislav Zvanovec, Mohammad-Ali Khalighi, Zhenyu Charlus Zhang, Steve Hranilovic, Maïté Brandt-Pearce, Jean-Paul Linnartz, Abderrahmen Trichili, Hyunchae Chun, Dominic O’Brien, Murat Uysal, Jian Song, Murat Yuksel, Hany Elgala, Zhengyuan Xu, Nan Chi, Takaya Yamazato, Chi-Wai Chow, Boon S. Ooi, Mohamed-Slim Alouini
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The increasing proliferation of internet-connected devices, together with the widespread reliance on wireless communication in daily activities, are limiting the capabilities of the radio frequency (RF) spectrum to meet data traffic demands. In particular, the rise in connectivity requirements to realize next generation networks, coupled with the growth in utilization of artificial intelligence (AI) and its associated transmission of massive amounts of data, contribute to the need to leverage the optical spectrum to complement the already-congested RF bands. Optical wireless connectivity encompasses a range of solutions that can support data transfer in various use cases, ranging from intra-chip interconnects to deep-space communication. They benefit from advantages in terms of operation within a broad unlicensed spectrum, minimization of RF interference, and inherent security. On the other hand, optical wireless communication (OWC) systems might experience a decline in performance as a result of factors like atmospheric conditions and link blockage. This requires customizing system designs according to the considered use cases and investigating new solutions for realizing optical wireless connectivity. In this article, we present an overview of OWC, encompassing both its conceptual frameworks and the scientific and technological advancements that hold the potential for shaping its future in next generation networks.

title: ScGDCF: Graphical Deep Clustering with Fused Common Information for Single-cell RNA-seq Data
authors: Yao Dong, Kunyu Li, Yongfeng Dong, Ziyu Ren, Jiaxue Zhang, Yushan Hu, Xuekui Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Unsupervised deep clustering plays a crucial role in analyzing single-cell RNA sequencing data (scRNA-seq) as it helps to identify potential cell types. However, most existing clustering methods face challenges in effectively fusing common information between feature and topological structure informa tion, and they may not perform well on the sparse data, which are common in single-cell analysis. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Graphical Deep Clustering with Fused Common Information method for scRNA-seq data, named scGDCF. This method can accurately segregate different cell types even in large and sparse scRNA-seq datasets. In scGDCF, we first introduce a sparse feature representation method that utilizes an adversarial loss function to address the sparsity problem in scRNA-seq data and improve the performance of the discriminator. Next, we design a mutual information extracting operator to deeply mine and fuse the common information from feature and topological structure data, thereby improving the clustering performance. Furthermore, we incorporate the varying degrees of contribution from different neighbor nodes and information sources. To handle this, we promote a dual adaptive attention mechanism that oper ates at both global and local levels. Finally, experiments on seven real-world datasets and two simulated datasets show scGDCF outperforms 17 state-of-the-art methods. We further extend the clustering results for visualization, analysis of gene differential expression and enrichment, showing scGDCF provides novel insights into cell developmental lineages and preserved inter cluster distances.

title: A Novel Reinforcement Learning-Based Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for Power Allocation in Multibeam Satellite Systems
authors: Dingxuan Fu, Benjamin K. Ng, Chan-Tong Lam
abstract: Multibeam satellite systems (MSS) are becoming a key component of future 6G non-terrestrial networks, providing wide-area coverage and high-throughput connectivity. However, their performance critically depends on power allocation, which leads to a high-dimensional and non-convex multi-objective optimization problem: the system must simultaneously minimize unmet system capacity and total transmit power. To address this challenge, this paper benchmarks representative multi-objective optimizers for MSS resource allocation using a simulation-based system model as a unified evaluator. Under static traffic demands, we systematically compare NSGA-II, MOEA/D, and multi-objective particle swarm optimization (MOPSO). Experimental results show that, under the same computational budget, MOPSO outperforms NSGA-II and MOEA/D in terms of hypervolume and inverted generational distance, establishing MOPSO as a strong baseline for MSS power allocation. Building on this baseline, we propose a reinforcement learning–basedMOPSO(RL-MOPSO), where a lightweight policy adaptively tunes swarm parameters based on online population statistics. We then evaluate RL-MOPSO in dynamic scenarios that emulate realistic channel conditions and demand variations. In these dynamic scenarios, RL-MOPSO achieves significant additional gains: it improves hypervolume by about 10%, reduces average USC by 40%, and lowers average transmit power by more than 90% compared with MOPSO. These results demonstrate that integrating multi-objective swarm optimization with reinforcement learning provides an effective approach for multibeam satellite systems power allocation.

title: UAV-Enabled ISAC with Fluid Antennas for Low-Altitude Wireless Networks
authors: Wenchao Liu, Xuhui Zhang, Jinke Ren, Weijie Yuan, Changsheng You, Shuangyang Li
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-enabled integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is regarded as a key enabler for next-generation wireless systems. However, conventional fixed-position antennas limit the ability of UAVs to fully exploit their inherent potential. To overcome this limitation, we propose a UAV-enabled ISAC framework equipped with fluid antennas (FAs), where the mobility of antenna elements introduces additional spatial degrees of freedom to simultaneously enhance communication and sensing performance. A multi-objective optimization problem is formulated to maximize the communication rates of multiple users while minimizing the Cram´er-Rao bound (CRB) for the angle estimation of a single target. Due to excessively frequent updates of FA positions may lead to response delay, a three-timescale optimization framework is developed to jointly optimize transmit beamforming, FA positions, and UAV trajectory based on their characteristics. To solve the non-convexity of the problem, an alternating optimization-based algorithm is developed to obtain a sub-optimal solution. Numerical results show that the proposed scheme significantly outperforms various benchmark schemes, validating the effectiveness of integrating the FA technology into the UAV-enabled ISAC systems.

title: Data-driven assessment of climate and topographic integration with satellite imagery for improved surface water detection under agricultural vegetation
authors: Alexis Declaro, Jin Xiao, Robert Galla, Shinjiro Kanae
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Optical satellite imagery provides valuable information on field conditions, yet its effectiveness is reduced under dense vegetation where water surfaces are obscured. The aim of this study is to improve surface water detection in vegetated rice paddies by developing and evaluating a multi-source framework that integrates Earth observation data with climate and topographic information. The approach combines optical imagery (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel) with antecedent climate conditions, soil moisture dynamics, and terrain-driven runoff potential, and is evaluated using supervised classification with in-situ water level records as reference data. Satellite-based spectral features, climate variables, and topographic attributes were spatially and temporally aligned with ground observations to construct a comprehensive dataset for model training and simulations. Results show that incorporating climate and terrain information significantly improves detection under vegetated conditions, increasing producer's accuracy for surface water presence (PA<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">presence</sub>) by 20% and user's accuracy (UA<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">presence</sub>) by 35% compared with satellite-only models. Performance remained stable across crop growth stages, while satellite-only models degraded with increasing vegetation density (EVI), widening the accuracy gap from 30% to nearly 50% at EVI <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$>$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.5 and yielding nearly twice as many detected flooding events. These results demonstrate that integrating Earth observation with environmental datasets provides a reliable and scalable approach for monitoring surface water dynamics in rice paddies under dense vegetation. The improved detection capability supports irrigation scheduling and enhances assessments of irrigation impacts, including greenhouse gas emissions estimation.

title: RAHBAR: A RoBERTa-Augmented Hybrid biLSTM-biGRU Architecture for Intent Recognition in Education for Urdu Conversational Agent
authors: Muhammad Farrukh Shafeeq, Muhammad Aslam
abstract: The use of conversational agents in educational systems has drawn a lot of attention lately because of their potential to increase student involvement, decrease administrative burdens, and improve information availability. However, as most conversational agents are largely proficient in English, creating an intelligent conversational agent for low-resource languages like Urdu continues to be a significant issue. The absence of annotated datasets, the scarcity of pre-trained language models, and the subpar performance of current systems on rich datasets make it challenging to meet the needs of such low-resource languages. This study creates a domain-specific Urdu educational conversational agent with an emphasis on intent classification, a crucial element in comprehending Urdu queries, in order to address these issues. This paper presents the Urdu Intent classification Dataset (UID), a novel dataset of 11,598 Urdu queries and 268 hand-labeled intent classes that were gathered from actual users and were also validated against official university sources in order to address the lack of high-quality data. This study carried out a number of trials using numerous baseline models, including conventional machine learning models and sophisticated deep learning models, to assess the effectiveness of intent detection. Additionally, different transformer-based multilingual models were fine-tuned for performance. Furthermore, the study proposes a hybrid model called RoBERTa-Augmented Hybrid biLSTM-biGRU Architecture for Intent Recognition in Urdu Student Query conversational agent (RAHBAR), which combines a fine-tuned RoBERTa Urdu model with bi-LSTM, bi-GRU, an attention mechanism, and a transformer encoder for enhanced contextual understanding. The proposed model outperformed all state-of-the-art models, achieving 99% accuracy. Moreover, LIME was incorporated to enhance the explainability of the model’s predictions. This research presents a scalable, interpretable, and high-performing solution for Urdu intent classification, opening new avenues for conversational agent development in low-resource settings.

title: From Latency to Value of Information: A Review of Timeliness Metrics for Safe Transportation Systems in the 6G Era
authors: Alexey Rolich, Mert Yildiz, Ion Turcanu, Alexey Vinel, Andrea Baiocchi
abstract: Traffic safety in the 6G era will depend on vehicles exchanging information that is not only timely but also accurate and contextually relevant for cooperative awareness, collective perception, and automated driving. Classic performance metrics such as latency and reliability describe communication behavior, but do not capture how shared data supports situational awareness. Timeliness- and semantic-oriented metrics including Age of Information (AoI) and Value of Information (VoI) provide deeper insight into freshness and usefulness, yet open questions remain on how to assess performance across increasingly complex vehicular functions. This paper reviews the state of the art in timeliness metrics for vehicular networks, surveys the corresponding standardization landscape, and examines the applicability and limitations of AoI-based and related metrics. It highlights persistent gaps in evaluating cooperative and automated driving services and outlines directions toward goal-oriented performance assessment suited to safety-critical 6G transportation systems.

title: A comprehensive study on LWR improvement in metal hard mask etching
authors: Yun Xiao, HeXin Zhou, Xi Chen, XiaoHui Ren
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Metal hard mask (MHM) etching constitutes a critical unit process in semiconductor manufacturing, enabling high-fidelity pattern transfer for sophisticated interconnect architectures. The continuing advancement of device scaling paradigms has progressively revealed critical limitations in conventional MHM etching methodologies, particularly regarding the simultaneous achievement of nanoscale precision and uniformity across patterned features. This technological challenge manifests most profoundly in the antagonistic constraints between Line Width Roughness (LWR) suppression and Critical Dimension (CD) loading control. Scaling-driven requirements for sub-3nm LWR to minimize device variability directly oppose near-zero CD loading to prevent pattern-missing defects. Conventional approaches fail to decouple these competing parameters, restricting the manufacturable process window. Leveraging Lam’s plasma process expertise, this work introduces a dual-strategy methodology to decouple these constraints, thereby enlarging the process window Lam’s on Versys<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">®</sup> Metal product. As a result, the LWR is improved by 40% and the CD loading is improved by 65%. A novel low-bombardment middle-layer (ML) etch process eliminates physical sputtering agents, preserving photoresist (PR) integrity while improving CD loading. Besides, plasma pre-process treatment (PPT) induces controlled PR reflow, improving LWR absent CD distortion. The synergistic integration of low bombardment etching with PPT demonstrates zero pattern-missing defects at weak point, enlarging the manufacturable process window while maintaining compatibility with 300mm fabrication infrastructure.

title: Predicting Remaining Useful Life of Batteries Using Machine Learning
authors: Sushil Kumar, Selvakumar K, Dhanalakshmi B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Many aspects should be considered for the estimation of the remaining useful life, such as operational safety, maintenance of the planning, and the enhancement of the capabilities of various applications, such as electric transportation and large-scale energy saving networks. In this paper, we have applied XGBoost-based regression techniques to evaluate battery life means Remaining Useful Life on a benchmark dataset that is consisting of 15,064 cycle records. There are different types of measurable indicators such as voltage behavior, discharge duration and charging trends which are overall projects trajectory of battery damage. For getting an optimal predictive capability, We tuned various hyperparameters systematically using Optuna’s automatic functionality of optimization with a 5- fold cross-validation techniques to reduce root mean square error. After employing the optimization techniques, the model shown outstanding performance on unseen data. It has shown 2.44 cycle of mean square error and 4.19 cycle of root mean square error, and got 0.9812 of R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> which indicates a good relation between lifetime of original battery and expected battery. By the feature importance analysis, it is identified that the Cycle index was a dominant predictor and the RUL is strongly correlated with the cycle count on every completion. This model demonstrates the high potential of gradient boosting for battery prognostics.

title: An Automated CNN-Based Model for Leukaemia Diagnosis from Blood Cell Images
authors: Gaurav Tuteja, Kanegonda Ravi Chythanya, Neha Sharma, Preeti Badhani, Garima Jaitly, Aryan Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The microscopic blood smear images have proven difficult in the accurate diagnosis of leukemia because of the finer morphological changes and also because of the requirement of manual examination. Deep learning methods availed by automation have the potential of enhancing the reliability and efficiency of diagnosis. The convolutional neural network structure is developed to classify the multiclass blood cell by using hierarchical feature extraction. The model combines convolutional layers, batch normalization, max pooling, and dropout to promote the stability in learning and curb overfitting. Training uses real-time data augmentation and normalization and adaptive learning rate scheduling is used to enhance convergence. The experimental data is collected as 3,242 labelled blood cell images that are arranged as benign, malignant pre-B, malignant pro-B, and malignant early pre-B. Experimental analysis shows the good classification performance, where the overall accuracy is 97.9. The values of precision, recall and F1-score are above 0.94 in all classes and very little misclassification has occurred. The trends of training and validation show that convergence remains constant with minimal loss in generalization. Such results promote clinical translation.

title: AutocleanEEG - ICVision: Automated ICA Artifact Classification Using Vision-Language AI
authors: Zag ElSayed, Grace Westerkamp, Gavin Gammoh, Yanchen Liu, Peyton Siekierski, Craig Erickson, Ernest Pedapati
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: We introduce EEG Autoclean Vision-Language AI (ICVision)—a first-of-its-kind system that emulates expert-level EEG ICA component classification through AI-agent vision and natural language reasoning. Unlike conventional classifiers such as ICLabel, which rely on handcrafted features, ICVision directly interprets ICA dashboard visualizations topography, time series, power spectra, and ERP plots, using a multimodal large language model (GPT-4 Vision). This allows the AI to "see and explain" EEG components the way trained neurologists do, making it the first scientific implementation of AI-agent visual cognition in neurophysiology. ICVision classifies each component into one of six canonical categories (brain, eye, heart, muscle, channel noise, and other noise), returning both a confidence score and a humanlike explanation. Evaluated on 3,168 ICA components from 124 EEG datasets, ICVision achieved κ = 0.677 agreement with expert consensus, surpassing MNE-ICLabel, while also preserving clinically relevant brain signals in ambiguous cases. Over 97% of its outputs were rated as interpretable and actionable by expert reviewers. As a core module of the open-source EEG Autoclean platform, ICVision signals a paradigm shift in scientific AI, where models don’t just classify, but see, reason, and communicate. It opens the door to globally scalable, explainable, and reproducible EEG workflows, marking the emergence of AI agents capable of expert-level visual decision-making in brain science and beyond.

title: Future-Proofing Identity Security for Agentic AI Systems: Design, Implementation, and Evaluation of an Identity Fabric
authors: Karthik Pappu, Badal Bhushan, Nilesh Jaiswal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Agentic AI systems composed of dynamically instantiated, tool-enabled agents operating across trust boundaries introduce identity failures that exceed the assumptions of existing identity and access management frameworks. This paper identifies five identity gaps in agentic AI systems and introduces six composable security primitives that address ephemeral identity, delegation integrity, capability-level control, cross-domain trust, behavioral assurance, and content provenance: Ephemeral Attested Agent Identity (EAAI), Intent-Bound Delegation Tokens (IBDT), Identity-Constrained Capability Sandboxes, Decentralized Agent Identity Registries (DAIR), Continuous Behavioral Attestation, and Content Provenance Binding. We implement the architecture on Kubernetes using SPIFFE/SPIRE workload identity and mutual TLS for all inter-service communication. Across 33 mTLS test cases (n = 100 per operation), the system achieved 100% delegation fidelity (20/20 narrowing tests), sub-25-ms mean latency for credential issuance and delegation, and 6/6 abuse-resistance rejections. In an end-to-end pipeline with Claude Sonnet, total identity overhead was 203ms, representing 2.6% of inference latency.

title: Advanced Multi-Layer Security Framework: Integrating AES and LSB for Protection of Sensitive Information
authors: Muhammad Sajid, Kaleem Razzaq Malik, Ali Haider Khan, Jianqiang Li, Muhammad Nabeel Asghar
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: In a progressively technological era, disseminating information via digital means has become prevalent. Image encryption is an important part of modern data security that protects sensitive data’s privacy, integrity, and confidentiality. This study proposes a novel and efficient framework that integrates cryptography and steganography techniques to capitalize on the benefits of these technologies regarding security and practicality, effectively addressing the complexities of a dynamic network environment while adhering to stringent information transmission security standards. In the initial phase, this research used symmetric encryption AES-128 along with Cipher Block Chaining (CBC-IV) and Initialization Vector to transform plain text into cipher-text, where a secret key is generated using a pseudorandom number and SHA256, establishing a foundational layer of protection. This study employed an enhanced LSB, utilizing LSB matching and replacement to provide robust steganography. The ciphertext is subsequently embedded into a specific position of a 2D image, which preserves image quality and facilitates accurate data extraction to enhance message security. The results showed that the suggested model performed well in terms of PSNR of 72.31 dB, SSIM of 1.0, correlation coefficient of 0.99, BER of 0.07, histogram MSE, and RMSE of 0.009. This study attained exceptional NPCR and UACI scores of 99.65 and 33.64, respectively, compared to other prevailing approaches.

title: Lightweight Transfer Learning-based SqueezeNet Framework for Multi-Stage Alzheimer’s Disease Classification on MRI
authors: Anu Joy, M Brindha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Diagnosing Alzheimer's disease (AD), a degenerative illness that significantly impairs memory and cognitive abilities, is extremely difficult for clinicians. The cutting-edge developments in deep learning (DL) have established substantial promise for enhancing computerized medical image analysis's precision and effectiveness. This work proposes a transfer learning method for classifying MRI images into four stages of AD utilizing the DL architecture SqueezeNet. In order to improve image quality and lower noise in MRI scans, the framework starts with a preprocessing step that applies a bilateral filter. The pretrained SqueezeNet model is then fed the processed images and adjusted to extract pertinent features and perform effective classification. Although SqueezeNet is lightweight, research findings show that the suggested transfer learning model maintains low computing complexity while achieving dependable classification accuracy. The results demonstrate SqueezeNet's efficacy in creating an automated, precise, and effective AD classification system that promotes early diagnosis and better clinical decision-making.

title: Transforming Environmental Literacy with AI: The AIEC Model for University–Industry Sustainability Alignment
authors: Constantine Andoniou
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Environmental literacy serves as an essential base for people to handle climate uncertainty, sustainability transitions and environmental data usage in various industries. The current environmental education methods fail to deliver sufficient analytical capabilities, real-time data interpretation and precise interpretation needed for modern ecological problems. Artificial intelligence technology enables the improvement of environmental literacy through its ability to extract valuable information from extensive environmental datasets, create interactive models, customized learning routes and enhanced interpretation methods, for educational and industrial environments. The current research develops the AI-Enabled Environmental Cognition (AIEC) model which explains AI-based environmental literacy development in higher education and demonstrates its value for university-industry sustainability partnerships. The AIEC model uses AI to create an interactive learning process which includes four stages: environmental data interpretation, simulation-based learning, personalized ecological education and cross-industry knowledge sharing. The research shows how AI technology enables universities and industry partners to share a common analytical framework which helps them understand climate data, create sustainability solutions and make decisions together. Furthermore, it discusses how AI applications in climate-tech development, environmental dashboards, explainable AI systems and sustainability analytics play an important role in creating opportunities for sharing transparent environmental knowledge. The paper concludes with recommendations about AI technology applications for environmental education which help achieve SDG targets and build stronger university-industry sustainability partnerships.

title: A Three-Stage Gate Driving DC–DC Converter With Ringing Suppression for Automotive Applications
authors: Hang Li, XingBing Chen, Kai Xu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: To address the stringent demands for high efficiency and low electromagnetic interference (EMI) in automotive DC-DC converters, this work proposes a three-stage gate driver(TSGD) architecture with ringing suppression capability. The circuit can suppress voltage ringing effectively by reducing the voltage and current slew rates during power transistor turn-on. Fabricated by a 0.18μm Bipolar-CMOS-DMOS process, this converter achieves 17.25dBμV EMI noise reduction at low frequencies and 21.11dBμV at high frequencies, while suppressing 60.2% of switching ringing. Meanwhile, efficiency exceeds 70% at input voltages of 12V and 48V.

title: A Unified Semantic Alignment Framework for Robust Text Classification
authors: Vinitha V, Jobin Thomas, Diana George, Pavithra A C, Vidyashree K P, Yathiraj G R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Existing methods to achieve robust text classification through fixed calibration and adversarial training have the limitation of failing to handle input uncertainty dynamically and offering no formal guarantees of robustness. In this work, we develop the framework of Adaptive Certified Semantic Alignment (ACSA), combining adaptive confidence calibration, contrastive semantic alignment, prototype-based regularization and certified robustness into a single multi-objective framework. In particular, we utilize input-adaptive temperature scaling to tackle overconfidence on adversarial examples and out-of-distribution inputs. The method aligns clean and adversarial inputs semantically through contrastive alignment, and simultaneously obtains certified robustness bounds over bounded semantic perturbation via randomized embedding smoothing. We show that the proposed ACSA lifts the accuracy, calibration and adversarial robustness over existing methods and is applicable to safety-critical NLP tasks.

title: Anomaly Detection for Weigh-in-Motion Sensor Health Monitoring
authors: Manuel Dömer, Biruk Hailesilassie, Ipsita Panda
publish: 2026 IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS)
abstract: Piezoelectric Weigh-In-Motion (WIM) sensors measure vehicle weight at traffic speed, but their signals are affected by transient disturbances and gradual drifts caused by environmental factors. We address this challenge using a Variational Autoencoder with Temporal Convolutional Network combined with Exponentially Weighted Moving Average control charts for anomaly detection in axle load time series. Evaluated on real data from operational WIM installations augmented with synthetic anomalies, the pipeline achieves an F1-score of 0.849 with 90 % recall, demonstrating reliable detection of both transient disturbances and gradual drift patterns.

title: Knowledge Graphs and Analysis for Research Communities
authors: G.L.K. Abhiram Reddy, Abhay Margabhandhu, Gyanant Nigam Nayak, Apurva Chaudhari, Suruchi Sabherwal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: The explosive growth of academic publications has made it very difficult for researchers and institutions to identify meaningful collaboration opportunities and understand the structural dynamics of research communities. This paper presents a comprehensive knowledge graph (KG)-based framework for modeling, analyzing, and predicting research collaboration networks using bibliographic metadata from a single institution’s Scopus publication corpus. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively surfaces non-obvious collaboration opportunities and maps the topology of institutional research activity. This work contributes a reproducible, graph-native pipeline that unifies community detection, similarity-based link prediction, and interactive Cypher-based querying for academic network intelligence.

title: SWARMS - Smart Wrist-based Assembly Recognition and Monitoring System for Small Parts
authors: Salsabil Abdelhamid Soliman, Vishnu Kakaraparthi, Troy McDaniel, Shenghan Guo
abstract: This paper introduces SWARMS - Smart Wrist-based Assembly Recognition and Monitoring System for Small Parts, a wrist-mounted vision system based on PERACTIV [1], originally developed for activity monitoring in daily living contexts. Designed for small-parts object detection in precision manufacturing environments, SWARMS employs a wrist-mounted camera to capture fine-grained hand-object interactions, enabling precise tracking of manipulation tasks. We also present a novel dataset recorded from this unique perspective. Experimental results demonstrate promising detection performance, laying the groundwork for future extensions to assembly-level tasks and procedural automation in industrial settings.Specifically, we trained a YOLOv8-based object detector on this dataset using transfer learning and a five-fold cross-validation approach. Our best-performing model achieved an mAP@0.5 of 0.825, establishing a strong initial benchmark for wrist-mounted detection. These findings highlight the potential of wrist-aligned vision systems to enhance AI-driven automation in industrial contexts. Future work will focus on integrating sequential activity recognition and real-time operator feedback to further automate and optimize manufacturing workflows.

title: A TinyML-Based Out-of-Distribution Security Monitor for Energy-Constrained Industrial and Medical IoT Systems
authors: Viswanathan Ranganathan, Arun Kumar Elengovan, Venkat Nutalapati, Deepak Kole, Milan Parikh, Nandagopal Seshagiri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The sudden increase in industrial and medical Internet of Things (IoT) systems deployment has created serious cybersecurity and reliability problems under tight energy and resource constraints, where unknown attacks and device failures are to be detected at the edge itself. In this work, a TinyML-based out-of-distribution (OOD) security monitoring framework is introduced for energy-constrained IoT devices, where normal operational behavior is learned in an unsupervised way and deviation is detected without the need for previous attack signatures. A lightweight autoencoder in conjunction with a confidence-weighted OOD scoring mechanism and persistence-based decision logic is used to achieve robust detection without continuous high-rate sensing. The proposed approach achieves a good performance in the detection, with a ROC-AUC of 0.9257 and a PR-AUC of 0.8913 for OOD detection, with no false escalation in normal operation. Deployment-time evaluation shows timely detection with an average latency of around 41.5 windows and 61.65% system-level energy reduction as compared to always-on monitoring. The whole framework is implemented with an INT8-quantized TinyML model with only 2.98 KB memory footprint, that can be used for practical on-device deployment. By jointly integrating TinyML, confidence-aware OOD detection and adaptive energy-aware security actions, this work addresses one critical gap in sustainable and reliable protection of industrial and medical cyber-physical IoT systems.

title: A Hybrid Feature Fusion Framework using CNN and EfficientNet-B2 for Brain Tumor classification
authors: Debasish Padhy, Pradeep Kumar Jena, Charulata Palai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Brain tumour (BT) detection based on Magnetic Resonance Image (MRI) is challenging for clinical decision making and improving treatment planning. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have shown strong performance in this application field due to their ability to learn spatially localized feature representations. However, the limited capacity of CNNs to capture long-range contextual dependencies often hinders comprehensive understanding of tumor structures. To address the identified gap, a hybrid model that fuses the features of EfficientNet-B2 with CNN is introduced. The proposed architecture integrates a pre-trained EfficientNet-B2 backbone for extracting high-level semantic features, while a parallel CNN branch focuses on learning low-level spatial details from MRI scans. The features from both branches are fused to improve the capability of the network. The approach is assessed using an open access MRI dataset encompassing 4 distinct categories. The model attains an overall accuracy of approximately 99%, outperforming several CNN-based baselines. Different Quantitative evaluation metrics confirms the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed model. Additionally Grad-CAM visualizations emphasize the salient tumor regions influencing the classifier’s predictions.

title: Optimizing Current Setting to Reduce Inter-Void Defects in Damascene Copper Interconnects
authors: Kristoff Wang, Lily Xu, Xiangbin Ren, Tu Zhang, Henry Wang, Jun Chen, Yunpeng Yin
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Copper electroplating, as the main production process for copper interconnections, is widely used in the chip manufacturing industry. Void defect is one of the most common defects in the copper electroplating process. Severe void defects can increase the resistance of copper wires, power loss, and even cause the wires to break under the action of Cu electron migration, leading to functional failure of the chip [1] [2].In this work, we introduced copper electroplating process and listed almost kinds of inter-void defect results during gap fill. The mechanisms of these inter-void defects were investigated based on the various test results. Optimized current of plating parameters was presented finally, and the defect level was reduced significantly.

title: Detecting Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary Tumors, and Normal Brain Tissues based on YOLOV8 and YOLOV11 Deep Learning Models
authors: Ahmed M. Taha, Salah A. Aly
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Accurate and quick diagnosis of normal brain tissue Glioma, Meningioma, and Pituitary Tumors is crucial for optimal treatment planning and improved medical results. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is widely used as a non-invasive diagnostic tool for detecting brain abnormalities, including tumors. However, manual interpretation of MRI scans is often time-consuming and exposes to human errors. This paper proposes an advanced AI-driven technique to detect glioma, meningioma, and pituitary brain tumors using YOLOV8 and YOLOV11 deep learning models. Utilizing a transfer learning-based fine-tuning approach, we integrate cutting-edge deep learning techniques with medical imaging to classify brain tumors into four categories: No-Tumor, Glioma, Meningioma, and Pituitary Tumors. The study utilizes the publicly accessible CE-MRI Figshare dataset and involves fine-tuning pre-trained models YOLOV8 and YOLOV11 of 99.49% and 99.56% accuracies; and customized CNN accuracy of 96.98%.

title: Sign Language Recognition with LSTM and Skeleton Image Merging
authors: Mikhail V. Khnyunin, Mikhail G. Grif, Maxim A. Bakaev
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Machine vision techniques are increasingly used to facilitate communication for the deaf people and make them more socially involved. In this paper, we describe the development of a Russian dynamic sign language translation system based on a hybrid approach combining two solutions. The first is the long short-term memory (LSTM) network with a component approach that determines the configurations of a signer's hands and their spatial position. The second is the usage of a convolutional neural network (CNN) to analyze the images obtained by merging the successive frames with a given pixel lifetime. The output of the two approaches is combined using a custom dictionary of subclasses based on our dedicated dataset. Our solution is based on the MediaPipe framework, which allows obtaining three-dimensional coordinates of key points of hands and the human body. This can be done using a regular camera, so the proposed solution can be used ubiquitously. We were able to achieve 88. 2% accuracy with a recognition time of one fragment of 50 frames in 0.57 seconds, which can allow real-time sign language recognition.

title: Single-Stage Solid-State Transformer Based on Cascaded H-Bridge Architecture with Integrated AC–DC Conversion for Traction Application
authors: Adnan Khan, Soumya Shubhra Nag, Bhim Singh
abstract: This paper proposes a novel SST architecture for railway traction applications based on a cascaded H-bridge topology employing a single-stage power conversion approach in each module. In each module, the active front-end ac–dc conversion and the isolated dc–dc conversion stages are integrated into a unified structure, significantly reducing the overall component count and system complexity. Multiple converter modules are connected in an input-series output-parallel (ISOP) configuration, forming a multilevel structure capable of withstanding high input voltages while delivering high output currents. A combined control strategy employing phase-shifted sinusoidal pulse-width modulation (PSSPWM) and phase-shift control is implemented to achieve unity power factor (UPF) operation at the input, regulate the output voltage, and enable controlled power transfer. The paper presents a comprehensive theoretical analysis of the proposed module, including the derivation of key governing equations and operating principles. Furthermore, the proposed topology inherently enables soft-switching operation, achieving zero-voltage switching (ZVS) for all active devices, which is analyzed in detail. The performance of the system is validated through simulation studies at a 1.2 MW power level using MATLAB/Simulink. To further validate the concept, a three module experimental prototype is developed and tested up to 10 kW, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed topology.

title: Carbon-Aware SLO Enforcement for Cloud-Native Microservices Using Adaptive Reliability Policies
authors: Jagadish Almaipeta, Subhash Madineni, Usha Rani Sheri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Cloud-native microservices architectures have transformed how modern enterprises build and operate distributed systems. However, the rapid expansion of elastic cloud workloads has significantly increased energy consumption and associated carbon emissions. Traditional Service Level Objective (SLO) enforcement mechanisms focus primarily on availability and latency guarantees without considering environmental impact. This paper introduces a carbon-aware SLO enforcement framework that dynamically adjusts reliability policies based on real-time carbon intensity signals and workload criticality. By integrating carbon telemetry with adaptive reliability controllers, the proposed system balances performance, resilience, and sustainability objectives. The framework enables enterprises to reduce carbon footprint while preserving business-critical reliability guarantees. Experimental validation in a Kubernetes-based microservices environment demonstrates measurable reductions in energy consumption without violating SLO constraints. The results highlight the feasibility of embedding sustainability directly into reliability engineering practices for next-generation cloud-native platforms.

title: A Simultaneous Topology Optimization Method of Magnet and Iron-core for Interior Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors Based on Rectangular Shape Correction
authors: Yuju Sasabuchi, Takahiro Sato, Shinya Watanabe, Kei Nishihara, Kazuhiro Izui
abstract: This paper presents a topology optimization method for interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSMs), in which rectangular magnet arrangements and iron-core shapes are simultaneously optimized. In the present method, an image-processing-based rectangular correction scheme is integrated into the evolutionary topology optimization framework. This enables high-design-freedom exploration of rotor structures while automatically determining the magnetization directions of the rectangular magnets. The present method is applied to rotor design optimization of an IPMSM. In the symmetric design problem, the electromagnetic average torque is improved by approximately 5% compared with the baseline model without prior information on magnet layouts and magnetization directions. Furthermore, in the asymmetric design problem, various asymmetric magnet arrangements are discovered, and the average torque is improved by approximately 10%. Torque analysis based on the dq formulation considering cross-magnetization reveals that the asymmetric structures exploit torque components induced by cross-magnetization, which contributes to the torque improvement.

title: BioSleepNet: Bio-Inspired Sleep Staging via Context Multimodal Fusion and Dynamic Temporal Memory
authors: Zhi Liu, Yunkai Gao, Yu Wu, Kangjia Tan, Guangdeng Li
abstract: Existing sleep staging research faces several challenges, including poor multimodal feature fusion, insufficient long-term dependency modeling, and weak adaptability to clinical staging logic. To address these issues, this study proposes an end-to-end multimodal sleep staging framework named BioSleepNet by leveraging the complementary characteristics of electroen-cephalogram (EEG) and electrooculogram (EOG) signals. Detailly, BioSleepNet incorporates three key modules. Multimodal feature fusion module performs differentiated feature extraction on EEG and EOG after Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to raw signals, then fuses extracted feature with adaptive weight, to achieve efficient cross-modal feature collaboration. Contextual Temporal Feature module uses ModernTCN modeling contextual temporal feature to capture dynamic characteristics of sleep. And Momentum Memory module models long-term dependencies in temporal signals using a feature memory bank with attention-driven mechanisms, effectively capturing cross-cycle sleep continuity features.Considering the biological characteristics of difficult-to-model sleep transition rules and data imbalance across stages, we introduce a novel hybrid loss function named BioSleepLoss. BioSleepLoss significantly enhances the model’s adaptability to complex staging scenarios through learnable stage importance weights, dynamic difficulty-aware parameters, and adaptive transition probabilities. Experiments on the Sleep-EDF-20/78 datasets demonstrate overall accuracy of 89.3% and 87.1% respectively, with a Kappa coefficient of 0.85 and stage sensitivity standard deviation below 1.5%, verifying the robustness and clinical consistency of the proposed method. Ablation studies and feature visualization validate the effectiveness of multimodal feature fusion, contextual temporal feature modeling, momentum memory module, and physiological constraint collaborative optimization, which jointly provide an efficient and clinically cognizant solution for automated sleep staging.

title: BadBone: Backdoor Attacks Against Backbone Models in Visual Prompt Learning
authors: Ziqing Yang, Rui Wen, Xinlei He, Yun Shen, Michael Backes, Yang Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Prompt learning is a new machine learning paradigm that has attracted ample attention due to its simplicity and proven efficacy. Despite its growing adoption, the security vulnerabilities associated with this paradigm remain underexplored. In this work, we take the first step to propose BadBone, a stealthy and adaptive backdoor attack against prompt learning using bi-level optimization. Instead of backdooring the prompt learning process, we aim to compromise a backbone model such that only target downstream tasks employing prompt learning inherit the backdoor vulnerability. Extensive experiments on three different models and three datasets from various domains show that our targeted/untargeted backdoored models achieve high attack performance while maintaining utility on both pretraining and downstream tasks. Moreover, we evaluate our approach against six state-of-the-art model-level defenses, including Neural Cleanse, ABS, MNTD, NAD, CLP, and D-BR. The results demonstrate that these defenses are largely ineffective against our backdoored models and thus leave the effective defense as an important direction for future work. Our code is available at https://github.com/TrustAIRLab/BadBone.

title: SDS 2026 Best Paper Awards
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS)

title: A Novel Method with Encoder-Decoder for Cross-Sensor Adaptation in Surface Shape Sensing with Sparse Strain Sensors
authors: Shuo Wang, Heng Luo, Dian Jin, Xiaoming Tao
abstract: Performance variations in sensor arrays, caused by intrinsic differences or installation conditions, can lead to inconsistent results during shape sensing. To obtain accurate results, a large amount of data is usually required, and a separate model must be retrained for each sensor array, thereby increasing the cost and time of data acquisition, transmission, and computation. To address this issue, this work proposes an encoder-decoder architecture for surface shape sensing based on sparse strain sensors and further incorporates meta-learning and few-shot adaptation strategies to enable adaptation across different groups of sensor arrays. Experimental results demonstrate that, after the cross-sensor adaptation, a newly deployed sensor array achieves a sensing error of approximately 4.0 mm relying on less than 5.0% newly labeled data and requiring an adaptation time of under 1 s, which represents a substantial improvement from 23.0 mm error without adaptation and 20-minute data collection time required to train a new model. Moreover, the number of points with errors below 5.0 mm increased by more than 65.0%. These results indicate that the proposed method can substantially reduce the cost and training burden of surface shape sensing, and it has broad potential applications in soft robotics and wearable devices.

title: Robust SIC Design in Downlink NOMA Under User-Dependent CSI Uncertainty
authors: Zainab Abdul Hassan, Asaad S. Daghal, Wafaa M. R. Shakir
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Successive interference cancellation (SIC) in downlink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) is highly sensitive to channel state information (CSI) mismatch. However, many existing studies still assume identical uncertainty across users, which can obscure the hierarchical nature of SIC error propagation. This paper investigates a user-dependent CSI uncertainty model for downlink NOMA and combines it with inverse-gain-based power allocation and adaptive SIC ordering. In the considered three-user Rayleigh-fading downlink scenario, weaker ordered users are assigned larger uncertainty levels to reflect more severe estimation distortion, while stronger users retain smaller residual interference factors. Numerical results show that, compared with a baseline dynamic NOMA scheme based on uniform CSI uncertainty, the proposed framework achieves higher robustness under mismatch. At a transmit power of 30 dBm and maximum CSI uncertainty level (ρ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> = 0.05), the sum rate increases from 4.70 to 9.78 bps/Hz, while the outage probability decreases from 0.1419 to 0.0203. These results indicate that heterogeneous CSI uncertainty modeling can improve SIC reliability and spectral efficiency under imperfect CSI, although the gains depend on the specific uncertainty profile and power allocation design.

title: Deep Learning-Based Prediction and Classification of Microplastics in Water Samples
authors: Abirami G, Triveni K, Gaddam Hemanth Reddy, Gadi Manohar Nadiu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Microplastics have become a major environmental and public health issue due to the large amounts of polyethylene (PA) particles in the drinking water supply. This project will develop an automated detection and classification method for polyethylene microplastics in drinking water using deep learning. A data set provided by the California State Water Resources Control Board contains a combined set of high-resolution microscopic images and structured metadata about particles (e.g., particle size, color attributes, and geospatial sampling coordinates). A convolutional neural network (CNN) model will be developed using hierarchical feature extraction to classify PE microplastics compared to traditional machine learning approaches such as random forests (RF) and support vector machines (SVM). The results of the experimental evaluation demonstrate that the CNN model outperformed both traditional machine learning approaches with an accuracy (92%), precision (93%), recall (91%), F1-score (0.92) and area under the curve (0.95). Geospatial analysis results identify contamination hotspots which can be used by water managers for environmental monitoring and industry decisions. The proposed automated method provides a scalable, fast, low-cost means to detect microplastics on a large scale and provide real-time assessments of drinking water quality.

title: AOTSS: Acoustic-optical Communication based Multi-AUV Collaborative Target Search Scheme via Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Xiang Li, Peijun Dong, Hang Tao, Siyao He, Hanjiang Luo, Jiehan Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: In complex underwater environments, multiple autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) typically rely on under-water acoustic communication when performing collaborative target search tasks. However, traditional underwater acoustic technology has communication constraints (e.g., high latency and low bandwidth), which leads to poor information sharing and degrade the performance of multi-AUV collaboration target search missions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a multi-AUV collaborative acoustic-optical communication based target search scheme (AOTSS), which consists of two main components: a particle filter-based path planning algorithm (PFPPA) and a multi-agent reinforcement learning-based multi-AUV Collaborative Search Algorithm (MASA). In PFPPA, to implement efficient multimodal communication among AUVs, we design a navigation algorithm based on particle filter method and deep reinforcement learning. This approach maximizes optical communication to enhance information sharing among AUVs. Furthermore, In MASA we leverage multimodal communication to enhance information sharing among AUVs to obtain precise probability maps, and incorporate pheromones into these maps to guide AUVs performing efficient cooperate search via the Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) approach. Through extensive simulations, the results demonstrate that the proposed scheme significantly enhances the multi-AUV collaboration target search efficiency.

title: Using Student Projects to Compare Model-Based Systems Engineering and Human-Centered Design
authors: Luke Schroeder, Adam Hanna, Diya Amat, Mohamed Elhawabri
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: This paper compares two approaches of systems design: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) and an iterative, human-centered design (HCD) approach. They are compared in a case study where both MBSE and HCD are applied to develop a makerspace information system. MBSE has become a common industry tool for architectural design of large-scale, complex, safety-critical systems that involve hardware/software integration and have long life cycles. It relies on rigorous upfront requirements and system modeling using SysML. In contrast, HCD is prototype-driven and requires continuous iterations given client feedback, incorporating users at every stage of development. This paper will discuss the team’s experience with both approaches in a case study centered around highlighting the perceived differences, strengths, and limitations of each method. Research outcomes stemmed from case study observations, comparison to existing research, and result validation via interviews with subject experts. The team's findings agree with observed industry trends of MBSE being better suited to large, complex systems with strict safety requirements, long development timelines, and lower error tolerance. On the contrary, they perceived HCD to be better suited to smaller projects with accessible users and projects with uncertainty in requirements.

title: Portable Ex Vivo Perfusion System for the Immediate Hemodynamic Rescue and Characterization of Ischemic Slaughterhouse Organs
authors: Shriraam KrishnaKumar, M Shankar, Priyanka N Prem, Mohanasankar Sivaprakasam, Jayaraj Joseph, P. M. Nabeel
abstract: Slaughterhouse derived organs represent a feasible, ethically compliant alternative to laboratory animals, offering an abundant source of tissue for biomedical research. Yet, this vast resource is often discarded due to rapid Warm Ischemia Time (WIT) and microvascular thrombosis challenges exacerbated by the lack of portable perfusion systems capable of immediate clot clearance and ischemic mitigation, which renders viable tissues unusable within minutes. This paper presents a portable, sensor integrated ex vivo perfusion system explicitly engineered to reclaim these organs at the point of harvest. Designed to operationalize this underutilized biological resource, the system employs a custom dual loop architecture supporting both hypothermic preservation and normothermic physiological simulation. Industrial grade sensors enable real time monitoring of perfusion pressure, flow rate, and temperature, providing the “operator in the loop” control necessary to mechanically clear vascular clots and prevent barotrauma at the site of collection. We demonstrate the system’s capacity to convert biological waste into standardized, high fidelity samples.
