This solution transforms slaughterhouse derived organs into a reliable, scalable replacement for live animal organ experimentation.

title: Preface
authors: N/A
abstract: Welcome to the 22th International Conference on Intelligent Environments (IE'26)!

title: Experimental Validation of Capacitive Touch Sliders: Recommendations for Human Machine Interface Design
authors: Adisha Ahmed, Bivushi Timilsina, Karigya Joshi, Nafia Farzana, Bryan Kuhr
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Human machine interface (HMI) devices commonly use capacitive touch sliders for user interaction, where accurate and stable position detection is critical. These systems are susceptible to position jitter, defined as variation in the reported touch location under nominally constant input conditions. While prior work addresses sensor design and noise mitigation, there is limited emphasis on experimentally repeatable methods for evaluating slider performance under controlled conditions. This study presents a repeatable experimental framework for characterizing capacitive touch slider response and supporting the evaluation of position jitter. A custom mechanical test rig was developed to control probe alignment, motion consistency, and board positioning, reducing operator-induced variability. Sensor data, including instantaneous delta values and centroid positions, were collected using a UART-to-USB interface and analyzed across repeated probe sweeps. Results demonstrate consistent delta–centroid response patterns across trials, indicating that the experimental setup enables reliable and repeatable measurement of sensor behavior. Variations observed in centroid response across repeated sweeps highlight the presence of position variability and establish a foundation for future centroid-based jitter quantification. The methodology provides a practical approach for controlled evaluation of capacitive slider systems and supports further development of standardized jitter characterization techniques.

title: Forecasting Next-Day Gold Returns under Market Volatility: A Comparative Study of Machine Learning and Hybrid Deep Learning Ensembles
authors: Sohaib Sulman, Maheen Abdul Rehman Jan, Muhammad Atif Saeed, Azka Atiq, Akhtar Jamil, Alaa Ali Hameed
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The non-linear, noisy, and highly volatile nature of the execution of gold makes it difficult to predict the return for the following day. This research evaluates the performance of eight models: Random Forest, Ensemble Machine Learning, Support Vector Regression, XGBoost, Long Short-Term Memory, hybrid CNN-LSTM network, Bidirectional LSTM, and deep learning aggregates combining CNN-LSTM, and GRU framework. Utilizing daily gold market data from 2020 to 2025, we evaluate error-based measures of RMSE and MAE and directional correctness. The tuned SVR model exceed all other models in terms of RMSE and MAE, analyzing a minimal prediction error. On the other hand, the highest directional accuracy of 60.45% has been achieved by a deep learning ensemble that averages CNN-LSTM and GRU predictions. Therefore, it is an excellent model for predicting the movement of tomorrow’s returns. Traditional tree-based models, for instance RF and XGBoost, worked worse than deep learning approaches in heading prediction. In a nutshell, the findings shows the mutual strengths of machine learning and deep learning techniques in financial return forecasting, underlining the fact that while SVR performs best in reducing forecast error, hybrid deep learning ensembles fare better in catching the direction of the market.

title: Estimating Ventilatory Thresholds from Heart Rate Dynamics During Incremental Exercise Using Supervised Learning
authors: Gayathri G Sri, Hafsa Fathima, Sp Preejith, Mohanasankar Sivaprakasam
abstract: Ventilatory thresholds (VT1 and VT2) represent key metabolic transition points used for individualised exercise prescription and performance assessment. While cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) with respiratory gas analysis remains the reference standard for threshold identification, its dependence on specialised equipment and laboratory settings limits its routine use. This study investigates the feasibility of estimating VT1 and VT2 from heart rate (HR) dynamics recorded during incremental treadmill exercise.A cohort of fifty-five healthy adults (43 males, 12 females; 18-50 years) performed a graded Balke treadmill protocol with breath-by-breath gas exchange measurement. Continuous HR signals, temporally aligned with CPET data, were analysed using a supervised learning framework, with gas-exchange derived VT1 and VT2 serving as physiological reference points. HRderived features capturing statistical, temporal, and dynamic characteristics of cardiovascular (CV) response were used to formulate VT1 and VT2 estimation as independent detection problems.Model performance was evaluated using 5-fold participantlevel cross-validation. Ensemble-based classifiers demonstrated superior performance compared to baseline models. VT2 estimation achieved higher discrimination (AUC $=0.953 \pm 0.019$) than VT1 ($\mathrm{AUC}=0.899 \pm 0.020$), indicating more distinct CV signatures at higher exercise intensities. Ablation studies support that HR-derived features constitute the primary predictive signal. These results suggest that VT1 and VT2 can be inferred from HR dynamics during graded exercise without using respiratory gas measurements as model inputs. The findings provide a methodological basis for further investigation of HR-based VT estimation in controlled and wearable-supported exercise settings.

title: A Deep Learning–Based Transformer Framework for Binary Mental Health Sentiment Analysis to Support Government Policy Decision-Making
authors: Subhasis Mohapatra, Sudhir Kumar Mohapatra, Sweta Samantaray
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Mental health monitoring is getting more vital in Public Administration. Comprehensive and evidence-based analytical tools are required to track mental health trends so that public policies can be effectively formulated. High operating cost, less coverage, and delayed feedback are the major shortcomings of traditional survey methods. This current study addresses these problems by conducting an in-depth mental health diagnosis using people's specified textual data. The proposed framework employs a deep learning-based transformer model for analyzing the polarity of the text. It performs a binary classification method to ascertain whether the sentiment is positive or negative. The proposed approach uses the RoBERTa-large model to efficiently interpret the linguistic patterns and emotions behind the text. To evaluate the performance of the suggested structure, multiple evaluation metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrix, and ROC-AUC, are used. After an in-depth analysis and fine-tuning of the RoBERTa-large model’s parameters, the proposed model achieved 97.92% accuracy on the test set. It also attained an F1-score of 98% and an AUC-ROC of 99.72%. These outcomes emphasize the efficacy of the current transformer-based framework, which can serve as a reliable decision-making tool for tracking the mental health trends across the entire population. It could play a vital role in government policy decision-making.

title: Forecasting Crowd Density and Directional Flow from Frame Sequences Using Deep Learning
authors: Batool R. Alhajji, Maha M. Aljaafari, Lateefa A. Alobaid, Aisha A. Almajed, Rawabi Abdulaziz Alsedais
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper presents a deep learning-based crowd analytics system for monitoring and forecasting crowd density and directional flow from video frame sequences. The proposed framework is designed to assist organizers of large-scale events in the early detection of critical crowd conditions, enabling timely and informed decision-making. The system analyzes sequential frames from the UCSD Pedestrian Dataset by integrating CNN-based detection, DeepSORT tracking with Kalman filtering, and LSTM-based temporal modeling. It generates both scalar density estimates and interpretable directional movement patterns. The system processes frame sequences in a near real-time manner, enabling continuous monitoring and predictive analysis. Experimental evaluation demonstrates strong predictive performance, achieving an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 97% for density estimation and 88% for directional flow prediction. The proposed approach contributes to proactive crowd management by combining forecasting capabilities with an interactive dashboard that transforms raw predictions into actionable insights. Limitations related to dataset density and generalization to high-density environments are also discussed.

title: Genome-wide Repetitive DNA Profile Workflow for Evolutionary and Disease Insight using ML Algorithms
authors: S Sreenivasa Charyulu, Banothu Ramji, O Nishwanth Patel, K Revathi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: For decades, the repetitive elements (REs) that constitute the majority of the human genome were dismissed as "junk". However, as sufficient advances in this field have been made, we now understand that these elements are critical to gene regulation, evolution, and human diseases. Analyzing them still poses significant computational challenges. In this paper, we develop and validate a machine learning-based workflow to profile this complex repetitive landscape. Our approach computationally transforms intricate publicly available genomic annotations from the UCSC RepeatMasker database into a high-dimensional feature set using a custom Python pipeline. This pipeline focuses on generating profiles based on 50kb genomic partitions, which we term "windows," and subsequently analyzes them using machine learning models, specifically Random Forest and PCA. We demonstrate that our workflow can successfully generate evolutionary insights, classifying Human, Mouse, and Chicken based on their repeat "fingerprints" with 94.49% accuracy. Additionally, we provide disease information by predicting known disease-associated loci (GWAS) on human chromosome 22 with 88.24% accuracy. We pinpointed the SINE elements as the most critical predictors. This work shows that machine learning can effectively decode biologically meaningful patterns from the "junk" genome and handle the complex data structures involved, offering a clear path to new evolutionary and medical insights

title: Energy storages in the context of energy security in the event of catastrophic failures
authors: Krzysztof Lowczowski, Damian Burzynski, Zbigniew Nadolny
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This paper presents alternative planning approaches that consider catastrophic failures and the role of energy storage in enhancing operational power system security. Key technical parameters, control strategies, and system restoration functions are discussed.

title: Cooperative Multi-Drone Coverage Path Planning with Graph-Attentive Feature Interaction
authors: Mingsheng Li, Jiaping Xiao, Mir Feroskhan
abstract: Multi-drone coverage path planning (CPP) coordinates multiple drones to systematically and safely explore unknown environments. However, existing CPP strategies often lack cooperation and require ideal communication between drones, which can cause redundancy. To address these challenges, we propose a new CPP method that integrates graph attention networks (GAT) into a centralized training decentralized execution (CTDE) structure. By aggregating information from neighbors, each drone makes a cooperative decision to avoid redundant coverage. The communication graph topology is constructed to adapt to communication limitations. Simulation results demonstrate that our method achieves 4% to 6% higher coverage than baseline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approaches with around 30% to 51% fewer repeat visits. Additional experiments show that partial communication under different communication ranges does not substantially degrade coverage performance, offering a practical and scalable solution for the CPP problem.

title: High-Speed 64-bit Recursive Pipelined Vedic Multiplier Using Carry-Save and Kogge-Stone Adders
authors: Monu Kumar Chouhan, Ajay Singh Raghuvanshi, Ankit Kumar Singh, Shalini Singh, Md. Irfanul Hasan
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Multiplication is the critical bottleneck in DSP, AI, and floating-point pipelines. This paper presents a fully recursive pipelined Vedic multiplier integrating a Carry-Save Adder (CSA) tree for partial-product accumulation and a Kogge-Stone Adder (KSA) for final carry resolution, implemented on a Xilinx Artix-7 XC7A100T FPGA. The primary novelty is a unified single-module architecture supporting unsigned integer, signed integer, and IEEE-754 double-precision (FP64) multiplication without hardware duplication, a capability unreported in prior Vedic or tree-based designs. Deep pipelining at every recursive stage yields a stable critical-path delay of 6.23–6.94 ns across 16-bit to 64-bit operands, with pipeline depths of 6, 12, and 13 clock cycles for 16×16 integer, 64×64 integer, and FP64 respectively. Total on-chip power of 0.109–0.270 W is significantly lower than non-pipelined Booth and Vedic counterparts. Behavioral simulation confirms correct results across all modes, including IEEE-754 special cases (NaN, Infinity, Zero).

title: Triple Active Bridge Converter with High-Frequency Transformer for Grid Integration of Photovoltaic and Storage Systems
authors: Abdullah Almutairi, Mohammed Alharbi
abstract: In multi-port active bridge converters, emphasizing their benefits in integrating solar PV and energy storage systems. These converters offer advantages such as high-power density, improved efficiency through high-frequency operation and zero voltage switching (ZVS), and the elimination of bulky low-frequency transformers. They facilitate efficient power conversion across various input voltages and loads. Additionally, the integration of wide bandgap semiconductor technologies like silicon carbide (SiC) MOSFET in triple active bridge converters enhances efficiency and reduces the size of passive components, making them suitable for compact and efficient power electronic systems in renewable energy applications.

title: Investigating the Impact of Speech Enhancement on Audio Deepfake Detection in Noisy Environments
authors: Angela Anacin, Shruti Kshirsagar, Anderson R. Avila
abstract: Logical Access (LA) attacks, also known as audio deepfake attacks, use Text-to-Speech (TTS) or Voice Conversion (VC) methods to generate spoofed speech data. This can represent a serious threat to Automatic Speaker Verification (ASV) systems, as intruders can use such attacks to bypass voice biometric security. In this study, we investigate the correlation between speech quality and the performance of audio spoofing detection systems (i.e., LA task). For that, the performance of two enhancement algorithms is evaluated based on two perceptual speech quality measures, namely Perceptual Evaluation of Speech Quality (PESQ) and Speech-to-Reverberation Modulation Ratio (SRMR), and in respect to their impact on a state-of-the-art audio spoofing detection system, known as Audio Anti-Spoofing using Integrated Spectro-Temporal Graph Attention Networks (AASIST). We adopted the LA dataset, provided in the ASVspoof 2019 Challenge, and corrupted its test set with different Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) levels, while leaving the training data untouched. Enhancement was applied to attenuate the detrimental effects of noisy speech, and the performances of two models, Speech Enhancement Generative Adversarial Network (SEGAN) and Metric-Optimized Generative Adversarial Network Plus (MetricGAN+), were compared. Although we expect that speech quality will correlate well with speech applications’ performance, it can also have as a side effect on downstream tasks if unwanted artifacts are introduced or relevant information is removed from the speech signal. Our results corroborate with this hypothesis, as we found that the enhancement algorithm leading to the highest speech quality scores, MetricGAN+, provided the lowest Equal Error Rate (EER) on the audio spoofing detection task, whereas the enhancement method with the lowest speech quality scores, SEGAN, led to the lowest EER, thus leading to better performance on the LA task.

title: SUVI: Scalable Unified Vector Intelligence for Efficient Edge Deployment
authors: Nakul Vyas
abstract: Many multimodal language models are implemented as dual-tower systems that combine a language model with a separate visual encoder. This design is effective, but it also adds parameters, runtime complexity, and memory overhead. SUVI (Scalable Unified Vector Intelligence) is an alternative architecture in which images are converted into discrete token IDs and processed in the same autoregressive stream as text.In the current implementation, a frozen Stable Diffusion VAE produces latent features, local latent patches are discretized into a fixed visual vocabulary, and the resulting image tokens are concatenated with text tokens for causal modeling by a decoder-only language model. We present SUVI as an implemented single-stream multimodal design and a practical training recipe for studying discrete early fusion with standard open-source components. In earlier development variants, we observed a sharp early optimization transient when newly introduced visual tokens were insufficiently aligned with the language-model embedding space; the current initialization and patchwise discretization recipe appears to reduce this multimodality-shock behavior. An approximately 7,500-step run on Flickr30k with 4-bit LoRA training on an NVIDIA L4 remained stable and used approximately 9.22 GB of allocated GPU memory at peak during training. Using the saved adapter for single-image generation on the same L4 required 6.15 GB of allocated memory after model load and peaked at 6.45 GB during inference. Together, these results position SUVI as a workable architecture for low-overhead multimodal systems research, while more extensive quality evaluation and ablation remain future work.

title: Online Continual Spatio-Temporal Learning for Change-Resilient Blocking Prediction in Elastic Optical Networks
authors: Farzaneh Nourmohammadi, Jaume Comellas, Uzay Kaymak
abstract: Elastic Optical Networks (EONs) provide flexible and spectrally efficient transport, yet their performance is highly sensitive to sudden traffic variations, spectrum fragmentation, physical-layer degradations, and unexpected events such as fiber cuts or equipment failures. These abrupt changes alter the spectral state and blocking behavior in ways that cannot be captured by traditional offline-trained machine learning models, which assume stationary data distributions and therefore become inaccurate once deployed. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a lightweight, change-resilient AI framework for blocking prediction in EONs, combining a spatio–temporal CNN–BiLSTM architecture with a dual offline–online learning strategy. The offline model establishes a baseline representation using Batch Normalization and class-weighted supervised training. An independent online model is then introduced to continually adapt to evolving network conditions using Layer Normalization, class-balanced streaming, gradient clipping, exponential learning-rate decay, and optional spectral perturbation augmentation. Additionally, an exponential moving average (EMA) shadow model ensures stable real-time predictions during adaptation. The proposed framework enables continual learning under non-stationary conditions and is designed to maintain blocking-prediction reliability in the presence of abrupt network changes. This work lays the foundation for future experimental validation and provides a scalable path toward autonomous, resilient, and self-adaptive control mechanisms for next-generation elastic optical networks.

title: Impact of Measurement Uncertainty on Accuracy of Transient State Estimation
authors: Tomáš Šedivý, Patrik Paclík, Jiří Drápela
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: This paper investigates the influence of measurement uncertainty on the accuracy of transient state estimation (TSE) applied to voltage events. A Monte-Carlo approach is used to evaluate the effect of uncertainty in the measurement chain under limited network observability using CIGRE 14-bus benchmark system. The study demonstrates that neglecting measurement uncertainty leads to an optimistic assessment of TSE accuracy and may underestimate estimation errors under transient conditions.

title: Mapping the Landscape of DDoS Attack Detection with Machine Learning in SDN
authors: Arti Gupta, Bineet Kumar Joshi
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) threat is among the most harmful cyber vulnerability, addressing the stability and safety of TCP/IP networks. By jamming systems with bulky traffic from varied origins, these attacks unable services and cause severe monetary loss. Traditional defense techniques often fall short as attackers develop more sophisticated methods. Software-Defined Network (SDN) addresses these difficulties by providing a programmable infrastructure that sets apart the control and data plains, enabling versatile network control. In parallel, Machine Learning (ML) has come up as a powerful approach for disclosing and diminishing DDoS attacks. ML models can intelligently observe traffic patterns to examine irregularity and time dependent vulnerabilities. It was recognized that supervised learning algorithms, like decision tree and deep neural networks, classify congestion of data, while unsupervised approaches like classification and autoencoders detect previously unseen attack patterns. Together, SDN and ML offer a reliable, evolving strategy to counter DDoS attacks effectively. In this paper, we conducted two types of reviews Systematic Literature Review (SLR) and Bibliometric Literature Review (BLR) efficiently analyze the work carried about different researchers in this area. We also suggested that a researcher must done BLR before SLR to get the better understanding of the topics. This study is closely connected to SDG 9 as it works toward making modern network systems more secure and reliable. By strengthening protection against cyber threats, it also supports SDG 16. Additionally, it contributes to SDG 8 by helping create stable and secure digital environments that support business activities and economic growth.

title: CannyEdit: Selective Canny Control and Dual-Prompt Guidance for Training-free Image Editing
authors: Weiyan Xie, Han Gao, Didan Deng, Kaican Li, Yongxiang Huang, April Hua Liu, Nevin L. Zhang
abstract: Recent text-to-image advances enable training-free regional image editing by leveraging generative priors of foundation models. However, existing methods struggle to balance text adherence in edited regions, context fidelity in unedited areas, and seamless integration of edits. We introduce CannyEdit, a novel training-free framework addressing this trilemma through two key innovations. First, Selective Canny Control uses structural guidance from a Canny ControlNet exclusively on unedited regions, preserving original details while enabling precise, text-driven edits. Second, Dual-Prompt Guidance combines a local prompt for specific edits with a global prompt for overall coherence. This synergy achieves controllable local editing for object addition, replacement, and removal, offering a superior trade-off among text adherence, context fidelity, and seamlessness compared to mask-based methods. Moreover, CannyEdit works effectively with rough masks or single-point hints for addition tasks and integrates seamlessly with vision-language models without training. Extensive evaluations highlight CannyEdit’s strong performance in complex instruction-based editing and object addition scenarios.

title: Spatio-temporal Modeling Using a Cost-Effective System for Air Quality Monitoring
authors: Manal Karmoude, Brenton Munhungewarwa, Ryan McKenzie, Jude Kong, Bevan Smith, Vongani Chabalala, Donald Ngobeni, Raghav Chandna, Mukesh Kumar, Redouane Benaini, Bruce Mellado
abstract: Ambient air quality remains a major environmental and public-health concern. While traditional reference-grade monitoring stations provide reliable pollutant measurements, their high installation and maintenance costs limit spatial coverage—particularly in low-resource regions. Moreover, these stations generally do not provide future pollutant concentrations. To address these constraints, the South African Consortium for Air Quality Monitoring (SACAQM) has developed a cost-effective air quality monitoring system that integrates cost-effective sensors, advanced IoT technologies, and predictive modeling capabilities. AI r system provides real-time pollutant data across multiple locations in Gauteng province through a centralized dashboard. After calibration against reference-grade stations, the system demonstrated high reliability, highlighting its potential as a practical alternative to traditional monitoring infrastructure. This study presents the first use of spatio-temporal modeling for 24-hour forecasting using data from a cost-effective air-quality monitoring system deployed in Africa. We applied spatio-temporal deep learning models to pollutant data collected from several sites monitored by AI r system, enabling the extraction of both spatial and temporal patterns relevant for forecasting. The results illustrate the feasibility and promise of combining cost-effective sensing networks with spatio-temporal approaches for air-quality forecasting and early warning.

title: Day-Ahead Heat Demand Prediction Using BiLSTM-Based Hybrid Neural Networks
authors: Jan Ullmann, Jiří Závorka, Jan Horák, Jana Jiřičková, Radek Škoda
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: District heating supplies a major share of thermal energy in many European countries, making accurate day-ahead heat-demand forecasting essential for efficient operation, fuel savings, and decarbonization. Most systems rely on combined heat and power (CHP) plants, where operational strategies depend on heat-demand forecasts and electricity market prices. This study presents an industry-ready neural-network framework for day-ahead heat-demand prediction, complemented by an outline for integrating electricity price forecasts and CHP optimization. We demonstrate its real-world deployment using operational data from the district heating system of Pilsen, Czech Republic. The proposed solution significantly improves city-scale forecast accuracy and can be easily adapted to other district heating networks due to its modular design. By combining neural networks with regression methods and operational expertise, the framework enables practical integration into live control environments, supporting more efficient CHP operation and contributing to ongoing decarbonization efforts in district heating.

title: Mitigating LLM Limitations in Learning Environments through Retrieval-Based Knowledge Integration*
authors: Rita Bezerra, Adelino Gala, Francisco S. Marcondes, Dalila Durães, Paulo Novais
abstract: Advances in large language models (LLMs) have transformed AI-driven education, enabling innovative applications across various domains of teaching and learning.Despite critical opinions and even bans in communities and regions, large language models are here to stay. They can bring great benefits and challenges to education, both from the perspective of students and teachers.Since LLMs were not originally developed for educational contexts, they often lack critical features for learning environments, such as mechanisms to prevent academic misconduct, misinformation, and overconfidence among students.Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) improves LLMs by retrieving relevant information from an external knowledge base and incorporating it into the LLM generation process. By integrating retrieval-based knowledge augmentation, RAG systems can provide explanations to students, adapt to curriculum changes, and reduce the risks of misinformation.

title: Meteorological data and Sky Images meets Neural Models for Photovoltaic Power Forecasting
authors: Inés Montoya-Espinagosa, Antonio Agudo
abstract: Due to the rise in the use of renewable energies as an alternative to traditional ones, and especially solar energy, there is increasing interest in studying how to address photovoltaic forecasting in the face of the challenge of variability in photovoltaic energy production, using different methodologies. This work develops a hybrid approach for short and long-term forecasting based on two studies with the same purpose. A multimodal approach that combines images of the sky and photovoltaic energy history with meteorological data is proposed. The main goal is to improve the accuracy of ramp event prediction, increase the robustness of forecasts in cloudy conditions, and extend capabilities beyond nowcasting, to support more efficient operation of the power grid and better management of solar variability. Deep neural models are used for both nowcasting and forecasting solutions, incorporating individual and multiple meteorological variables, as well as an analytical solar position. The results demonstrate that the inclusion of meteorological data, particularly the surface long-wave, radiation downwards, and the combination of wind and solar position, significantly improves current predictions in both nowcasting and forecasting tasks, especially on cloudy days. This study highlights the importance of integrating diverse data sources to improve the reliability and interpretability of solar energy prediction models.

title: An Exploratory Study on Intervention Behavior of a Driver Under Cognitive Load in a Simulated Autonomous Driving Scenario
authors: Kolja Kühnlenz, Jonas Blattner, Mohammed Dayyene El-Mejbri, Benedikt Hessner, Meret Janssen, Karl Lindner, Constantin Resch, Louisa Schippers, Florian Schmitt, Jana Barbara Schmitt, Max Sommerer, Simon Thau, Julien Zuniga, Barbara Kühnlenz
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: This paper explores whether cognitive load affects intervention behavior of a driver in terms of taking control of the driving situation in autonomous driving. A video of a simulated autonomous driving scenario from a driver's perspective including critical situations is presented to the participants. In parallel, extraneous cognitive load is induced by letting participants mentally solve arithmetic problems as a secondary task. Whenever participants feel that they want to intervene and take control of the driving situation, they are required to press the button of a stopwatch. In contrast to expectations of increased impulsive behavior, results reveal an inverse association of cognitive load and counts of interventions paired with increased trust in the autonomous vehicle, but reduced perceived safety and situation awareness. This behavior may be explained with impaired switching of attentional focus and lack of possibility of internalization of the primary task under depletion of cognitive resources. The observed effects are suggested to be accounted for towards shared autonomy concepts in order to promote the design of safe autonomous vehicles and their integration in society.

title: On the study of the shape of language: A Topological Analysis of Universal Embedding Spaces generated by Large Language Models
authors: Luis Balderas, Miguel Lastra, José M. Benítez
abstract: Encoders have become fundamental to the development of Natural Language Processing tasks, Generative AI and other machine learning domains. However, the incompatibility of their generated embedding representation spaces constitutes a significant challenge for their productive use in real world applications. Methodologies such as vec2vec are highly important in this context, raising questions about the possibility of generating a universal embedding representation space. In this article, persistence homology is employed to study the common representation spaces generated by vec2vec from various text encoders, combining models with identical and different backbones across multiple datasets. Our methodology utilizes topological data analysis tools, such as persistence diagrams and persistence landscapes, along with distance metrics such as the Wasserstein distance or the l<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> norm to objectively evaluate the similarity of the generated representations. Our findings indicate that while the translated space produced by vec2vec is effective in terms of cosine similarity, the translation process is shown to introduce topological artifacts. Besides, a statistically significant correlation was not found between geometric and topological metrics. Furthermore, no statistical evidence was observed to suggest that a common backbone leads to a more robust preservation of topological features. Finally, it was determined that a common representation space with similar topological characteristics is generated by vec2vec across different encoders, although the degree of similarity is shown to be dependent on the specific dataset used for training.

title:
LSBMAR: Latent Schrödinger Bridge for Metal Artifact Reduction in CT Scans
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Luca Zaccagna, Andrea Salfinger, Tiziano Vallisa, Lauro Snidaro
abstract: Computed Tomography (CT) scans are often degraded by metal artifacts arising from implants or prosthetic devices, which manifest as bright and dark streaks that compromise diagnostic interpretability. Current Metal Artifact Reduction (MAR) methods typically treat this task as sonogram-domain inpainting, using the image domain as a guide for enforcing consistency. Recently, Diffusion Models (DMs) have been employed and show improved performance. However, because these models are applied directly in the high-dimensional spaces, they remain computationally demanding, while the well-established baselines struggle to achieve the same results as diffusion models. Thus, to overcome these limitations, in this work, we propose a novel MAR framework based on Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) that reinterprets the task as an image-to-image translation problem in latent space. By leveraging the Schrödinger Bridge formulation, our method learns to map corrupted latent representations to their artifact-free counterparts, conditioned directly on the distribution of the degraded images. This design maintains the expressiveness and yields to a more efficient generation process while preserving anatomical fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive performance compared to traditional MAR baselines, effectively reducing artifacts and enhancing image quality.

title: Range-Level Preloading with Scalable Watch-Time Estimation for Billion-User Streaming Systems
authors: Yucheng Zhang, Zhiwei Fan, Tong Liu, Guanyan Peng, Haodan Zhang, Zhen Wang, Pengjin Xie, Liang Liu, Huadong Ma
abstract: Short-video platforms have grown rapidly by allowing users to browse rich media content through seamless swiping. However, the inherently random nature of swipe behavior creates significant challenges for bandwidth efficiency and playback continuity, often resulting in stalls and unnecessary data transfers. We present OffLoad, a new preloading framework that enhances bandwidth efficiency and playback quality using range-based downloading, which generalizes traditional chunk-based preloading to arbitrary-length segments for finer-grained control. At the core of OffLoad is a two-dimensional watch-time estimation model that jointly captures user preferences and video characteristics. Guided by this estimator, OffLoad introduces a hybrid preloading algorithm that integrates heuristic rules with a learning-based module trained directly on large-scale production data, enabling strong generalization in deployment. Following extensive system-level optimization, OffLoad has been deployed on a commercial short-video platform for more than six months. Our A/B testing results show that OffLoad increases overall user watch-time by 1.1‰, while simultaneously reducing 0.13% rebuffering events and 4.92% of bandwidth consumption.

title: DBHN-Net: Dual-Branch Hybrid Neural Network for Low-Complexity Monaural Speech Enhancement
authors: Cunhang Fan, Enrui Liu, Jing Zhou, Jian Kang, Jie Li, Andong Li, Jian Zhou, Zhao Lv, Xuelong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Although artificial neural network (ANN) based speech enhancement (SE) methods demonstrate excellent performance, the high computational complexity and high energy consumption hinder their deployment in practical front-end processing tasks. Currently, the spiking neural networks (SNNs) have shown potential in reducing power consumption. However, the discrete binary activation and complex spatio-temporal dynamics of SNNs often result in information loss. The current challenge therefore focuses on how to maintain performance and reduce computational complexity. To address this issue, this work pro pose a Dual-Branch Hybrid Neural (DBHN) Network. 1) In terms of network architecture: A dual-branch network integrating ANN and SNN was designed, where the SNN branch reduces power consumption while the ANN branch addresses information loss; The BandSplit and Time-Frequency (TF)-Mamba modules were developed to simultaneously compress energy consumption and enhance model performance; Spiking Feature Extraction Group (SFEG) and Information Transformation Block (ITB) components were implemented with residual connections to mitigate information loss while further refining feature representations. 2) To facilitate inter-branch information fusion: An Interaction module was designed to promote information exchange at various stages of the dual-branch network; A TF-Cross Attention-Fusion module was designed to perform time-frequency domain fusion of dual-branch information while data-adaptively guiding the SNN branch to retain more critical information. Results show that the proposed model maintains superior performance across three public datasets while achieving an average 7.5 fold reduction in computational complexity compared to baseline models.

title: MGA-CLIP: A Multigranularity Attribution Framework for Cross-Modal Explainability in CLIP_supp1-3696329.pdf
authors: Weiping Ding
abstract: The contrastive language–image pretraining (CLIP) model has demonstrated remarkable performance in multimodal tasks, but the interpretability of its similarity-based cross-modal alignment mechanism has attracted considerable attention. However, existing visual explanation methods typically rely on single-gradient information, which is easily affected by noise and activation saturation, resulting in unstable and rough interpretations. To address this issue, we propose a multigranularity attribution framework for CLIP (MGA-CLIP) that provides a systematic analysis of the internal learning behaviors of deep multimodal neural networks. At the coarse-grained level, the method adopts the concept of integrated gradients (IGs) to construct an integration path from a baseline to the input, accumulating path gradients to obtain a stable gradient tensor, which is then used to weight the feature space for a robust global interpretation. At the fine-grained level, the method leverages the model’s cross-modal alignment characteristics and its internal attention dependencies to quantify the semantic relevance between visual patches and text embeddings, thereby capturing localized embedding interactions. Unlike previous works that treat global and local cues independently, our framework establishes an explicit linkage between channel-level global attributions and patch-level semantic reasoning, enabling consistent explanations across different granularity levels. By fusing the coarse- and fine-grained results, MGA-CLIP generates semantically consistent and cross-modally aligned explanations. Moreover, we employ the proposed attribution framework in a text-based adversarial patch experiment, demonstrating its strong capability to reveal the internal reasoning behavior of the CLIP and to provide insightful interpretability References for analyzing multimodal alignment mechanisms. Extensive experiments show that MGA-CLIP significantly outperforms existing methods in attribution stability and semantic focus, effectively enhancing the interpretability of deep multimodal neural networks.
