title: Assessing the Impact of Inter-Electrode Distance on EHG-based Features for Contraction Analysis
authors: Elisa Missori, Alessandra Galli, Elisabetta Peri, Paul Hamelmann, Massimo Mischi
abstract: Electrohysterography (EHG) is a promising non-invasive technique for monitoring uterine activity and represents a potential alternative to intrauterine pressure (IUP) measurements and external tocography (TOCO). However, accurate detection and characterization of uterine contractions strongly depend on the electrode configuration. In particular, the inter-electrode distance (i.e., the distance between consecutive electrodes) can influence the ability of EHG-derived features to describe the contraction pattern reliably. This study proposes a methodological framework to identify the inter-electrode distance that enables the extraction of the contractile pattern based on EHG features with the highest similarity to TOCO waveforms. Bipolar EHG signals were computed using three different vertical inter-electrode distances ($\mathbf{1 7. 5 ~ m m}, \mathbf{3 5 ~ m m}, \mathbf{5 2. 5 ~ m m}$). The similarity between the contraction pattern obtained with the EHG features and the TOCO-derived pattern was assessed using Pearson correlation. A lag range of -60 to +60 s was assesed in order to account for the delay between the electrical activity (captured by EHG) and the mechanical response (captured by TOCO) of uterine contractions. The results show that a greater electrode distance $(52.5 \mathrm{~mm})$ yields significantly higher correlation values across all features, indicating the highest correlation with TOCO waveform ($r \gt 0.86$) and improved discrimination of contractions. These findings highlight the importance of electrode spacing in EHG acquisition systems and provide a practical framework for optimizing electrode configuration in both research and clinical monitoring.

title: Leveraging Wireless Sensor Nodes for Efficient & early flood Prediction
authors: Jailsingh Bhookya, Bhaskar Gugulothu, Abhinav Raj V, Adhyuth Narayan, Adwayith K S, Agnel M S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Floods are among the most common natural disasters, and they cause significant damage and financial losses each year that could have been avoided. Heavy rain, snowmelt, river overflows, storm surges, the shape of the land, and how people use the land are all natural and human-made factors that affect when they happen. This makes it hard to predict and manage them. Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) are a reliable and affordable way to improve flood forecasting and strengthen early warning systems. The proposed work presents a hub-and-spoke WSN architecture in which a central gateway connects each sensor cluster. This structure simplifies the internal network, allowing the server to process data and provide users with a single interface for their questions. One of the biggest problems is getting the system to use as little power as possible while still meeting soft real-time requirements. LoRa and other technologies can find a balance between long-range communication and low energy use. The system’s goal is to provide easily deployable sensing units in areas that currently lack instruments. It does this by monitoring rainfall, soil moisture, water levels, and flow rates.

title: Gate-Engineered Tunnel FET Based LIF Neuron for Scalable and Energy-Efficient AI Systems
authors: Faisal Bashir, Ali Alzahrani, Usman Isyaku Bature, Haider Abbas, Furqan Zahoor
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper proposes a leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neuron design based on band-to-band tunneling transistor (Tunnel FET). The steep sub-threshold swing of a Tunnel FET is leveraged to replicate LIF neuronal behavior. Through calibrated simulations, we demonstrate that this single-device neuron accurately emulates neural activity without using external circuitry. The design achieves a remarkably low energy consumption of 20 fJ per spike, which is 2.4 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> times lower than existing single MOSFET based neurons existing in the literature. Further, we validated the neuron’s capability at a system level by simulating a network for image recognition tasks. The system achieved a recognition precision of 97.3 % on the standard MNIST benchmark, confirming the practical applicability of our design for complex, energy efficient AI (Artificial Intelligence) application. This work offers a pathway to building highly scalable and energy efficient threshold architectures for next-generation neuromorphic computing.

title: Impact of Low-n Absorber on SMO in High-NA EUV Lithography for Sub-3nm Nodes
authors: Xinping Shen, Qi Wang, Yanli Li, Ying Li, Qiang Wu, Xianhe Liu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This study investigates how absorber materials and source-mask optimization (SMO) influence high-NA EUV lithography at sub-3 nm nodes. Through rigorous simulations comparing conventional TaBN and low-n high-k absorbers on 1D and 2D BEOL patterns, we demonstrate that low-n high-k materials under SMO deliver substantially wider exposure latitude and improved mask error factor, thereby enhancing process-window robustness for high-volume manufacturing.

title: Nanoindentation-based mechanical characterization of Poly(vinyl alcohol) for biological phantom realization
authors: Eleonora Lancia, Claudia Laganà, Ludovica Apa, Mauro Palmieri, Maria Vittoria Martire, Francesco Castelli Gattinara Di Zubiena, Zaccaria Del Prete, Emanuele Rizzuto
abstract: Intramedullary spinal cord surgery requires surgical instruments capable of operating within highly delicate neural tissue, where localized mechanical and thermal effects may critically influence procedural safety. Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator, such as the Cavitron Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator (CUSA), represents a promising tool for controlled soft-tissue resection; however, its interaction with spinal cord tissue remains insufficiently characterized. The use of tissue-mimicking phantoms may therefore provide a controlled and reproducible platform to investigate such interactions. Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) hydrogels are widely used as tissue-mimicking materials due to their stability, biocompatibility, and tunable physical properties. In this study, PVA was selected as a candidate material for spinal cord phantoms since specific formulations have been shown to exhibit thermal properties comparable to those of native neural and tumoral tissues. Since ultrasonic aspiration inherently involves mechanical vibration, accurate mechanical characterization is essential. Accordingly, PVA hydrogels fabricated at three polymer concentrations ($10 \%$, $15 \%$, and $20 \% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w}$) were mechanically characterized using a nanoindentation-based approach. Depth-resolved nanoindentation analyses showed that increasing the polymer concentration leads to higher effective elastic modulus and lower viscoelastic dissipation in PVA hydrogels. The measured effective modulus values are slightly higher than those reported for native spinal cord tissue (on the order of tens of kPa) and intramedullary tumors (on the order of a few kPa), but they remain within the regime of soft hydrated materials and enable systematic modulation of mechanical properties across formulations.

title: The Architecture of Digital Trust: A Multi-Level Framework for Bridging the AI Value Gap
authors: Daniel Glinz
publish: 2026 IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS)
abstract: Despite increasing investment in artificial intelligence (AI), enterprises still face challenges in converting technical capabilities into enduring value. This paper argues that the widening AI value gap, the disparity between AI's potential and its realized value, is fundamentally a trust deficit rather than a technical shortcoming. The study employs a Grounded-Theory Design Science hybrid methodology that integrates Design Science Research with a grounded-theory literature review across a 56-source interdisciplinary corpus, drawing on human-computer interaction, complexity science, AI governance, and digital identity standards. It presents a Multi-Level Architecture for Digital Trust. This framework comprises four interrelated layers: Agency (human experience and perceptions of authenticity), Engineering (technical infrastructure and cryptographic verifiability), Governance (organizational adaptation and continuous assurance), and Institutional (regulatory legitimacy and societal oversight). Five design principles emerge from this architecture: Layered Architecture (structural separation of concerns), Forward-Looking Trust (anticipation of digital trust requirements), Productive Friction (advantageous tension between layers), Paradox Management (resolution of conflicting requirements), and Ecosystem Integration (synchronization of digital trust across organizations). The framework is illustrated through real-world cases, including the Swiss e-ID vote, the contrasting AI marketing approaches of Coca-Cola and Apple, and documented failures in AI governance, demonstrating its explanatory adequacy across diverse trust phenomena. The framework implies that closing the AI value gap requires demonstrable authenticity, reliability, transparency, and accountability across all layers of the ecosystem, turning digital trust from a reputational claim into a verifiable system attribute.

title: A Learnable and Generalized Few-Shot Semantic Segmentation Framework with Prototype Fusion for Remote Sensing Images
authors: Srilakshmi H, S. Eliza Femi Sherley, Nivetha T, Abarna B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Pixel-wise annotation required by semantic segmentation tasks is expensive and time-consuming, especially in remote sensing scenes. Remote sensing images captured by high-resolution aerial sensors bring even more challenge for semantic segmentation, such as cluttered backgrounds, scale variation, and larger intra-class variations. These factors heavily affect the generalization ability of previous segmentation models. In this paper, we propose a learnable and generalizable few-shot semantic segmentation framework that jointly considers multi-scale feature extraction, adaptive prototype learning and confidence-aware prototype fusion to tackle the limitation. First, we adopt Pyramid Scene Parsing Network (PSPNet) to extract richer multi-scale context features from both support and query images. Second, a VAE-based module is designed to generate adaptive class prototype that can better represent intra-class variations instead of vanilla masked averaging operation. Third, a Class-Aware Confidence Reweighting (CACR) strategy is introduced to fuse support, query and background prototypes via reliability-driven weighting schemes, which effectively enhances foreground-background separation. Experiments are conducted on the benchmark of iSAID-5i in 3-way 1-shot setting. The results demonstrate that our method achieves remarkable improvement over baseline with 88.93% Pixel Accuracy and 76.85% mean Intersection over Union (mIoU). Our method shows promising generalization capacity and produces segmentation results with better stability in remote sensing scenarios with few annotated samples.

title: SIAGeTrans: Traffic Optimization Based on Game Theory and IoT
authors: Morgana D. Campbell, Victor A. Gomes, Frederico Lopes, Flavia C. Delicato
abstract: Given population growth and its increasing concentration in urban areas, traffic congestion increasingly affects various aspects of daily life. From long commutes to stressed workers, traffic congestion reduces mobility, limits accessibility, and negatively impacts the environment and urban quality of life. Intersections play a critical role in managing traffic flow, and many approaches leverage technology to optimize Traffic Signal Control (TSC). However, most adaptive models in the literature focus only on pedestrian and vehicle demand, overlooking broader contextual factors. In this paper, we propose SIAGeTrans, an adaptive system based on IoT and Game Theory that aims to consider inputs beyond demand fluctuations, including factors like weather, flooding, and emergency vehicles. As a result, it reduces overall waiting time by up to one-third and significantly lowers fuel consumption and emissions compared to the fixed-time approach.

title: Advanced Classification of Pancreatic Cancer: Harnessing Bag-of-Features with Linear SVM and Fine KNN
authors: Hala S. Abuelmakarem, Esraa Asem Shaker, Alshimaa S. Teaima, Nourhan Yehia, Shimaa M. Khder
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Pancreatic cancer is the 7<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide and over 510,000 new cases are diagnosed annually internationally. While its incidence is relatively low (about 3% of all cancers), it accounts for a disproportionately high mortality rate, approximately 7% of all cancer deaths. Pancreatic cancer (PC) is a highly aggressive malignancy that does not cause symptoms in its early stages, so it is usually diagnosed when it has already advanced, making effective treatment more challenging. These reasons highlight the critical need for better early detection methods and more effective treatment options. In this study, 1411 CT images specifying two classes: 646 cases of normal and 765 cases of pancreatic tumor led to an efficient image classification with linear SVM and fine KNN. The classification stage attained an accurate level of KNN 98.59% and SVM 99.29%, yielding outstanding results that contributed to the early detection process.

title: Synthesis of a Novel Fluorinated Methacrylate Polymer used in Immersion Photoresist
authors: Sheng Huang, Wei Zhu, Alan Gu, Allen Wu, Xiangfei Zheng, Changbin Ji, Yuchen Weng
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: For ArF immersion lithography, water is put between the lens and the resist. However, performance will be deteriorated as a result of the interactions between the water medium and photoresist. In order to resolve this matter, a specific type of fluorinated polymer was synthesized and incorporated into photoresist. This polymer has the ability to migrate to the resist surface and form a hydrophobic water-repellent layer. The hydrophobic layer effectively prevents components of photoresist extraction into the water, and avoid water penetration into the photoresist. This paper provides a potential approach for advanced ArF immersion photoresist development.

title: Vision-Based Automatic Registration for Surgical Navigation: Accuracy Assessment on Phantoms
authors: Vignesh V Sree, Bala Siva Surya, Manojkumar Lakshmanan, Mohanasankar Sivaprakasam
abstract: Registration is the process of mapping a patient’s anatomy to the patient’s pre-operative images to enable tracking and visualization of surgical tools relative to the patient’s preoperative images. Current registration methods involve manually collecting a set of points on the patient’s anatomy and compared with pre-operative images to compute the registration matrix. However, this method takes a significant amount of time during surgery which reduces the overall efficiency of the surgical workflow. Automatic registration is a promising concept in computer assisted surgery where registration between the patient’s preoperative scans and the patient’s anatomy is done automatically with a secondary source of information like a depth camera instead of the surgeon manually collecting landmark points followed by surface points with a probe being tracked by an optical tracking system. We propose a novel automatic registration framework for femur and cranial surgeries where the patient’s anatomy is captured and segmented from RGB images. The segmented section from the RGB image is then mapped to the depth image to obtain the segmented 3D points of the patient’s anatomy. The extracted 3D points are then transformed into a frame of reference that is static relative to the patient’s anatomy and is recognised by the optical tracking system so that the patient’s anatomy can be registered to preoperative images in the same frame of reference. This approach eliminates the need for using probes for collecting landmark points and surface points manually which increases procedure time as well as makes the registration accuracy dependent on the surgeon’s ability to collect precise points. The system achieves registration accuracy of $1.10 \pm 0.09 \mathrm{~mm}$ (inlier RMSE) and tool tracking accuracy of $2.91 \pm 0.83 \mathrm{~mm}$ (TRE) on cranial phantoms, with hand-eye calibration reprojection error of $0.27 \pm 0.07$ pixels.

title: Bayesian Change Point Detection and its Applications in Artificial Intelligence
authors: Golnaz Shahtahmassebi, Jose Maria Sarabia
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Bayesian Change Point Detection provides a principled probabilistic framework for identifying structural shifts in sequential data. In many Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, observations arise as event counts that are naturally modeled using Poisson processes. Abrupt changes in event intensity often correspond to meaningful transitions such as anomaly onset, cyber-intrusion events, or environmental regime shifts.This paper develops a Bayesian change point model for Poisson processes with conditionally specified Gamma priors that capture structural dependence between pre- and post-change event rates. The formulation enables tractable posterior inference for the change point location and rate parameters using Gibbs sampling. We illustrate the approach through a cybersecurity intrusion-detection example, where sudden increases in login attempts are detected via the posterior distribution of the change point. The proposed framework provides uncertainty quantification and supports adaptive AI decision logic through posterior thresholding, offering a statistically rigorous method for event-driven, nonstationary AI systems.

title: Security Review on Strategies to Defeat Network Filters
authors: Jean Rosemond Dora, Ladislav Hluchý, Michal Staňo
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Traffic in network security is a topic that consistently draws attention. Companies implement several robust security measures to maintain a high standard of security within their environment. Monitoring and blocking anomalies are among the features they support. Moreover, security analysts configure network filters to minimize the probability of being exploited. We will examine the challenges that these security measures can pose to attackers. A thorough review of its utilization will also be addressed. Since these security solutions may hinder our activities, we will discuss their weaknesses and strengths, as well as their performance. In a hardened network infrastructure, i.e., that was built with security in mind, manipulating those measures is complex. Notwithstanding, it is of high interest to an attacker to defeat them. We will describe and explain the strategies that, if addressed, can circumvent these solutions.

title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of a Bridging Program in Mathematics and Basic Engineering Sciences: Evidence from First-Year Engineering Students
authors: Marites E. Olesco, Zendy D. Mañago, John Raymond B. Barajas
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: When the Philippine K–12 reform reduced engineering programs from five to four years and relocated foundational mathematics and science subjects to Senior High School (SHS), it introduced a structural readiness gap among incoming engineering students—particularly those from non-STEM tracks—who often enter without sufficient academic preparation or institutional support. This study therefore evaluated the Bicol University College of Engineering (BUCENG) Bridging Program, a structured, pre-semester, faculty-delivered intervention covering 20 instructional topics in mathematics and basic chemistry. Program effectiveness was assessed using two complementary instruments: a standardized competency-based diagnostic examination and a 271-item, 5-point developmental self-assessment survey. Both instruments were administered before and after the intervention to n = 142 incoming first-year students (diagnostic) and n = 70 matched pairs (self-assessment) in SY 2025–2026. Results from the diagnostic assessment indicated a substantial and statistically significant improvement in performance (M: 42.6% → 68.9%; Δ = +26.3 percentage points; t(141) = 21.74, p < 0.001, d = 1.83), reflecting a very large effect size. Similarly, the self-assessment results showed a significant increase in perceived competency (M: 2.37 → 2.67 out of 5; t(69) = 3.31, p = 0.001, d = 0.40), with 13 of 23 content modules demonstrating statistically significant gains. The largest module-level effects were observed in Electrochemistry (d = 0.59), Stoichiometry (d = 0.48), and Trigonometry (d = 0.42)—areas corresponding to the weakest entry-level preparation. Notably, students from non-STEM strands exhibited the greatest relative improvement (d = 0.71); however, their post-intervention competency levels remained substantially lower than those of their STEM counterparts, indicating a persistent structural equity gap that cannot be fully addressed by a single intervention. Taken together, these findings provided strong empirical support for the institutionalization of bridging programs as an evidence-based policy response to curriculum compression and uneven pre-university preparation.

title: Efficient Privacy-Preserving Ridesharing: An Online Matching-Based Approach
authors: Yifan Xu, Jun Tao, Xinyu Chen, Jun Yan, Jun Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: While ridesharing provides substantial convenience, it also raises several security concerns, with location privacy being a primary issue. A common state-of-the-art solution is to add random noise to user locations to preserve privacy. However, this approach often degrades matching efficiency due to reduced location accuracy. In this paper, we study the real-time matching problem between ridesharing requests and drivers, aiming to maintain high matching efficiency despite obfuscated locations. We model the order dispatching process as an online bipartite matching problem, where drivers are offline and requests arrive sequentially following a known distribution. We construct benchmark linear programs (LPs) and propose an LP-based online matching algorithm with provable performance guarantees. To address privacy concerns, we further develop a privacy-aware LP-based method that mitigates the impact of Laplace noise. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithms and support our theoretical findings.

title: Preparation and Performance Optimization of Boron-doped Ultrasmall Cerium Oxide Abrasives for Higy-Efficiency CMP
authors: Junjie Xue, Simin Li, Hui Shen, Fan Zhang, Wei Yuan
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: In the field of chemical mechanical polishing (CMP), cerium oxide polishing slurries serve as a critical component. However, the insufficient content of trivalent cerium in conventional cerium dioxide slurries remains a challenge. To address this issue, this study introduces boron into cerium dioxide abrasives, resulting in a modifiedy material referred to as B@CeO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>. This approach effectively enhances the trivalent cerium content in cerium oxide abrasives, thereby improving the polishing rate. Additionally, key experimental parameters, including temperature and doping ratio were systematically optimized to achieve higher reactivity.

title:
CsPbBr
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Perovskite Quantum Dots and One-Dimensional ZnO nanorods composites applied for Ultraviolet/Visible Broadband photodetectors
authors: Yu-Jhih Chu, Yi-Hsing Liu, Chih-Chiang Yang, Shoou-Jinn Chang, Sheng-Joue Young
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Broadband photodetection covering the ultraviolet and visible spectral regions remains a critical challenge for low-cost optoelectronic devices. In this work, a hybrid photodetector architecture was realized by integrating one-dimensional (1-D) ZnO nanorods (NRs) with CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots (PQDs), forming a sensitized heterostructure for enhanced visible-light response. The structural and compositional features of the pristine 1-D ZnO NRs and PQDs-capped NRs samples were systematically examined through electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, and optical spectroscopies, revealing successful decoration of CsPbBr3 PQDs on the surface of 1-D ZnO NRs. Photoluminescence and absorption analyses demonstrate that the introduction of PQDs effectively extends the optical response of ZnO from the ultraviolet into the visible region. Electrical measurements further show that the PQDs/1-D ZnO NRs heterostructure exhibits markedly improved photoresponse under blue and green illumination compared with unmodified 1-D ZnO NRs, while maintaining low dark current characteristics. This enhancement is attributed to efficient interfacial charge transfer and suppressed carrier recombination enabled by favorable energy-level alignment between CsPbBr3 PQDs and 1-D ZnO NRs. The proposed hybrid structure provides a viable route toward compact, cost-effective, and low-power broadband photodetectors suitable for ultraviolet and visible-light sensing applications.

title: Reusable, Secure, and Compliance-First CI/CD Pipeline Architectures for Regulated Enterprise Environments
authors: Sameena Begam Savukath Ali, Saikrishna Tarakampet, Subhash Tatavarthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Organizations in regulated industries like financial services, healthcare, and insurance rely more heavily on Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipelines [1] to speed up their software delivery, increase operational reliability and overall quality. CI/CD automation allows organizations to run builds, tests and deploy applications with as little manual intervention as possible while providing developers with quick feedback [7]. However, in a regulated industry, simply implementing CI/CD automation isn't enough to meet compliance requirements [9]. Regulated industries are governed through excessive regulation that has many requirements regarding governance, security, auditability, and compliance; these requirements cannot be met through CI/CD-only practices [12]. The regulatory frameworks that have been established by HIPAA, SOX, and PCI-DSS create an extensive list of requirements and expectations governing access to sensitive information, tracking changes made to that information, documenting approvals for changes, and segregating employees to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information [9].In this paper, we present a reusable compliance-first ci/cd pipeline architecture that is developed specifically for multi-stack enterprise environments [8]. The methodology integrates governance systems, security scanning [4], compliance as code [6], and automated audit trails into the DevOps workflow [2]. Included below, you will find multiple real world case studies from insurance, healthcare, and financial services that demonstrate how using standardized pipeline templates and embedded controls will help reduce auditing preparation effort and increase deployment reliability while supporting scalable DevOps adoption without compromising regulatory obligations and delivery speed.

title: A Hybrid and Adaptive Deep Learning Framework for Robust Image Classification Across Diverse Domains
authors: Pratik Singh, Vigyat Singh, Nishita Kindo, Gananatha Bhuyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Data acquisition is much easier with the advancement of imaging technology. End to end CNN’S have been limited by inherent design, and because they must deal with human data patterns, and also due to their inherent limitations of the "No Free Lunch" Theorem when using structured inputs from different sources (satellite, radar, underwater). To remedy this design limitation within our contributions to the field we are introducing a novel framework called the Hybrid And Adaptive Deep Learning Framework (HADLF). HADLF consists of three complementary components: 1) an Adaptive Convolutional Front-End (ACFEF) that allows an adaptive form of structure based on the type of data being processed; 2) DenseNet-121 — Dense connectivity will allow for feature extraction in a compact representation; and 3) SVM Classifier Head. The use of SVMs and a radial basis function(RBF) kernel helps achieve better classification results with efficient decision boundaries in high-dimensional and nonlinear feature spaces. The model was evaluated using the EuroSAT, RSI-CB256, and MSTAR datasets that showed excellent generalizability for the presented framework. The achieved classification accuracy on the aforementioned datasets is 99.1%, 98.8%, and 98.5%..

title: MedAgent: Trustworthy Personalized Consumer Health Search via Multi-Agent Retrieval and Verification
authors: Keshav Trikha, Blazej Madrzyk, Ashleyn Castelino, Aram Bahrini
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Access to reliable consumer health information is increasingly challenging as more people seek medical guidance online. Prior studies show that a majority of adults seek health information online, while the quality of search results remains inconsistent. However, large language models can generate fluent answers that may contain hallucinations or reflect bias, which can be especially harmful for users with limited health literacy. A key challenge is the mismatch between how users express health queries in natural language and how medical knowledge is structured in authoritative sources. Although modern search interfaces increasingly present AI-generated summaries, their underlying retrieval pipelines often remain insufficiently aligned with biomedical ontologies and rarely incorporate patient-specific context, thereby limiting grounding and personalization. We present MedAgent, an agentic consumer health information retrieval system built around three design principles. First, MedAgent uses a coordinated multi-agent architecture that decomposes health search into four distinct agents: intent interpretation, retrieval planning, evidence synthesis, and verification. Second, it applies a tiered source strategy that distinguishes authoritative medical literature from community health experiences, allowing the system to weight evidence differently and express uncertainty when sources conflict. Third, it constructs personal health knowledge graphs from a user’s medical history to support context-aware query interpretation and more relevant retrieval. We evaluate MedAgent on 200 sampled examples from OpenAI’s HealthBench, a benchmark based on 5,000 physician-authored rubrics graded by GPT–4o. MedAgent achieves an overall score of 0.468 compared with 0.320 for a GPT–4o direct-answer baseline. These results indicate that structured agentic retrieval and synthesis can improve benchmark performance relative to direct-answer large language model baselines. MedAgent provides a practical framework for grounded, personalized consumer health information retrieval.

title: Towards Automated Grading of Hand Drawings: A Dataset and Classification Framework
authors: Alaa Abdulfattah Sayed, Khaled F. Hussain, Majid Ahmed Askar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Hand drawing skills are essential for computer science students to effectively communicate ideas and visualize concepts. However, evaluating hand-drawn sketches is subjective and time-consuming. This paper presents a novel dataset of 1,356 hand-drawn geometric composition sketches from first-year computer science students at Assiut University, Egypt, expanded to 10,817 through systematic augmentation. We developed a ResNet-50 classification framework achieving 100% test accuracy on binary classification of cube_cone and cube_sphere compositions. The dataset exhibits natural class imbalance (99.7% in two dominant categories), providing insights for deploying machine learning in authentic educational contexts. Our contributions include: (1) a novel annotated dataset of actual educational hand drawings with quality grades, (2) a comprehensive analysis of class imbalance in educational contexts, and (3) baseline classification results that demonstrate the feasibility of automated assessment.

title: Oscillation Mechanisms of Laser-Sustained Plasma: A Key to Stabilizing Light Sources for Wafer Defect Inspection
authors: Dongheyu Zhang, Yangyang Fu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Laser-sustained plasma (LSP) is a high-temperature, high-radiation plasma widely used in semiconductor wafer inspection. However, the mechanisms of its brightness instability remain mysterious and strongly impede the development of ultra-broadband stabilized light sources. In this work, we elucidate that the LSP oscillation behaviors stem from buoyancy-driven vortex dynamics through the high-speed shadow image and an advanced multi-physics fluid model. A vorticity-resolved scaling law for LSP oscillation frequency, incorporating the gas properties and gravitational acceleration, is proposed for the first time. The generalized scaling law enables predictive control of LSP stability, enhancing the precision of high-resolution wafer optical inspection.

title: Design and Optimization of 60v LDMOS in 0.18mm BCD Platform with Enhanced Performance Via STI Engineering
authors: Huan Zhong, You Ke, Jian Dai, Dawei Gao, Kai Xu, Dianyu Qi
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: This work investigates the design and optimization of a 60V STI LDMOS transistor in a 0.18μm BCD process. The study uses TCAD simulations and experimental validation to optimize structural parameters, such as field-plate length and drift-region implantation conditions, to improve breakdown voltage (BV) and on-resistance (Ron,sp). A new asymmetric step-type STI (SSTI) structure is proposed to enhance BV while mitigating the BV-Ron,sp trade-off. The simulation results demonstrate a significant improvement in both BV and figure of merit (FOM), showing that the SSTI design improves the overall performance of STI LDMOS devices on BCD platforms.

title: Distributed Adaptive Predefined-Time Bipartite Consensus for Nonlinear Multiagent Vehicle Systems With Experiments: Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Ziqi Bai, Wenhai Qi, Ju H. Park, Huaicheng Yan
abstract: This article addresses the predefined-time bipartite consensus for nonlinear multiagent vehicle systems with unmodeled dynamics, parametric uncertainties, and external disturbances over directed signed graphs. The primary objective is to ensure that all follower vehicles converge to the leader’s reference state and their opposite values depend on the faction affiliation within a predefined-time, irrespective of initial conditions. To achieve this, an adaptive reinforcement learning framework featuring a tripartite evaluation-actor neural network architecture is proposed. This framework integrates a predefined-time performance function with an optimized second-order backstepping design. By reducing the general <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{n}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-order backstepping procedure to a physically meaningful second-order structure that aligns more closely with vehicle dynamics, the proposed approach significantly lowers control complexity while preserving predefined-time convergence guarantees. The resulting controller ensures that all closed-loop signals are practically predefined-time bounded with strict stability established via Lyapunov analysis. The efficacy of the proposed strategy is validated through hardware experiments on a multivehicle platform.

title: A Failure Taxonomy and Trace-Driven Debugging Toolkit for Reliable Agentic LLM Systems
authors: Nikita Kothari, Ankush Agarwal, Varun Joshi, Lav Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Agentic Large Language Model (LLM) systems extend language generation with iterative planning and tool invocation. However, benchmarks often mask a critical reality: these systems are operationally brittle in production. Failures such as planning drift, schema violations, and state inconsistencies recur systematically, distinct from the model’s reasoning capacity.This paper presents AgentTrace, a middleware architecture that operationalizes execution-level reliability. We introduce a formal seven-class failure taxonomy (F1–F7) and a trace normalization engine that converts heterogeneous agent events into "StepFrames" for deterministic analysis. Unlike prior work that relies on passive logging, AgentTrace implements an active Mitigation Auto-Repair Layer that intercepts and corrects failures in real-time without additional LLM inference.Evaluated across a rigorous suite of 300 interactive tasks and a high-complexity subset of WebArena, AgentTrace improves task success rates by 16.5 percentage points (from 52.4% to 68.9%) and reduces execution-critical failures by over 50%. Crucially, we demonstrate that these gains are achieved with <5% latency overhead and without fine-tuning, establishing AgentTrace as a viable production standard for reliable agent orchestration.

title: An Efficient Ensemble and Boosting Based Regression Framework for Air Quality Index Forecasting in Hassan District of Karnataka
authors: Gowri Shankar Manivannan, Rifa Mariyam, Fathima Zahara
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Air pollution is currently an urgent environmental and public health issue that requires proper prediction of the AQI to improve mitigation and decision-making. An ensemble and boosting-based regression time-series forecasting model is used in this work to analyze the AQI data of Hassan district, Karnataka. To improve the quality of data and improve the time-related representation, the proposed methodology combines STL as the trend and seasonal extraction method, residual threshold noise elimination, and rolling statistical feature extraction. Several ensemble and boosting regression algorithms such as Random Forest, AdaBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost and NGBoost are applied and compared. Performance assessment is conducted using MSE, MAE, and the R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The experimental findings reveal that boosting-based algorithms are superior to the traditional ensemble algorithms, where CatBoost performs the best (MSE = 0.0006, MAE = 0.0154, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9788), then NGBoost and LightGBM, respectively. The results indicate the strengths of the advanced gradient boosting methods to represent the nonlinear temporal variation and short-term AQI variability, which offer a credible methodology to predict air quality in regions and support environmental decision-making.

title:
Emulation of Intensity-Dependent Neuronal Firing Latency Using A HfO
                    <sub>x</sub>
                    -based Volatile Memristor
authors: Xiaqing Fu, Yichun Gong, Lixiang Jiang, Yuzhe Lin, Xinwei Zhang, Guanyu Liu, Yu Kang, Bin Yu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The dynamics and adaptive characteristics of biological neurons can be mimicked by neuromorphic electronic devices, but the development of compact and uniform artificial neuron remains challenging. In this work, we present a volatile hafnium oxide (HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf>)-based memristor that emulates the intensity-dependent response latency of biological neurons, successfully demonstrating that volatile HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> memristors are capable of mimicking stimulus-dependent firing latency in biological neural systems. The device exhibits a distinct modulation behavior in the discharge delay: larger voltage stimuli induce a faster response than weaker voltage stimuli. The device shows strong potential as a compact building block for neuromorphic computing circuits.

title: Beyond Distribution Shift: Investigating Generalisation in Automated Algorithm Selection for Single-Objective Black-Box Optimisation
authors: Behzad Moradi, Mario Andrés Muñoz, Michael Kirley
abstract: Automated algorithm selection enhances black-box optimisation by recommending best-performing algorithms tailored to problem features. However, key factors influencing the generalisation ability of automated algorithm selection remain poorly understood. This study proposes a triple-level evaluation framework to systematically investigate the generalisation ability of such models in single-objective black-box optimisation under progressively increasing distribution shift. Problem instances are generated from two distinct benchmarking suites, represented using Exploratory Landscape Analysis features. Density-based and distance-based metrics are employed to explicitly quantify the distribution shift between training and test problem sets. The complementarity of training and test problem sets is further assessed in both feature and algorithm performance spaces to uncover additional factors influencing generalisation. The results show that automated algorithm selection models consistently outperform a baseline, although performance degrades as the distribution shift between training and test problem sets increases. Distribution shift alone does not fully explain performance variations. Problem class–specific landscape features, feature–algorithm performance associations, and training set representativeness are also identified as critical determinants of generalisation. Furthermore, reallocating even a small fraction of target-domain data to training problem sets significantly enhances cross-domain generalisation.

title: Corrections to “TD-AMRKNet-Based Radar Image Processing Framework for Sea Clutter Suppression”
authors: Xiaolin Du, Di Ma, Xiaolong Chen, Guolong Cui, Jibin Zheng
abstract: In the above article [1], after publication, the authors identified several inadvertent figure-related errors. The corrections are as follows. The scope of this correction is limited to replacing two incorrectly placed qualitative result figures and correcting two interchanged figure captions. These changes do not affect the experimental data, numerical results, comparative analysis, or conclusions of the article.

title: Multiview Graph Learning for Mental Stress Recognition in Human–Machine Interaction
authors: Qunli Yao, Heng Gu, Shaodi Wang, Xiaoli Li
abstract: Mental stress manifests as dynamic reconfiguration of functional brain networks. However, stress-related electroencephalography patterns involve both relatively stable neurophysiological connectivity and subject- and context-dependent dynamic interactions, whereas single-view modeling typically emphasizes only one aspect, thereby limiting robustness and cross-subject generalization. This article proposes a multiview graph neural network (MVGNN) for robust stress recognition. The framework constructs a dual-view architecture comprising a cognition-driven branch, which utilizes heat-kernel diffusion to encode multiscale physiological patterns, and a data-driven branch that captures latent dependencies via adaptive topology learning. These complementary views are integrated through a cross-view fusion module employing semantic alignment and structural attention. Furthermore, a structure-aware adversarial domain adaptation mechanism is introduced to mitigate cross-subject distribution shifts at both feature and topological levels. Experiments on a self-collected dataset (NeuroUAV) and public benchmarks (DEAP and SEED) demonstrate that MVGNN achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a robust and interpretable solution for mental stress assessment in human–machine interaction.

title: Dynamic Minimum Loss Routing Strategy and Low-Carbon Economic Operation Optimization for Cyber-Physical Energy Routers
authors: Zilong Wang, Alian Chen, Tong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: With the widespread penetration of renewables, the energy router (ER) plays a crucial role in the energy system. It promotes the transition of the energy system from a single, centralized energy supply to more diversified and decentralized energy sources. This enhances the resilience of the power grid. However, the uncertainty and unstable flow of renewable energy have resulted in significant energy losses and increased carbon emissions, ultimately leading to imbalances in energy system scheduling. This paper proposes a dynamic minimum loss routing strategy to accommodate the distributed energy supply model and effectively reduce carbon emissions. Firstly, a novel cyber-physical ER structure is introduced to cater to diverse energy demands and mitigate power fluctuations by utilizing energy buffer registers (EBR). The EBR effectively alleviates the intermittency issues associated with renewable energy sources. Secondly, based on graph theory, the ER model is built. It integrates economic, carbon, and power flows. Furthermore, a dynamic minimum loss routing algorithm is introduced to optimize energy routing between the various ports. Thirdly, a multi-objective operation optimization strategy based on low-carbon and the economy is established. Finally, the simulation verifies the effectiveness of the routing strategy in optimizing energy flow path choices and reducing operational losses compared to conventional scheduling strategies.

title: LiDAR Teach, Radar Repeat: Robust Cross-Modal Navigation in Degenerate and Varying Environments
authors: Renxiang Xiao, Yichen Chen, Yuanfan Zhang, Qianyi Shao, Yushuai Chen, Yuxuan Han, Yunjiang Lou, Liang Hu
abstract: Long-term autonomy requires robust navigation in environments subject to dynamic and static changes, as well as adverse weather conditions. Teach-and-Repeat (T&R) navigation offers a reliable and cost-effective solution by avoiding the need for consistent global mapping; however, existing T&R systems lack a systematic solution to tackle various environmental variations such as weather degradation, ephemeral dynamics, and structural changes. This work proposes LTR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, the first cross-modal, cross-platform LiDAR-Teach-and-Radar-Repeat system that systematically addresses these challenges. LTR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> leverages LiDAR during the teaching phase to capture precise structural information under normal conditions and utilizes 4D millimeter-wave radar during the repeating phase for robust operation under environmental degradations. To align sparse and noisy forward-looking 4D radar with dense and accurate omnidirectional 3D LiDAR data, we introduce a Cross-Modal Registration (CMR) network that jointly exploits Doppler-based motion priors and the physical laws governing LiDAR intensity and radar power density. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive fine-tuning strategy that incrementally updates the CMR network based on localization errors, enabling long-term adaptability to static environmental changes without ground-truth labels. We demonstrate that the proposed CMR network achieves state-of-the-art cross-modal registration performance on the open-access dataset. Then we validate LTR<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> across three robot platforms over a large-scale, long-term deployment (40+ km over 6 months), including challenging conditions such as nighttime smoke. Experimental results and ablation studies demonstrate centimeter-level accuracy and strong robustness against diverse environmental disturbances, significantly outperforming existing approaches.

title: MAD-MulW: A Multi-Window Anomaly Detection Framework for BGP Security Events
authors: Songtao Peng, Yiping Chen, Xincheng Shu, Wu Shuai, Shenhao Fang, Zhongyuan Ruan, Qi Xuan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: In recent years, various international security events have occurred frequently and interacted between real society and cyberspace. Traditional traffic monitoring mainly focuses on the local anomalous status of events due to a large amount of data. BGP-based event monitoring makes it possible to perform differential analysis of international events. For many existing traffic anomaly detection methods, we have observed that the window-based noise reduction strategy effectively improves the success rate of time series anomaly detection. Motivated by this, we propose an unsupervised anomaly detection model, MAD-MulW, which introduces a multi-window serial framework. The W-GAT module adaptively updates sample weights within the window to reduce noise, while the W-LAE module captures temporal trends through predictive reconstruction, enhancing inter-class separation. Our model has been experimentally validated on multiple BGP anomalous events with an average F1 score of over 90%, which demonstrates the significant improvement effect of the stage windows and adaptive strategy on the efficiency and stability of the timing model. The source code is available at://github.com/2024ChenYP/MAD-MulW.

title: ConvShareViT: A Vision Transformer-Like Architecture for Free-Space Optical Accelerators
authors: Riad Ibadulla, Thomas M. Chen, Constantino Carlos Reyes-Aldasoro
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: This article introduces convolutional shared vision transformers (ConvShareViT), a novel deep learning architecture that adapts the vision transformer (ViT) architecture to the 4f free-space optical system. ConvShareViT replaces linear layers in multi-head self-attention (MHSA) and multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) with a depthwise convolutional layer with shared weights across input channels. The effectiveness of the attention mechanism was analyzed systematically in 12 experiments with different Models. Experimental results demonstrated that configurations with valid-padded shared convolutions successfully learned attention, achieving comparable quantitative attention scores to those obtained with standard ViTs. However, same-padded convolutions showed limitations in attention learning and operated like regular convolutional neural networks (CNNs) rather than transformer models. In terms of speed, ConvShareViT can theoretically achieve up to 3.04 times faster inference than GPU-based systems. This potential acceleration makes ConvShareViT an attractive candidate for future optical deep learning applications.

title: Phy-OSShip-MS Dataset: A Paired Optical-SAR Ship Imagery Generation Method Via Hierarchical Physics-Guided Style Transfer
authors: Jing Li, Yan Zhang, Zhuanze Yang, Chao Song, Ranhui Yuan, Feifan Ma, Jintao Chen, Zhongxing Jiao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-quality spatiotemporally paired optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) datasets are fundamental for cross-modal ship detection tasks. However, due to distinct imaging mechanisms, difficulties in acquiring spatiotemporal consistency, and data privacy concerns, such public datasets are extremely scarce. Current cross-modal SAR image synthesis methods often struggle to balance physical consistency and perceptual fidelity, resulting in images with scattering distributions that violate imaging mechanisms and speckle statistics that deviate from real data. To address these challenges, this article proposes a Hierarchical Physics-Guided Style Transfer framework for Optical-to-SAR image generation. In this architecture, style transfer serves as an underlying generative backbone to ensure perceptual fidelity, while a novel “Radiometric-Geometric-Fusion” three-level physical guidance strategy is introduced to explicitly constrain the generation process. First, a scene-universal category-level radiometric prior is constructed to establish physical intensity references for different categories. Second, phase congruency features are introduced to compensate for the deficiency of radiometric priors in fine structural depiction, precisely preserving the critical geometric details of strong scattering targets like ships. Finally, a synergistic fusion mechanism based on physical attribute intersection is established to apply directional gain to strong scattering targets by coupling material attributes with geometric saliency, thereby ensuring intensity-level physical consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing image-to-image translation models in terms of texture details and scattering characteristics. Consequently, we establish a Physics-guided Optical-SAR Ship Imagery dataset with Multi-Source attributes (Phy-OSShip-MS), providing target-level synthetically paired samples as an effective generative data augmentation strategy to reliably alleviate the data scarcity issue in cross-modal ship detection. Downstream evaluations show that our dataset significantly improves cross-domain generalization, boosting Average Precision (AP50) on unseen domains by over 56%. The constructed dataset will be made publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lilili0612/Phy-OSShip-MS-Dataset</uri>.

title: Seeing with Words: Grounding-Aware Refinement for Vision-Language Model Captions in Multi-Level Synthetic Traffic Scenes
authors: Khulan Khalzaa, Stephen Karungaru, Kenji Terada, Tsend-Ayush Chimed-Ochir
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated notable performance in image captioning, yet their reliability in structured, domain-specific environments such as traffic scenes remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce a five-level synthetic dataset of urban traffic scenarios, designed to represent progressively increasing scene complexity, from static environments to anomalous and high-interaction situations. To address the grounding limitations of existing captions, we define grounded objects as visually detected entities (e.g., vehicles, pedestrians) that should be explicitly referenced in textual descriptions, ensuring alignment between visual content and language. We propose a model-agnostic refinement module that filters hallucinated content and inserts factual references for omitted but visually grounded objects. We demonstrate the utility of this approach by applying it to Moondream2, a lightweight VLM, as an initial use case, showing that the refined captions align more closely with scene content and improve interpretability under diverse-complexity conditions. This lightweight refinement strategy highlights a practical pathway toward enhancing factual consistency in captioning systems, underscoring the need for further research on relational reasoning and risk-aware scene understanding.
