title: Reaction-Aware Planning With Preference Cost Learning for Human-Like Autonomous Driving in Unsignalized Intersections
authors: Heng Deng, Nuoyan Chen, Jingyuan Zhan, Liguo Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Planning tasks for unsignalized intersection scenarios present significant challenges due to the frequent interactions required with surrounding vehicles. Most autonomous driving systems often treat prediction and planning as two sequential and independent tasks, which fails to predict other participants’ reactions to the ego-vehicle’s actions. For such a purpose, this paper proposes a reaction-aware planning framework using Transformer to model the interactions between traffic participants and the ego-vehicle, and employs preference cost learning in the decision-making stage to learn the latent pReferences of human drivers for trajectory decisions from samples. Our method is validated through closed-loop simulations utilizing the nuplan dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves improved performance in unsignalized intersection scenarios compared to other methods, and exhibits driving behaviors that more closely align with those of human drivers.

title: Equalization Strategy for Double-Layer Lithium Batteries Based on Hierarchical Model Predictive Control With Fuzzy Weights
authors: Liping Chen, Zihan Wei, Antonio M. Lopes, Mo-Yuen Chow, YangQuan Chen
abstract: To address the issues of shortened service life and safety risks caused by inconsistencies in Li-ion battery modules, a dual-layer equalization topology based on Buck–Boost and single-ended primary-inductor converter (SEPIC) configurations is proposed. The inner layer employs an improved Buck–Boost circuit to divide the battery module into several smaller groups, which are interconnected through an enhanced SEPIC circuit structure. The equalization time of the proposed design is reduced by 26.84% compared with the adjacent battery topology using a Buck–Boost circuit, and by 34.3% compared with the cascaded Boost circuit based on the mean-difference algorithm. For the control strategy, adaptive-weight model predictive control (AMPC) and adaptive hierarchical model predictive control (AHMPC) are successively introduced to enhance intra-group and inter-group equalization performance. These approaches significantly improve equalization speed and efficiency. Specifically, compared with the conventional model predictive control algorithm, AMPC and AHMPC reduce the equalization time by 56.2 and 55.35%, respectively, while increasing energy utilization by 2.67 and 2.05%. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments validate that the simulation results are consistent with the experimental outcomes, confirming both the enhanced flexibility of the equalization circuit and the overall effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Minimal Controllability of Sphere and Torus Grid Graphs
authors: Tianhao Li, Yibei Li, Zhixin Liu, Xiaoming Hu
abstract: Network controllability is essential for designing the control algorithms of networked systems, among which the minimal controllability problem has attracted much attention of researchers due to the need of conserving control resources in practical applications. In this paper, we investigate the minimal controllability problem of Laplacian dynamics on two classes of non-planar graphs, namely sphere grid graphs and torus grid graphs. Since the Laplacian matrices of sphere grid graphs are closely related to the adjacency matrices of cylinder grid graphs, we first analyze the minimal numbers of control nodes required to guarantee controllability of networked linear systems whose system matrices are adjacency matrices of cylinder grid graphs. By exploring the relation between Laplacian matrices of sphere grid graphs and adjacency matrices of cylinder grid graphs, we then establish upper and lower bounds for the minimal number of control nodes required to guarantee controllability of Laplacian dynamic systems on sphere grid graphs. Finally, we provide the exact minimal number of control nodes and a corresponding control node set to guarantee controllability of the Laplacian dynamic systems on torus grid graphs.

title: Pushing physical limits and uncovering motion templates of spine-based quadruped locomotion via reinforcement learning_supp3-3697171.mp4
authors: Long Cheng
abstract: Flexible spines are critical to the remarkable agility and speed of animals. Translating this biological advantage to quadruped robots presents a significant control challenge, particularly in coordinating the spine and limbs for maximal velocity. In this work, we utilize reinforcement learning (RL) to develop high-speed locomotion for a bioinspired mouse robot with a lateral flexible spine. The resulting controller achieves motor performance that demonstrably surpasses non-spined and model-based methods. More importantly, our analysis reveals the principles behind this performance: the emergence of two distinct motion templates. For high-speed walking, the robot learns a “whip-like” spinal oscillation to increase leg swing frequency, while for agile turning, it adopts a dynamic “bend-and-straighten” pattern. These findings demonstrate the capability of RL to not only generate high-performance controllers but also to produce emergent strategies that, upon analysis, reveal underlying principles of high-speed, spine-driven locomotion.

title: Comparative Analysis of Determining the Characteristics of Voltage Sags in the Electrical Network
authors: Anatolii Voloshko, Danylo Filyanin
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The paper presents the results of a comparative analysis of determining the characteristics of Voltage Sags in the electrical network by various methods. It is shown that the application of the traditional method based on the RMS values of the voltage shape in relation to the results of determining the duration of sags, taking into account the time values of the start and end points of sags on the instantaneous values of the voltage waveform, have errors within 32 % and 4 %, respectively. It is shown that the traditional method of describing Voltage Sags does not identify different types of sags and does not determine all possible features of Voltage Sags that can significantly affect the sensitivity of electrical equipment. At the same time, it is noted that POI and POR are two important parameters of Voltage Sags, the determination of which is equally important both for the characterization of sags and for assessing their impact on the operation of electrical equipment in the electrical network.

title: Increasing the Energy Efficiency of the Electric Drive of the Conveyor for Bulk Cargo Using the Linear Loading Stabilization System
authors: Anton Toropov, Liliia Toropova, Volodymyr Dubovyk
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The use of conveyor loading control systems allows ensuring maximum energy efficiency of electromechanical systems. At the same time, the presence in the control loop of a link of pure delay and non-linear expression on state variables makes the procedure of developing a control system significantly difficult. Modern regulators often do not provide the ability to regulate the load in a wide range and require readjustment when changing the operating parameters. Therefore, an important task is to implement the procedure for synthesizing a control algorithm that provides the nature of load stabilization in the presence of disturbing effects close to optimal. We solve the problem of analytical design of a quasi-optimal load controller that provides high accuracy of stabilization in the presence of nonlinear dependencies in the control loop. It is used concept of the Pearson method, which allows the original nonlinear synthesis problem to be replaced by a linear, solvable in each cycle of operation of the programmable logic controller of the conveyor control. During investigation of the dynamic characteristics of the conveyor using mathematical modeling, the comparison of the synthesized regulator with the classical LQR was made.

title: Monitoring the Performance of the Safety Managment System at Electricity Enterprises
authors: Oksana Ilchuk, Elina Rebuel, Larysa Tretiakova
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: It is relevant to increase labour safety at electric power enterprises, which can be achieved by monitoring the performance of the safety management system. Monitoring the effectiveness of the safety management system makes it possible to assess the effectiveness of risk management as a result of an alternative combination of economic, technological, administrative, social and psychological management methods. Multi-criteria decisionmaking methods help to make better and more informed responsible decisions in practice. Their main application is to rank alternatives, with the best solution ranked first and the worst solution ranked last. The aim of the article is to increase the effective management decisions of electric power companies in the field of health and safety of employees based on the ranking of the generalized methods of pairwise comparison of Copeland and Bord. A rating algorithm based on formal standardized safety indicators has been developed. The results of rating of four electricity supply organizations of Ukraine have been presented.

title: Implementation of Forecasting Models and Dynamic Assessment of Correlation to Improve Smart Control in Microgrid Systems
authors: Volodymyr Khilenko, Shvydenko Mykhailo, Halyna Bielokha, Oleksii Stepanov, Serhii Denysiuk, Rohoza Kostiantyn
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The issues of optimizing the functioning of Microgrid networks in real time based on the implementation of relevant smart management systems are considered. The network optimization technology is proposed based on the use of Microgrid dynamics calculations, taking into account forecasts for network participants regarding the processes of both generation and energy consumption. The dynamic adjustment of smart contracts is proposed and the elements of the appropriate technology for implementation in the smart Microgrid control system are considered. Possible software products for creating smart contracts for a Microgrid system with a limited number of distributed energy generators are given.

title: Experimental Validation of Autonomous Search for an RF Emitter in an Unknown Indoor Environment
authors: Kim Hoanh Ly, Du Yong Kim, Branko Ristic, Ehsan Asadi, Akram Al-Hourani
abstract: This paper presents a practical demonstrator and experimental verification of an autonomous search robotic system for the localization of a radio frequency (RF) emitter in an unknown, GNSS-denied indoor environment. The system combines: (1) RSS-based inference for emitter localization—without prior knowledge of the environment or emitter amplitude, with (2) LiDAR-based simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) for mapping and self-localization. An online information-theoretic path planner selects actions to maximize entropy reduction in the emitter parameter space. Real-world challenges such as signal variability and sensor noise are addressed by adopting an interval valued path loss exponent. Experimental results demonstrate practical emitter-localization performance and fully autonomous operation in realistic settings.

title: FedSAE-IDS: Robust Semi-Supervised Federated Intrusion Detection with Denoising Contractive Autoencoders and Trimmed Power Aggregation for IoT Networks
authors: Tanmay Gupta, Sumitra
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The rapid growth of IoT networks has raised the level of cybersecurity threats, and both efficient and privacy-sensitive intrusion detection is necessary. The conventional centralized IDS systems lack scalability and privacy, whereas federated learning systems have the drawbacks of Non-IID distributions of data and unreliable updates of clients. In this paper, the suggestions made are FedSAE-IDS, which is a semi-supervised federated intrusion detector that integrates a Denoising Contractive Autoencoder (DCAE) to learn robust features and novel Trimmed Power Aggregation (TPA) to enhance aggregation reliability in a heterogeneous client environment. The framework facilitates the collaborative learning process without exchanging raw information and learns the normal traffic pattern through reconstruction-based anomaly detection. The experiments are conducted on the N-BaIoT dataset using IID and Non-IID conditions so that FedSAE-IDS can be used to achieve scalable and stable performance, with a detection accuracy of 97%-99%, outperforming baseline techniques, regardless of the number of clients or the extent of their participation.

title: SIRA: A Semantic LLM-Based Agent for Interpreting and Detecting Vishing Attacks
authors: Yasser Hmimou, Mohamed Tabaa, Azeddine Khiat, Zineb Hidila
abstract: Voice phishing has evolved rapidly with the rise of large language models, allowing attackers to create persuasive, context-aware conversations at scale. Traditional machine learning systems struggle to detect these adaptive behaviors, as they rely primarily on superficial acoustic cues or static text classification. To overcome these limitations, this paper introduces SIRA, an LLM-based semantic agent designed to interpret, contextualize, and detect vishing attacks. Unlike previous approaches that treat acoustic and textual signals independently, SIRA combines three complementary layers: (i) semantic reasoning on call transcripts generated by speech-to-text conversion, (ii) evaluation of prosodic and behavioral anomalies, and (iii) generation of human-readable incident reports using structured prompts.We evaluate SIRA using a heterogeneous benchmark comprising real vishing conversations, deceptive speech corpora, and benign telephone interactions. The results show that SIRA systematically identifies manipulation patterns such as urgency, abuse of authority, and financial coercion. The agent achieves a 13.4% improvement in F1-score over the best conventional reference and reduces false positives by 18.7%, demonstrating its ability to maintain robust semantic reasoning under noisy or ambiguous conditions.Beyond detection, SIRA provides interpretable explanations that boost analyst confidence and accelerate decision-making. The study confirms that a single, carefully designed LLM agent can significantly strengthen telecommunications fraud defense, representing a promising step toward AI-assisted operational protection against modern vishing threats.

title: Analysis of PD-generated UHF Signals and their Classification using PRPD and Machine Learning
authors: Oskar Zmarzły, Michał Kozioł, Daria Wotzka, Tomasz Boczar, Tomasz Kołtunowicz, Michał Kunicki
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Partial discharges (PD) are localized electrical breakdown events in dielectric media, typically initiated by elevated local electric fields, voids or surface irregularities, and can be replicated in laboratory conditions using controlled electrode gaps. In this work, oil-immersed PD activity was generated for five representative electrode geometries (needle–sphere, sphere–plate, sphere–sphere, plate–sphere, plate–plate), serving as analogues of defect scenarios relevant to power transformer insulation systems. UHF-based measurements were conducted using a MPD600 system and phase-resolved partial discharge (PRPD) patterns were extracted from the recorded signals. PD fingerprints were observed, motivating the formulation of a data-driven classification concept. A set of statistical and phase-resolved descriptors is proposed to parametrize the PRPD data and to enable subsequent machine learning–based recognition of PD mechanisms using only recorded PD signals. The study demonstrates that controlled PD measurements can be mapped to transformer-relevant defect mechanisms and provide a viable basis for automatic PD classification frameworks, with potential applications in transformer condition assessment and HV diagnostics.

title: Management of System Elements of Regional Critical Infrastructure in the Context of Military Actions in Ukraine
authors: Nataliia Derzhak, Maryna Morneva, Svitlana Golubieva
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The article explores managerial coordination in the case of infrastructure failure, social vulnerability, and minimum service provision. It also addresses the issue of responsibility for ensuring the minimum provisioning functions by government authorities. The key idea of this approach is that society's basic needs are covered by critical functions dependent on infrastructure objects. Their criticality is assessed based on reliability, alternatives, and interdependence. This allows significantly limiting the scope of critical infrastructure, as not every part of it is necessarily considered critical. Energy is a particularly crucial part of critical infrastructure because it ensures the viability of other components and requires more attention from local government authorities. From the authors' perspective, managerial actions should have a high level of performance, sufficient to fully meet the needs of consumers of all categories, comply with EU requirements in terms of the ratio of obtained electrical energy to resources expended for it, and also have environmental cleanliness and revitalization properties.

title: Studying the Biogas Temperature Control System to Be Used at Stand-Alone Power Plants
authors: Vozniak Oleksandr, Shtuts Andrii, Kolisnyk Mykola, Zozulyak Igor, Burlaka Serhii
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Technical progress is characterized by a continuous expansion of automation in all areas of human activity, transition from partial automation to complex one, and then from complex automation to overall one, which ensures the highest possible technical-and-economic efficiency. Century XXI is called the century of information, since main successes of technical progress are now observed specifically in introduction of information technologies to various tools and automation systems. Automation is impossible without control and measurement equipment. The development of control and measurement equipment for automation systems is largely determined by achievements in compatible areas of science and technology in microelectronics, computing machinery, solid state physics, etc. It is for the developers of the systems meant for automation and control of various technological parameters that the most favorable environment for practical activity has been created in the form of a huge range of miniature-sized and reliable sensors that are easily connected to secondary equipment and allow for transmission of measurement data over considerable distances in conditions of intensive industrial disturbances.

title: Singular Value Fine-Tuning for Efficient Device-to-Device Adaptation of Large Language Models
authors: Yang Guangzhao, Hiroshi Esaki, Hideya Ochiai
abstract: Device-to-Device (D2D) communication enables collaborative model training in ubiquitous computing but suffers from heavy synchronization load. We propose WAFL-SVFT, a communication-efficient fine-tuning framework that integrates Singular Value Fine-Tuning (SVFT) into Wireless Ad Hoc Federated Learning (WAFL). SVFT updates only the singular values of weight matrices while keeping bases fixed, achieving compact, information-preserving adaptation. Furthermore, we extend WAFL-SVFT with a classification-then-expert mechanism, in which a lightweight classifier first identifies the task category and then routes the input to a specialized SVFT expert module trained for that category. This modular design enhances reasoning accuracy and mitigates Non-IID heterogeneity across devices. Experiments with large language models on mathematical reasoning tasks show that WAFL-SVFT attains accuracy comparable to LoRA with about half the total communication cost and up to 7,000× compression per update. The method further adapts well to heterogeneous data via modular training, enabling efficient continual learning under strict communication constraints.

title: Causal Modeling of the Fairness and Usefulness Trade-off in Remedial Social Programs with Application in Student Financial Support
authors: Maher Alhossaini, Mohammed Aloqeely
abstract: There is often a trade-off between the ability to measure the impact (usefulness) and the fairness of social programs. To measure a program impact accurately, often an invasive causal inference procedure (e.g. Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)) that negatively affects the fairness must be applied. We present a novel causally-defined fairness concept: Remedial Intervention Fairness (RIF); which corresponds to the level to which an intervention corrects existing inequality. We distinguish two kinds of RIF in social programs: global fairness, where fairness is guaranteed for all units, and local fairness, where it is guaranteed only within groups (e.g. city). Given that measuring the counter-factual impact of the latter is possible (using Multi-Threshold Strict Regression Discontinuity (MTSRD)) but not of the former, we claim that using a locally-fair design with acceptable threshold is more advantageous than the globally-fair design. Therefore, we introduce an algorithm that takes as input an active locally-fair binary program’s design (as a causal graph) and data, and estimates its causal impact and the counter-factual impact of its imaginary globally-fair design counterpart. It also compares their fairness. The algorithm uses Bayesian MTSRD causal AI model. We demonstrate our algorithm with an existing locally-fair financial support program that is intended for low-income school students. We show that the program’s usefulness and fairness are comparable to its globally-fair counterpart, while having measurable impact.

title: Predicting Food Shortages at Food Pantries using Machine Learning and Predictive Analytics
authors: Samuel B. Mazzone, Eduardo Carrasco, Casey Hoff, Carey Redmann, Jeff Joslyn, Walter Bialkowski
abstract: Food insecurity remains a widespread public health challenge in the United States, where charitable food systems often rely on reactive approaches to meeting community needs. This study investigates whether artificial intelligence and machine learning techniques can be used to forecast unusually large food requests from food pantries, enabling proactive resource planning. Historical food distribution data from 427 US food pantries were combined with county-level demographic and economic indicators. Agglomerative clustering was used to group food pantries by shared ordering patterns and regional characteristics, reducing the need for individualized models. Cluster-specific logistic regression models were then developed to predict food shortage events, defined as orders exceeding two standard deviations above rolling historical averages. Results identified eight meaningful clusters with strong internal similarity (agglomerative coefficient 0.99) and demonstrated high predictive accuracy (mean accuracy 96%, AUC 0.95). Prior order weight and selected community variables were consistently associated with increased likelihood of shortages. These findings show that relatively simple predictive tools can detect early warning signals of elevated demand and support improved food allocation strategies. The proposed framework is transferable to similar food assistance systems, offering a scalable pathway to strengthen resilience and shift hunger relief efforts from reactive to proactive operations.

title: Sorting Recyclable Waste under Uncertainty
authors: José G. Quenum
abstract: Waste management is a critical function of urban area management. It includes the collection, sorting and processing of recyclable materials (e.g., paper, glass, plastic and metal). Waste management offers benefits such as conserving natural resources, reducing pollution, maintaining cleaner streets and households, and saving energy. This paper proposes an artificial intelligence (AI)-based approach to sort recyclable domestic waste. In recent years, we have witnessed an increasing adoption of AI-based technologies for waste management. For example, robots assist in the sorting process, and Internet of Things (IoT)-embedded intelligent bins can help optimise the collection route. This work employs decentralised collaborative contextual bandits to sort recyclable domestic waste. Agents, arranged in groups, learn the best arm for each context and subsequently determine the best intelligent bins for each type of waste item.

title: Performance Comparison of Square and Cylindrical Metal Cavity Antennas for Hyperthermia
authors: Shashi Bhushan Kumar, Soni Singh
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: In this paper, the design and performance comparison of square and cylindrical cavity antennas are proposed at 2.45 GHz for hyperthermia. The antenna features a circular patch fed coaxially to enhance directional radiation, improving focus and penetration depth. A metal cavity is placed at the top of the radiating antenna to create a gap between the tissue and the antenna, thereby improving impedance matching, minimizing outgoing radiation, and enhancing directional radiation. The performance of the proposed antenna was evaluated on specific absorption rate (SAR), penetration depth (PD), and effective field size (EFS).

title: Can only LLMs do Reasoning?: Potential of Small Language Models in Task Planning
authors: Gawon Choi, Haechan Chong, Seonghyeon Kim, Hyemin Ahn
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: In recent robotics, Large Language Models (LLMs) have been noted as domain-agnostic task planners due to their advanced perception and reasoning capabilities. Especially, the reasoning capability of LLMs based on Chain-of-Thought (CoT) is gaining attention for robotic task planning. However, we claim that LLMs are excessive for robotic task planning for two reasons. First, the language commands given to robots have much lower linguistic complexity than what LLMs can handle. Second, modern robots in practice are primarily tailored to a specific domain or environment (i.e., kitchen). Building on this, we further believe that within a single, specific environment, small language models (LMs) also have the potential to be effective robotic task planners. To explore this, we generate ‘COmmand-STeps dataset’ (COST) via LLMs, which is a dataset designed for environment-specific robotic task planning. This dataset is then used to fine-tune small LMs to plan action steps for robots in response to given human commands. The applicability of COST dataset to real-world robots is verified by showing that the action steps generated by fine-tuned small LMs on COST dataset are practically executable by the robot. Furthermore, through comparison between LLMs and small LMs fine-tuned on COST dataset within specific task environments, it is demonstrated that the fine-tuned GPT-2 medium is comparable to GPT-3.5 in environment-specific task planning.

title: AI-Enabled Sustainable Medical Supply Distribution Using Hybrid GA-QL in Dynamic Urban Environments
authors: Fathi M. Smaili, Fatima A. Omar, Manar O. Alaklbi, Nouran M. Alqarni, Rahaf N. Albishi, Noura A. Almutawa, Shahad G. Alshahrani
abstract: This paper proposes an AI-enabled smart drug distribution framework for sustainable medical supply delivery in dynamic urban environments. The system integrates IoT-based real-time data, Big Data analytics, and a hybrid Genetic Algorithm–Q-learning (GA–QL) approach to solve a fleet-based capacitated vehicle routing problem (CVRP) under traffic congestion, road events, and weather uncertainty. A sustainability and emission modeling module is incorporated to quantify fuel consumption, CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions, renewable fuel usage, and overall environmental performance. Computational experiments on benchmark instances demonstrate that the proposed approach improves routing efficiency and significantly enhances sustainability compared to conventional methods, highlighting its potential for resilient and environmentally responsible healthcare logistics.

title: An Efficient Transformer-Based Intrusion Detection System in IoT Environment Using XAI-Driven Feature Reduction
authors: Navpreet Singh, Prashant Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Transformer-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) for packet-level sequences achieve strong performance but are computationally expensive for Internet of Things (IoT) environments due to high feature dimensionality. To address the strict resource constraints of IoT edge devices, we present a solution that minimizes feature dimensionality while preserving detection accuracy, thereby reducing inference overhead. By employing explainable artificial intelligence (XAI)-based attribution, we quantify the contribution of individual input features and remove those with negligible impact under a validation-based performance constraint. Unlike standard post-hoc explanation, we repurpose XAI as a practical tool for building an efficient model suitable for IoT deployment. Experiments on the UNSW-NB15 dataset demonstrate that the proposed system maintains competitive performance using substantially fewer features, resulting in improved suitability for real-world IoT edge deployment.

title: Data Analytics Application in Standardizing Transformer Capacities in Substaions
authors: Abdullah A. Almehizia
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of selecting standardized substation transformer capacity to accommodate different load demands. Data analysis (DA) has recently become a significant tool in power system analysis. One prominent (DA) tool is the K-means algorithm, which is utilized to specify the transformer standard capacities. In addition, the elbow method and the Davies-Bouldin (DB) Criterion are implemented to determine the optimal number of transformer standards. The simulations are carried out using the MATLAB simulation software.

title: Analytical Initial Design and Optimal Sizing of PMSMs for Mobility Applications Based on the Loading Separation Concept
authors: Jeonghan Lee, Byungtaek Kim
abstract: This paper presents an analytical framework for the initial design and optimal sizing of surface-mounted permanent magnet synchronous motors for mobility applications. Although many geometric variables are typically considered independent during permanent magnet synchronous motor optimization, numerous parameters are analytically constrained by magnetic-loading limits, slot–conductor relationships, and flux-path geometry. By formalizing these dependencies, this work demonstrates that permanent magnet synchronous motor sizing can be parameterized using only four geometric degrees of freedom: air-gap diameter, stack length, magnet thickness, and number of pole-pairs. To accurately relate these variables to flux linkage and magnetic loading, a nonlinear iterative effective air-gap model is developed incorporating slotting effects and iron-core magnetomotive-force drops. Closed-form expressions for winding turns, voltage constraints, field-weakening capability, and demagnetization robustness are then established to form the proposed design framework. A 5-kW laboratory prototype with optimal size is designed using the proposed methodology and validated via detailed finite-element simulations and experimental measurements. Results confirm that the proposed design technique combined with the loading separation concept enables fast, accurate, and interpretable initial permanent magnet synchronous motor design suitable for diverse mobility-system requirements.

title: Classification of Slope Grade Using Knee Joint Angle Data Based on GAF-CNN Algorithm
authors: Zhuo Wang, Fangliang Yang, Hui Chen, Jiale Zhang, Xiangyang Wang, Chunjie Chen, Xinyu Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: This study proposes a Gramian Angular Field-Convolutional Neural Network (GAF-CNN) framework to classify slope-grade groups from knee joint angle data. First, non-negative matrix factorization was employed to analyze muscle synergies during slope walking using an open-source dataset, indicating that the biceps femoris (BF), semitendinosus (ST), and rectus femoris (RF) were major contributors associated with knee-angle variations. Based on Pearson correlation analysis, a weighted combination of the root-mean-square (RMS) values of BF and RF was selected as a representative indicator. Using this indicator, the 27 treadmill slope levels (-6% to 20%) used in the experiments were clustered into 13 groups as classification labels. The three input representations (Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">stance</sub>, Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">swing</sub>, and Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gait</sub>) were each evaluated using three CNN architectures-a 1D-CNN, a Gramian Summation Angular Field CNN (GASF-CNN), and a Gramian Difference Angular Field CNN (GADF-CNN)-resulting in nine input–model combinations. The GADF-CNN with Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gait</sub> achieved the highest classification accuracy (95.27%), followed by Datastance (95.18%), whereas Data<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">swing</sub> yielded the lowest accuracy (95.05%), using five convolutional blocks. The proposed framework exhibited stable performance under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation and group-wise five-fold cross-validation, and demonstrated strong potential for real-time deployment. Overall, transforming 1D knee-angle sequences into 2D images via the GAF representation markedly improved slope-grade classification accuracy.

title: Vision Transformer-Empowered Predictive Beamforming for Integrated Sensing and Communication in Vehicular Networks
authors: Yichen Zhong, Jian Zhao, Baile Xu, Furao Shen, Kun Yang
abstract: Vehicular networks are a critical application area of future wireless communication technologies. However, ensuring ultra-low latency for high data-rate communications in vehicular networks is particularly challenging due to high-mobility vehicles and constantly changing environments. With the emergence of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), sensing and communication systems can be integrated to improve resource efficiency and realize mutual benefits. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end sensing-assisted predictive beamforming framework for vehicular networks, where the roadside unit (RSU) directly predicts future transmit beamforming matrices from the sensing signals of the vehicles.We introduce a Vision Transformer (ViT)-based neural network called PBViT, which splits the historical sensing signals into patches to capture spatio-temporal dependencies. Furthermore, we propose a light-weight predictive beamforming neural network called Mobile-PBViT, which integrates convolutional blocks for local feature extraction with a Transformer encoder for global representation learning. The hybrid architecture significantly reduces the parameter count, facilitating deployments at RSUs while maintaining competitive performance. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed ViT-based schemes outperform existing learning-based predictive beamforming methods in terms of system data rate. Compared to optimization-based beamforming methods under perfect channel information, the proposed schemes achieve competitive data-rate performance with substantially lower online inference latency.

title: LLM-Assisted Software Development in Aerospace Engineering Education: A Structured Workflow
authors: Raed Kafafy, Mohamed Okasha, Ossama Abdelkhalik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being applied to software development tasks; however, their structured integration into aerospace engineering workflows remains insufficiently documented. This paper presents a structured workflow for LLM-assisted software development in aerospace engineering education, emphasizing problem decomposition, modular prompt design, iterative validation, and human-in-the-loop verification.The proposed methodology is demonstrated through a case study involving the development of a web-based visualization tool for orbital mechanics instruction. Rather than focusing on the software architecture itself, the study analyzes the interaction between domain expertise and AI-generated code, identifies common error categories, and evaluates the role of structured prompting in improving reliability and development efficiency. The results highlight both the potential and limitations of LLM-assisted engineering, underscoring the necessity of expert validation in physics-based applications. The presented workflow provides a practical framework for integrating large language models into aerospace software development and educational tool prototyping.

title: Leveraging Gated Recurrent Units for Real-Time Tracking of Student Engagement in Healthcare LMS using LLM Analytics
authors: Hassan Rehan, Wasim Akram, Muhammad Bilal Shoaib Khan
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Medical student is vital in successful learning in online healthcare education where attention and participation cues are constrained by conventional standards. This article introduces an engagement tracking method in real-time on Healthcare Learning Management Systems based on a combination of temporal deep learning and LLM-enhanced analytics. We present CTP-GRU, a GRU-based model that predicts the engagement levels in real-time using sequential logs of behavior. The proposed approach, unlike the previous ones which base their results on fixed or aggregate characteristics, takes into account interactions patterns over time, session behavior and activity trends in order to generate engagement insights that would be more timely and reliable. An analytics layer based on LLM summarizes the evidence of interaction into actionable indicators that can be used to provide instructor feedback. Testing on real-world LMS data demonstrates that CTP-GRU is a better choice compared to traditional machine learning baselines, with better accuracy and robustness to predict engagement and early disengagement.

title: Alumni Employment Outcomes as a Basis for Curriculum Evaluation in a State University’s Bachelor of Arts in Communication Program
authors: Ramon Gian A. Bron
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Graduate employability is often regarded as an important indicator of efficacy of the academic program, particularly in respect to areas of study that involve communication and that lie at the interface of media production, digital technologies, and workforce preparation. Where state universities are concerned, employment outcomes are applied both as measures of instructional success, but also as indicators of curriculum development, resource allocation and policy development. The application of the use of employability assessments, however, is more frequently assumed to be a compulsory-based tracer study, rather than a systematic curriculum redesign mechanism. In evaluating the curriculum and improving academic system, the paper uses the employment performance of graduates of a Bachelor of Arts in Communication program of a state university in the Philippines as a point of reference. The evaluation model was of a mixed-methods approach with a combination of quantitative tracer survey findings and qualitative interpretation of findings. Quantitative findings were used to establish the employment trends and competency scores, but qualitative findings, founded on interviews and input by employers or industry collaborators, were used to elucidate job transition experiences, use of skills, adequacy of training and curriculum shortcomings. The number of responses received was 80 out of 252 graduates which was approximately 32 percent of the whole population. Out of the respondents, 57 were working within half a year after graduation. The congruence between degree and job was largely positive with 32 congruence showing high, 30 moderate and 8 not congruent. Competency assessment results were desirable and a grand weighted mean of 3.97, which is verbally rated as Agree. Irrespective of such outcomes, graduates were able to mention the issues connected with the lack of experience, limited work, inapplicability of training, knowledge deficiency, and ineffective preparation in some technical knowledge. The study uses employment outcomes as system-level feedback to evaluate and redesign the curriculum unlike compliance-oriented tracer approaches. The findings would help in curriculum development, increased access to industry level resources, improved job placement and higher industry relationships.

title: Teryaq: An IoT-Enabled System for the Smart Delivery of Temperature-Sensitive Medications
authors: Durrah Aloulah, Futun Alqudayri, Mozn Alzunidi, Farah Alsubaheen, Shadan Aldhawi, Amal Aljohani
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Temperature-sensitive medications transportation faces significant challenges, especially in maintaining the needed environmental conditions to guarantee safety and efficacy. Preserving the quality of the medication is critical, and many factors play a major role in the medication safe to be used. Temperature stability is a critical factor that needs to be monitored and controlled, especially during hospital-to-patient deliveries of temperature-sensitive medications. However, traditional transportation methods show limitations in this regard, increasing the risk of temperature excursions that can compromise patient safety. This paper presents a smart system called Teryaq which is designed to enhance the transportation of temperature-sensitive medications by ensuring optimal delivery conditions. The system integrates real-time environmental monitoring with intelligent routing algorithms to minimize risk and improve efficiency. Leveraged with wireless communication and embedded sensors, the system continuously monitors temperature and automatically transmits reports and alerts. The system identifies the safest and most efficient routes by analyzing data such as delivery time and traffic patterns. It combines hardware and intelligent software to ensure reliable performance, even under rapidly changing conditions. This work aims to ensure safe, reliable, efficient, patient-centered delivery of temperature-sensitive medications while minimizing medication waste.

title: Language-Driven Portrait Editing and Animation via Diffusion and Motion Control
authors: Shoaib Mustafa Khan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: We present a feasibility study for a novel pipeline that unifies natural language–driven portrait editing with temporally consistent talking-head animation by integrating GPT-4o instruction parsing, YOLOv8-Seg region masking, Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) inpainting, and HunyuanPortrait motion transfer. Unlike existing frameworks that treat image editing and motion reenactment as isolated tasks, our system bridges them into an end-to-end workflow where user queries (e.g., "make the man wear a hat") are automatically decomposed, spatially localized, semantically edited, and animated. Experiments were conducted on dual RTX 3090 GPUs with separate CUDA allocation for SDXL and HunyuanPortrait to enable joint execution. Quantitative evaluation demonstrates that identity preservation and perceptual quality are largely maintained across edits, with PSNR of 16.78 and SSIM of 0.596 for original image-to-video consistency, compared to PSNR of 15.95 and SSIM of 0.500 for inpainted image-to-video. Cross-video comparison further confirms stability, yielding SSIM of 0.519 and LPIPS of 0.134 between original and inpainted outputs. These results highlight the viability of combining diffusion-based semantic editing with portrait animation, while also revealing current limitations in fine-grained segmentation, inpainting completeness, and inference efficiency.

title: RAST 4.0: Restorable Arbitrary Style Transfer via Content Leakage Correction
authors: Yingnan Ma, Xudong Li, Anup Basu
abstract: Style transfer aims to synthesize images by combining the content structure of one image with the target style. The RAST framework has been developed to enhance content fidelity through a multi-restoration mechanism. However, prior versions, such as RAST 1.0 and RAST 2.0, encountered issues related to color deviation and insufficient style embedding. In this work, we introduce the RAST 4.0 framework to refine the style transfer process. RAST 4.0 incorporates content leakage correction to maintain content integrity. Additionally, we modify the restoration process to optimize the style embedding performance, ensuring a more effective and balanced integration of style information. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that RAST 4.0 surpasses previous RAST frameworks and state-of-the-art methods in both content preservation and style consistency, while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Predicting Startup Success: A Machine Learning Framework Integrating Technology, Founder Psychology, Mentorship, and Sustainability
authors: Debani Prasad Mishra, Abhijeet Raj, Subham Mohanty, Rohnit Guru, Rohan Jha, Dibya Jyoti Mishra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Imagine a world where new companies are driving technology forward, and the movement of the economy even lags behind. Unbalanced outcomes become apparent very soon and indicate severe limitations to the traditional methods of predicting success that neglect morality and fulfilling the human needs. The given study seals the given gap and establishes the four pillars of technological change, connections in mentorship, the psychology of a founder, and sustainability habits, combined into one predictive model. A select list of 100+ new companies was constructed with other criteria such as flexibility, people skill, and leadership involvement compiled with numerical scores. Generation of the scores was done by a hand coded approach. To illustrate, Resilience Scores (1-10) were based on a Likert-scale assessment of problems and changes perceived and logged in a database, whereas Decision-Making Speed (hours) was based on time patterns in actions of founders. This mode takes the k-means clustering on the new businesses to classify them into behavioural categories and applies logistic regression to predict their probable feasibility in associating deep knowledge with formal computations. This paper presents a consistent framework of investigating the regulations of various aspects of business prosperity. The framework challenges the old money-focused concept and changes the focus to power, moral response, and leadership in threatening circumstances. Altogether, the research has a certain positive impact on the field of startup analytics and provides real-world instruments to assist investors and founders to proceed with managing the current business issues.

title: A Deep Learning–Enabled Computer Vision System for Real-Time Tomato Spider Mite Detection
authors: Gagandeep Kaur, Manjeet Kour, Rohini Sharma, Meenakshi Rana, Mohit Singh Bisht, Dinesh Singh Dhakar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Tomato spider mite disorder is a problem which affects the heath of the tomatoes and is due to the mite Tetranychus urticae. The mite at first affects the leaf and causes its surface to have yellowish/whitish patches and the effect of the mite feeding at last cause pale/bronze patches. The disorder on becoming more damaging results in leaf falling and curling. The areas under the leaf are also damaged and webs are formed. The growth of the leaves is also inhibited. The photosynthesis also reduces and the fruits come out as bad. The mite flourishes in hot/humid weather and disorder causes market loss. To inhibit the growth of mite which is difficult in open areas the farmers use the chemicals, removal of infected leaves and natural predators. Due to these reasons the early detection of the disorder inhibits the issue. This work uses a GoogLeNet based model on an online dataset. The performance-metric attained by this work is very efficient and results in the accuracy of 100 percent which is perfect. This model in short is efficient, simple and can help farmers get the work done.

title: System Verification of Compute Express Link over Optics Towards Large Scale Memory Pooling
authors: Xuhui Liu, Chun-Zhang Chen, Yue Geng, Liang Wang, Xiaoli Fang, Quan Pan, Hanming Wu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: With growing interest in enhanced bandwidth and reduced latency between the central processing unit (CPU) and memory devices, numerous system-level interconnecting technologies have been focused on system solutions in recent years. Among them, compute express link (CXL), developed based on peripheral component interconnect express (PCIe) physical layer, has emerged as one of the most promising solutions, owing to its capability to ensure efficient and flexible utilization of memory resources. Nevertheless, existing platforms still fail to meet all the comprehensive requirements for practical optical and electrical applications, especially in modern data centers between computing and memory cabinets. In this work, we have proposed and experimentally demonstrated a system interconnecting verification platform including an Intel Xeon CPU, an Agilex field programmable gate array (FPGA) and two linear-drive pluggable optics (LPO) optical modules. Benefiting from robust compatility and adaptability, this verification platform has obtained minimal end-to-end latency (629.7 ns) and high bandwidth (14.4 GB/s). Moreover, the eye diagrams of the whole loopback link have been monitored to confirm the feasibility of our verification platform system. Current work also demonstrates high performance in signal transmission and provides potential perspectives for the realizing scale-up memory pooling and further enabling next-generation optoelectronic interconnecting system.

title: Pre-trained Under Noise: A Framework for Robust Bone Fracture Detection in Medical Imaging
authors: Robby Hoover, Nelly Elsayed, Zag ElSayed, Chengcheng Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Medical Imagings are considered one of the crucial diagnostic tools for different bones-related diseases, especially bones fractures. This paper investigates the robustness of pre-trained deep learning models for classifying bone fractures in X-ray images and seeks to address global healthcare disparity through the lens of technology. Three deep learning models have been tested under varying simulated equipment quality conditions. ResNet50, VGG16 and EfficientNetv2 are the three pre-trained architectures which are compared. These models were used to perform bone fracture classification as images were progressively degraded using noise. This paper specifically empirically studies how the noise can affect the bone fractures detection and how the pre-trained models performance can be changes due to the noise that affect the quality of the X-ray images. This paper aims to help replicate real world challenges experienced by medical imaging technicians across the world. Thus, this paper establishes a methodological framework for assessing AI model degradation using transfer learning and controlled noise augmentation. The findings provide practical insight into how robust and generalizable different pre-trained deep learning powered computer vision models can be when used in different contexts.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Intelligent Workload Scheduling for Cloud-Native Systems Under Bursty Demand
authors: Venkata Raja Satya Teja Nagavalli, Sapan Bharadwaj Bonala, Sharath Chandra Samineni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Cloud-native systems often have bursty and highly imbalanced workload dynamics that undermine the performance of traditional resource schedulers especially when peak demand is challenging to predict on a short-term basis. This paper introduces an uncertainty-aware, burst-risk-directed workload scheduling scheme in cloud systems that integrates window-based workload modeling, burst-aware temporal feature engineering, long short-term memory learning, and risk-sensitive CPU allocation. In contrast to normal heuristic or forecast-only schedulers, the one presented actually marks high-risk burst windows and becomes more conservative in allocation when the burst likelihood is high in order to align the scheduling decisions with the operational risk. Large-scale cloud workload trace experimental evaluation indicated good burst-risk detection performance with accuracy of 0.9707, precision of 0.9594, recall of 0.9829, F1-score of 0.9710, and ROC-AUC of 0.9868. The proposed method minimized the SLA violation rate to 0.5426, reduced mean under-provision to 0.0112, and boosted burst-window protection to 0.1441 in terms of minimizing the violation rate, minimizing mean under-provision, and maximizing burst-window protection, respectively, compared to heuristic and forecast-based baselines. These findings point to the originality and practical significance of incorporating burst-risk intelligence into dependable cloud-native resource management.

title: Non-Invasive Driver Activity Recognition using mmWave Radar
authors: Basim Alhumaily, Fahad Ayaz, Sajjad Hussain, Lina Mohjazi, Ahmed Zoha
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Driver monitoring systems are crucial for automotive safety, yet conventional camera-based solutions suffer from lighting variability and significant privacy concerns. This paper presents a privacy-preserving, illumination-invariant alternative using a single Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) mmWave radar sensor to classify subtle driver head and upper-body movements indicative of distraction or fatigue. We collected a dataset from 15 subjects performing six distinct and kinematically similar activities. A specialized signal processing pipeline transforms raw radar data into micro-Doppler spectrograms using the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT). To address the critical challenge of data scarcity, we introduce a tailored data augmentation strategy that adapts the SpecAugment technique to generate a robust training set while preserving the physical characteristics of the radar signatures. Without augmentation, a lightweight, fine-tuned MobileNet-V2 model achieves a baseline accuracy of only 60% on an unseen test set of subjects. By applying our proposed augmentation method with an augmentation factor of 10, the classification accuracy increases significantly to 91.32%. This result underscores the effectiveness of our approach and demonstrates the feasibility of using a single radar sensor for robust, non-invasive driver monitoring, paving the way for the next generation of reliable and privacy-compliant automotive safety systems.

title: A Deep Learning-Based Surrogate Model for LOCOS Etch Process TCAD Simulation Using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder
authors: Yan Pan, Yiting Ye, Boying Meng, Kai Xu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Drain extended MOS (DEMOS) devices are critical components in power management and amplifier circuits, whose performance hinges on the breakdown voltage (BV). Traditional BV characterization via TCAD simulation or wafer fabrication is accurate but costly and slow. We overcome this limitation with a hybrid Deep Neural Network (DNN) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework, achieving 96% accuracy on simulation data and high performance on measured data, drastically cutting development time and cost.

title: Enhanced Parkinson’s Disease Detection using Ensemble Machine Learning and Spiral-Derived Handcrafted Features
authors: Vaibhav Dutt Trivedi, Sanjana Malhotra, Rajkumar S, Arumuga Arun R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The early diagnosis of Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is vital for early clinical treatment and better patient outcomes. Hand-drawn spiral images have recently been identified as a non-invasive and economically viable method for neurological analysis. In this paper, a holistic machine learning approach is proposed for distinguishing between healthy and Parkinson’s-affected individuals using hand-designed features derived from spiral images. The proposed feature set comprises statistical features, Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) texture features, Hu moment-based shape features, and edge density features. Various classifiers such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN), XGBoost and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) were used. A weighted soft-voting ensemble of the top three best-performing classifiers (Random Forest, kNN and Logistic Regression) further improved the classification accuracy. Hyperparameter tuning was performed using stratified 5–fold cross-validation. Experimental analysis shows that the proposed ensemble method provides stable and competitive results with lower computational complexity compared to deep learning-based approaches.

title: Local Oscillator Tuning in Heterodyne Integrated Coherent Receivers for Post-Fabrication Compensation of Polarization Impairments
authors: Natalia Herguedas, David Izquierdo, Pascual Sevillano, Ignacio Garcés
abstract: We present a straightforward and effective post-fabrication compensation method to achieve polarization-independent coherent detection in photonic integrated circuits (PICs). The approach exploits the adjustment of the local oscillator (LO) state of polarization (SOP), combined with thermal tuning of an integrated polarization beam splitter (PBS), to compensate for fabrication-induced polarization sensitiveness. A compact analytical model is developed, based on the measured TE and TM PIC responses to the signal and the LO, to identify the optimal PBS operating point and LO SOP that minimize the beat-power variation to the signal SOP. Experimental results confirm the predicted trends, showing that the receiver sensitivity variation across different signal polarizations is reduced from 8 dB to 3 dB for a BER of 10-2. This technique reduces performance variability in integrated coherent receivers due to fabrication tolerances, enabling the recovery of PICs that would otherwise be discarded. Its simplicity and generality make it applicable to a wide range of coherent integrated receiver architectures, and compatible with existing component-level tuning strategies.

title: Maximize Equipment Capacity by EI Products and Digital Twins
authors: JunjieJeremy Wu, Jacob Kim, ChengFrank Wu, Pengfei Lyu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Within high-volume manufacturing (HVM) foundries, tool efficiency constitutes a critical operational parameter, directly governing wafer throughput capacity. Performance deficiencies in specialized tool types can precipitate significant production bottlenecks. Lam Research addresses this challenge through a comprehensive throughput optimization methodology utilizing its Equipment Intelligence (EI) product suite. Systematic analysis of tool running logs enables the identification of prevalent alarms and potential failure precursors, thereby reducing unscheduled downtime (UDT). Concurrently, the application of video guidance and specialized tooling enhances the precision of preventive maintenance (PM) activities. This continuous improvement in PM quality and reduction in PM duration minimizes both scheduled downtime (SDT) and monitor duration. Collectively, these actions increase overall tool uptime (UT). Furthermore, digital twin simulations facilitate the virtual execution of process flows, enabling the identification of transfer and process bottlenecks. This integrated approach typically achieves a minimum sustained throughput enhancement of 3%, maximizing wafer output within equivalent timeframes.

title: High-Speed ADC CLK Optimization Using Advantest V93000 Wave Scale Precision Source
authors: Ya Gao, Jingyuan Zhou, Wenjun Zhou
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Clock optimization is both a critical challenge and a key determinant of performance in high-speed ADC systems. Clock jitter, which can be categorized into two core types: the random jitter and the deterministic jitter, which introduces sampling errors that are exacerbated by higher input frequencies and increased converter resolution. Primary sources of jitter include crosstalk, electromagnetic interference (EMI), power supply noise, ground bounce, and suboptimal circuit layout. This paper explores two clock-related phenomena observed during high-speed ADC testing on the Advantest V93000 platform. First, when evaluating ADC performance, the quality of the input clock is just as critical as the input signal itself in determining the final signal-to-noise ratio (SNR); the detrimental effects of both random and deterministic jitter on SNR can be effectively mitigated by leveraging low-phase-noise clock sources. Second, a flawed clock system, such as one affected by crosstalk-induced deterministic jitter, which generates clock spurs that undergo convolution with the input signal, distorting the ADC’s output spectrum and even triggering undersampling aliasing in severe cases. To address these issues, the solution lies in adopting the Advantest V93000 Wave Scale Precision Source (WSPS) with narrow band-pass filter (BPF) to optimize ADC’s sampling clock jitter. By suppressing the spurs, eliminated the aliasing and improved the overall ADC test accuracy.

title: Safeguarding Scientific Algorithms: A Framework for Detecting Algorithmic Plagiarism in Metaheuristic Research
authors: Colin L. Heffern, Halbert N. Nguyen, Walter H. Cook, Gunni U. Kamran, Giovanna Camacho, Matthew L. Bolton
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Academic misconduct increasingly occurs through algorithmic duplication, in which existing, lightly modified methods are presented as novel. This problem is especially relevant in metaheuristic optimization, a field that has experienced rapid growth along with concerns about derivative algorithms. This paper presents an AI-driven framework for detecting algorithmic plagiarism in metaheuristic research by comparing the computational logic of algorithms rather than their surface-level similarity. The proposed pipeline extracts pseudocode from academic PDFs, canonicalizes algorithms, and compares them against a reference database using four levels of similarity analysis: surface, structural, primitive, and semantic. Scores from each level are combined into an overall similarity score. The system was tested through controlled verification cases and real non-novel examples identified by experts. Results showed clear separation between disguised derivations of the same algorithm and genuinely distinct algorithms, supporting the framework’s ability to measure algorithmic novelty. These findings suggest that automated detection of algorithmic plagiarism is feasible. It could support research integrity efforts by helping journals and reviewers identify submissions in need of close expert review.

title: Synergistic Fusion of Multimodal Data for Automated and Accurate Diagnosis of Parkinson's Disease
authors: Daria Hemmerling, Martyna Fröhlich, Marta Kaczmarska, Miłosz Dudek, Justyna Krzywdziak, Kamil Burza, Marek Wodziński
abstract: We present a mixed-reality (MR) protocol on HoloLens 2 that standardizes multimodal acquisition across 17 brief, self-guided tasks probing speech/voice, ocular motor control (IR eye tracking), head/upper-limb motor function (IMU, RGB), and gait/posture. After quality control, datasets with ≥ 70% completeness from 137 participants (66 Parkinson’s disease (PD), 71 heathy-control (HC)) were analyzed. Signals underwent domain-specific processing and feature extraction, enabling a comparison of data-fusion strategies. Early fusion with L1-regularized logistic regression achieved AUC 0.995, Accuracy 0.964, F1 0.963 (stratified 5-fold cross-validation). Late-fusion stacking reached AUC 0.985, while decision-level soft voting yielded AUC 0.982. Ablation and meta-model analyses identified head-IMU dynamics and speech/voice as the dominant contributors, with hand-motor and gait/posture providing complementary signal. The results indicate that MR-standardized acquisition combined with principled fusion delivers robust, interpretable PD/HC discrimination and offers a practical foundation for longitudinal and multi-site validation.

title: LAPO: A Load-Aware and Priority-Based Offloading Strategy for Latency-Critical Applications in Vehicular Fog Computing
authors: Md Altab Uddin Molla, Asif Uddin Khan, Binayak Kar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: For critical real-time service delivery, task offloading acts as a key function, primarily for latency-sensitive and high-priority applications such as emergency response or collision avoidance. The paper presents a new strategy for task offloading called Load-Aware Priority-Based Offloading (LAPO), where the offloading of computational tasks from vehicles to fog nodes is performed dynamically. The LAPO functions are based on the prevailing system load conditions on two fronts: task load and priority. Contrarily to conventional offloading methods that route or treat all tasks in an equal manner, the LAPO follows a dual-metric decision framework, considering some load parameter of the fog node and some urgency factor of the task so as to improve resource utilization and minimize response time. In the simulation evaluation, LAPO performed better than the two baselines in terms of latency, task failure rates, and, most importantly, the prioritization of critical tasks in congested network scenarios. This method is particularly effective in dense urban environments characterized by high vehicular traffic. By incorporating intelligent load balancing and priority scheduling mechanisms, the system can significantly enhance safety-critical response times while improving overall Quality of Service (QoS).

title: SAM-Guided Local-Global Feature Fusion for Pan-Cancer Nuclei Segmentation
authors: Aditya Vijay Shinde, C. K. Mohan, Ishu Priya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Accurate nuclear segmentation is crucial in cancer tissue images for diagnosis and treatment. Deep learning approaches have been effective before, but their performance degrades significantly under different image settings. While new foundations like SAM generate stable general features, they still need to be fine-tuned to suit the medical field. This work proposes a hybrid method leveraging local boundary information obtained by the modified U-Net3+ and the global context provided by the pre-trained SAM encoder. The combination is done using an innovative X-Gated Fusion Block that implements squeeze-excitation and cross-attention operations. Adaptive feature selection is enabled through the usage of gated linear units. The proposed solution was evaluated on three publicly available datasets of cancerous images: CryoNuSeg (30 images), NuInsSeg (665 images), and CoNIC (4,981 images). For CryoNuSeg, the SAM-guided model produced the Dice score of 0.8414 and mIoU 0.7284. It demonstrates 26.4% improvement over the U-Net3+ baseline, while enhanced U-Net3+ alone improved the metrics by 7.8%. Model training brought many interesting findings concerning overfitting and necessity of early stopping.

title: Agri Hub: The Farmer's Digital Partner (Central platform for all farming needs)
authors: Supriya Telsang, Nishant Ninawe, Piyush Pagar, Surabhi Nikam, Om Agarwal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Indian farming, which accounts for 45% of the total workforce and 18% GDP, has faced challenges such as disease outbreaks, wrong choice of crops, loss of profit due to intermediaries, and fragmented technology. This paper proposes a multilingual AI-driven digital farming platform named "AgriConnect," which combines disease diagnosis of crops, choice of crops, prediction of the crop calendar, and lastly a farmer-seller market into one. This platform has a scalable three-tier architecture that combines a React and Vite frontend, a Node.js and Express backend with MongoDB, and finally a FastAPI machine learning inference service. Plant disease diagnosis is processed by a Vision Transformer Model "google/vit-base-patch16-224-in21k," which was fine-tuned on an increased PlantVillage dataset with 38 classes. The top-1 validation accuracy reached 95.12%. With the combination of disease diagnosis, choice of crops, prediction of the crop calendar, and finally creating a farmer-seller market, the tool has brought an end to tool fragmentation, which is part of the aims of Agriculture 4.0 enabled by technology.

title: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
authors: N/A

title: Predictors of Licensure Examination Performance Among BEEd Graduates Toward a Random Forest Predictive Framework
authors: Jocelyn Sagun-De Vera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This study examined the statistical predictors of licensure examination performance among Bachelor of Elementary Education graduates and used the findings as a basis for proposing a Random Forest predictive framework. The study utilized a quantitative predictive research design and analyzed the records of 51 graduates from academic years 2018 to 2022 who had taken the Licensure Examination for Teachers (LET). Predictor variables included age, sex, attendance to LET review, academic awards, entrance examination scores, and college grade point average (GPA), while overall LET rating served as the target variable. Descriptive results showed that most respondents were female, had attended formal review sessions, and obtained a GPA of 88. Statistical findings from the available dataset revealed that sex, attendance to LET review, and college GPA were significantly associated with LET performance, with review attendance showing the strongest influence. Correlation and multiple regression analyses indicated that sex, attendance to LET review, and college GPA were significant predictors of LET performance. Based on these findings, a Random Forest predictive framework is proposed for future model implementation and validation.

title: Enterprise Governance for Generative AI: Prompt Lifecycle and Secure Middleware
authors: Subhash Tatavarthi, Raghunath Reddy Koilakonda, VenkatasatyaRavikiran Bikkavolu, Sai Krishna Tarakampet, Manoj Gudala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Organizations are rapidly adopting Generative AI in enterprise decision-making, but governance has not kept pace. Traditional software controls are not designed to manage systems where behavior is shaped by prompts, context, and user intent. These elements change frequently and are rarely tracked, tested, or audited. As GenAI integrates with enterprise platforms, this lack of control introduces real risks. These include policy drift, unintended data exposure, inconsistent outputs, and declining trust in AI-driven decisions.This paper introduces a governance approach built on two foundations: Prompt Lifecycle Management (PLM) and Secure Middleware Design. Prompts are treated as governed assets with defined lifecycle controls, while middleware acts as a control layer to enforce access, policy, and consistency.Applied in enterprise scenarios, this approach leads to more consistent outputs, reduced prompt drift, stronger access control enforcement, and improved auditability. It enables organizations to move from experimental AI use to reliable and compliant enterprise deployment.

title: Ultra-low Power Backscatter for Large-Scale and High-Rate Sensing
authors: Mingqi Xie, Meng Jin, Fengyuan Zhu, Yuzhe Zhang, Xiaohua Tian, Xinbing Wang, Chenghu Zhou
abstract: This paper presents μTag, an ultra-low-power backscatter sensor that supports high-rate sensing of a large number of targets simultaneously. The core of μTag is an RF “gene editing” technique that embeds both the identity of the sensor and the real-time motion or vibration state of the attached target intensively in the transient features of the sensor’s RF signal, in a collision-resilient manner. We provide practical techniques which i) generate such “genetic signal” with purely analog and extremely simple circuits; and ii) separate the signals from a large scale of sensors reliably. Our experimental results show that our design can support concurrent tracking of 150 targets with a 12 kHz per-tag sampling rate. We also demonstrate with multiple sensing applications that μTag can achieve high-speed and large-scale motion tracking, rotation frequency sensing, and contactless sensing. The PCB power consumption of μTag is 38∼107 μW, according to the operating frequency of the tag. Our ASIC simulation based on the 40 nm CMOS process shows that the power consumption can be further reduced to 0.13∼0.52 μW.

title: mmGuard: A Countermeasure against Physical Adversarial Attacks on mmWave Radar Sensing_supp1-3698581.pdf
authors: Yan Chen
abstract: Physical adversarial attacks (PAAs) pose a serious security threat to millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar systems used in safety-critical applications such as autonomous driving and security checking. These attacks, manipulating radar signals via specially crafted materials, are proven feasible and can cause severe sensing failures; however, effective defenses remain unexplored due to the difficulty of distinguishing adversarial examples from normal environmental objects. This paper presents mmGuard, a physics-based defense framework that addresses this challenge by exploiting a fundamental insight: the engineering process that makes materials adversarial inevitably creates detectable physical signatures. We identify three key domains where adversarial examples show artificial nature: spatial phase discontinuities, anomalous radar cross-section patterns, and violations of natural physico-kinematic relationships. mmGuard systematically captures these signatures through multi-domain feature extraction, enhances their discriminability via neural refinement, and enables efficient per-object attack detection and mitigation compatible with automotive radar update rates. To enable evaluation, we introduce mmAD, comprising over 110,000 annotated radar frames with diverse adversarial examples across realistic deployment scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that mmGuard achieves over 90% detection accuracy while exhibiting strong in-distribution performance, with few-shot adaptation enabling calibration to unseen settings Case studies further validate that mmGuard can reliably defend against PAAs in real-world settings.

title: A Novel Medical Images Random Cryptosystem
authors: Eman Alnagi, Amal Saif, Ashraf Ahmad
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: Medical images are considered highly sensitive data that must be protected for privacy purposes. Encryption algorithms are one way to secure medical images before transmitting them to concerned parties. In this paper, a novel cryptosystem is proposed for medical images. The encryption and decryption algorithms utilize linear feedback shift registers (LFSRs) to generate random numbers to represent the positions of image blocks to be processed. Accordingly, the blocks are encrypted using Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) with a new mode of operation, random vector cipher (RVC), in a random order. This random encryption results in shuffled cipher blocks, and the number of these blocks would exceed the actual number of original image blocks, which may confuse the attacker in distinguishing the type of data encrypted. In addition to the traditional AES key, several secrets are used to leverage the robustness of the cryptosystem. The algorithm is implemented and tested on samples of the Medical MNIST dataset, resulting in uniform histograms for the encrypted images while preserving the correctness and integrity of the original images.

title: CARRIE UGV: A Human-Centered Terminal UGV for Carry-on Transport and Wayfinding Support
authors: Arjun Nambiar, Chin Yong Chung, Chuyang Yang, Gunwoo Lee
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: Airports are facing increasing demand for accessible passenger support, particularly for older adults and passengers with disabilities navigating post-security terminal environments. This paper presents CARRIE UGV, an Uncrewed Ground Vehicle (UGV) concept designed to augment existing assistance services by transporting carry-on luggage and supporting indoor wayfinding after Transportation Security Administration (TSA) screening. Guided by a design thinking approach, the concept development was informed by accessibility needs, prior airport assistance studies, and practitioner input from wheelchair-assistance personnel at Daytona Beach International Airport, Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, and Incheon International Airport. These inputs highlighted persistent service gaps in carry-on handling and terminal navigation, particularly in complex and high-demand operating environments.
