In response, CARRIE UGV was designed as a human-centered system that integrates real-time environmental sensing with visual and auditory guidance to enable safer, less burdensome passenger movement. The paper further discusses preliminary feasibility through system-level risk, cost-benefit, and sustainability considerations. As a conceptual design, CARRIE UGV demonstrates the potential for UGV-enabled airport services to enhance accessibility and improve the post-security passenger experience without replacing existing human assistance.

title: A Rigid Harness with Haptic Feedback for Robotic and Traditional Service Dogs
authors: Amalie J. Keefe, Justin Kauff, Blake Hament
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: This project aims to develop a harness for the Unitree Go1 and Go2 quadrupeds that implements haptic feedback to the user when obstacles are encountered in their path. This research builds upon previous robotic Seeing Eye dog designs, incorporating feedback from a focus group session, which has led to further understanding what some members of the visually impaired community value in assistive devices. Though future implementations of this work may include more advanced object detection, with computer vision tools such as LiDAR, this project uses an Arduino Uno and ultrasonic sensors for proof of concept. The final deliverable of this project is a functional prototype implemented on the robotic quadruped that includes vibration motors, adaptable mechanical integration, and an ergonomic handle for the harness. Technical design requirements have been outlined, including battery life, budget, and handle dimensions and weight. The prototype will be reviewed at an additional upcoming focus group session for feedback and iterative design. Incorporating user responses to make a useful and desired product, this research looks to advance equity, inclusion, and accessibility for members of the visually impaired community, aiding their independence through ease of navigation in unfamiliar spaces.

title: A Hadoop-Enabled Framework for Detecting Crop Yield Anomalies and Optimizing Irrigation in Smart Agriculture
authors: Rishuvan Selvakumaran, Kanishka Anantha Narayanan, Rakshan Dass, P.Malin Bruntha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: In precision agriculture, effective irrigation management and early identification of anomalous crop yield variations continue to be major obstacles, especially when dealing with extensive heterogeneous agricultural datasets. Current methods frequently concentrate on isolated statistical techniques or small-scale machine learning models without scalable distributed processing. A Hadoop-enabled framework for scalable agricultural anomaly detection and irrigation optimization is put forth in order to overcome this constraint. For distributed feature extraction, the framework combines MapReduce and Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). Z-Score analysis, Isolation Forest, One-Class Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest classification are then used for hybrid anomaly detection. Experiments conducted on multi-region historical agricultural datasets (100K–2M records) demonstrate linear scalability in processing and superior anomaly detection performance using Random Forest (F1-score: 0.94). When compared to single-node execution, the suggested architecture cuts the feature extraction time by about 83%. The framework supports the deployment of sustainable smart agriculture, improves water-use efficiency, and makes data-driven irrigation advisories possible.

title: Holding the Line: A Digital Twin Framework Supporting Policy Design for U.S. Power Grid Resilience
authors: Daniel Freedman, Alison Pike, Avery Suriano, Walker Watson, Henning Mortveit
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: The U.S. power grid faces growing reliability challenges as rising peak demand, renewable intermittency, and baseload capacity retirements strain existing infrastructure. While long-term solutions require grid expansion, near-term resilience depends on optimizing current resources. Price-based demand response (PBDR) incentivizes households to shift electricity use away from peak periods; however, existing evaluations aggregate load reductions across entire service territories, obscuring impacts on specific grid assets and communities. To address this gap, this paper develops:• A high-resolution digital twin framework that couples a synthetic population, hourly electricity demand profiles, and a distribution network to derive system-level demand reductions from individual households• A stochastic adoption model and demand reallocation algorithm to capture how household demographics drive load shifts across the distribution network• A case study of Virginia’s Eastern Shore under two candidate PBDR policies, producing 4–5% peak load reductions consistent with empirical literature while resolving shifts at the household and substation levelsThe results demonstrate that established demand response effects can be reproduced at household and substation resolution without loss of fidelity. By linking demographic characteristics and load-shifting behavior to substation-level outcomes, the framework can inform more resilient demand-side energy policy. This framework is extendable nationwide, providing a scalable approach to evaluate the grid impacts of utility policies.

title: Optimizer-Driven Techniques for Retuning Prompts on LLM Backend with Migration across Models for Healthcare and Cyber Security Operations
authors: Hassan Rehan, Zane Zimmerman, Jamshaid Iqbal Janjua
publish: 2026 Systems and Information Engineering Design Symposium (SIEDS)
abstract: The relocation of the workflows that are provided by one model to another is becoming more and more frequent, yet it remains particularly difficult in the healthcare and cyber security operations where the accuracy, safety, privacy, and cost factors are off the table. We present an optimizer-based design where migration is a black-box, constraint-sensitive, optimization problem, automatically re-optimizing existing instructions to a new LLM backend, and not based on internal model information. The method combines a meta-optimizer, transformation operators, and warm-starting using history evaluation traces to accelerate adaptation and minimize the cost of trials. We test the framework on popular LLM backends, such as OpenAI GPT-4, Anthropic Claude, and Google Gemini, on operationally motivated healthcare tasks and cyber security tasks, such as clinical note summarization and triage, compliance-oriented question answering, incident report analysis, alert triage, and threat intelligence extraction. The proposed approach is effective, better at adaptation and the cost of operations in various settings, such as compared to manual retuning, zero-shot transfer, and random search, and constrained to domains and safety. The findings indicate that optimizer-based migration is a viable and scalable approach to ensuring stable LLM performance when quickly changing the backend in production environments.

title: Promoting Generalization for Exact Combinatorial Solvers via Adversarial Instance Augmentation_supp1-3698451.pdf
authors: Jie Wang
abstract: Machine learning has been successfully applied to accelerate Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solvers. However, the learning-based solvers often suffer from severe performance degradation on unseen MILP instances due to the limited number of training instances and diversity of training distributions. To tackle this problem, we propose a novel data augmentation approach, called Adversarial Instance Augmentation, to promote the data diversity for learning-based branching modules in branch-and-bound (B&B) Solvers (AdaSolver). To generate diverse augmented instances, AdaSolver uses an augmentation policy to augment the structures of the bipartite graphs representing MILP instances. The major technical contribution is that AdaSolver formulates the non-differentiable learning problem of the augmentation policy as a contextual bandit problem, enabling efficient gradient-based adversarial training for the learning-based solver and augmentation policy. To the best of our knowledge, AdaSolver is the first general and effective framework for understanding and improving the generalization of both imitation-learning-based and reinforcement-learning-based B&B solvers. Experiments demonstrate that AdaSolver leads to a remarkable roughly 35% improvement in solving time over the B&B solver across various distributions. Moreover, AdaSolver significantly improves the sample efficiency by roughly 40% reduction in solving time compared to the competitive GNN branching baseline with only 1% of the training instances.

title: Exposing the Illusion: A Comprehensive Survey on Deepfake Detection Techniques, Challenges, and Future Horizons
authors: Zainab Rafiq, Muhammad Wasim, Rwaida Obaid Alssadi, Widian Alssadi
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The rapid advancement in deep learning methods has made it possible to produce extremely realistic synthetic media, which are referred to as deepfakes. Deepfakes have creative value in film and media production but are also pose significant threats to privacy, security, and public trust. This paper conducts an extensive overview of deepfake detection techniques, classifying them according to their inherent techniques, such as machine learning, deep neural networks, frequency-based, and multimodal analysis. We analyze the efficacy and limitations of these techniques under different datasets and real-world cases. We further discuss the essential challenges in deepfake detection, including generalizability to out-of-distribution data, adversarial robustness, and lack of high-quality annotated datasets. Lastly, we emphasize new trends and provide directions for promising future work, such as explainable AI integration, lifelong learning, and cross-modal forensics. The survey endeavors to inform researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive view of the existing state and direct future advancements toward protecting the authenticity of digital media.

title: A Comparative Study on Current Spreading Layers in n-AlGaN to Enhance the Quantum Efficiency of Deep Ultraviolet Light-Emitting Diodes
authors: Hongyu Liu, Hansong Geng, Mingyue Lou, Bowei Yu, Zongyi Feng, Huaxin Shen, Weiquan Wang, Yukai Lin, Shengli Qi, Wei Guo, Jichun Ye
abstract: In AlGaN-based deep ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (DUV-LEDs), the significant imbalance in carrier concentration between electrons and holes induces a severe electron crowding effect and current overshoot, ultimately limiting the external quantum efficiency (EQE). To address this issue, three types of current-spreading layers (CSLs)—namely, a superlattice, a high-Al-content insertion layer, and a modulation-doping layer—were respectively introduced between the multiple quantum wells (MQWs) and the n-AlGaN layer. The corresponding band diagrams and carrier distributions were investigated through technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations. Experimental results further demonstrate that the modulation doping structure achieves the most effective current spreading, substantially mitigating sidewall current crowding. This configuration yields a peak EQE exceeding 9% and the lowest junction temperature among all designs. This work presents a deeper understanding of the development of high-performance DUV light emitters.

title: AI-Accelerated Optimal Test Position Selection for MIMO RTS Over-the-Air Measurements
authors: Aoqi Ye, Jun Li, Yunpeng Guan, Chuan Huang, Yihong Qi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Electromagnetics, Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Multi-input multi-output technology is fundamental to modern wireless systems, including 4G, 5G, and emerging 6G networks, making over-the-air testing essential for performance evaluation. This paper investigates how the spatial placement of test probes affects data-stream isolation in radiated two-step testing and proposes a fast, systematic method for determining the most robust test location. We first show that phase variations directly govern the achievable isolation, yet phase information is difficult to obtain and unstable in high-frequency measurements, leading current test-location selection to rely heavily on inefficient trial-and-error procedures. To address this issue, we examine the relationship between isolation and the condition number of the spatial transmission matrix, demonstrating that the condition number is a reliable proxy for evaluating isolation. Building on this insight, we introduce the concept of an optimal test location and derive its mathematical formulation. To further accelerate the search across antennas and frequency bands, we develop a compact artificial-intelligence-based predictor that maps amplitude-only radiation patterns to the expected minimum isolation. Experimental validation in an anechoic chamber confirms a strong correlation between isolation and condition number and verifies that the proposed algorithm efficiently identifies the optimal test position without requiring additional measurements.

title: Efficient Task Offloading in AI-Agent Communication Networks: A Joint Latency and Energy Optimization Approach
authors: Xiaowen Zhu, Yuting Zhang, Junle Liu, Jie Zeng, Wei Feng, Tiejun Lv
abstract: Efficient task offloading in dynamic, collaborative multiagent networks is critical for latency-sensitive and energy-constrained applications, yet it presents a significant optimization challenge due to its combinatorial complexity and the non-Euclidean nature of network topologies. While traditional methods struggle with scalability and dynamics, existing learning-based approaches often fail to generalize across diverse network structures. The primary aim of this study is to propose and validate the hierarchical graph transformer (HGT), a novel neural architecture designed to learn powerful and generalizable policies for such complex, mixed-variable optimization problems. Instead of decoupling the problem, the HGT approaches the joint optimization in a holistic, end-to-end manner. By representing the network as a graph, HGT first employs edge-aware graph attention networks (EAGANs) to encode rich and localized physical-layer information into node embeddings. The graph transformer (GT) subsequently processes these embeddings to capture the global context and latent interagent competitive relationships. The resulting context-aware representations are then decoded into a complete action through a multihead architecture that is designed to handle hybrid discrete–continuous action spaces with global constraints. We demonstrate the efficacy of the HGT on the problem of joint latency and energy optimization in collaborative multiagent networks. Compared with strong heuristic, iterative, and recent learning-based baselines, the proposed policy achieves the best weighted utility and latency across the tested task-composition range while remaining highly competitive in energy, and it also shows strong zero-shot transferability to networks of varying scales and topologies. Our work signifies a tangible step towards creating more autonomous and efficient wireless systems, with direct implications for fields like swarm robotics and the Internet of Things.

title: IECCT 2026 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)

title: Dual-Scale Antenna Deployment for Pinching Antenna Systems
authors: Xu Gan, Zhaolin Wang, Yuanwei Liu
abstract: A dual-scale deployment (DSD) framework is proposed for pinching antenna systems (PASS), under which four implementation protocols are developed. In the proposed framework, coarse-scale deployment moves the pinching antenna (PA) over a wide range along the waveguide, while fine-scale deployment adjusts the PA with high precision within a local region. By jointly optimizing these two scales, the DSD framework fully exploits the flexibility of PA deployment while maintaining low computational complexity. Based on this framework, we establish a practical power-consumption model and derive closed-form expressions for the energy efficiency of PASS. An energy-efficiency maximization problem is then formulated to jointly optimize transmit precoding, PA radiation power, and dual-scale PA deployment. To solve this non-convex and highly coupled problem, a low-complexity penalty-based alternating optimization algorithm is proposed. Simulation results validate the accuracy of the theoretical analysis and the convergence of the proposed algorithm. The proposed DSD framework delivers about 70% higher energy efficiency than the conventional cell-free architecture and nearly a twofold improvement over MIMO systems.

title: Robust Secure Resource Allocation for RIS-assisted ISAC-NOMA Networks with Hardware Impairment
authors: Shidang Li, Xinyu Zhang, Weiqiang Tan, Chunguo Li
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) efficiently utilizes the wireless spectrum by combining communication and sensing functionalities. Focusing on a Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) system assisted by Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) in an ISAC framework, this study proposes a robust secure communication algorithm that accounts for hardware impairments, aiming to address information leakage caused by potential eavesdroppers in complex urban environments. By superimposing artificial noise (AN) onto the base station’s transmission beam and optimizing the propagation path through the RIS, the system leverages NOMA to efficiently transmit large volumes of data. A joint resource allocation problem is formulated to maximize the secure rates for all users, subject to constraints such as Successive Interference Cancellation (SIC) decoding order, user transmission rates, base station transmit power, and beamforming gain. By applying semidefinite relaxation (SDR), Taylor series expansion, and second-order cone constraints, the initial non-convex problem is reformulated into a standard convex problem. Building on this reformulation, an alternating optimization algorithm tailored for robust and secure power allocation is introduced. Simulation outcomes reveal that the proposed robust secure algorithm substantially enhances the secure transmission rates of users, thereby guaranteeing the security and reliability of communications.

title: Sub-Volt and PCIe-Compatible Optical I/O for Scalable Inter-Rack Interconnection
authors: Aoxue Wang, Xingyu Liu, Fangchen Hu, Zhipei Wang, Chenyu Wang, Yaotian Zhao, Bo Xu, Yiran Wei, Peng Zou, Fengxin Yu, Xu Wang, Jinwen Song, Xiao Hu, Haiwen Cai, Wei Chu
abstract: With the surge of data-intensive AI and cloud workloads, optical interconnects have become a key enabler for high-speed, low-latency, energy-efficient, and scalable compute-node interconnection. However, current inter-rack optical interconnects still rely on Ethernet protocol incurring microsecond-level latency, which constrain the scaling of compute-nodes across racks. To mitigate this issue, we propose a novel architecture that enables direct rack-to-rack communication through a PCIe-compatible optical I/O, leveraging the inherent nanosecond-level latency of the PCIe protocol. Furthermore, leveraging a 95 GHz bias- and electrical amplification-free silicon microring modulator (MRM) and a 85 GHz Ge/Si photodetector, this optical I/O is demonstrated supporting PCIe 6.0 transmission and 64 Gbps NRZ transmission over 2 km fiber with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< 0.8$</tex-math></inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V_{pp}$</tex-math></inline-formula> drive. The link achieves sub-pJ/bit energy consumption in the optical domain, confirming the feasibility of low-power, high-speed optical interconnects for next-generation scalable computing systems.

title: Evaluating the Englishscore Test as a Digital Alternative for Academic English Assessment in Mongolian Higher Education
authors: Bolormaa Ayurzana, Zolzaya Choijin
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: This study investigates the reliability, validity, and user perceptions of the EnglishScore test as a digital alternative for academic English assessment among students at Mongolian public universities. Aligned with the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) and motivated by national education reforms, the EnglishScore test offers a mobile-based platform designed to assess core English skills efficiently. Data collected through a structured questionnaire reveal that while many students appreciate the test's accessibility, convenience, and quick results, concerns persist regarding its comprehensiveness and comparability to established exams such as IELTS and TOEFL. Technical challenges, including unstable internet connections and time constraints, also affect the testing experience. The findings suggest that although EnglishScore holds promise as a flexible assessment tool, further refinement is necessary to enhance its reliability and acceptance as a formal academic evaluation. This research contributes to the growing discourse on digital language assessments and provides insights for educators and policymakers seeking to implement effective, technology-driven evaluation methods.

title: A Lightweight Approach to Spam Link Identification Using Automated Domain Reputation Scoring
authors: Petar Alilović, Davor Cafuta, Danijela Pongrac, Brigitta Cafuta, Ivica Dodig
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: This paper examines the extraction and reputation analysis of domains identified within an email dataset containing both spam and ham messages. Using the Enron corpus, data processing was conducted in a virtualised Ubuntu environment (VMware) to ensure security. A custom Python application extracted domains from the message bodies, with the structured output stored in .parquet format for subsequent analysis on the host system. The reputation of each identified domain was assessed using the Open PageRank API. The methodology includes comprehensive statistical analysis, such as calculating average scores and examining the distribution of domains across a categorised ranking scale (1–10). Experimental results reveal a significant disparity between the two categories, with domains associated with spam exhibiting a notably lower average reputation. The study outlines the technical implementation, the technology stack, and provides an evaluation of the benefits and limitations of this approach, highlighting its potential application in improving automated spam filtering systems

title: A Comparative Analysis of Predictive and DRL-Derived Target-Based Energy Management Smart Charging Scheduling for Small EV Fleets
authors: Maya J, Suyampulingam A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: The fast-paced increase in the number of electric vehicles deployed each year makes it essential to develop efficient and cost-effective charging stations that consider the benefits of all stakeholders involved. The rise in high-power extraction tends to stress the grid beyond the acceptable threshold, which can put the electric network at higher risk of instability. Alongside, uncontrolled battery charging can significantly reduce battery health condition. This work proposes a cost-effective grid-tied solar-powered public charging station with a hybrid optimization framework that can allocate varied charging ports (AC/DC) dynamically to the vehicles based on their battery state of health. The battery management system is integrated with this optimization structure to acquire the temperature profile and battery health status for a safer charging schedule. The system is designed to evaluate the energy optimization with three strategies. A basic heuristic approach is compared with the model predictive control method and an advanced deep reinforcement learning-derived target-based control policy (DRLDTBC). The system is simulated for a period of 24 hours, and the result shows that the DRL-derived TBC policy achieves the lowest total grid cost, Rs.2703.43, and a targeted peak grid demand of 25 kW, demonstrating the superior potential of learned control methods in smart charging environments.

title: Full Predictive Cascade Control for PMSM Based on Differential-Free Disturbance Observer and Online Parameter Identification
authors: Yujing Guo, Ruohan Zhang, Ping Jin, Gang Lei, Jianguo Zhu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a novel full predictive cascade control (FPCC) scheme for a permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM), integrating online parameter identification (OPI) with sensorless control technology. The proposed FPCC scheme is implemented through a dual-loop control structure, featuring an outer loop for speed control and an inner loop for current control. The recursive least squares method is employed for online parameter identification to estimate the resistance, magnetic flux linkage, and inductance of the PMSM. The sensorless control strategy is realized through a differential-free disturbance observer (DFDO) for the estimation of motor speed and torque. Experimental results demonstrate that this control method exhibits a faster response and superior dynamic performance across various operating conditions.

title: Privacy-Preserving AI using Federated Multimodal Attention Networks for Healthcare and Robotics
authors: Sathish Kumar Natarajan, Azween Abdullah, Ilangovan Perumal, Prabhu Natarajan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: The integration of multimodal data in healthcare and autonomous navigation presents privacy challenges while demanding high accuracy. This paper introduces a novel framework combining multimodal attention networks with federated learning for privacy-preserving AI. Our approach leverages modality-aware attention mechanisms to fuse heterogeneous data, including medical images, clinical records, RGB-D streams, and LiDAR data, while ensuring differential privacy (ε = 1.0, δ = 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−5</sup>). The system demonstrates 96.3% accuracy in healthcare diagnosis and 89.4% success rate in navigation, with an 85% communication reduction and <200ms latency. Our key contributions include a novel multimodal attention architecture for federated learning, a privacy-utility trade-off analysis, and comprehensive experimental validation across various domains.

title: The Impact of Data Analytics On Decision Making Evidence Based Practices
authors: Mahmoud Allahham, Bashar Khaled Almagharbwh, Jawad Hasan Alkhateeb, Taha Houda, Jihad Jaam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: The paper examines how educational data analytics influence student-level decision-making in schools and will focus on how evidence-based practice mediates increases in the effectiveness of data-driven strategies. The research design was a quantitative one and a structured questionnaire was given to 350 teachers, principals and school administrators in Jordanian schools. The research paper focuses on the impact of the systematic use of the education data on the strategic, teaching and learning, and administrative decision-making. The findings indicate that educational data analytics plays a significant role in enhancing the quality of decisions made by the decision-makers as it gives them the correct information with regard to student performance, resource allocation and curriculum effectiveness. Also, this relationship is partially mediated by evidence-based practice, which means that the more sustainable educational outcomes are achieved based on the analyzed data and empirical evidence. Nevertheless, the factors that limit the comprehensive adoption of analytics in the school practice are lack of data literacy, intermittent data quality, and inadequate analytical infrastructure. According to the findings, the practice of forming data-informed culture at the school level is crucial and relies on the fact that evidence-based practice can turn crude data into actionable plans. The paper hypothesizes that to enable educational data analytics to contribute to effective, transparent, and informed decision-making, it is crucial to amuse educators with their independent and evaluative abilities.

title: An Investigation into the Determinants of Aesthetic Prosthesis Development: A Case Study at Citi University
authors: Enkhbold Chuluunbaatar, Chantsaldulam Narandelger, Khos-Erdene Galbaatar
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Aesthetic prostheses play a vital role in enhancing psychological well-being and social acceptance by replicating the natural appearance of lost body parts. This study applies “Full-Profile Conjoint Analysis” to investigate consumer preferences for aesthetic prostheses in Mongolia, a region with limited research on prosthetic adoption. Four key attributes were analyzed: realistic appearance, usability, material selection and cost. Using SPSS Orthogonal Design, 16 product variants were generated and evaluated. The results revealed that usability (27.372%) and material selection (27.558%) had the highest impact on consumer choices, followed by appearance (23.942%) and price (19.855%). These findings provide valuable insights for improving prosthetic design, manufacturing, and affordability in Mongolia, laying the groundwork for future research and development in the field.

title: Neural Representation Learning for Unsupervised Control-Flow Concept Drift Detection
authors: Atyasha Apurva, Ananya Kulkarni, B Swathi, Manoj Kumar MV, Prashanth BS, Sneha HR
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Process mining techniques traditionally presuppose that business processes do not change, nevertheless, in reality, business processes may and often do change with changes of policy, system upgrades or reduced business needs. The concept drift of control flow is brought about by such changes and this reduces the accuracy and reliability of discovered process models. The paper provides an unsupervised neural network-based system that identifies and locates instances of concept drift in control flow when using streaming logs of events. The strategy is in a continuous state of analyzing event sequences to learn latent representations of both past and new traces with no labeled data in the form of information. The comparisons of the temporal distributions of these representations allow observing the sudden and gradual shifts in the process behavior. The noise or weighty occasions that result in false detections are minimized by statistical distance metrics and reconstructive validation. When drift is identified, the framework isolates the fragments of the processes, which are altered, and the activities and transitions are distinguished. The testing on realistic and synthetic process mining benchmarks proves good detection accuracy, known false alarm, and localization.

title: Azure Windows Defender for Enterprise Cybersecurity: Architecture, Automation, and Measured Operational Impact
authors: Jagadish Almaipeta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Modern enterprises operate in increasingly complex hybrid and cloud-native environments, where traditional perimeter-based security controls are no longer sufficient. The rapid adoption of cloud platforms, remote work models, and distributed applications has expanded the attack surface and introduced new cybersecurity challenges. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of leveraging Azure-based security services, with a particular focus on Windows Defender technologies, to build a scalable, automated, and resilient enterprise cybersecurity posture. The study explores system architecture, operational deployment strategies, automation capabilities, and measurable security benefits observed in real-world enterprise environments. The findings demonstrate that integrating Windows Defender with Azure security services enables proactive threat detection, reduced response times, and improved operational efficiency, while supporting compliance and governance requirements across large organizations. The evaluation is based on enterprise operational metrics collected over a three-month post-deployment observation period. This study contributes empirical evidence and an operational framework for enterprise-scale security automation using Azure-native XDR.

title: Energy-Efficient Container Orchestration for HPC Workloads using ML in Kubernetes on Cloud Platforms
authors: Abhimanyu Bajaj, N.S.Pradeep, V.Aarthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advancement in Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (IECCT)
abstract: Cloud computing has revolutionized application development by enabling scalable, flexible, and automated deployment. Kubernetes is at the heart of container orchestration and continuous integration. Although containerization and orchestration technologies are widely deployed, they have not yet been integrated into the High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment, especially for energy efficiency and smart workload distribution. The standard Kubernetes scheduling mechanisms tend to result in underutilized resources, and creating new custom schedulers introduces additional complexity, testing difficulty, and maintenance issues. This research presents an Energy-Efficient Container Orchestration (EE-CO) architecture for HPC workloads that combines Machine Learning (ML) with Kubernetes on the cloud infrastructure. EE-CO optimizes adaptive workload allocation, performance surveillance, and resource management in heterogeneous CPU architectures. It enhanced workload allocation efficiency and cost-effectiveness, and reduced energy consumption and computational costs. The potential of ML-powered Kubernetes orchestration for developing sustainable, scalable, and energy-efficient HPC deployments in the cloud.

title: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
authors: N/A

title: Cross-Scene HSI Classification Combined with Channel-Spatial Attention Fusion Network
authors: Yalin Li, Aili Wang, Haibin Wu
publish: 2025 International Symposium on Computer Science and Educational Technology (ISCSET)
abstract: Hyperspectral imagery (HSI) classification faces challenges stemming from intricate spectral signatures and distributional discrepancies. In this paper, we introduce a new classification method that fuses spatial-spectral attention and domain generalization technology, and propose a hybrid deep attention architecture that integrates CNN and transformer for dual-branch (local-global) hyperspectral classification, enabling efficient extraction of discriminative spectral features. In addition, we use a coordinated optimization framework with a Lion optimizer and cosine annealing strategy to reduce local optimality. Results from experiments on various HSI datasets indicate the proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art HSI classification models, which validates its efficacy in cross scene HSI classification.

title: Governed Agentic Cloud Data Pipelines with MCP Gateways
authors: Aswathnarayan Muthukrishnan Kirubakaran, Vinoth Punniyamoorthy, Akshay Deshpande, Adithya Parthasarathy, Nachiappan Chockalingam, Mythili Annamalai Sekar, Naga Surya Pasupuleti, Shiva Carimireddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Autonomous agents are increasingly used to orchestrate cloud data and machine learning pipelines to improve adaptability and operational efficiency. However, most agent-tool integrations rely on loosely specified interfaces and prompt-driven invocation, providing limited guarantees on correctness, security, and governance. This restricts adoption in production and regulated environments where auditability, policy compliance, and operational reliability are mandatory. We present a governed orchestration architecture in which all agent actions are mediated by a Model Context Protocol (MCP) gateway that enforces bidirectional JSON Schema validation, semantic compatibility, and policy constraints while recording append-only telemetry for auditing and root cause analysis. The gateway establishes a protocol-level trust boundary between autonomous reasoning and production tools. We implement a prototype and evaluate it on open datasets and workloads across batch and micro-batch pipelines with controlled failure and violation injection. Results show that MCP mediation improves pipeline success rate by up to 7.6%, reduces mean time to recovery by 58.3%, blocks over 96% of injected schema, policy, and security violations, and introduces less than 6.2% p95 end-to-end latency overhead. These findings indicate that MCP-based gateways enable reliable and auditable deployment of agentic orchestration in production cloud pipelines.

title: Dyn-SSM: Towards the Efficient Long Sequence Learning via Bio-interpretable Dynamics in Spiking State Space Models
authors: Yan Zhong, Ruoyu Zhao, Chao Wang, Jiaqi He, Qinghai Guo, Jianguo Zhang, Zhichao Lu, Luziwei Leng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) provide a lowpower, energy-efficient solution by utilizing the spike-based and sparse nature of biological systems. Since the advent of Transformers, SNNs have struggled to compete with artificial networks on long sequential tasks, until the recent emergence of state space models (SSMs), which offer superior computational efficiency and modeling capability. However, despite the initial effectiveness of applying highly capable SSMs to SNNs for long sequence learning, existing SNN-based SSMs ignore the complex dynamics of biological spiking neurons, which are crucial for the functionality but challenging to simulate and exploit effectively in large networks. In addition, the membrane potential is determined by the past spiking history of the neuron, leading to reduced efficiency for sequence modeling in parallel computing scenarios. Therefore, we propose Dyn-SSM, which aims to explore and leverage biologically motivated and more interpretable dynamics in SNN-based SSMs for long sequence learning. Specifically, we propose to incorporate the reset-refractory mechanism in SNN-based SSMs to leverage the inherent temporal dimension for long sequence modeling. Then, to enable parallel processing, a novel parallel max-min boundary compression strategy (PMBC) that can be widely applied is proposed to accelerate the inference process. Finally, we hierarchically integrate the proposed neuron model to the original SSM block, and enhance the dynamics of Dyn-SSM by incorporating trainable thresholds and refractory magnitudes to balance accuracy and sparsity. Extensive experiments illustrate the effectiveness and robustness of Dyn-SSM, showing the potential of dynamic spiking neurons for balancing sparsity and precision in efficient long sequence learning. The code and Supp. Mat. are publicly available in <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/zhyan567/Dyn-SSM</uri>.

title: AI-Enabled Remote Proctoring: Architecture and Implementation for Digital Examinations
authors: Vishal Shukla, Aatam Prakash Sharma, Surbhi Ghai, Ashwath Suresh Hegde, Lalit Rupani, Pradnya Desai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: With the increased use of online examinations since the pandemic, academic integrity has come under assault like never before, without bodies in the room to guarantee the proper integrity and reliability of assessment. Therefore, educators turn to new solutions and mechanisms that can ensure reliability and integrity across virtual assessment situations. This paper presents an AI-based online examination assessment system that monitors student activity in front of a camera during an exam, and at the same time, renders warnings to the student and/or assesses integrity breaches after the exam. This system relies on computer vision-based techniques, the YOLO object detection model—to detect complicated behaviors, unfocused eyes, multiple bodies, and other devices in view, as well as browser actions like minimizing screens and switching windows—during an online examination and assesses browser activities like minimizing windows and toggling tabs. Warnings issued are rendered during the exam in real time, while video footage and pixelated warning frames will be electronically stored for further integrity assessments. By merging multiple approaches to vigilance under one umbrella through an AI-integrated system, a scalable, efficient, and privacy-preserving solution is enabled to accommodate the demand for an appropriate online assessment solution in the modern world since student motivations are not always discernable.

title: LLM-Enhanced Multi-Agent Transfer Reinforcement Learning for Sensing, Communication, Computing, and Control Co-Optimization in Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Junyuan Zhang, Chi Xu, Haibin Yu
abstract: Dear Editor, With the rapid development of new-generation information and communication technology, the fusion of sensing, communication, computing, and control (S3C) is becoming increasingly significant for cyber-physical systems (CPS). However, due to the non-convexity, the curse of dimensionality, and the partial observability faced by CPS, traditional convex optimization algorithms are challenging to deal with S3C co-optimization. Thus, this letter establishes the S3C problem as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and proposes a large language model (LLM)-enhanced policy transfer (PT) framework for multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL), denoted as LLMPT-MADRL. Herein, MADRL learns a co-optimization policy while LLM-enhanced policy transfer improves the exploration efficiency, as LLM utilizes large-scale dynamic neural neurons like human brain to form powerful intelligent reasoning capabilities. However, MADRL and LLM exhibit heterogeneity in neural network scale. Thus, we further design an adaptive policy transfer loss function to achieve dynamic adaptation between MADRL and LLM, avoiding the mismatch of policy transfer information. Furthermore, we conduct a case study on the co-optimization of sampling coefficient, communication bandwidth, computing frequency, and control input to minimize the system delay and control error of CPS. Experimental results show that the reward of LLMPT-MADRL is increased by 16.8%, the system delay is reduced by 24.5%, and the control error is reduced by 10.4% compared with the benchmark MADRL-based algorithms.

title: Is It Too Early to Talk “Quantum Software”?
authors: Hassan Soubra
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing
abstract: Quantum computing (QC) is often framed as a technological race defined by qubit counts, hardware breakthroughs, and error-correction milestones. Between roughly 2014 and 2025, however, a distinct quantum software layer began to emerge alongside advances in physical devices. Drawing on indicators such as investment patterns, patent activity, the institutionalization of university programs, and emerging measurement standardization efforts, this essay suggests that the mid-2020s may represent a formative period in the consolidation of quantum software as a recognizable field. The question is not simply whether it is premature to speak of “quantum software” but what historians can observe when a discipline begins to take shape ahead of its anticipated underlying hardware.

title: Industrial Scene Gas Leakage Detection: A Cross-Attention Based Multimodal Feature Difference Network and A New Benchmark
authors: Ziming Wang, Linlin Yang, Xingyu Guo, Ran Zhang, Sheng Xu, Xianbin Cao, Baochang Zhang
abstract: Industrial gas leakage detection is critically important for safety and environmental protection. While infrared imaging enables detection of invisible gases, two challenges remain: existing datasets lack realistic industrial scenarios, and current methods struggle to distinguish gas plumes from background interferences or segment discontinuous gas distributions. This paper introduces a benchmark comprising an Industrial RGB-Thermal Dataset (IRTD) with gas emission and leakage data from laboratory and industrial sites. A VLM-assisted RGBThermal detection framework with a Cross-Attention based Feature Difference (CAFD) module is designed to enhance gasspecific feature differentiation by computing inter-modal feature discrepancies. Evaluations on public datasets and IRTD demonstrate state-of-the-art results.

title: A Hybrid Three-State Step-Down DC-DC Converter With Reduced Inductor Current and Wide Conversion Ratio for Battery-Powered Low-Profile Electronic Devices
authors: Junyu Zhong, Wendi Wang, Yanqi Zheng, Ka Nang Leung
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper reports a hybrid three-state converter (HTSC) which features inductor-current reduction and a wide range of voltage conversion ratio. With the proposed four types of switching mechanism of power stage incorporated with the proposed three-state operation for each switching-mechanism type, the HTSC structure is able to achieve optimized efficiency over a wide step-down range by controlling both the inductor and capacitor currents to be small simultaneously. A feedforward control scheme for the transitions between the types of switching mechanism is proposed to achieve smooth transitions with small transient errors. The proposed HTSC is implemented on board for quick validation of concept and evaluation of performance. Upon the use of a low-profile inductor of 22 μH with dc resistance (DCR) of 230 mΩ, the proposed HTSC provides a regulated output of 3.3 V with a maximum of output current of 2.5 A over an input range of 3.7–16.5V. It corresponds to a voltage conversion ratio of 0.2–0.9. Efficiency at the maximum output current is 91.3%, which reveals the effectiveness of the reduction of conduction loss at the high DCR.

title: Generation Expansion Planning Toward the Decarbonization of the Baja California Sur Electrical System
authors: Antonino Lopez Rios, Gilberto Lopez Rios, Pablo Moreno Villalobos, Mario R. Arrieta Paternina
abstract: This article analyzes the energy transition and decarbonization of the Baja California Sur Interconnected System (BCSIS), an isolated electrical grid with a high dependence on fossil fuels. It proposes a generation expansion plan (GEP) that incorporates renewable technologies such as wind power, photovoltaic (PV), concentrated solar power (CSP), and battery energy storage systems (BESS), with the aim of improving reliability, reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and lowering operating costs. To achieve this, three expansion scenarios are evaluated for the period 20252039, using simulations carried out with PLEXOS software.

title: Tri-Polarization Effect and Performance Analysis for Dual-Polarized Distributed MIMO Communications
authors: Yuan Xu, Chongwen Huang, Li Wei, Wei E. I. Sha, Zhaohui Yang, Tierui Gong, Yijian Chen, Zhaoyang Zhang, Erry Gunawan, Mérouane Debbah
abstract: This paper unveils the underlying tri-polarization effect in distributed multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems with dual-polarized access points (APs) and tri-polarized users. Specifically, APs and users follow independent Poisson point processes, and the multi-polarized multi-user channel is modeled by the dyadic Green’s function. The eigenvalue analysis reveals that the spatial distribution of the APs and the AP-user height difference contribute to the excitation of an additional polarization dimension. The varying propagation angles of signals from multiple spatially distributed APs lead to angular spread, while the height difference further amplifies this effect by reducing signal coherence, thereby facilitating the excitation of the additional polarization dimension. Based on the derived channel eigenvalues, the capacity gap between tri-polarized receivers and baseline dual-polarized receivers is quantified, as well as the energy projection into the additional polarization dimension. Simulation results demonstrate that distributed MIMO can effectively excite the additional polarization, increasing the effective degrees of freedom by up to 1.5 times compared with dual-polarized configurations. Moreover, energy dispersion across three polarization dimensions reduces capacity by about 30% for dual-polarized receivers. In contrast, tri-polarized receivers fully capture the additional polarization gain and thereby eliminate this loss. The excitation is maximized at an average AP-user elevation angle of 45°, where the energy projection into the additional polarization dimension reaches 20%. Finally, the close agreement between theoretical derivations and simulations provides practical guidance for antenna deployment and a new paradigm for exploiting polarization diversity.

title: Convergence Study of the Process of Nonlinear Evaluation of Parameters of Dynamic Objects when Using Series-Parallel Models
authors: Anton Silvestrov, Mykola Ostroverkhov, Liudmyla Spinul, Valery Chibelis, Heorhii Veshchykov
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The algorithm of sequential using of models of three types - serial, parallel and serial-parallel is considered for determining the parameters of the mathematical model of a dynamic object if disturbances are present in the input and output signals in order to ensure the ellipticity of the functional of the proximity of the model and the object and the convergence of the relaxation process of the optimization of the model coefficients. In order to obtain guaranteed unbiased estimates of the parameters of mathematical models of dynamic objects in real conditions of limited data samples and dynamic ranges of object state variables, the sequentially using a parallel model with orthogonal operators, a sequential-parallel model for orthogonal approximation and further using of these models is effective strategy for a more accurate display of “input-output” of the object and, as a result, unbiased evaluation of the sought parameters of the dynamic object.

title: Edge–Cloud Predictive Maintenance Models for Industry 4.0
authors: Ahmad Junaid, Abuzar Khan, Abid Iqbal, Ghassan Husnain
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Conference on Computing and Machine Intelligence (ICMI)
abstract: This paper presents a predictive maintenance framework for Industry 4.0 that combines preprocessing, feature engineering, machine learning, deep learning, explainability and prescriptive optimization within a single unified system. The dataset was first cleaned, balanced and engineered to retain 10 key features, with torque identified as the most important predictor. Baseline models such as CatBoost, LightGBM, and TabNet showed strong performance, with CatBoost achieving an accuracy of 0.994 and a ROC-AUC of 0.994. Deep learning simulations were also compared with lightweight edge models and cloud based architectures, and the shallow MLP achieved an accuracy of 0.962 with a latency of 1.2 milliseconds. SHAP and LIME further showed that torque related ratios and failure type labels played an important role in accurate prediction. This helps make the system more transparent and easier to trust in real-world industrial use. Final benchmarking showed that edge models delivered inference nearly 10 times faster, whereas the highest accuracy of 0.993 and F1 score of 0.992 were obtained by the cloud models. Overall, the results indicate a scalable, balanced and sustainable predictive maintenance system that improves efficiency, accuracy and operational cost.

title: Parallel Test Strategy for Chiplet Testing for HPC/AI Devices - Leveraging Smartest 8 Interleaved Testflow Solution
authors: Zhang Jun, Vince Li
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: With the rapid growth of computing demands for artificial intelligence (AI) and high-performance computing (HPC) chips, traditional monolithic chip architectures are facing bottlenecks. In this context, multi-chiplet heterogeneous integration (adopting 2.5D/3D technologies) has become a core technical path in the post-Moore’s Law era. However, this integration brings significant increases in testing complexity, which prolongs the product time-to-market. To address this challenge, Advantest’s SmarTest 8 software on the V93000 platform proposes the "Interleaved Testflow Execution" solution. It enables parallel testing of heterogeneous chiplets through optimized and deterministic interleaving of testflows, minimizing idle time of testing hardware without modifying the original test logic. This solution is easy to operate and significantly reduces test time.Verified by detailed test data from customer’s large-scale HPC device, this study marks the first practical application validation of the interleaved testflow feature, providing reliable support for improving the testing efficiency of multi-chiplet AI/HPC chips.

title: Prediction of Chip Aging-Induced VMIN Shift Based on Spice Simulation
authors: Haiyong Wang, Qi Wei, Chenfei Wu, Hailiang Zhang, Guohua Zhou, Keqing Ouyang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: Aging effects significantly degrade transistor performance, leading to increased minimum operating voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf>) in deep sub-micron technologies. This paper presents a SPICE-based simulation methodology to predict aging-induced V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf> shift by integrating compact aging models. The V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf> shift of simulation prediction align good with high-temperature operating life (HTOL) measured result. Results demonstrate the feasibility of simulation-driven V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">min</inf> shift prediction for robust circuit design over product lifetime.

title: Using the Internal Operational Logs of the hinotori™ Surgical Robot System to Evaluate Latent Structural Metrics of Robotic Surgical Skill
authors: Munenori Uemura, Kiyoyuki Chinzei, Takeshi Urade, Nobuaki Yamasaki, Junichiro Hirata, Yasuyoshi Okamura, Tomoko Yamaguchi, Hideto Ueki, Kaito Nanchi, Tatsuya Hattori, Yuki Mitani, Yasuo Chihara, Seiichi Ozawa, Takumi Fukumoto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Surgical robot systems generate detailed kinematic telemetry, yet these internal logs remain underexplored as a resource for quantitative analysis of operative behavior. This study investigated whether motion features derived from maintenance-grade telemetry of the hinotori™ Surgical Robot System capture the characteristics of robotic surgical expertise. Thirty-nine participants—robotic surgeons, laparoscopic surgeons without robotic experience (Lap), and novices—performed a standardized suturing task while internal motion logs were recorded. Motion features, including conventional kinematic metrics and geometric coordination descriptors, were computed from instrument and endoscope trajectories. Feature importance was analyzed using multiple tree-based machine-learning models with repeated resampling. Across all models, spatial coordination features consistently ranked highest. The most robust feature was the Triangular Stability Index (TSI), which represented the geometric stability of the endoscope–instrument configuration. Robotic surgeons exhibited significantly lower and more stable TSI values than Lap and novice participants, indicating coordinated scope–arm control. These findings suggest that robotic expertise may be characterized by robot-specific visuomotor coordination rather than general surgical experience. Internal telemetry provides an objective and scalable foundation for behavioral analysis, surgical training, and adaptive robotic assistance.

title: A Vision-Based Deep Learning Framework for Egyptian Sign Language Recognition Using CNN and VGG-16
authors: Fatma G. El-Megharbel, Mennatalla T. Elgamal, Mazen Ebrahim, Tamer M. Nassef
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Systems, and Emerging Technologies (ICAISET)
abstract: Sign language is considered the main linguistic bridge for the Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing community; however, a continuous communication gap still exists because only a limited number of hearing individuals have achieved fluency in sign languages. This study presents an efficient vision-based deep learning system for recognizing Egyptian Sign Language (ESL) using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transfer learning techniques.The proposed models were developed using a dataset that contains 20 signs that are frequently used in everyday life in Egypt, expressed in two different forms according to the Egyptian dialect and Standard Arabic.The Model’s accuracy was evaluated through the integration of spatial feature extraction using CNNs with temporal modeling to capture sign motion, specifically employing the VGG16 architecture. To measure real-world hand and body landmarks, Media Pipe Holistic was utilized to enhance recognition performance during testing. The experimental results indicate that the VGG16-based model achieved an overall accuracy of 96.12%, while the CNN-based model achieved an overall accuracy of 96%. To further develop and validate the proposed algorithm, a real-time graphical user interface application was created for sign prediction. These results establish the proposed framework as a scalable and effective solution for automating sign language recognition and improving the overall quality of life for individuals in the Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing communities.In this paper, Arabic Sign Language is referred to as ArSL to avoid confusion with American Sign Language (ASL).

title: The influence of ISSG hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio on the growth of tunnel oxide for Nor flash memory cycling disturb loss
authors: Xing Wang, Ye-Tao Lu, Cheng Yu, Min Zuo, Yuan-Feng Xu, Lan Sun, Yi-Hua Shen, Zhen-Sheng Xue, Huang Liu
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: The tunnel oxide in NOT OR(Nor) flash memory transistors is one of the most critical and fundamental layers that enable data storage functionality. The quality of the tunnel oxide directly determines data retention capability. During read, write, and erase operations, the thickness and quality of the tunnel oxide are crucial. If this oxide layer has defects, uneven thickness, or contamination, leakage paths can form, leading to charge loss and resulting in data errors. The characteristics of the In-Situ Steam Generation (ISSG) perfectly align with these requirements. The oxide layers grown by ISSG exhibit extremely low initial defect density and a near-perfect Si-SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> interface. This paper provides an in-depth exploration of improving Nor flash memory cycling disturb loss by adjusting the hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio in the ISSG process. The results indicate that a lower hydrogen-to-oxygen ratio enhances the endurance of Nor flash memory performance, increases the number of program/erase cycles, and improves the Chip Probing(CP) yield of Nor flash memory .

title: AI Assisted Toll Revenue Forecasting and Reconciliation using Flower Pollination Optimized Dl Model
authors: Venkateswarlu Mullangi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Networks, and Security (IC-ICNS)
abstract: Accurate toll revenue forecasting and reconciliation is essential for effective financial planning and operational control in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). This paper presents an Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based predictive analytics framework for toll revenue forecasting and reconciliation. The raw data is fed to data pre-processing technique, containing data cleaning and null value removal, followed by univariate and bivariate using data visualization to identify key patterns and trends. Feature engineering with normalization enhances data consistency and model stability. The proposed Spatial-Temporal based Long Short-Term Memory (ST-LSTM) model is employed to capture temporal dependencies and traffic variations influencing toll revenue. Further the Flower Pollination Algorithm (FPA) is incorporated for hyper parameter optimization. The model’s performance is evaluated using standard prediction metrics to ensure robustness and reliability. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed FPA optimized ST-LSTM have the MAE of 0.034, MSE of 0.00190 and RMSE of 0.04, r2 of 99% effectively improves toll revenue prediction accuracy and supports efficient reconciliation by identifying potential anomalies, constructing it a valuable decision-support tool for transportation agencies.

title: Reconstruction of Arterial Blood Pressure Waveforms from Photoplethysmogram Using a Modified Transformer-CNN Model
authors: Mathew Cigi, V Raj Kiran, P. M. Nabeel, Jayaraj Joseph
abstract: Accurate and continuous monitoring of arterial blood pressure (ABP) is essential for the diagnosis and management of cardiovascular diseases. While photoplethysmography (PPG) signals offer a convenient and non-invasive alternative to conventional cuff-based measurements, predicting continuous ABP waveforms from PPG remains challenging due to the complex and non-linear relationship between these signals. This study presents a novel deep learning framework that integrates a transformer encoder with a convolutional neural network (CNN) decoder to reconstruct ABP waveforms directly from PPG signals. The transformer encoder is designed to capture long-range temporal dependencies and contextual relationships within the PPG signal, enabling modeling of dynamics over extended time windows. In contrast, the CNN decoder focuses on reconstructing morphological details of the ABP waveform, including characteristic features such as the systolic upstroke and diastolic decay. The proposed model was evaluated on the PulseDB dataset and achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of $\mathbf{4. 5 6} \boldsymbol{\pm} \mathbf{5. 7 2 ~ m m H g}$ for continuous ABP waveform reconstruction, demonstrating strong correlation between predicted and reference signals. Additionally, the model achieved MAEs of 4.79 ± 6.82 mmHg for systolic blood pressure (SBP) and $3.93 \pm 5.34$ mmHg for diastolic blood pressure (DBP). The obtained error magnitudes and variability are within the limits recommended by the Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI), indicating estimation accuracy. Overall, this work highlights the potential of transformer-CNN hybrid architectures for accurate, continuous, and non-invasive blood pressure monitoring in clinical and wearable healthcare applications.

title: Analysis of the impact of SACQ solder balls and high-reliability solder paste on board-level temperature cycle life
authors: Yunong Zhao, Zhuqiu Wang, Niuyi Sun, Dan Yang, Na Mei, Keqing Ouyang
publish: 2026 Conference of Science and technology of Integrated Circuits (CSTIC)
abstract: As advanced packaging and chip applications continue to evolve, board-level thermal cycle life remains a challenging requirement. This study, through specialized board-level thermal cycle testing, systematically analyzed the effects of SACQ solder balls and high-reliability solder paste on thermal cycle life, drawing three key conclusions that can guide chip life improvement efforts.

title: BadIQA: Backdoor Attack against No-Reference Image Quality Assessment Models
authors: Yinghao Wu, Liyan Zhang
abstract: No-Reference Image Quality Assessment (NR-IQA) methods aim to predict image quality without any reference information, which is broadly employed for medical image diagnosis, social media content moderation, e-commerce product image assessment and so on. In the past decade, the rapid advancement of deep learning makes Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based NR-IQA methods show superior performance compared to traditional NR-IQA methods based on Natural Scene Statistic (NSS), and become a foundational component for high-level complex applications. However, recent researches have proven that Deep Neural Networks (DNN) are susceptible to backdoor attacks, which can cause model misclassification by providing attacker-chosen specific trigger. Therefore, to safely deploy NR-IQA models in security-critical applications, it is imperative to thoroughly investigate potential vulnerabilities to facilitate further model refinement. In this paper, we make the attempt to explore the backdoor attack against NR-IQA models. However, naïvely transplanting mislabel-based backdoor attacks from classification task to regression task will cause catastrophic degradation in normal regression performance. To break the hurdle, we treat the backdoor injection process as a rank reordering problem and correspondingly propose two special loss functions (KL divergence loss and Rank loss) to jointly optimize the whole regression model, while maintaining its original functionality. Extensive experiments further demonstrate the effectiveness of BadIQA under different datasets, backbones, and trigger settings, as well as its resilience against representative backdoor defense methods.

title: Evaluating the Resilience of Urban Road Networks Against Recurrent Disruptions: A Hysteretic Approach
authors: Leonardo Flores, Jorge Vargas, Gonzalo Panizo, Edwin Salas, Jeremy Ramos
abstract: Performance-based resilience measures arise from the need to measure the dynamic response of transportation systems to extreme disruptive events, where recovery is central. Extending the scope of these measures for recurrent events demands a proper assessment of the disruptive stage, which exhibits a progressive behavior determined by cascading failure. To account for both disruptive and recovery stages, we propose a hysteretic model based on commonplace data from microsimulation models, whose arriving flow can be described by an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic process that addresses the stochastic nature of the performance variable. By relating the deterministic component of the calibrated stochastic process with the solution of a differential equation analogous to a mass-spring system, the resulting hysteresis curve can describe system resilience as a unified process, covering both the disruptive and recovery stages, while its area can describe performance loss. Finally, we propose a new quantitative resilience measure, the normalized hysteretic resilience (NHR), associated to the hysteresis loop. The methodology is demonstrated for the case study of the influence area of a bus rapid transit (BRT) terminal when a critical segment is blocked by a traffic accident. Findings show that the NHR measure is suitable to quantify the nonlinear behavior of traffic flows and complements well with existing resilience measures.

title: Hybrid Mode Rectifier With Reduced Impact on the Tuning Condition for Efficient Wireless Power Transfer Systems
authors: Xiaoqiang Wang, Jia Mao, Shipeng Qin, Fei Xie, Ping Yang, Jianping Xu
abstract: In wireless power transfer (WPT) systems, active rectifiers (ARs) are widely used for output-power regulation without dual-side communication. Their main drawback is the additional reactance introduced at the receiver side, which perturbs the resonant condition, reduces power-transfer capability, and degrades system efficiency. This article proposes a hybrid mode rectifier (HMR) that combines full-bridge rectifier with voltage-doubler rectifier to regulate the output with substantially less impact on the tuning condition. The regulation characteristics of the HMR are derived for both current-source-mode compensation topologies (CMCTs) and voltage-source-mode compensation topologies (VMCTs). Compared with conventional ARs, the proposed HMR maintains a lower input-impedance phase deviation and lower reactive-power circulation, thereby enabling high power factor and high efficiency. Zero-voltage switching is also achieved, which reduces switching loss. Comparative analysis with conventional ARs is provided, and a concept-of-proof 600 W laboratory prototype is built to validate the proposed method.

title: Experimental Assessment of Multiple Underwater Image Enhancement Techniques
authors: Dharmendra Kumar Mahato, Md. Imtiyaz Anwar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: In this paper, various underwater image enhancement and restoration methods are compared on the basis of results obtained using U-45 image dataset with the help of evaluation metrics. The methods evaluated with comparison of various image restoration techniques, including the Dark Channel Prior (DCP), the blue-green channel dehazing and red channel correction-based approach known as Image Blurriness and Light Absorption (IBLA), a novel optical model (NewOpticalModel, NOM), the Underwater Dark Channel Prior (UDCP), which is a modification of DCP for underwater noisy images, and a method based on the Underwater Light Attenuation Prior (ULAP). In contrast, the image enhancement techniques assessed include histogram equalization (HE), an improved method known as contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), a technique incorporating gamma correction (GC), the unsupervised color correction method (UCM), and methods based on Rayleigh-stretching and dual-intensity image composition, specifically the Rayleigh Distribution (RD). While all of the aforementioned techniques are effective with nearly all kinds of underwater image, UCM and RD produced the best results which are validated by evaluation criteria. To improve the results in the future, we could attempt a combination based on the fusion of two or more methods.

title: Short-Circuit Contributions of Current Controlled Voltage Source Converters
authors: Thomas Hunger, Goetz Lipphardt
publish: 2026 International Scientific Conference on Electric Power Engineering (EPE)
abstract: Electrical power grids are more and more penetrated by power electronic converters used in renewable energy sources, storage systems or DC links. The voltage source converter type is widely used in these applications. A fundamental task in planning and operation of electrical power grids is the short-circuit current calculation, which is based on the standard IEC 60909-0:2016 [1]. The short-circuit current calculation has to consider the influence of power electronic converters and the standard provides information accordingly. This paper thoroughly analyzes the short-circuit current contribution of voltage source converters. It gives a detailed description of the behaviour of the converter during grid faults and identifies the parameters which influence the size and time response of the short-circuit current. The results are compared with the short-circuit current calculation according to the standard.

title: DRIFTS: Distributed Robustness for Time Series is Feasible, Distributed, and Fun
authors: Daniel Amadori, Emanuele Chini, Pietro Sala
abstract: As complex machine learning models are increasingly integrated into critical systems, ensuring their robustness, transparency, and trustworthiness becomes essential. Explainability methods that offer formal and verifiable guarantees about model behavior are crucial not only for interpretability but also for robust decision assurance. In this work, we address the problem of generating formally grounded and robustness-aware explanations for classifiers operating in the time series domain. Our main objective is to introduce the concept of a continuous anti reason. We formalize this concept as an Interval Constraint Function (ICF) which, given a classifier and a sample, assigns an interval for each feature value of the sample. An ICF that constitutes a continuous anti reason for a given sample ensures that all data points whose feature values lie within the corresponding intervals are classified differently from the sample class. Each continuous anti reason is associated with a cost, quantifying the extent of perturbation required to induce a class change. The higher the cost of a continuous anti reason, less robust the classifier is on that sample. Therefore such a property is paramount for precisely characterizing the robustness boundaries of the classifier on a sample-wise basis. Given that computing a continuous anti reason is CONP-complete, we develop the framework called Distributed Robustness Interval for Time Series (DRIFTS), a distributed algorithm that leverages an in-memory key-value store to coordinate multiple workers. The effectiveness and scalability of our approach are validated empirically on Random Forest models using univariate time series datasets from the UCR Archive.

title: Prosumer as the Smallest Energy Hub and the Prerequisites for its Operation at a Decentralized Power Grid
authors: Andrii Ivakhnov, Oleksandr Kulapin, Stanislav Fedorchuk, Kostiantyn Makhotilo, Dmytro Danylchenko, Olexii Bulhakov
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: Modern global energy trends are characterized by a paradigm shift from traditional centralized management toward decentralized architectures. This transition involves incorporating distributed generation and structuring local networks into functional energy hubs, which serves to mitigate the operational challenges imposed by the volatile nature of intermittent renewable energy sources. This study explores the structural layout of energy hubs, focusing specifically on prosumers as the fundamental, granular units within decentralized power systems.
